topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 2/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
Introduction
There are various different tissues within the ovary
The surface of the ovaries is covered by a peritoneal epithelial layer. Underneath that,
there is the ovarian stroma which consists of connective tissue and sex cord stroma.
The sex cord stroma contains the ovarian follicles from which the oocytes and the
hormone-producing cells (granulosa cells and theca cells) emerge. During sexual
maturation, FSH causes individual follicles to mature during each cycle (primordial
follicles that are already present at birth mature into primary, then secondary and
�nally tertiary follicles, also known as Graa�an or antral follicles). In the normal
course of events, rupture of the follicle (ovulation) occurs. After the follicle ruptures,
the Graa�an follicle becomes the corpus luteum. The ovary is a dynamic organ. It not
only changes depending on a woman's cycle, but also changes throughout her life.
Ovarian tumors
As with all organs, the ovary can be affected by both benign and malignant tumors.
The benign tumors include actual neoplasms as well as non-neoplastic tumors.
Benign neoplasms may arise from almost any ovarian tissue (epithelium, connective
tissue, sex cord stroma or germ cells), with benign epithelial tumors being the most
common benign neoplasms.
Non-neoplastic ovarian tumors include endometriomas, abscesses, and functional
ovarian cysts. A distinction is made between cystic, solid, and cystic-solid ovarian
tumors based on morphology (which is determined through pathoanatomical
analysis, ultrasound and computed tomography). This distinction is purely
descriptive and says nothing about malignancy. Ultimately, evaluation of ovarian
tumors always involves obtaining the histology to rule out a malignant process.

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 3/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
DDee��nitions
• Ovarian tumor: A benign or malignant growth in the ovarian tissues
• Benign ovarian tumor: A benign neoplasm of the ovarian tissue (epithelium,
connective tissue, sex cord stroma, germ cells) or a non-neoplastic ovarian
tumor (endometrioma, abscess, functional cyst).
• Cyst: A �uid-�lled sac lined with epithelium (exact de�nition). In everyday
clinical practice, the term “ovarian cyst” is often used colloquially for all cystic
ovarian structures that measure more than 3 cm in diameter.
• Functional cyst: A follicular cyst that has not ruptured, measures more than 3
cm in diameter and is usually the result of a functional disorder (e.g., a
hormonal disorder).
Overview ooff the benign tumors ooff the ovary
Classi�ed according to the origin/the tissue of origin and the Benign neoplasm
arising from it.
(see also the WHO classi�cation of all ovarian tumors) [B12]
Benign neoplasms
Epithelium
Epithelial tumors (60 % of all benign ovarian neoplasms)
• Adenomas (serous 30 %, mucinous 15 %, endometrioid 5 %). The serous
cystadenoma contains �uid (Figure 1D), whereas the mucinous cystadenoma
contains a mucinous (gelatinous) substance (Figure 1C). Serous cystadenoma
(= serous ovarian cyst = serous cystoma), Mucinous cystadenoma (=
pseudomucinous cystoma = ovarian mucinous cystoma). Cystadenomas can
become very large and can �ll the entire abdominal cavity (see Figures 1A and
1B).
• Urothelial tumors (Brenner tumor: 2 % of all ovarian tumors, 98 % benign).
Figure 1: Adenomas.
Germ cells

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 4/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
Germ cell tumors
• Mature teratomas (solid, cystic or fetiform = homunculus) (�gure 2): Most
common form: mature cystic teratoma, also known as dermoid cyst (contains
portions of all three germ layers: �uid portions, fat, sebum, hair (�gure 2A), bone
portions and teeth (�gure 2B)). 25 % of all benign ovarian neoplasms.
• Monodermal (e.g., struma ovarii)
Figure 2: Mature teratomas.
Sex cord stroma
Sex cord stromal tumors
• Granulosa cell tumors are low-malignant potential tumors or tumors with
unclear potential for malignancy (90 % adult, 10 % juvenile), they often produce
estrone or estradiol in a deregulated manner. Important note: estrogen-
producing ovarian tumors can lead to endometrial hyperplasia and even
endometrial carcinoma due to stimulation of the endometrium.
• Theca cell tumors (= thecoma) often produce progesterone or
hydroxyprogesterone in a deregulated manner).
Note: most other sex cord stromal tumors tend to be malignant more often than
benign. Sertoli-Leydig tumors (= androblastoma, arrhenoblastoma) may produce
testosterone.
Organ scaffold made ooff connective tissue
Connective tissue tumors
• Fibroma
• Lipoma
• Meigs Syndrome: ovarian �broma with ascites and pleural effusion (benign)
Meigs Syndrome: ovarian �broma with ascites and pleural effusion (benign)
Non--neoplastic ovarian tumors
Endometriosis

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 5/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
Endometrioma (= endometrioid cyst)
Endometriomas contain endometriosis lesions that bleed each month as normal
intrauterine endometrium does. The endometriosis lesions deposit themselves on
the surface of the ovary and form cysts known as inclusion cysts due to the
adhesion. They are also known as endometriomas (�gure 3A). When an
endometrioma is surgically opened, old blood drains out of it and the blood is often
chocolate-colored (�gure 3B). This is why endometriomas are often also called
chocolate cysts.
Figure 3: Endometrioma.
IInn��ammatory processes
• Hydrosalpinx (= fallopian tube �lled with serous �uid)
• Pyosalpinx (= fallopian tube �lled with pus)
• Tubo-ovarian abscess (transvaginal ultrasound (�gure 4A) and intraoperative
site (�gure 4B))
Figure 4: Tubo-ovarian abscess.
Cysts based oonn the ovarian function
Funktionelle Zysten: Follikelzysten & Corpus-Luteum-Zysten (Theka-/Lutein-Zysten;
Morphologisch und klinisch fällt die Unterscheidung zwischen Theka- und Lutein-
Zysten kaum ins Gewicht, daher subsummiert unsere Darstellung die beiden unter
dem Begriff Corpus-Luteum-Zysten.)
• Follicular cyst: A follicle that does not rupture at the right time and whose

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 6/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
diameter reaches more than 3–4 cm is considered a follicular cyst. The cause
of this may be a central hormonal imbalance or a localized �uid imbalance of
the follicle. Follicular cysts can rupture and bleed from the rupture site, causing
signi�cant lower abdominal pain (hematoperitoneum). Follicular cysts can also
hemorrhage (due to increased tension in the wall of the cyst) or torsion can
occur, which can also cause signi�cant pain.
• Corpus luteum cyst: In some cases, the corpus luteum may remain intact for
months and become cystically dilated and �lled with blood. Corpus luteum
cysts are not uncommon during pregnancy; the corpus luteum can grow up to 8
cm in size during pregnancy. A corpus luteum cyst can have a quite normal
“endocrine function” Complications, which may also occur in pregnant women,
can include torsion or rupture.
• Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS): PCOS affects both ovaries and is
associated with endocrine symptoms that help establish the diagnosis. The
majority of follicles exhibit lutein-cystic transformation. Ovulation is disrupted
or does not occur (--> E4).
Pregnancy
Ectopic pregnancy
• Tubal pregnancy
• Ovarian pregnancy
Figure 5: Ectopic pregnancy.

Epidemiology
In Switzerland, approximately 700 benign ovarian tumors are newly diagnosed each
year. Overall, the incidence in Europe is about 1.5 % of the female population.

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 7/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
Clinical picture and complications
Ovarian tumors often cause no symptoms or only minor non-speci�c symptoms,
even though they may already be very large.
• Characteristic clinical symptoms are rare because ovarian tumors are:
• freely mobile in the lesser pelvis and
• do not affect the neighboring organs in the �rst instance.
By the time many of these tumors are diagnosed, they already have a diameter of
more than 10 cm. Ovarian tumors are often discovered as incidental �ndings during
clinical screening, during a transvaginal ultrasound, or in the course of imaging
diagnostics for other diseases.
The following symptoms can occur:
• Increase in the abdominal circumference
• Lower abdominal discomfort (pain)
• Feeling full, back pain
• Problems with urination or defecation
• Compression symptoms in the large vessels of the abdominal region
(thrombosis, vena cava syndrome, lymphedema/venous insu�ciency)
• In the case of hormone-producing ovarian neoplasms: postmenopausal
bleeding, pseudopubertas praecox, virilization in the case of testosterone
production
Torsion ((ovarian tumor torsion))
Torsion manifests as an acute abdomen with sudden abdominal pain, usually arising
from complete health. It is often accompanied by nausea or vomiting, and is
sometimes in�uenced by a change in position. However, the clinical picture may also
be subacute and recurrent.
The torsion can be triggered by sudden body movements, which are suddenly slowed
down again (e.g., gymnastics or working out at the gym). Due to its mass, the
ovarian tumor rotates around its own axis (around the infundibulopelvic ligament (=
ligamentum suspensorium ovarii), which contains the arteria ovarica and vena
ovarica, and in this position it blocks the blood vessels (> initially venous congestion,
then stagnant arterial blood �ow > extreme ischemic pain).
The series of images below (�gure 6) shows the surgical site from left to right:

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 8/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
• Torsion of the right adnexa (�gure 6A)
• Surgical detorsion of the right adnexa (�gure 6B)
• Recovery of the fallopian tube and ovary (�gure 6C)
Emergency surgery is often required in the case of torsion of an ovarian tumor.
Figure 6: Surgical site.
If the time interval between torsion and surgery is too long, the adnexa can become
completely necrotic, in which case it must be removed, as shown in the next series
of images (Figure 7).
Figure 7: Necrotic adnexa.
Rupture ((ruptured ovarian tumor))

Rupture of an ovarian tumor can also cause acute abdomen. In most cases, blood
leaking from the rupture causes peritoneal irritation, which is what causes the acute
pain. However, the disease course can also be oligosymptomatic.
Benign ovarian tumors carry some risk of acute complications such as pain caused
by stretching of the walls – and are also potentially dangerous due to the risks of
torsion, hemorrhage, or rupture.
The development of malignancy in an initially benign ovarian tumor is considered to
be very rare (examples: endometrioid ovarian carcinoma in the context of
endometriosis [P11]; mucinous ovarian carcinoma from mucinous cystadenoma).
[B11]

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 9/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
Diagnostics
Today, ultrasound diagnostics (e.g., transvaginal ultrasound, possibly in combination
with Doppler ultrasound blood �ow measurement) can be used to detect subtle
morphological changes (space-occupying lesions in the millimeter range).
From a diagnostic perspective, it is important to recognize physiological changes in
the ovary that do not require further clari�cation on the one hand, and on the other
hand, not to overlook any pathological changes (especially malignant changes).
History//investigations
• Family history (ovarian cancer, breast cancer, endometriosis)
• Menstrual cycle disorders? (common in the case of functional cysts)
• Signs of virilization or hyperandrogenism? (tumors with endocrine activity)
• Acute abdomen? (torsion, hematoperitoneum)
• Dysmenorrhea? (endometriosis)
• Dyspareunia? (endometriosis, abscess)
• Pain when pressure is exerted on the bladder or intestines (space-occupying
lesion)
• History of surgery or infertility (sactosalpinx after hysterectomy, sterilization, or
postinfectious adhesions)
Palpation ooff the abdomen
• Space-occupying lesion
• Peritoneal signs
Bimanual examination
Space-occupying lesion (size, consistency), pain in the adnexal spaces, cervical
motion tenderness. It is possible to miss small and non-palpable tumors, but also
very large and very mobile tumors that have moved upwards from the small pelvis
into the abdominal cavity.
Transvaginal ultrasound

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 10/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
The most important additional examination to complement the bimanual
examination.
It is used to assess size, internal structure (solid, cystic, septate/chambered,
papillary projections inside), and margins (smooth, projections outside).
Single-chambered, homogeneous, purely cystic �ndings are generally benign.
Ascites often occurs in ovarian cancer, but also after ruptured benign �ndings
(ovarian cyst, endometrioma) and in benign Meigs syndrome, which is very rare.
MRI
Increasingly, MRI is being used to answer more unique questions, and in particular, to
plan complex interventions [P12].
Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is becoming increasingly important in the
differential diagnosis of adnexal tumors of unclear origin [P18]. There are still no
clear indications for MRI of the pelvis. Nevertheless, some surgeries can be avoided
if transvaginal ultrasound is supplemented with an MRI scan. [B12][B18]
Laboratory ��ndings:: Leukocytes,, CRP,, tumor marker CCAA--112255
iinn serum
• Infection parameters to exclude an infection (abscess)
• CA-125: Limited validity for the identi�cation of ovarian carcinoma. CA-125 may
also be elevated at times in benign ovarian tumors, endometriosis,
in�ammatory processes and/or non-gynecological causes involving peritoneal
irritation (e.g., liver cirrhosis). Also note: CA-125 is not elevated in every ovarian
cancer!
CA-125 on its own is not suitable for the diagnosis of ovarian cancer (sensitivity 71
%, speci�city 83 %). CA-125 may be a helpful indicator in combination with other
tests and during the course of the disease.
Pregnancy test
To rule out pregnancy (ectopic pregnancy).
Remarks

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 11/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
Doppler and color Doppler ultrasound: This provides information about the blood
supply to a tumor, but is of only limited help since increased vascularization can also
be found in the case of in�ammatory processes and even in the case of a corpus
luteum.
In premenopausal women, a smooth, cystic "ovarian tumor" may be "only" a
functional cyst, even if it is 4–5 cm in diameter. If malignancy criteria are not met
and the patient is symptom-free, watchful waiting with a follow-up ultrasound is
justi�ed in these cases.
In postmenopausal women, there are no non-pathological reasons why an ovarian
mass is likely to recur. Therefore, the clari�cation process should always be carried
out in such cases. CA-125 is usually determined.
However, cysts that spontaneously resolve can also occur during the menopause.
Histological con�rmation by laparoscopic adnexectomy is often necessary.
Do not under any circumstances perform diagnostic puncture of a �nding that is
suggestive of ovarian carcinoma!
IMPORTANT: doing so may risk seeding tumor cells and implanting metastases
in the puncture canal!

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 12/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
Differential diagnosis and complications
The following must be differentiated from benign tumors of the ovary:
Ovary
• Malignancy of the ovary
• Borderline tumor of the ovary
• Ovarian ectopic pregnancy
• Ovarian metastasis of another primary tumor (endometrium, stomach, colon)
Uterus/fallopian tube/fallopian tube and ovary combined
• Pregnancy in the case of uterine malformation
• Fibroids (subserosal/intraligamentary)
• In�ammatory tumor (e.g., edema in the case of salpingitis)
• Tubal pregnancy (ectopic pregnancy)
• Neoplasm of the fallopian tube
• Paraovarian cyst (see below)
• Sactosalpinx, etc. (see below)
Lesser pelvis/abdomen/retroperitoneum
• Tumor of the sigmoid colon (diverticulitis abscess, colorectal carcinoma)
• Metastases in the pouch of Douglas
• Pelvic kidney, cystic kidney
• Lymphoma, neurinoma, etc.
Paraovarian cysts (synonym: hydatid cyst of Morgagni):
• Paraovarian cysts are benign masses adjacent to the fallopian tubes, which
arise from remnants of the Müllerian ducts. They often have a stalk (danger of
stalk torsion). If they occur close to the ovary, there is a risk that they will be
confused with ovarian cysts.
Sactosalpinx (hydrosalpinx), pyosalpinx, hematosalpinx (ectopic pregnancy,
endometriosis)
• all of these lead to "tumor-like" enlargement of the fallopian tube

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 13/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
Treatment
Treatment is generally based on the following principles:
• Symptomatology
• Malignancy prognosis; menopausal status
• Risk factors (age, comorbidities)
• The patient’s wishes
The key question is: Is surgical clari�cation and therapy necessary, or can watchful
waiting be justi�ed?
Often, malignancy cannot be ruled out, which means surgery and histology are
necessary for con�rmation. This in turn leads to a relatively large number of
"unnecessary" surgeries.
Watchful waiting
This is often the �rst-line approach for premenopausal women, but it is largely
impossible for (long-term) postmenopausal women.
Post-menopause, the large group of functional cysts drops out of the differential
diagnostic spectrum.
Cysts that may require “watchful waiting” follow-up exhibit the following
characteristics (as a rule of thumb): smooth-walled, no internal structures, no more
than 5 cm in diameter.
Prescribe an ovulation inhibitor if necessary.
Clinical and ultrasound examinations must be performed at de�ned intervals, with
the �rst examination usually taking place after 6 weeks (e.g., after the next
menstruation):
• Harmless �ndings (such as persistent follicular cysts) should have resolved
spontaneously by then.
• If there is no change in the �ndings (inpatient �ndings), it is permissible to
continue to wait in many situations.
• In the case of morphological or clinical changes (see below) during the
observation period, a (usually laparoscopic) clari�cation or therapy is indicated.

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 14/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
Surgical treatment
The following are indications for a surgical approach in the case of cystic �ndings:
• Increase in size of a cyst at follow-up
• Mobile cyst over 6 cm in size (due to risk of torsion or rupture)
• Persistent symptoms
Surgical treatment is always required in the case of:
• Suspected torsion / rupture / bleeding
• Any suspicion of malignancy (including B symptoms, thromboembolisms of
unclear etiology, ascites, family history, etc.)
• Most postmenopausal patients
• Indications of a benign neoplasm, especially in the case of certain ultrasound
�ndings (e.g., dermoid cyst, endometrioma)
Surgical procedures
In postmenopausal patients, bilateral laparoscopic resection of the adnexa is the
usual approach. In patients who wish to have children, an attempt should be made to
operate in a manner that preserves the organ (enucleation or resection of the cyst).
Laparoscopy
Every patient must be properly informed prior to surgery that it may be necessary to
switch to a laparotomy at any time during a laparoscopy. Examples of when this may
be necessary include:
• Extensive adhesions that make it impossible to continue the laparoscopic
procedure.
• Severe bleeding or iatrogenic organ injury (bowel, bladder, ureter, etc.)
• Intraoperative suspicion of malignancy or evidence of malignancy in the frozen
section examination
• Intraoperative cytology of the abdominal washings has a high diagnostic value
[P14].
• Tubo-ovarian abscesses in patients who wish to have children are usually
operated on in an organ-preserving manner, i.e., they are lanced, irrigated and
drained.
• If the patient feels her family is complete, abscesses should be completely
removed (IMPORTANT NOTE: this can be a very challenging surgery in the case
of actinomycetes!).
[B14]

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 15/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
Laparotomy
If the diagnostic criteria already result in a clear suspicion of malignancy
preoperatively, laparotomy is usually performed as the �rst-line treatment – because
ovarian carcinomas should not be operated on laparoscopically.

Clinical practice and research
Clari��cation ooff adnexal ��ndings
Ultrasound, especially transvaginal ultrasound, is the most important tool in the
diagnosis and differential diagnosis of ovarian �ndings. There are de�ned criteria
that are used for diagnostics. A good summary of these can be found in "Guideline
Adnexbefund, Schweizerische Gesellschaft für Gynäkologie und Geburtshilfe" [P03].
In the case of unilocular cysts up to 7 cm in diameter, transvaginal ultrasound is
su�cient. In the case of larger �ndings, or �ndings that cannot be clearly classi�ed
by ultrasound, an MRI of the pelvis can be performed. In cases of high suspicion of
malignancy, a CT scan of the abdomen (possibly combined with a CT scan of the
thorax) is usually performed. [B03]
The relevant criteria that are included in the ultrasound evaluation of adnexal
�ndings are the following:
• Size
• Locularity / septa
• Echogenicity
• Wall thickness / septal thickness
• Papillary projections
Size
As the size of the �nding increases, so does the risk of malignancy.
Table 1: Size
Size Malignancy
< 5 cm 1,9–13,9 %
5–10 cm 7,0–35,6 %
> 10 cm 12,5–71,8 %
Locularity//septa
As the number of cysts increases, the risk of malignancy increases. Septa with a
thickness of more than 3 mm are very likely to be malignant.
Table 2: Risk of malignancy [B15]

keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
Locularity Malignancy
Unilocular (0–6,1 %) 1 % [P15]
Multilocular 18–37 %
Echogenicity
Echogenicity is determined by the composition of the �ndings or the cyst contents.
• Echo-free, homogeneous, acoustic enhancement > serous
• Greyish, homogeneous > mucinous, purulent
• Reticular pattern > coagulum
• Hyperechogenic, acoustic shadow > solid
Figure 8: Examples of ultrasound �ndings
A – Follicle persistence; B – Dermoid cyst; C – Abscess
Papillary projections
Papillary projections within the cyst or on its surface are a sign of malignancy.
Papillary projections are malignant in 40,5 to 83,7 % of cases. If the Doppler
ultrasound shows high perfusion of the septa or papillary projections, this is an
additional sign of increased risk of malignancy.
Figure 9: Papillary projections.

Scores, i.e., points-based systems, were developed to evaluate the ultrasound criteria
of unclear adnexal �ndings in a semiquantitative way.
The Sassone Score is a simple and easy-to-use score.

Combining ultrasound criteria with tumor markers, most importantly CA-125, is
routine in clinical practice and helpful in decision making. There has been much
discussion about the use of tumor markers as a screening method. Although the
tumor marker CA-125 is often elevated in ovarian cancer, it is not useful as a
screening tool.
CA-125 is not elevated in every ovarian cancer. Approximately 50 % of ovarian
cancers show elevated CA-125 in stage I and approximately 90 % in stages II to IV.
Elevated CA-125 occurs in numerous benign conditions, which is why approximately
98 % of moderate CA-125 elevations are false positives.
Elevated levels of CA-125 can be found in the following benign conditions:
• Fibroids
• Endometriosis and adenomyosis
• Benign ovarian neoplasms
• In�ammation of the peritoneum
• Pleura or pericardium including adhesions and tuberculosis
• Pregnancy
• Liver disease
Elevated levels of CA-125 can be found in the following malignant conditions:
• Ovarian cancer
• Lung cancer
• Breast cancer

topic: Benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis (Teil 19/23)
keywords: benign ovarian tumor findings and their differential diagnosis
content:
The scores described above have been further developed by the IOTA group. The
objective was to improve sensitivity and speci�city. In a �rst step, IOTA de�nes
"simple rules", with 5 criteria for malignancy (M-features) and 5 criteria for benign
tumors (B-features). M1 tumors are irregular solid tumors, M2 tumors have ascites,
M3 tumors have more than 4 papillary structures, M4 tumors are irregular
multilocular solid tumors larger than 100 mm, and M5 tumors have strong perfusion
(color score 4). The criteria for benignity are B1 unilocular cyst, B2 largest solid
portion less than 7 mm, B3 acoustic shadow, B4 soft multilocular �nding less than
100 mm in size, and B5 weak perfusion (color score 1). The ultrasound �nding is
most likely malignant if at least one M-feature and no B-features are present.
Conversely, if there is at least one B-feature and no M-features, the �nding is most
likely benign. This allows approximately 75% of all �ndings to be assessed with a
sensitivity of 92% and a speci�city of 96% [P22]. The simple IOTA rules have been
further developed. When the simple rules are combined with the age of the patient, a
logistic regression model (LR2) can be calculated. The simple IOTA rules, combined
with menopausal status and CA-125 value allow the calculation of the Risk of
Malignancy Index (RMI). When the IOTA simple rules are combined with the age of
the patient, the CA-125 value, and the experience level of the examining center,
another index, the ADNEX (Assessment of Different NEoplasias in the adneXa)
model, can be calculated. You can download iPhone and Android apps for this model
on the IOTA website. When the user launches the app and enters the information in
the �elds provided, the app calculates a probability (in %) for benignity, malignancy,
borderline, and even early or late stages of ovarian cancer and ovarian metastases
from other malignomas. The value of the ADNEX model was analyzed [P23] and it
was concluded that the ADNEX model discriminates well between benign and
malignant and that the model is a useful tool to support decision-making with the
aim of reducing morbidity and mortality in the assessment of ovarian tumors. [B22]
[B23]
Website IOTA
Special patient populations
• Ovarian tumors can also occur in children and adolescents. Patients become
symptomatic either because of pain (torsion!), endocrine symptoms
(hormonally active tumors) or because of the space-occupying lesion in the
abdomen. Benign �ndings are considerably more frequent and the same
ultrasound criteria apply for the assessment of malignancy as in adults. Germ
cell tumors are found in 26 to 47 %, germ line tumors in 3 to 5 %, and epithelial

Last update: december 2022
Cervical and endometrial polyps
Author:: DDrr.. med.. Susanne Lanz
Oberärztin, Fachärztin für Gynäkologie und Geburtshilfe, Schwerpunkt operative Gynäkologie und
Geburtshilfe, speziell Myomsprechstunde, Dysplasie, minimalinvasive Chirurgie, Universitätsklinik für
Frauenheilkunde, Inselspital Bern
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr. med. Michel Mueller

Introduction
Polyps are hyperplastic protrusions of the mucosa. In the uterus, they are
categorized based on their location as endometrial (uterine) polyps or cervical
polyps.
Polyps are a common phenomenon, often oligo- or asymptomatic, often discovered
incidentally during a routine gynecological examination or a transvaginal ultrasound.
The following questions are relevant and will be discussed with an evidence-based
approach in this chapter: Do asymptomatic polyps require any treatment? How are
polyps removed correctly? Does the procedure differ for pre- and postmenopausal
patients? Or for asymptomatic patients versus those with abnormal uterine bleeding
(AUB)? Can polyps show a malignant histology? If so, what are the parameters for
assessing the risk of malignancy? What is the relevance of endometrial polyps for
fertility/infertility?

DDee��nitions
Endometrial polyps are localized hyperplastic overgrowths of stroma and
endometrial glands. They can have a wide base or be attached to the uterus with a
pedicle, and range from a few millimeters to several centimeters. Large endometrial
polyps or those with very elongated pedicles can even prolapse through the cervix
and be visible in the vagina (= in statu nascendi).
Figure 1: Hysteroscopic images of endometrial polyps (with kind permission of the author S. Lanz).
Cervical polyps arise from a hyperplasia of endocervical mucosa. They are found
inside the cervical canal or may prolapse into the vagina.
Endometrial polyps consist of:
• Endometrial glands
• Stroma
• Blood vessels
Figure 2 (with kind permission of the author S. Lanz).
A & B – Colposcopic view of cervical polyps
C – Hysteroscopic view of polyp pedicle (center of image B)

Epidemiology
Endometrial polyps are common. The exact incidence remains unclear as they are
often asymptomatic. High resolution transvaginal ultrasound is nowadays widely
used, even for routine examinations with no clear indication, and device technology
has improved So asymptomatic polyps are diagnosed more and more frequently.
Studies suggest that the prevalence ranges from approx. 8 to 35 %, depending on the
study population. Known risk factors are advanced age, obesity, hypertension,
diabetes, and tamoxifen treatment.
There is little data on the natural course of endometrial polyps, but according to one
study, polyps can spontaneously regress and disappear by up to 27 % within an
observation period of one year.
The prevalence of polyps with premalignant (hyperplastic atypia) or malignant
(endometrial cancer or sarcoma) histology is between 3,4 and 4,9 % in
postmenopausal and 1,1 % in premenopausal patients. This risk is much higher in
symptomatic patients, and the aforementioned risk factors also increase the risk of
malignancy.

Clinical presentation
Endometrial polyps
• Smaller polyps are usually asymptomatic. They may cause brownish spotting.
• Larger polyps can cause hypermenorrhea, menorrhagia or metrorrhagia. There
is some discussion as to the role of endometrial polyps in the diagnosis of
sterility (see below).
• If polyps enter the cervical canal, this can cause contraction-like pain.
40 % of postmenopausal bleeding is caused by endometrial polyps.
Cervical polyps
• Often asymptomatic
• Occasionally increased discharge and/or bleeding on contact or spotting
Polyps and risk ooff malignancy

The vast majority of endometrial polyps are benign. However, as in normal
endometrium, premalignant (atypical hyperplasia) or malignant (carcinoma,
carcinosarcoma) histology can be found in endometrial polyps. If a polyp with
atypical hyperplasia is removed by means of hysteroscopy, the risk of concomitant
carcinoma in the surrounding endometrium is 30,8 %. A hysterectomy (usually with
bilateral adnexectomy) is therefore recommended for postmenopausal patients in
this case. For premenopausal women, a hysterectomy or - in speci�c cases, where
the patient wishes to preserve fertility - progestogen therapy is an option.
In general, the risk of premalignant or malignant polyps is higher in postmenopausal
(nearly 5 %) than in premenopausal women (1,1 %), and higher in symptomatic
patients with AUB (5 %) than in asymptomatic patients (1,8 %).
The known risk factors for endometrial cancer, such as obesity, advanced age and
diabetes, are also risk factors for premalignant or malignant histology in an
endometrial polyp.
Attempts to de�ne other parameters (e. g. size on ultrasound) for assessing the risk
of malignancy have not been successful so far.
For this reason, as there are no clearly de�ned criteria suggesting that a malignancy
is very likely, or can be ruled out with relative certainty, the hysteroscopic resection of
polyps is widely recommended in practice. The surgery is relatively minor, the rate of
complications is low and the accuracy of histological results after total resection is
very reliable.
Polyps and fertility//infertility

Endometrial polyps are found in approx. 8 to 12 % of women of childbearing age.
There is no clear link between their presence and impaired fertility.
However, the prevalence of polyps increases to approx. 32 % in patients with
infertility. Possible explanations for the negative impact of polyps on fertility, in
addition to the mechanical effect, include the secretion of certain molecules that
affect sperm transport or embryo implantation and can worsen endometrial
receptivity. As evidence of the causal link between polyps and infertility, in a
randomized trial of infertility patients with endometrial polyps, twice as many
pregnancies were achieved in the group with hysteroscopic polypectomy before
intrauterine insemination as in the group with polyps left in utero.

Diagnosis
In the case of AUB, the usual general diagnostic procedure is carried out:
• Gynecological examination (speculum)
• Transvaginal ultrasound (where 3D ultrasound with Doppler is somewhat
superior to 2D ultrasound) If it is di�cult to distinguish between endometrial
thickening and endometrial polyps, sonohysterography, whereby the uterine
cavity is �lled with saline via the cervical canal, can be helpful.
• Diagnostic hysteroscopy
Endometrial polyps
Often discovered as an incidental �nding during vaginal ultrasound.
Figure 3: 2D ultrasound, power Doppler ultrasound and hysteroscopic image of an endometrial polyp (with
kind permission of the author S. Lanz).
Cervical polyps
Size ranges from a few millimeters to several centimeters. When directly visible, they
present on the ectocervix as smooth-edged, round, usually reddish lesions.
Figure 4: Pedunculated serrated polyp protruding into the vagina (uterus opened longitudinally).

Differential diagnosis
Cervical polyps have a typical presentation, and are hardly ever mistaken for other
pathologies.
All endometrial polyps undergo histological testing to differentiate:
• Adenoma/Adenomyoma
• Submucosal �broid
• Carcinoma (most signi�cant cause of bleeding for differential diagnosis!)
• Sarcoma
• Retained products of conception/placental polyp

Treatment
Endometrial polyps should always been removed under visual control.
Instrumental polyp removal (always with the pedicle) plus in most cases subsequent
additional measures if indicated on differential diagnosis:
Endometrial polyps
1. If a polyp is seen protruding into the vagina, in premenopausal patients with
normal menstruation (no AUB), it can simply be twisted off at the base. (in
absence of histopathological abnormalities, no further measures).
2. In all other cases (location in uterine cavity, post menopause, AUB), a
hysteroscopy for targeted resection under visual control is indicated. There is
still insu�cient evidence as to which technical instruments should be preferred.
Resection can be carried out with a loop electrode, or using hysteroscopic
devices that separate the tissue with rotating or vibrating blades with direct
aspiration of the removed tissue.
3. In order to avoid missing potential endometrial carcinoma or sarcomatoid
changes, hysteroscopic resection is followed by uterine curettage in high-risk
patients.
4. Note: "Blind" removal methods using uterine curettage alone (without a
hysteroscope) are obsolete.
The combination of hysteroscopic polypectomy and curettage provides a high
level of accuracy for the diagnosis or exclusion of endometrial carcinoma.
Hysterectomy should be considered, particularly in post- menopause:
• In case of numerous polyps - polyposis.
• In case of additional pathologies (e.g. adenomyosis, myoma, etc.)
• Before a hysterectomy is carried out, histological analysis of a mucosa sample
is mandatory, either with a Pipelle de Cornier (alone, if results con�rm
malignancy) or hysteroscopy/resectoscopy and curettage.

Figure 5
A – Endometrial polyposis (surgical specimen)
B – Uterine polyposis (vaginal sonohysterography)
Cervical polyps
1. Pedunculated polyps in premenopausal patients with no AUB can be removed
by twisting off at the base. Histological testing is recommended.
2. In post menopause or when there is any doubt about the diagnosis (for example
unclear information on AUB, limited conditions in ultrasound), a hysteroscopy
and complete curettage should be carried out in addition to the removal of the
cervical polyps to assess the uterus.

EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Benign uterine tumors 〉 Uterine �broids (uterine leiomyoma)
Last update: April 2024
Uterine ��broids ((uterine leiomyoma))
Author:: DDrr.. med.. Susanne Lanz
Oberärztin, Fachärztin für Gynäkologie und Geburtshilfe, Schwerpunkt operative Gynäkologie und
Geburtshilfe, speziell Myomsprechstunde, Dysplasie, minimalinvasive Chirurgie, Universitätsklinik für
Frauenheilkunde, Inselspital Bern
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr. med. Michel Mueller

Introduction
Uterine �broids are the most common benign tumors in women. Not all �broids
cause symptoms, but depending on their location, size and number, they can lead to
bleeding disorders, pain or pressure pain and have a negative impact on fertility.
Although they consist of benign tissue, they cause considerable morbidity worldwide
and continue to be the most common indication for hysterectomy in our part of the
world. Women of child-bearing age are most frequently affected. Fibroids can be
single or multiple, and they can measure just a few millimeters or �ll the whole
abdomen, weighing several kilograms.
They grow expansively. Large nodes or multiple �broids can cause dysfunction
by increasing pressure on their surroundings (neighboring organs or the uterus
itself), for example hydronephrosis due to ureter compression.

DDee��nition
Many common synonyms:
• Myoma = Uterine Fibroma = "Fibroid" = Uterine Leiomyoma
Fibroids are benign uterine tumors that grow from the smooth muscle of the
uterine wall (thus technically leiomyoma). The neoplasm(s) generally grow as
‘knots’, and are more rarely diffuse. Fibroids grow at different speeds. Several
�broids exposed to the same hormonal environment in the same uterus can
display very different patterns of growth. Connective tissue also proliferates to
different degrees. The connective tissue gives the rough texture of the �broid.
• Uterine corpus �broids are much more common than cervical �broids (only 5
%).
• Uterus myomatosus (myomatous uterus) expansion of the uterus due to one or
more �broid nodes, often growing to several times the original size.
FIGO staging
Fibroids are classi�ed according to their location within the uterine wall. This
nomenclature is designed to simplify the uniform description of �broids and is
helpful for treatment planning.
Illustration 1: Locations and directions of proliferation.
A – Submucosal leiomyoma (FIGO 0, 1, 2), B – Intramural leiomyoma (FIGO 3, 4), C – Subserosal leiomyoma
(FIGO 5, 6, 7), D – Hybrid leiomyoma
• FIGO 0: Pedunculated intracavitary
• FIGO 1: < 50 % intramural
• FIGO 2: ≥ 50 % intramural
• FIGO 3: Contacts endometrium, 100 % intramural

• FIGO 4: intramural
• FIGO 5: subserosal ≥ 50 % intramural
• FIGO 6: subserosal < 50 % intramural
• FIGO 7: subserosal pedunculated
• FIGO 8: others live e.g. cervical; parasitic
According to the location and direction of proliferation, a distinction is drawn
between:
Submucosal leiomyoma ((FIGO 00,, 11,, 22))
Growth begins directly under the endometrium, protruding to different extents
towards the uterine cavity. The covering endometrium is often hypotrophic. Quickly
lead to symptoms, in particular heavy menstrual bleeding. If these �broids become
pedunculated and grow into the cavity, they can be pushed towards the cervix and
vagina by uterine contractions (nascent �broids).
Intramural leiomyoma ((FIGO 33,, 44))
Fibroids most often grow within the uterine wall. As they grow, they can cause the
wall between the endometrium and the serosa to expand several times over.
Subserosal leiomyoma ((FIGO 55,, 66,, 77))
Proliferation close to the surface of the uterus. Subserosal �broids cause the serosa
to visibly protrude. If a �broid originates directly below the serosa, a stalk can form
as it increases in size (pedunculated subserosal �broid, FIGO type 7).
If the �broid is in a lateral position, it may protrude towards the broad ligament of the
uterus (intraligamentary parametrial �broid). Growth of the �broid can lead to
compression of the ureter, blood vessels or nerves.
Transmural ((hybrid)) leiomyoma ((FIGO 22--55))
Fibroids that take up the whole uterine wall and push the endometrium inwards and
the serosa outwards are classi�ed as type 2-5.
Special cases ((FIGO 88))

Speci�c �broids that do not �t the aforementioned categories, such as cervical or
parasitic leiomyomas, are classi�ed as FIGO 8. Parasitic �broids: this is the term
used to describe �broids that originate in small fragments of �broid tissue. Such
fragments can remain in the abdomen after morcellation of �broids in laproscopic
myomectomyUnder favorable conditions, these fragments can attach to tissue (e.g.
peritoneum) and grow into independent tumors without any contact to the uterus.
Illustration 2: MRI examples.
A – Multiple submucosal leiomyoma (FIGO 0, 1, 2) (sagittal section)
B – Transmural leiomyoma (FIGO 2-5) (coronal section)
C – Intracavity (FIGO 0) and large subserosal (FIGO 6) leiomyomas (sagittal section)
D – Multiple intramural (FIGO 3,4) and subserosal (FIGO 5,6) leiomyomas (sagittal section)
Figure 3: A – Hysteroscopy with submucosal �broid Type O, B – Hysteroscopy with submucosal �broid Type I

Epidemiology
• Approximately 80 % of �broids occur between 30 and 50 years of age. Fibroids
can be found in approximately 70 % of all women aged 40 or more, if targeted
microscopic (�broid up to 5 mm) investigations are carried out. However, only
15 to 25 % of these �broids are symptomatic.
• Fibroids are often multiple.
• They generally stagnate or improve after menopause (regression, calci�cation) -
unless postmenopausal hormone replacement therapy is used, for example.
Etiopathogenesis
The most important risk factor for uterine �broids is race: compared to light-skinned
women, African American women are 3x more affected, Hispanic women 2x more.
Other risk factors include older age (about 60 % African American women 35-49
years with �broids, versus 80 % > 50 years), hypertension, obesity, vitamin D
de�ciency, exposure to endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDC). The familial clustering
suggests a genetic predisposition (e.g. polymorphisms in the gene that codes for
COMT (catechol-O-methyltransferase)).
A great deal of scienti�c work is being invested in deciphering the complex
pathogenesis of �broids. This much is known so far:
A normal myometrial stem cell becomes a so-called TIC, a tumor initiating cell,
through DNA damage, DNA driver mutations and epigenetic changes under the
in�uence of in�ammatory factors (cytokines, interleukins), growth factors and
hormones, which then proliferates into abnormally differentiated myoma cells. An
altered genital microbiome is also discussed as a favoring factor. The growth of
�broids can be characterized by abnormal cell proliferation, excessive deposition of
extracellular matrix (collagen �bronectin), inhibited apoptosis and DNA instability.
EDC

“endocrine disrupting chemicals” are substances that occur in the environment, in
food or articles of daily use and that interfere with hormone biosynthesis,
metabolism or action. They are absorbed into the body by inhalation, ingestion or
transdermally. For example, parabens (found in sunscreen, lipstick, depilatory
creams and many other cosmetics), phthalates (�oor coverings, paints, �lms, cables,
nail varnish, etc.) or bisphenol A (toys, baby bottles, plastic tableware, etc.). EDCs are
metabolized in the body and excreted in the urine. By measuring the metabolites in
the urine, conclusions can be drawn about the exposure to the respective EDC.
EDCs can promote the transformation of a myoma stem cell into a TIC, but they can
also have a direct effect on the myoma cells and stimulate proliferation,
in�ammation and �brosis and inhibit apoptosis.
As various cosmetics that are used almost exclusively by dark-skinned women (skin
lighteners, “hair relaxers” for hair straightening) are particularly contaminated with
EDCs, they are also discussed as being partly responsible for the disproportionate
incidence of �broids in this population.

Clinical morphology
Histopathology shows the interior of �broid nodes as whorl-like. The outer muscle
layer of the �broid and the surrounding myometrium are often concentric, leading to
a pseudocapsule. When cut, �broids appear white. Secondary signs of degeneration
are necrosis, bleeding, �brosis, calci�cation, myxoid degeneration.

Clinical picture
Fibroids do not necessarily cause symptoms. Epidemiological studies show that only
approx. 25 % of �broids lead to symptoms that require treatment.
The type and extent of symptoms depend on the location (FIGO type) and size of the
�broid.
Bleeding disorders (e. g. excessively heavy bleeding with clots, metrorrhagia or
prolonged bleeding) are found in half of �broid patients - particularly those with
submucosal or transmural �broids. Signi�cant iron de�ciency anemia is possible!
Location and size dictate the symptoms of �broid. The main symptom is often a
bleeding disorder (such as heavy or prolonged menstrual bleeding,
metrorrhagia). In addition, approx. 30 % of �broid patients experience pain.
• This is usually localized stretching or pressure pain due to the tumor mass(es).
• Secondary dysmenorrhea (with metrorrhagia) is often observed with
submucosal �broids.
• Cramp-like pains can be caused by uterine contractions as a reaction to the
�broid as a “foreign body”.
• Acute peritoneal symptoms including acute abdomen occur with twisted
pedunculated subserosal �broids or with �broid necrosis.
Symptoms as a result of pressure on surroundings structures (such as the bladder,
ureter, intestine or blood vessels) are rare, but possible for various organ systems
(for example taking the form of renal congestion, constipation, constant urge to
urinate or urinary retention, impaired venous �ow from legs with potential thrombus
formation).
Fibroids and fertility
Several studies have shown that submucosal �broids reduce fertility and that
removing these submucosal �broids under appropriate care improves fertility.

No impact on fertility has been clearly shown for either intramural or subserosal
�broids. However, it should be noted that there are only a few prospective
randomized studies that have investigated this issue. The studies available are
mostly retrospective and have a very different study design. Intramural �broids larger
than 4 cm and causing uterine cavity distortion may impact fertility. Many authors
therefore think that intramural �broids > 4 cm should be removed before fertility
treatments.
If intracavity (particularly pedunculated) �broids necrotize, this can lead to
in�ammation, bleeding and malodorous vaginal discharge.
Fibroids very rarely develop into leiomyosarcomas. Various genetic studies have
shown that �broids and leiomyosarcomas have different origins and
leiomyosarcomas.

Diagnosis
Diagnosis is based on history, physical examination and vaginal ultrasound. This
triad of methods is su�cient in 90 % of cases for differentiating uterine �broids
from adnexal masses of unknown origin.
Transvaginal ultrasound is the imaging method of choice. This can generally
diagnose, delimit and measure �broids reliably, as well as providing good follow-up
control of the size. The typical ultrasound image shows a hypoechoic round or oval
mass, potentially with a whorled interior and often acoustic attenuation or
calci�cations. Vascularization is typically predominantly found as a net-like structure
on the surface of the �broid. It is not possible to fully exclude sarcoma on
ultrasound.
If a submucosal �broid is suspected, a suitable additional examination, which is also
available in practice, is sonohysterography (the uterine cavity is �lled with sterile
saline via a thin catheter inserted across the cervix, so that the distended cavity is
anechoic and visibility of the extent of �broid protrusion towards the cavity is
optimal). 3D ultrasound also allows a very good assessment of submucosal �broids
Hysteroscopy is a more costly but often worthwhile option. This offers a clear
differentiation between submucosal �broids and uterine polyps, for example, and is
often combined directly with resection or curettage in the clari�cation procedure for
bleeding of unknown origin or cycle length disorders.
In exceptional cases, where differential diagnosis is challenging, other imaging
methods may be used, including computer tomography (CT), intravenous pyelogram
(IVP) or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI).
With multiple �broids, a pre-operative MRI is often logical and helpful for precise
planning of treatment. For example, for deciding whether a myomectomy can be
carried out laparoscopically or whether a laparotomy is required, or which �broids
can be treated with radiofrequency ablation.
If ultrasound or MRI are unable to clarify whether a �nding is malignant, a pre-
operative PET-CT should be carried out, as sensitivity for sarcoma diagnosis is much
higher with PET than MRI.

Figure 5: MRI with evidence of subserosal and intramural �broids.
A diagnostic laparoscopy is sometimes carried out - as any uncertainty as to
whether there is also an adnexal mass must be clari�ed!

Differential diagnosis
DD of nodular masses in lower abdomen:
• Adnexal mass (neoplasia, endometriosis, tubo-ovarian abscess)
• Adenomyosis
• Severely retroverted/retro�exed uterus
• Uterine malformation (e.g. double uterus)
• Hydrometra, hematometra, pyometra
• Pregnancy
• Intestinal tumors (diverticulitis, peritoneal abscess, carcinoma)
• Retroperitoneal tumors (e.g. spreading from lymphatic system)
• Pelvic kidney (rare)
• Sarcoma (rare)

Progression
Natural progression, potential complications and secondary changes relating to
�broids.
When advising patients on treatment and the urgency of starting treatment, it is
useful and important to be able to give a reasonably reliable prognosis on the natural
growth pattern of the disease. In view of the undisputed hormonal connection, it is
expected that �broids will start to grow after puberty, continue this growth
throughout the reproductive years, then shrink after menopause. However, this is not
the case, as consistently shown by studies of �broid growth using sequential
imaging (MRI or ultrasound). Fibroids can show various non-linear and largely
unpredictable growth patterns, from spontaneous regression with shrinkage to slow
or very fast growth. On average, they grow approx. 9 % every 6 months, but this
ranges from -89 % to +138 %. Submucosal �broids appear to grow more slowly than
intramural and subserosal �broids, and on average �broids in white women aged
over 45 years only grow slowly. Other constant parameters have not been identi�ed.
Over time, �brosis, hyalinosis and calci�cation can occur, as well as central necrosis
and myxoid degeneration in some cases with rapid growth. With pedunculated
�broids, a twisted pedicle can lead to acute hemorrhagic necrosis. Fibroids can also
regress fully and spontaneously.

Treatment
The indication for treatment and prioritization of the various options is based on the
symptoms (bleeding disorders? pain/pressure pain? infertility? etc.), the number, size
and location of �broids, current lifestyle, patient’s wishes (e. g. family planning?
secondary diagnosis? surgical pre-procedure? age? avoidance of hormones?
avoidance of general anesthetic?) and the treatment objectives. Treatment options
range from medication to conservative surgery and interventional radiology
procedures to radical surgery in the form of hysterectomy.
• Fibroids with clear clinical symptoms should be removed.
• With asymptomatic uterus myomatosus, regular clinical and vaginal ultrasound
check-ups (every 6 to 12 months) may be considered.
• It is often stated that �broids with “rapid growth” or “clear size progression”
should be removed, but there are no uniform de�nitions of the speed of this
growth
Fibroid treatment: A general rule applies treatment is only indicated if symptoms
requiring treatment exist (approx. 1/4 cases). Sterility and status post
miscarriage/premature birth* are also symptoms.
* In individual cases causality may be di�cult to establish. Caution should therefore
be applied with regard to an indication for surgery in the context of sterility/infertility.
Submucosal and large intramural �broids can be treated with surgery if necessary.
Drug treatment
Symptomatic,, non--hormonal treatment
Heavy menstrual bleeding can be treated with tranexamic acid, perimenstrual
administration of NSAIDs, or iron supplements.
Hormonal treatment

Combined contraception administered orally, vaginally or transdermally in the
traditional regimen (3 weeks medication, 1 week break with withdrawal bleeding), but
above all in a long-term regimen can reduce blood loss.
Progesterone preparations can also reduce bleeding or cause amenorrhea due to
their atrophying effect on the endometrium. Slightly higher expulsion rates should be
expected with levonorgestrel IUDs (Mirena®) due to uterine deformation caused by
�broids. Parenterally administered long-term progesterones (medroxyprogesterone
acetate = Depo Provera®) have a similar effect but more frequently lead to
inconvenient metrorrhagia.
These drug-based treatment options regulate the bleeding cycle. They are
therefore predominantly used in patients with heavy menstrual bleeding, for
example when it is a question of bridging the gap until menopause without
carrying out a major procedure.
Causal hormonal treatment
GnRH analogues (11,25 mg leuprorelin or 10,8 mg goserelin (Zoladex LA) injected
every 12 weeks) reduce gonadotropins (LH and FSH) and ovarian hormones via
pituitary down-regulation, thus leading to amenorrhea and reducing �broid mass by
approx. 40 %. It can be sensible to administer GnRH analogues for 3 to 6 months
before surgery in order to achieve better baseline hemoglobin and facilitate a less
invasive surgery with less blood loss due to the reduction in �broid size. They are not
suitable as long-term treatment due to the signi�cant adverse effects caused by
hypoestrogenism (in particular osteoporosis and menopausal symptoms).
GnRH antagonists
Since 02/2023, the peroral GnRH antagonist Relugolix- CT has been approved in
Switzerland under the name Ryeqo® by swissmedic for the treatment of
hypermenorrhea in premenopausal women with �broids.
Composition of Ryeqo®: 40 mg relugolix, 1 mg estradiol, 0,5 mg norethisterone
acetate

The GnRH receptors in the anterior pituitary gland are blocked by Relugolix, FSH and
LH are suppressed and the production of estrogen and progesterone in the ovary is
suppressed. Thanks to integrated add back therapy, estradiol levels are kept stable
at a level corresponding to the early follicular phase, which alleviates the myoma-
associated symptoms but preserves bone density and alleviates the vasomotor side
effects of hypoestrogenemia.
The studies on e�cacy and tolerability currently cover a treatment period of 2 years.
At the end of this period, the primary endpoint of the study, namely the response rate,
de�ned as blood loss < 80 ml/mt and reduction in monthly blood loss > 50 %
compared to baseline, was achieved in around 70 % of the candidates. 58,5 % of the
test subjects had amenorrhea after 2 years of therapy.
Relugolix CT was also signi�cantly superior to placebo in most secondary endpoints
(pain, amenorrhea, stress due to bleeding and lower abdominal discomfort, uterine
volume, anemia, monthly blood loss).
Compared to GnRH analogs, the GnRH antagonist (without add back) achieved an
equivalent improvement in hypermenorrhea more quickly by eliminating the initial
�are up effect and the cycle returned to normal more quickly after the end of therapy.
(The selective progesterone receptor modulator (SPRM) ulipristal acetate (Esmya®)
was temporarily authorized in Switzerland following enthusiastically received study
results concerning bleeding pro�le and volume reduction versus placebo and GnRH
analogues, but has since been removed from the market for the indication of
myomas due to rare but very serious hepatic adverse effects.)
Supportive therapies
The role of vitamin D

Vitamin D is synthesized in the skin from sunlight. After hydroxylation in the liver and
kidneys, it is transported as 1,25 dihydroxy vitamin D to the target organs, where it
docks onto the vitamin D receptor (VDR). Age, climate, sun protection and clothing
in�uence the amount produced. The darker the skin pigmentation, the lower the
vitamin D production with the same sunlight exposure. Around 80 % of dark-skinned
women have a vitamin D de�ciency according to standard values, compared to
around 20 % of fair-skinned women. Studies consistently show that vitamin D levels
in the serum of myoma patients are signi�cantly lower than in myoma-free control
groups, suggesting a link between vitamin D levels and myoma development and
growth.
First small intervention studies show encouraging results: Vitamin D administration
can reduce �broid growth and possibly also the risk of recurrent �broids after
myomectomy. Compared to other medical treatments for �broids, vitamin D is a low-
cost option for women trying to conceive and has no limit on duration of use.
Diet
Food products can in�uence hormone-dependent diseases - including �broids.
However, clear evidence is di�cult to generate: the spontaneous growth behavior of
�broids is very variable and different foods probably in�uence each other. There is
relative agreement in the study results that fruit (especially citrus fruits) and
vegetables (especially green vegetables) reduce the risk of �broids, whereas meat
and fat increase the risk. The results on dairy products, alcohol, wholegrain products
and soy are contradictory.
Green tea plays a special role between nutrition and phytotherapy. Green tea extract,
in particular epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), appears to slow down the growth of
�broids. In vitro, antiangiogenic, antiproliferative, anti�brotic and proapoptotic
activities of EGCG on leiomyoma cells have been observed. First studies with
myoma patients show promising results, especially when EGCG was combined with
vitamin D.
Uterus--preserving invasive methods

Risk of uterine rupture following myomectomy: The risk of uterine rupture during
pregnancy does not depend on the chosen access but on the technique used. In the
literature, the danger of uterine rupture during subsequent pregnancy following
myomectomy is lower than after a Cesarean section, regardless of whether the
surgery was laparoscopic or open. In a systematic review, the risk of rupture during
birth was 0,47 %, and 1,52 % during pregnancy before birth. It is important that the
cavity resulting from �broid removal is closed correctly, so that there is no post-
operative hematoma, and the layers can be adapted correctly. Extensive coagulation
should be avoided as much as possible. Intraoperative opening of the uterine cavity
increases the risk of intrauterine synechiae and should therefore be avoided
wherever possible. However, the risk of uterine rupture is not increased by accidental
opening of the uterine cavity, despite persistently circulated statements to the
contrary. Various studies have shown that scar formation is complete after three
months. Pregnancy can therefore be attempted after this time. Birth after
myomectomy does not have to be via Cesarean section. The recommended type of
birth should be determined by the surgeon, based on the length, number and location
of myomectomy scars.
Fibroid enucleation (generally laparoscopy or hysteroscopy): Treatment option for
women who may wish to have (more) children or who wish to keep their uterus (e. g.
for patients for whom it is important to keep the uterus for personal reasons).
Fibroid enucleation techniques:
• Submucosal �broid: Hysteroscopic removal/electroresection with sling. For
large �broids and anemia, preparative treatment with GnRH analogues can take
place. Nascent pedunculated submucosal �broids can - if the connection is
clear - be gripped with forceps at the root of the peduncle and then twisted until
removal.
• Subserosal �broid: Mostly removed successfully via laparoscopy. A special
cutting instrument (cutting tube/morcellator) is used to remove large �broids
from the abdomen. The �broid is broken up inside a plastic bag inserted into
the abdomen to ensure that no small fragments remain in the body. Any such
fragments could, under certain conditions, develop into parasitic �broids. If
there is any suspicion of sarcoma, intra-abdominal fragmentation of the �broid
must be avoided and open surgery must be carried out if necessary.
• Intramural �broid: Laparoscopic removal with reliable, usually multilayer, careful
suturing of the myometrium. Potential pretreatment with GnRH analogues in
case of anemia. Potential laparotomy for multiple or large �broids.

Radiofrequency ablation: For women who wish to keep their uterus and desire
minimally invasive �broid treatment, transcervical US-guided radiofrequency ablation
(Sonata®) is a possibility. The �broid or �broids are �xed in place with a metal trocar
under ultrasound guidance, then exposed to radiofrequency energy via fan-shaped
electrodes placed in the �broid and adapted to its size. The �broids in the prede�ned
area are then heated to 105 degrees Celsius. The resulting coagulation necrosis
causes the �broids to shrink by up to 70 % of their volume over the following months.
If the endometrium is within the ablation area, there is also a rapid improvement in
heavy menstrual bleeding. This technique is particularly suited for larger submucosal
�broids type 1 and 2, and transmural �broids type 2-5. Intramural �broids type 3, 4
and 5 are also accessible. Fibroids with subserosal sections cannot be fully ablated,
as a temperature safety zone must be established around neighboring organs to
protect them from heat damage.
Uterine �broid embolization
• A potential treatment option for individual �broids.
• A femoral catheter is inserted into the uterine artery under x-ray guidance. It is
then placed precisely at each arterial end branch that supplies a �broid.
Insoluble particles are then introduced into this arterial branch via the catheter,
leading to embolization. The �broid shrinks over the following months.
• Risks/complications: General risks associated with catheter procedures; post-
embolization syndrome (predominantly nausea, vomiting, pain); accidental
infection (potentially after a long latency period - rarely with uterine suppuration
--> in some circumstances “still” requiring hysterectomy --> uterine �broid
embolization is therefore not indicated for women who wish to have children!).
Figure 6: Laparascopic myomectomy - technique
A – Temporary ligature of uterine arteries.
B – Careful, layer-by-layer enucleation of the �broid.
C – Careful, layer-by-layer enucleation of the �broid. Very little bleeding due to temporary ligature of uterine
arteries.
D – Two-layer suturing of wound cavity, ensuring that layers are adapted correctly.
Hysterectomy

If the patient does not want (more) children and agrees to removal of the uterus, a
hysterectomy can be proposed as a de�nitive solution. It is associated with a much
lower morbidity rate than laparoscopic myomectomy, and is usually technically
simpler to carry out. Hysterectomy can be carried out vaginally, laparoscopically or
via laparotomy. The advantages of vaginal/laparoscopic hysterectomy are shorter
hospitalization times and quicker recovery. Vaginal surgery is possible if the uterus
can be su�ciently descended.

Fibroids during pregnancy
Although uterine �broids are very common tumors, and pregnancy with uterus
myomatosus is therefore a common phenomenon, statements on the growth
patterns of �broids during pregnancy are not always consistent. Causes for growth
and shrinkage are often speculative and not well substantiated.
There is broad consensus concerning the observation that many uterine �broids gain
signi�cant volume in the �rst trimester, with this growth slowing in the second half of
pregnancy. Occasionally, central �broid necrosis can occur during pregnancy, leading
to severe abdominal pain in those affected.
Other �broid-related pregnancy complications include early miscarriage, anomalies
of presentation, growth restriction, premature placental abruption, preterm
contractions, PROM, postpartum uterine atony with postpartum hemorrhage, and
postpartum sepsis. Depending on their size and location, �broids can occasionally
present an obstacle to vaginal delivery.
Fibroid surgery during pregnancy is associated with increased blood loss and a risk
of miscarriage/preterm birth. Myomectomy during Cesarean section is also not
recommended due to the high risk of hemorrhage.

Uterine artery embolization
Author:: Prof.. DDrr.. med.. Christoph JJ.. Zech
Leitung intervent. Radiologie, Angiogra�e, Qualität und Sicherheit, Mitglied Tumorzentrum,
Universitätsspital Basel
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Dr. med. Thomas Hess, Prof. Dr. med. Christoph Binkert

Introduction
Uterine artery embolization (UAE) is a symptom-oriented, organ-conserving
procedure for treating benign uterine �broids.
For this, a deliberate obliteration of the terminal arteries is carried out in the uterus
via the uterine artery, thus interrupting the blood supply to the vessels around the
�broid. For this purpose, during UAE, gelatin or plastic particles the size of a grain of
sand (500 to 900 µm) are introduced via catheter through the femoral arteries into
the relevant uterine arteries. Reducing or interrupting the blood supply leads to all
�broids in the uterus being treated at the same time during the procedure and
shrinking within a few months. The extent of �broid shrinkage does not correlate
with the extent of symptom improvement. Most �broids shrink by some 50 to 70 % in
volume.

History
The method of particle embolization for acute bleeding in the uterus (tumor
hemorrhage, unstoppable postpartum or post Cesarean bleeding, or bleeding from
arteriovenous malformation in the uterus) has been carried out since the 1970s. In
1989, transarterial embolization of symptomatic uterine �broids (�broid
embolization) was �rst carried out by Merland, an interventional neuroradiologist.
Since it was �rst described in 1995 by Ravina, the angiographic treatment of
symptomatic uterine �broids using embolization under angiographic control has
experienced rapid spread across the world. After �rst taking place mainly in France,
Great Britain and the USA (referred to as uterine �broid embolization, UFE), this
minimally invasive angiographic procedure has been used in Germany since 2000
and is approved in Switzerland as an essential treatment subject to conditions since
2012.

Diagnosis

Before non-surgical, minimally invasive uterine �broid embolization, pregnancy and
in�ammatory/(pre)malignant diseases of the uterus must be ruled out with an
annual gynecological check-up including normal PAP smear and a transvaginal or
additional abdominal ultrasound. For further diagnosis of the �broid and its blood
supply, an MRI with MR-angiography is useful for more precise assessment,
particularly of circulation. A �broid that is already infarcted should not be treated. In
addition, other causes such as diffuse adenomyosis, endometriosis and ovarian
tumors can be diagnosed. Before and after the intervention, it can make sense to
determine/monitor ovarian function via blood E2 and FSH levels. Fibroid-induced
foreign body sensations and symptoms such as pain, cramps, dyspareunia, dysuria,
dys- and algomenorrhea, and bleeding disorders such as hypermenorrhea and
menometrorrhagia generally improve or completely disappear thanks to the
intervention. After the intervention, embolized �broids shrink over a duration of
several months. Submucosal �broids can occasionally be expelled after varying
intervals. This is accompanied by contraction-like pains and bleeding.
The procedure should be carried out under interdisciplinary collaboration between an
interventional radiologist (embolization) and a gynecologist (patient preparation and
clinical follow-up).
Indications include symptomatic �broids extending to roughly the level of the navel
and the patient’s desire and consent to carry out the intervention after an information
session. Contraindications include asymptomatic �broids, manifest infection of the
urinary or genital organs, endometriosis lesions worthy of clari�cation,
(pre)malignant uterine changes, iodine allergy (contrast) and hyperthyroid state.
Relative contraindications are subserosal or submucosal pedunculated �broids and
large �broids extending beyond the level of the navel, as e�cacy can be limited or
the procedure can be complicated in these cases.
UAE is not a treatment method for fertility in patients with �broids. However, before a
hysterectomy (not indicated if children are desired) is considered for a patient who
has not yet completed her family, the possibility of UAE, ideally with a temporary
embolizing agents (gelatin particles), should be veri�ed. For patients who wish to
have children, the role of UAE as a treatment option has not yet been clari�ed. UAE is
not a contraceptive measure and subsequent pregnancies carry an elevated level of
medical risk.

Summary ooff indications and
contraindications
An indication for �broid embolization should only be decided upon in close
collaboration between a gynecologist and an interventional radiologist, in full
knowledge of the range of treatment options available.
The literature currently gives the following indications:
Established indications
• Symptomatic �broids in submucosal, intramural and subserosal locations with
broad contact with the uterine wall and an individual �broid diameter of up to
approx. 12 cm with an overall uterine length of up to 22 cm, whereby the fundus
should not extend beyond the level of the navel
• Recurrent �broids after organ-conserving surgery (myomectomy)
• Contraindications for surgery
Relative contraindications
Technical
• Previous GnRH analog treatment in previous three months (increased risk of
insu�cient embolization of uterine artery), although this therapy should no
longer be carried out for uterine �broids.
According to current knowledge, the administration of ulipristal acetate has no
signi�cance for the indication and performance of UAE and does not in�uence the
results of uterine artery embolisation.
Anatomical
• Isolates submucosal �broids, type 0 and 1 as per ESGE, for which hysteroscopic
ablation is available
• Isolated subserosal pedunculated �broids
• (Additional) treatment of �broid(s) via ovarian artery; the bene�ts and risks
associated with cumulative reversible embolization of the relevant ovarian
artery must be weighed up
Other
• Documented allergic reaction to contrast containing iodine

• Postmenopausal patients
• Allergy to local anesthetic
• Latent hyperthyroidism
• Kidney failure (creatinine value > 1.5)
• IUD in situ
• Wish to have more children
• Immunosuppression
Absolute contraindications
Clinical
• Suspected malignancy (the overall risk of an unrecognised uterine malignancy -
including uterine sarcomas - is stated in the current literature to be between
0,09 and 0,18 % in patients who have undergone surgery for �broids)
• Pregnancy
• Acute genital infections
• Manifest hyperthyroidism or �orid thyroiditis with hyperthyroidism without
thyrostatic treatment, either scheduled or started iodine radiotherapy, if contrast
containing iodine is used

Results
In the medium term, UAE leads to a clear improvement in �broid symptoms
(intensi�ed and/or prolonged periods, pressure pains and pain on urination) in
approx. 80 to 85 % of embolized patients. Patient satisfaction with the treatment
result is very high, as is readiness to recommend the procedure. Serious
complications or those requiring treatment are rare.
Data from prospective randomized studies (EBM level I and II), case studies and data
from registries have con�rmed UAE as an established treatment procedure. The
standardized procedure and technical descriptions with guidelines for safety and
quality aspects have been published by Societies for Interventional Radiology.
Approval for UAE as a medical service covered by health insurance has been granted
in most European countries.

Technical procedure
Fibroid embolization should only be carried out by trained, experienced interventional
radiologists. Catheter-based embolization of the uterine arteries is carried out in an
x-ray room suitable for interventional procedures (angiography laboratory).
The femoral artery is punctured (usually unilateral access in the right groin) and a
catheter is inserted into each of the two uterine arteries in turn, under x-ray control.
In order to see the vessels angiographically, a contrast agent is injected via the
catheter. The blood vessels supplying the �broid are only embolized once the
catheter is unmistakably located in the relevant vessel. The small gelatin or plastic
particles used in most cases feed into the terminal artery of the �broid and stay
there permanently. Embolization is continued until the almost complete blockage of
the blood �ow to the �broid. For good clinical success, both uterine arteries need to
be embolized. The intervention generally lasts 60 to 90 minutes, depending on the
vascular anatomy and any di�culties placing the catheter.
Figure 1: Technical procedure of �broid embolization.
A – A catheter is inserted into the leg artery via the groin and moved through to the uterine artery.
B – Embolisation of the vascular system supplying the �broid
Small particles are delivered into the uterine article and stop the blood supply to the �broid.

How embolization works
The speci�c objective is to occlude the peri�broid vascular plexus and thus
de�nitively stop the supply of blood to the �broid. The transarterial embolization of
uterine leiomyoma carried out in such a way leads to the hyaline degeneration and
shrinkage of the �broids, and the regression of symptoms. Pathophysiologically, this
is attributable to the different vascular architecture of leiomyoma and normal
myometrium/endometrium: �broids are supplied via a large-caliber external vascular
plexus with centripetal parenchymal vessels, while the myometrium has many
intraparenchymal collaterals.
Normal uterine tissue around the �broid therefore reacts differently to ischemia and
the targeted occlusion of the plexus vessels with particles (embolizing agent) leads
to a controlled/selective embolization of the �broid while maintaining circulation to
the surrounding uterine tissue.

Anesthesia
Adequate, e�cient analgesia is required to treat intensive ischemic pain brought
about peracutely by embolization. Suitable analgesia should be selected according
to experience with the various procedures and the logistical requirements of the
institution in question.
In principle, �ve procedures can be used:
1. Opiate analgesia with a PCA pump (patient-controlled analgesia).
2. Regional anesthesia with an epidural catheter.
3. Sedation and intravenous opiate anesthesia without PCA pump.
4. Spinal anesthesia.
5. Presacral block (under radiological control).
In most cases, methods 1 and 2 are used in collaboration with anesthesiologists.
Without anesthesia, a presacral block is preferable.
Suitable analgesia and anti-in�ammatory treatment are key to avoiding the
development of ischemic pain and post-embolization syndrome and ensuring that
the patient does not suffer after the intervention. Post-embolization syndrome is
characterized by unresponsive pain associated with transient fever and malaise.

Summary and overview ooff treatment
pathway
Fibroid embolization takes place according to the Winterthur Cantonal Hospital
treatment pathway as follows:

Christoph Binkert, Chief Physician, Institute for Radiology, Winterthur Cantonal Hospital).
