Side effects
The following have been described as relevant and typical side effects of UAE:
• Amenorrhea (3,9–4,3 %)
• Pain (3,6 %)
• Vaginal discharge (3,4 %)
• Angiography-related complications (e.g. inguinal hematoma) (2,9 %)
• Vaginal leakage or discharge of �broid tissue (1,5–4,7 %)
• Hot �ushes and/or ovarian failure (1,4 %)
• Endometritis/myometritis (1,4 %)
• Post-embolization syndrome (0,2–2,9 %)
• Deep vein thrombosis/pulmonary embolism (0,2 %)
• Misembolization (< 0,1 %)
Uterine discharge can be normal in the �rst weeks after UAE. If discharge is
abnormal, infection diagnostics and treatment should be carried out. Menorrhagia,
cramp-like lower abdominal pain and leakage of tissue can occur, in particular with
submucosal �broids. Depending on the clinical symptoms and imaging, a
hysteroscopic �broid resection or vaginal �broid ablation, as for a �broid in statu
nascendi, can be indicated. If there is an increased risk of thrombosis, prophylactic
thrombosis prophylaxis makes sense. Antibiotic prophylaxis can also be indicated if
there is an increased risk of infection.

Aftercare and check--ups
After embolization, the catheter is removed and the puncture site is closed with a
compression bandage, which is left in place for four hours. Generally, patients stay in
hospital for one night after this 60 to 90 minute intervention and require suitable, i.e.
not restrained, analgesia for the �rst 30 to 36 hours. A gynecological check-up within
the �rst 6 weeks is recommended, with check-ups by the interventional radiologist
after 3 months and 2 years.

Economic assessment
The cost of �broid embolization is comparable to that of �broid enucleation.
Abdominal hysterectomy is by far the most expensive intervention. The frequency of
conservative (organ-conserving) operations with laparoscopic myomectomy/
enucleation or laparoscopic and hysteroscopic removal is only limited to 6 to 10 % of
cases and embolization is generally preferred.
An advantage of UAE is that smaller �broids can be treated as compared to the
treatment of enucleation. The rate of recurrence with �broid enucleation is therefore
higher than with UAE. The economic comparison with abdominal hysterectomy
should therefore be in the foreground. According to four clinical studies in the USA
and Canada between 2001 and 2004, the direct costs of �broid embolization are
lower than both hysterectomy and myomectomy, particularly due to lower
hospitalization costs. The cost assessment in Switzerland is comparable with the
information from the literature. The cost is also dependent on the price and amount
of embolization particles and diagnostics, in particular MR-angiography. For indirect
costs, UAE quickly fares better due to the shorter hospitalization, shorter time off
work and rapidly normal quality of life. In Europe, also a comparison of UAE and
laparoscopic hysterectomy is relevant. Here, the costs of both methods are likely
similar, although there is no direct comparable data.

Cervical cancer
Author:: PPDD DDrr.. med.. Christian Kurzeder
Gynäkologische Onkologie, Chefarzt Senologie, Schwerpunkt Gynäkologische Onkologie, Stv. Chefarzt,
Universitätsspital Basel
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr. med. Mathias Fehr, Prof. Dr. med. Patrick Petignat

Introduction
Unlike ovarian cancer, for example, cervical cancer and its precancerous stage,
cervical dysplasia, are "accessible" for easy examination due to their location and
can thus potentially be identi�ed earlier.
With a perfect cervical cancer screening program, there would be no more invasive
cervical cancer, as SIL is a precancerous stage that can be detected using cervical
cytology and colposcopy.
In a historical cohort study, 31 % of patients with HSIL diagnosed using punch biopsy
alone were found to have developed invasive cervical cancer within 30 years. If the
HSIL had been treated, only 0,7 % would have developed invasive cancer in the
following 30 years [P06]. [P06]
According to the current 2014 WHO classi�cation, the term CIN I–III (cervical
intraepithelial neoplasia) has been replaced by the term Squamous Intraepithelial
Lesion (SIL) [B08]:
• Low-grade Squamous Intraepithelial Lesion (LSIL) replaces CIN I
• High-grade Squamous Intraepithelial Lesion (HSIL) replaces CIN II and CIN III
[B08]

DDee��nitions
Invasive cervical cancer is either:
• Squamous cell: cervical HSIL (precancerous) --> invasive squamous cell
carcinoma (80 %)
• Glandular epithelium: Adenocarcinoma in situ (precancerous) --> invasive
adenocarcinoma (20 %)

Epidemiology
Cervical cancer is the most common female genital cancer worldwide.
The global age-standardized incidence rate is estimated at 14 per 100'000 per year,
ranging from 2 (Palestine) to 75,9 (Malawi).
Its incidence is kept low in countries with high socioeconomic status thanks to early
detection and treatment of precancerous stages.
In Germany, the age-standardized incidence according to the European population is
8,7 per 100'000 women per year (versus 16,5 for endometrial cancer and 119,6 for
breast cancer) [P01].
In Switzerland, the incidence is above 4 and mortality is 2,5 to 1,5/100'000 per year.
[P01]
Infection with oncogenic human papillomavirus (HPV) is an essential condition for
the development of cervical dysplasia and thus cervical cancer (=> G10A).
• 99,7 % of cervical cancer (and 100 % of HSIL and adenocarcinoma in situ) is
associated with persistent oncogenic HPV infection.
• HPV type 16 or 18 can be found in 71 % of all cervical cancer.
• A changing "HPV inoculum" as the result of multiple sexual partners correlates
closely with the later occurrence of cervical cancer.
Other, non-causal, tumor promoters include:
• Smoking
• Vitamin de�ciency (carotenoids, vitamin C, folic acid)
• Immunode�ciency (e. g. HIV, history of organ transplantation)
• Long-term use of ovulation inhibitors
Cervical cancer must be understood as a possible consequence of persistent
infection with high-risk types of HPV:
• The most oncologically dangerous (as most common) high-risk HPVs are 16
and 18
• Rarer high-risk HPVs: 31, 33, 35, 39, 45, 51, 52, 55, 56, 58, 59, 66, 68, 70, 73, 82

The malignant degeneration of HPV-infected epithelial cells is associated with
overexpression of the viral oncogenes E6 and E7. These inactivate tumor suppressor
genes.

Early detection and HPV vaccination
--> see G11A

Clinical morphology
Histological types:
• Squamous cell carcinoma: Adenocarcinoma = 80 : 20
Neuroendocrine, clear cell and serous papillary cervical cancer are very rare
histological types with poor prognosis.
Neuroendocrine, clear cell and serous papillary cervical cancer are very rare
histological types with poor prognosis.
This area of the cervix is especially prone to oncogenic transformation, as human
papillomaviruses can infect the proliferating cells of the basal epithelial layer here.
Spread, metastasis:
• Direct spread is often from the cervix to the parametrium, descending into the
vagina. The bladder and rectum can also be invaded.
• Lymphogenic metastasis in various directions is typical (in the parametrium, the
fossa obturatoria, along the internal/external iliac arteries and �nally (pre)sacral
and along the common iliac artery and paraaortic).
• Hematogenic metastasis is found in less than 5 % of patients.
With regard to direct spread, a distinction is drawn between
• the ventral parametrium (vesicouterine ligament)
• the lateral parametrium (paracervical tissue of the cardinal ligament)
• the dorsal parametrium (uterosacral ligament)

Clinical picture
• Most precancerous and early stages have few or no symptoms.
• Premenopause: metrorrhagia, contact bleeding (typically after intercourse).
• Premenopause and postmenopause: vaginal discharge (turbid to bloody,
malodorous) and/or contact bleeding.
• Postmenopause: postmenopausal bleeding.
• If tumor growth is advanced, there may be pain in the pelvis, sacrum and lumbar
region. Leg lymphedema is also a late symptom.
• Note regarding late stages: (i) hydronephrosis and subsequent uremia, (ii)
arrosion bleeding, (iii) �stula.

Prognosis ((factors))
Tumor stage is the most important prognosis factor in cervical cancer.
Thus, tumor spread (local invasion of parametrium, vagina, bladder, rectum), lymph
node status (metastasized or not) and/or distant metastasis (organ metastasis) play
a decisive role.
For tumors limited to the uterus (FIGO I), other characteristics are also important for
the prognosis resp. treatment procedure:
• Depth of invasion (invasion of cervical stroma)
• Clinical tumor diameter (or tumor volume)
• Lymphatic invasion
• Blood vessel invasion
The presence of residual tumor after surgery (positive resection margins) is also
important for prognosis. Incomplete resection has a negative impact.
Table 1: Stage-dependent 5-year survival rate (regardless of treatment type!)
FIGO stage 55--year survival
0 100 %
I approx. 95 %
II approx. 70 %
III approx. 55 %
IV approx. 20 %
Across all stages about 2/3 of the patients will be cured.

Diagnosis
Diagnosis is determined histologically (tissue biopsy)! Clinical and cytological
suspicion, together with one or more of the following tests, help guide the
practitioner:
• Speculum: The tumorous process can be identi�ed with the naked eye at a
relatively early stage if it is located on the ectocervix.
• Colposcopy of the ectocervix and vagina: Precancerous growths can be
identi�ed with a microscope (after application of acetic acid and if visible), and
in general the location and spread of suspected/manifest �ndings.
• If changes are seen, punch biopsies of the suspicious sites must be taken. For
ectocervical lesions visible macroscopically, a biopsy should take place for
con�rmation of the diagnosis, rather than a conization.
• Endocervical �ndings can only be identi�ed with cervical curettage (outpatient,
without general anesthetic) or conization.
The tumor spread is then determined (staging).
Locoregional spread:
• Clinical examination to assess local spread: microscopic vs. macroscopic
expansion con�ned to the ectocervix, in�ltration of vaginal walls.
• Examination under general anesthetic with bimanual rectovaginal examination:
the best preoperative method for determining parametrial involvement: with
experienced practitioners, this examination is more reliable than CT or MRI.
• Cystoscopy and rectoscoscopy (often carried out at the same time as
parametrial examination under general anesthetic, to rule out in�ltration of the
bladder or rectum).
• MRI and expert transvaginal ultrasound (tumor size and additional parametrial
examination, ureteral congestion).
Lymph node metastasis, organ metastasis: additional examinations then complete
the staging information:
• PET-CT (lymph node and organ metastasis, "additional" information on tumor
spread into neighboring organs).
• Surgical lymph node staging (lymphadenectomy) remains the best method for
ruling out lymph node metastasis.
• Depending on the available diagnostic methods: chest x-ray (to rule out lung

metastasis); iv urography (ureteral congestion)
In a meta-analysis, PET-CT showed the highest success rate with regard to lymph
node metastasis, although the false-negative rate is still 6 to 15 %, with a positive
predictive value of 86 to 100 % [P09]. [P09]
Molecular biological evidence of HPV infection?
• is not indicated if HSIL or carcinoma has already been diagnosed. (HPV will be
positive anyway, "what type it was" is no longer relevant).
Staging

Until 2018, cervical cancer staging was carried out clinically taking into account the
following examinations: gynecological examination with colposcopy and biopsy
sampling, examination under general anesthetic (with bimanual rectovaginal
examination to assess parametrial in�ltration), cystoscopy and colonoscopy, as well
as renal ultrasound (hydronephrosis) and chest x-ray. The FIGO stage was then
established and remained unchanged. Treatment was naturally adapted to any
additional information that became available concerning the disease (e.g. lymph
node involvement or organ metastasis), but the FIGO stage remained the same. It
was a "clinical" staging.
Cervical cancer is widespread in developing countries where medical diagnosis is
more limited. This is why staging was carried out on a clinical basis only, in order to
initiate further treatment.
However, this led to major discrepancies between classi�cation systems (TNM,
FIGO). Diagnostic methods such as MRI and PET-CT were also increasingly common,
leading to their inclusion in the new 2018 FIGO classi�cation [B01]. The FIGO
classi�cation (2018) was adapted to/aligned with the TNM system. Following this,
the FIGO stage could only be established after complete staging, taking into account
all available methods. This means the classi�cation includes both radiological
�ndings from MRI or PET-CT and pathological �ndings following lymphadenectomy.
The su�xes (r) and (p) are used to distinguish between lymph nodes suspected of
being metastatic on imaging or proven to the metastatic pathologically (after biopsy)
respectively. For example: FIGO IIIC(r) = Lymph node involvement based on imaging.
The new FIGO 2018 classi�cation also included some other changes (e.g. the new
FIGO stage IB3). This is relevant for patients' medical histories but also when reading
expert literature. It is important to note which classi�cation is being used so it can be
looked up if necessary. [B01]

Treatment
Cervical cancer treatment -- microcarcinoma
Stage IA1 (lymph node involvement if L0: < 1 %, if L1: 8 %)
• LVSI (lymphovascular space invasion) is a risk factor for lymph node
metastasis.
• L1 = presence of lymphatic invasion in tumor
• V1 = blood vessel invasion in tumor
• Stage IA1: lymph node involvement if L0 < 1 %, if L1 8 %
• Conization with endocervical curettage: if the excision of the tumor is complete
(free margin), with no lymphatic system invasion, if patient desires children.
Requires regular cytological and colposcopic check-ups.
• Simple hysterectomy: Can be recommended if the patient does not wish to have
(more) children.
• Surgical lymph node staging should be discussed in stage IA1 L1.
Stage IA2 (8,3 % with lymph node metastasis even if L0)
• With 0 or 1 risk factors: extrafascial hysterectomy with surgical lymph node
staging
• If the patient wishes to have children: Conization in sano with cervical curettage
and surgical lymph node staging, trachelectomy with surgical lymph node
staging
• IA2 with two risk factors (lymphatic system invasion, V1, G3): Radical
hysterectomy type B with Staging lymphadenectomy, in case of pelvic lymph
node involvement, paraaortic lymphadenectomy
Cervical cancer treatment -- macrocarcinoma
Hysterectomy classi�cation according to the FIGO 2018 Report (table 2)
This classi�cation gives an overview of the indication and extent of resection in case
of cervical cancer [P02]. [P02]
The traditional Wertheim hysterectomy and the Piver-Rutledge-Smith classi�cation
I–V from 1974 use imprecise anatomical terminology and do not take into account
the autonomous pelvic nerve cords and the extent of lymphadenectomy. In addition,
the extent of vaginal resection is overevaluated. This is why the Querleu-Morrow
classi�cation of radical hysterectomy is more frequently used [P07]. [P07]

This uses an international anatomical nomenclature:
• Paracervix: Lateral connective tissue of the cervix: includes cardinal ligament =
Mackenrodt’s ligament, lymphovascular tissue and paracolpium. The deep
uterine vein runs between the cranial and caudal paracervix with the
hypogastric plexus.
• Parametrium: Lymphovascular tissue around the cranial uterine vein of the
paracervix.
• Vesicouterine ligament: paracystium
Type A:
• This uses an international anatomical nomenclature:
• Paracervix: Lateral connective tissue of the cervix: includes cardinal ligament =
Mackenrodt’s ligament, lymphovascular tissue and paracolpium. The deep
uterine vein runs between the cranial and caudal paracervix with the
hypogastric plexus.
• Parametrium: Lymphovascular tissue around the cranial uterine vein of the
paracervix.
• Vesicouterine ligament: paracystium
Type B1:
• Partial resection of the vesicouterine ligament and uterosacral peritoneal fold of
the rectouterine ligament.
• Ureter is unroofed and mobilized laterally, transection of the paracervix at the
level of the ureteral tunnel.
• Caudal paracervix (under the deep uterine vein) not resected.
• At least 1 cm of the vagina is resected.
Type B2:
• B1 plus selective lymphadenectomy in lateral paracervical tissue
Type C:
• Transection of vesicouterine ligament at bladder and rectouterine ligament at
the rectum.
• Ureter is completely mobilized.
• 15–20 mm vagina with paracolpium resected.
Type C1:
• With autonomic nerve preservation: The hypogastric nerves are separated in the

lateral rectouterine ligament and only the uterine branches with the rectouterine
ligament are detached. The bladder branches of the pelvic plexus in the
vesicovaginal ligament are preserved.
Type C2:
• The paracervix is completely resected.
Type D1:
• Resection of the entire paracervix at the pelvic sidewall, exposing the roots of
the sciatic nerve.
• Gluteal inferior, internal pudendal and obturator vessels are resected.
Type D2:
• laterally extended endopelvic resection: whole entire paracervix with internal
iliac vessels and fascial/muscular structures resected.
Lymphadenectomy
• Level I: internal and external iliac
• Level II: + common iliac with presacral lymph nodes
• Level III: + paraaortic lymph nodes caudal to inferior mesenteric vessel
• Level IV: + paraaortic lymph nodes infrarenal
Importance of surgical lymph node staging for cervical cancer > Stage IIB
Surgical lymph node staging by means of laparoscopic lymphadenectomy in cervical
cancer that has progressed beyond IB is more sensitive than PET-CT with regard to
lymph node metastasis. In 4–15 % of patients, paraaortic lymph node metastasis
undetectable on PET-CT could be diagnosed using this method. These patients could
potentially still be treated by expanding the radiation �eld. However, there are
currently no prospective randomized studies on this [P09]. [P09]
Stage IB
• Radical hysterectomy (Wertheim or variants): The "traditional" Wertheim
operation (extended radical hysterectomy) removes the uterus, parametrium,
vaginal cuff, pelvic lymph nodes and if necessary other lymph nodes via
laparotomy.
• The ovaries are generally preserved in order to help prevent menopausal side
effects in premenopausal women.
• A similar operation can also be carried out vaginally (Schauta) or

• Randomized, prospective, international multicenter Phase III study
• Non-inferiority trial
• Hypothesis: Laparoscopic or robotic radical hysterectomy is not inferior to
abdominal radical hysterectomy with regard to disease-free survival (DFS) after
4.5 years. Secondary endpoints were recurrence and overall survival.
• The study was stopped prematurely (n=631 vs planned n=740) for safety
reasons, as a signi�cantly worse outcome was observed in the minimally
invasive surgery arm.
• Results: 27 recurrences (of which 41 % in the fornix/pelvis) with 19 deaths in
the minimally invasive group and 7 recurrences (of which 43 % in the fornix)
with 3 deaths in the open group. Disease-free survival (DFS) after 4.5 years
showed a difference of -10,6 % in the laparotomy group (DFS 86 % in the
minimally invasive group vs 96,5 % in the open group). The hypothesis of non-
inferiority was therefore not veri�ed. The minimally-invasive procedure was
signi�cantly worse in terms of disease-speci�c mortality (HR 6.6; 95% CI 1.5–
29.0) and locoregional recurrence (HR 4.3; 95 % CI 1.4–12.6).
• Summary: Minimally invasive radical hysterectomy is associated with a higher
rate of recurrence and poorer overall survival and disease-speci�c survival than
open radical hysterectomy.
[B02]
In stages IB and IIA, particularly in young women and where permitted by the
patient’s overall condition, extended radical hysterectomy (traditionally: Wertheim
operation) is carried out.
Variations of radical hysterectomy attempt to preserve the autonomic nerves
(branches of the inferior hypogastric plexus and splanchnic nerves S2-S4).
Uterus-preserving radical trachelectomy is also admissible with small IB1 (< 2 cm
diameter) in case of urgent desire to have children (= removal of cervix and partial
resection of parametrium and pelvic lymph nodes).
Stage IIA
• Also removes a large part of the vagina (2 cm safety margin from tumor
margin).
• In principle, beyond FIGO stage IIA, combined chemo-radiotherapy is indicated
rather than surgery.
Stage IIB

n=617 (Intention To Treat Population)
In patients with persistent, recurrent, or metastatic cervical cancer, a bene�t for PFS
(progression-free survival) and OS (overall survival) has been shown when the
checkpoint inhibitor pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1-antibody) is combined with platin-
based standard chemotherapy as �rst-line treatment. This bene�t exists with and
without additional treatment with an angiogenesis inhibitor (bevacizumab).
Median PFS was 10.4 months and median OS 24.4 months for pembrolizumab in the
ITT population, versus 8.2 months and 16.3–16.5 months with placebo (hazard ratio
[HR] for progress: 0.65; p < 0.001) (HR for all-cause mortality: 0.66; p < 0.001).
There was then strati�cation according to PD-L1 expression. The effect of the
checkpoint inhibitor was predominantly observed in the PD-L1 positive group (Pd-L1
combined positive score ≥ 1 or ≥ 10). The signi�cance of the checkpoint inhibitor for
biomarker-negative tumors is therefore still unclear.
Combined chemo-radiotherapy
• For cervical cancer, a combination of chemotherapy and bimodal radiotherapy
is used.
Chemotherapy: cisplatin, as a radiosensitizer, is administered without hospitalization
and at a low dose on a weekly basis (alternative: carboplatin in case of kidney
failure).
40 mg/m2 body surface i.v. weekly
This is carried out simultaneously with an intensive course of radiotherapy,
comprising percutaneous high-voltage irradiation, followed by local contact
irradiation.
Contact irradiation enables a biologically highly effective local dose to be applied.
Postoperative radiotherapy
• Exception, to be avoided wherever possible though careful indication of the
operation.
• For example, there is an indication for postoperative radiotherapy if, despite all
preoperative clari�cations, affected lymph nodes or several other risk factors
(parametrial involvement, lymphatic invasion, etc.) are found by surgery.

• Signi�cant morbidity is expected with postoperative radiotherapy: leg
lymphedema, radiation enteritis, �stula formation
• Diarrhea, blood or mucus in stool etc.
• Radiation damage to the bladder (mucosal ulceration or necrosis) --> painful
urination, blood in urine, urinary tract obstruction, urge and bladder spasm, etc.
• In general, chemo-radiotherapy with cisplatin as a radiosensitizer is carried out
in the same way as for primary therapy.

Cervical cancer and pregnancy
Cervical carcinomas and especially HSIL are often overlooked during pregnancy.
HSIL is found in 0,33 % of all pregnant women, invasive cancer in 0,05 %.
Pregnancy does not appear to have any speci�c effects on the progression of
cervical cancer. Treatment takes place depending on the stage, as for cases outside
of pregnancy. It is currently not clear how far the start of treatment can be delayed:
• In the �rst trimester, the pregnancy is generally terminated and treatment
started immediately.
• Later, depending on the �ndings and the expectations of the patient, it is
possible to wait until the third trimester before carrying out a Cesarean section
and (at the same time) radical surgery.

Managing relapse
If relapse is suspected, histological con�rmation should be performed (biopsy, if
necessary CT-guided).
In case of relapse, renewed staging should be done in order to determine the extent
of the disease (chest/abdomen/pelvis CT, if necessary PET-CT, potentially MRI to
determine local extent).
Treatment for local recurrence (no remote metastasis) depends on the primary
therapy, location and spread of the recurrence and the patient’s general condition.
Therapy can range from radio(chemo)therapy to the full spectrum of surgical
resection (up to exenteration).
• If radio-chemotherapy has not yet been carried out, this option is preferred. The
chemotherapeutic agent of choice is cisplatin for combination with
radiotherapy. However, carboplatin can be used alternatively in case of kidney
failure or reduced tolerability.
• For localized recurrence with previously successful radio-chemotherapy,
surgery aiming for R0 resection can be discussed with the patient. The patient
should be informed about the possibility of an anterior and/or posterior
exenteration (removal of the bladder/rectum). The procedure requires an
interdisciplinary approach: gynecologists, abdominal surgeons, urologists,
psycho-oncologists and potentially plastic surgeons are involved. The patient
must be given comprehensive information on the potential life-altering
measures. Exenteration requires extensive planning. For the patient, it means a
permanent stoma (nephrostomy, colostomy), pelvic �oor reconstruction, loss of
sex organs (change in sexuality), frequent postoperative complications (wound
healing disorders, empty pelvis syndrome, recurrent infections, etc.). The
patient should re�ect on the consequences of surgery in advance.
• If surgery is not a possibility (due to patient desire or in fragile patients),
systemic therapy is also indicated for local recurrence/persistent tumor.
Systemic therapy is indicated for metastatic recurrence.
• Platin-based combination chemotherapy, +/- bevacizumab (monoclonal
antibody against VEGF), with the recent addition of immunotherapy
(pembrolizumab), is indicated as �rst-line therapy [B05].
• Monotherapy is generally recommended for patients with progression after

Patients require long-term care. The side effects of treatment are signi�cant and
include radiation cystitis, radiation vulvovaginitis, lymphedema, vaginal dryness,
vaginal stenosis and vaginal �brosis. Locoregional side effects (particularly of radio-
chemotherapy) can also occur at a later point.
Sexual medicine care is essential, as sexuality is often affected after treatment
(including with radical hysterectomy: sensory disorders, voiding dysfunction, vaginal
shortening; with radio-chemotherapy: radiation dermatitis, telangiectasia with
bleeding, �brosis). Patients should therefore be advised in a specialized preventive
consultation covering vulva/vagina care and vaginal dilatation. Regular stretching of
the vagina with (silicon-) dilator (tube-shaped device, slowly increasing in size) can
help prevent vaginal adhesion and thus preserve organ function.

Practice and Research
Increasingly, the sentinel lymph node biopsy is also used for staging purpose in
cervical carcinoma. These sentinel lymph nodes are extensively processed with
ultrastaging methods and examined for carcinoma cells. However, data on the safety
of this staging procedure if used alone – meaning without systematic pelvic
lymphadenectomy – is not yet mature (phase III studies still pending). The sentinel
lymph node biopsy as staging procedure alone - is therefore not yet a standard in
cervical cancer and should be carried out in the context of studies.

Cervical dysplasia
Author:: Prof.. DDrr.. med.. Mathias Fehr
Chefarzt Gynäkologie & Geburtshilfe, Ärztliche Leitung Brustzentrum Thurgau, Kantonsspital Frauenfeld,

Introduction
Unlike ovarian cancer, cervical cancer and its precancerous, cervical dysplasia, are
"accessible" to clinical examination due to their anatomical location and thus can be
identi�ed at an early stage.
For the uterine cervix, the latency period from preinvasive stages to invasion or
metastasis is estimated to be 5 to 10 years. This means that there is a window
of opportunity of almost a decade during which cancer prevention (screening
with cervical smear or laboratory testing for the human papillomavirus (HPV))
can allow cervical dysplasia to be identi�ed and treated before it becomes
invasive.
Diagnosis of cervical cancer peaks between 35 and 45 years of age. Thanks to
cervical cancer screening, the precancerous stages of cervical cancer can be
identi�ed and treated before they develop into invasive cancer. Cervical dysplasia is
therefore typically diagnosed in patients between 25 and 35 years of age.
Human papillomavirus ((HPV)) and cervical dysplasia
Persistent infection with oncogenic human papillomavirus (HPV) is an essential
condition for the development of high-grade cervical dysplasia and thus cervical
cancer. HPV-negative adenocarcinomas of the cranial cervical canal are very
rare.
There are over 130 different types of HPV, approx. 30 of which are found in the
anogenital region. There are low-risk HPVs that mainly cause genital warts and are
only rarely found in cancers. The 12 oncogenic HPV types cause precancerous
lesions of the cervix, vagina, vulva, anus and oral mucosa (oncogenic HPV types: 16,
18, 31, 33, 35, 39, 45, 51, 52, 58, 59).
Epidemiology of oro-genital and anal HPV infection:
• HPV is transferred via sexual contact or close skin contact (e.g. petting,
orogenital kissing), including oral sex without penetration. It is the most
common sexually transmitted infection.
• Condoms only have a limited protective effect (approx. 50 %).
• HPV infection can also be transferred “non-sexually” from mother to child

vertically during pregnancy or horizontally via breastfeeding and kissing.
• HPV infection is very common. 70 to 80 % of all women and men will be
infected at some time point. Between 15 and 45 % of all women are likely to
come into contact with oncogenic virus types.
• In approx. 90 % of cases, HPV infection is transient and heals spontaneously
within 3 years without symptoms.
[P03]
In women, the prevalence of HPV infection is highest immediately after becoming
sexually active (prevalence approx. 25 %) and slowly declines after 30 years of age
to under 10 %. In men, the incidence of HPV infection remains high at around 60 %
from 18 to 70 years. 56 % of HPV infections are caused by non-oncogenic HPV types
and 44 % by oncogenic HPV types. The persistence of HPV infection and the
development of high-grade precancerous stages depends on the type of HPV. The
risk of high-grade cell changes with HPV 16 (20 %) is seven times higher than that of
an oncogenic HPV type that is not 16 or 18 (3 %) [P10].
HPV viruses are protein-enveloped double-stranded DNA viruses that are
omnipresent. There are different HPV viruses. Some are found only in humans, and
others also in animals. They have a strong tissue tropism, and can only be found in
the epithelial cells of the skin and mucosa. They have high environmental stability.
[P10]

LSIL,, CIN11 ((mild cervical dysplasia))
CIN = Cervical intraepithelial neoplasia as histological diagnosis, LSIL as cytological
diagnosis
• LSIL may be caused by HPV infection with a non-oncogenic type (lacking
integration of HPV DNA into the host cell genome, low-risk HPV type) or HPV
infection with an oncogenic HPV type (in a small percentage of cases with
integration of the HPV DNA into the host cell genome, high-risk HPV type).
• In most cases, LSIL is simply the morphological expression of a transient HPV
infection - in which case it is inconsequential and not precancerous.
• Only LSIL due to high-risk HPV types - in particular with integration of the viral
DNA - are often persistent or can develop into high-grade dysplasia with
oncological consequences.
LSIL due to non-oncogenic HPV types always return to normal - provided that the
immune response is intact!

If the immune response is not intact, as with patients living with HIV or a transplant,
lifelong LSIL is possible even with non-oncogenic HPV types.
HSIL,, CIN22//33 ((moderate and severe dysplasia))
• CIN2/3 are precancerous stages and are caused by persistence of high-risk
HPV types. In clinical practice, separation of CIN2 from CIN3 is of particular
importance in women of childbearing age. This is because with CIN2, a wait
and see approach is preferred for up to two years with careful colposcopy and
histological monitoring.
• Histopathologically, the difference between CIN3 and carcinoma in situ is not
reproducible.
The annual incidence of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (HSIL, CIN2 and
CIN3) is 1,5 per 1000 women. [P01]
Moderate and high-grade cervical dysplasias have oncological signi�cance as
progressive precancerous lesions. HSIL, CIN2/3, should be interpreted as stages
of an initiated (but not (yet) irreversible) malignant transformation! Persistence
and regression are still possible.

Note: The aforementioned �gures are in part estimations, as few prospective studies

[P07][P08]
The natural progression of HSIL depends not only on the HPV type but also on age.
This means regression is rarer and progression more common in women older than
30 years. A meta-analysis showed 60 % regression of CIN2 within two years for
women under 30 and only 44 % regression for those over 30. [P07]

The progression rate for individuals cannot be calculated!
The incidence of adenocarcinoma in situ (AIS), 1,25 per 100’000 women per year, is
much lower than that of HSIL, CIN3 (41,4/100’000).

Progression ttoo cervical cancer
Thanks to cytological screening, younger women are more likely to be diagnosed
with precancerous cervical dysplasia. Diagnosis of cervical cancer is more likely
from 40 years of age.
Risk factors for cervical cancer:
• Persistent HPV infection is the most important tumor promoter, together with
individual predisposition (e.g., HLA system).
• HPV infection with oncogenic HPV types (see below) is very common in those
who are sexually active: 50 % of all women within 5 years of becoming sexually
active
• But even most “oncogenic” HPV infections heal without consequence (90 %)!
• In 5 to 10%, persistent infection will lead to HSIL CIN2/3 with potential
malignant transformation within approx. two years.
• How long a HSIL CIN3 will take to transform into an invasive carcinoma varies
greatly and can be up to ten years, as estimated on the basis of different peak
ages.
• 99,7% of cervical cancers (and 100 % of HSIL CIN3 and most adenocarcinoma
in situ) are high-risk HPV-positive.
• There are rare cervical adenocarcinomas that are not associated with HPV
(mostly of the upper cervical canal).
An infection with oncogenic HPV types must be persistent in order to develop
into high-grade cervical dysplasia (CIN2/3).
Contrary to earlier opinion, HPV persistence must not necessarily lead to LSIL or
CIN1. Morphologically, high-grade dysplasia can occur directly without �rst going
through the LSIL stage.
Other non-causal tumor promoters:
• Smoking
• Vitamin de�ciency (carotenoids, vitamin C, folic acid)
• Immunode�ciency (e.g. HIV, history of kidney transplantation)
• Long-term oral combined use of ovulation inhibitors
• Risk factors for HPV infection (frequent changing of sexual partner, beginning

sexual activity at a young age)
Oncogenic transformation iinn squamous cell cervical cancer
In some women, high-risk HPV types resist the immune response, persisting in the
epithelial cells of the cervix, vulva, vagina, perianal area and mouth. Viral DNA
integrates into the host cell genome. With low-risk HPV types, the HPV genome
remains epigenetic.
• Clinically, virus-induced CIN2 is initially found, followed by CIN3 on progression.
These stages of dysplasia are the expression of genomic changes in the host
cells and indicate the latent oncogenic transformation.
• Further trigger mechanisms then take CIN3 past the point of no return. The
oncogenic transformation is irreversible. The new features are invasiveness and
metastasis. It is not currently possible to predict which cases of CIN3 will
develop into cancer. This step takes place in approx. 30 % of CIN3.
• The malignant degeneration of HPV-infected epithelial cells is associated with
overexpression of the viral oncogenes E6 and E7. These inactivate tumor
suppressor genes.
Figure 1: Infection with high-risk HPV types (following [P09]). [P09]
Cervical cancer must be understood as a possible consequence of persistent
infection with high-risk types of HPV:
• The most oncologically dangerous high-risk HPVs are 16 and 18
• Rarer high-risk HPVs: 31, 33, 35, 39, 45, 51, 52, 55, 56, 58, 59, 68, 73, 82

Prevention
Primary prevention with HPV vaccination
See also expert letter Nr. 44 (about HPV vaccination) of Swiss Society for
Gynecology and Obstetrics (SGGG) (available in German and French).
gynécologie Suisse SGGG – expert letter
One nine-valent HPV vaccine is available in Switzerland which protects against
the two most common carcinogenic HPV types, 16 and 18, as well as the
additional high-risk HPV types 31, 35, 45, 52 and 58. It also protects against the
two most common low-risk causes of warts, 6 and 11.
Vaccination against the 7 high-risk HPV types prevents approx. 90 % of cervical
cancers. As the vaccine is purely prophylactic, its bene�t is greatest when
vaccination is carried out before infection. This is why the Swiss Federal O�ce
of Public Health recommends vaccination before the beginning of potential
sexual activity, i.e. in adolescents aged 11 to 14. In this period, only two
vaccinations are required (0 and 6 months). In Switzerland these vaccinations
are provided by cantonal vaccination protocols.
• In addition, HPV vaccination is recommended as an additional vaccination for
all young men and women up to 26 years old. This is also covered by health
insurance under cantonal vaccination protocols in Switzerland. However, from
the age of 15, three vaccinations at 0, 2 and 6 months are required.
• The nine-valent vaccine is also approved for the vaccination of boys and young
men for the prevention of premalignant lesions and malignant cancers of the
vulva, vagina, anus and oropharynx as well as genital warts.
EE��cacy ooff HPV vaccination

The vaccine contains virus-like particles with L1 capsid protein antigens against the
aforementioned virus types. The L1 proteins do not contain any virus particles. They
are produced via biotechnology in yeast cells (nine-valent vaccine) using
recombinant DNA technology and spontaneously assemble into empty capsids that
do not contain virus genomes.
Immunogenicity is very high and causes much higher blood antibody levels than a
natural infection induces. It has so far been proven that these antibody levels remain
high for over 10 years. It is therefore assumed that immunization lasts for a lifetime.
Immunization to all vaccine-speci�c HPV types is excellent, which is astonishing for
a vaccine for an intraepithelial infection. The induced antibodies can obviously
neutralize the virus before it enters the epithelial cells. All studies have shown the
adverse effects of the vaccine to be negligible, as is to be expected with an
inactivated/protein vaccine.
Protective effect of nine-valent HPV vaccine against lesions caused by the HPV
types contained in the vaccine (CIN2/3, adenocarcinoma in situ, cervical carcinoma,
VIN2/3, VAIN 2/3, vulvar and vaginal carcinoma) is very high at 97,4 %.
Secondary prevention with cervical cytology and colpyscopy

The goal of early cancer detection is to identify and eliminate precancerous lesions
which might lead to cervical cancer. Most cervical dysplasias do not show clinical
symptoms, which is why screening is so important. Between 5 and 7 % of women
will develop an HSIL requiring treatment by their 40th birthday. The goal of cancer
screening with cytology and, in case of pathological cytology, colposcopy and
possible biopsy, is to identify and remove such precancerous lesions.
Repeated cervical cytology sampling (“smear”) is a highly effective measure for
identifying cervical dysplasia and early cervical cancers. The sensitivity of a single
smear is low at 66 %, but the speci�city is high at 96 %, and the sensitivity increases
with repeated sampling.
For cervical cytology, two samples are taken: one from the ectocervix (with a wooden
spatula or a special sampling brush), and one from the cervical canal with a
cytobrush.
Sampling using a cotton swab is obsolete as most cells remain in the cotton. The
removed cells are either “smeared” on a slide, �xed in 90 % alcohol and sent for
cytological staining (Papanicolaou), or the brush/spatula is deposited into a liquid.
The liquid is �ltered in a laboratory in order to place the cells on a slide in a single
layer (thin layer cytology).
Both methods are equivalent if sampling is carried out correctly.

In case of abnormal cytology, colposcopy (magni�ed visual inspection) of the vulva,
vagina and ectocervix is recommended in Switzerland. For this, the cervix is
swabbed with 3 to 5% acetic acid, which causes para or hyperkeratotic epithelium to
swell and appear white due to increased light scattering. Pathological vessels can
also be identi�ed. Pathological colposcopy �ndings can be clari�ed with targeted
biopsy.
Thanks to this screening, the incidence of cervical cancer in industrialized countries
has declined sharply with a consistent or increasing incidence of cervical dysplasia.
In Switzerland, the incidence of cervical cancer has decreased by 28 % since the
1980s, from an average of 440 cases per year (1980 to 1983) to 220 cases per year
(2018 to 2022), while the incidence of precancerous stages is estimated to have
remained the same.
Due to the frequency of HPV infection, suspicious cancer smears are common,
although two thirds of HPV infections do not cause cytological changes: approx. 6 to
7 % of all cervical cytology tests are assessed as suspicious.
Secondary prevention with aa HPV test ((primary HPV
screening))

12 major screening studies with a total of over 77’000 women have shown that the
sensitivity of the HPV test for precancerous stages and cancer is higher than that
obtained with the conventional Pap smear or thin-layer cytology. At 63 to 100 %, the
sensitivity of the HPV test for CIN2/3 was higher in each individual study than that of
the conventional smear (40 to 86 %) or thin-layer cytology (38 to 94 %).
HPV screening is used to identify patients with a HPV infection, not precancerous
lesions. The positive predictive value of the HPV test with regard to CIN2/3 is
therefore between 4 and 23 % depending on the population investigated. However,
the negative predictive value is very high at 98,2 to 100 %.
For women aged over 30, HPV screening is superior to cytological screening.
However, it requires careful explanation, so that high-risk HPV positive patients do
not confuse a high-risk HPV infection with cervical cancer and worry unnecessarily.
A second important condition for good screening using the HPV test is using a
validated HPV test.
Currently, primary HPV screening is not yet covered by Swiss health insurance (as of
2025).
It is important to differentiate primary HPV screening from:
• Secondary HPV screening: Carrying out a screening HPV test in addition to
cytology is sensible in patients > 30 years, if the screening interval will be
extended in case of negative HPV test. This is covered by most Swiss health
insurance companies.
• Postoperative HPV screening (test of cure): Follow-up care after excision of an
HSIL should include co-testing with a validated HPV test and cytology. Co-
testing is far superior to cytology alone in predicting a recurrence of dysplasia.
See also expert letter Nr. 50 (about recommendations for cervical cancer
screening) of Swiss Society for Gynecology and Obstetrics (SGGG) (available in
German and French).
gynécologie Suisse SGGG – expert letter

Diagnosis
The diagnosis must be based on histology (tissue biopsy) not just cytology! Clinical
and cytological suspicion, together with one or more of the following tests, help
guide the practitioner:
• Speculum: The examination always begins with an examination of the vulva,
then the speculum is inserted into the vagina with careful inspection so the
cervix is visible. Pronounced cervical dysplasia is visible to the naked eye, and
appears as a noticeably distended irregular cervical surface.
• Colposcopy of the ectocervix and vagina: Precancerous growths can be
identi�ed with a microscope after application of acetic acid.
• If signi�cant changes are seen, punch biopsies of the suspicious sites must be
taken.
• Endocervical �ndings can only be identi�ed with cervical curettage.
• A HPV DNA or RNA test is not indicated if CIN2/3 or carcinoma is already
diagnosed. (HPV test will be positive anyway, "what type it was" is no longer
relevant).

Treatment
See also expert letter Nr. 50 (about recommendations for cervical cancer screening)
of Swiss Society for Gynecology and Obstetrics (SGGG) (available in German and
French) as well as the algorithms based on expert letter Nr. 50.
gynécologie Suisse SGGG – expert letter
In case of CIN1, a follow up visit is scheduled after 6 months. Due to the high
regression rate, regular 6-monthly colposcopic monitoring of mild dysplasia
allows a watch and see period of at least 2 years. . Given the frequency and high
regression rate of cytological LSIL and histological CIN1, unnecessary excisions
(and their complications) must be avoided at all costs.
CIN2: Particularly for women of childbearing age who wish to have children in
the future, regular colposcopies should be carried out, as the risk of preterm birth
increases after conization and the spontaneous healing rate is 50 %.
CIN3 is a precancerous lesion requiring treatment. A wait and see strategy in
CIN3 should only be recommended in women ≤ 24 years and only under
colposcopic control..
The treatment involves excision for histological examination.
LSIL, CIN1 have a high rate of spontaneous remission: In a Brazilian study, 90 % of
cytological LSIL regressed within 24 months. In a Dutch study lasting four years, all
LSIL patients with non-oncogenic HPV types experienced regression, as did 70 % of
LSIL patients with oncogenic HPV infection [P02]. In adolescents, the regression rate
of LSIL within 36 months is 91 % regardless of HPV type [P03]. In case of colposcopy
biopsy-con�rmed CIN1, the risk of development into CIN2/3 within the subsequent
two years is only 13 %. [P02][P03]

The preferred treatment of CIN2/3 is conization which means excision of the visible
lesion using a loop wire under colposcopic guidance (LLETZ: large loop excision of
the transformation zone, LEEP conization: loop electrosurgical excision procedure),
as this can be carried out quickly, blood loss is minimal, and the transformation zone
remains visible after healing of the wound bed.
The width of the loop electrode is adapted to the extent of the dysplasia.
Scalpel or laser conization leads to greater tissue loss and thus a higher risk of
incompetent cervix leading to preterm birth and neonatal morbidity and mortality in a
subsequent pregnancy, as well as more frequent cervical canal stenosis and thus
more di�cult follow-up care.
Recurrence rate after conization: LEEP conization has a recurrence rate of less than
5 %. If the resection margins are free from CIN, the recurrence rate is less than 2 %.
However, even if the resection margins are CIN-positive, the recurrence rate is still
only 13 %. This means that even with CIN-positive resection margins, a non-surgical
approach with cytological, molecular biology (HPV test) and colposcopy check-ups
every six months after conization can be taken. The probability of regression of
residual CIN is over 85 %. [P05]
Treating adenocarcinoma iinn situ
• In case of a cytological or histological diagnosis of adenocarcinoma in situ
(AIS) of the cervix, conization with endocervical curettage is indicated to rule
out invasive adenocarcinoma.
• For women not intending a further pregnancy, hysterectomy is also
recommended for excision with negative margins due to the high recurrence
rate.

If the patient wishes for further pregnancies and the endocervical curettage reveals
involvement of AIS, re-excision is recommended. However, in this situation a re-
assessment without repeat surgery 6 months later with colposcopy, cytology, HPV
test and endocervical sampling is also acceptable.
If conization margins are not free of AIS, repeat conization is recommended even if a
hysterectomy is planned. This is because invasive lesions in the remaining AIS
cannot be ruled out and are relatively common. If a hysterectomy is performed
directly on AIS that has not been excised with negative margins and an invasive
adenocarcinoma is found, the sentinel lymph node excision method becomes
impossible and an unnecessary pelvic lymphadenectomy has to be performed.
Therefore, it is best to discuss the case with an interdisciplinary tumor board.
Complications ooff conization
The most serious complication of conization is preterm birth in a subsequent
pregnancy. This risk has been proven for all types of conization, but is signi�cantly
lower with LEEP conization. The rate of premature rupture of membranes with
preterm birth in the control group was 4 %, in the laser evaporation group 5 %, with
LEEP conization 9 % and with laser conization 19 %.
The rate of premature rupture of membranes with preterm birth depends on the
height and volume of the excised tissue cone: With a cone height of 1 to 10 mm, it is
6 %, with a height of 11 to 16 mm 9 % and with a cone height of over 16 mm 18 %.
[P04]
Follow--uupp
After treatment of CIN2/3, there is a long-term increased risk of developing an
invasive cervical cancer in this patient group of 56 per 100’000 women over at least
20 years versus 5,6/100’000 in the normal population. Lifelong cancer screening
according to national guidelines is therefore necessary for patients with treated
dysplasia.

Ectopic pregnancy ((EEPP))
Author:: PPDD DDrr.. med.. Edward Wight
ehem. Chefarzt Gynäkologie und gyn. Onkologie (pensioniert seit März 2017), Universitätsfrauenklinik
Basel

Introduction
• Terminology: Fallopian tube, salpinx and tube are synonyms.
• Salpinx is only commonly used in word compositions (salping-, -salpinx).
• Implantation and nidation are often used synonymously.
Memory aid: Fertilization refers to the encounter and fusion between egg and sperm
cell. This usually occurs outside the uterus, in the ampulla of the fallopian tube. The
zygote is the fertilized egg. Through the immediate onset of cell division, the zygote
becomes the embryo. A distinction is made between its two parts, namely the
embryoblast and trophoblast (which later becomes the placenta).
• On Day 6 post-conception, the endometrium is decidualized.
• On Day 6 post-conception, the embryo is at blastocyst stage, in which it reaches
implant maturity and implants itself where it currently is situated.
From a physiological standpoint, on Day 6 p.c., the blastocyst has reached the cavity
of the uterus and implants itself orthotopically in the endometrium.
Extrauterine pregnancy results from a disturbance of the physiological transport or
of the maturation processes during the �rst week after fertilization.

DDee��nition
Synonyms: Extrauterine pregnancy = ectopic pregnancy (EP)
An extrauterine pregnancy occurs when the blastocyst is implanted outside the
endometrium of the uterine cavity.

A tubal pregnancy never produces a living child.
Ovarian pregnancy
• Rare.
• Possible mechanism: During ovulation, the oocyte was not released from the
ruptured follicle and was therefore fertilized within the ovary.
• approx. 1 to 3 % of all extrauterine pregnancies
Abdominal pregnancy
• 1,4 % of all extrauterine pregnancies, dangerous as massive hemorrhage is
possible in case of placental separation with maternal mortality up to 20 %).
• Caused via primary implantation or, more frequently, secondary implantation of
the blastocyst (after tubal rupture, after tubal abortion).
• Placentation: generally in the pelvis, rarely in the upper abdomen, e.g. in the
omentum (implantations at liver, spleen, diaphragm are possible and
particularly dangerous).
IInn the uterus,, the following forms ooff ectopic pregnancy are
possible::
Cervical pregnancy
• Rare
• May occur due to impaired decidualization of endometrium (e.g., after repeated
curettage/after cesarean section/after infertility treatment).
• approx. 1 % of extrauterine pregnancies
• Excessive bleeding during curettage might occur, rarely requiring emergency
hysterectomy.
Cesarean scar ectopic pregnancy

• In recent times, there has been an increasing number of EPs in dehiscent
cesarean scars (increased rate of cesarean sections.).
• This form of extrauterine pregnancy accounts for 6 % of EPs in women after
previous cesarean section: 1:2000 pregnancies after cesarean section.
Intramural pregnancy
• Very rare (less than 50 cases reported in the literature) in intrauterine device
(IUD) users (e.g., following partial perforation of the IUD).
Interstitial pregnancy
• Pregnancy in the proximal part of the tube which passes through the uterine
musculature. 2–3 % of all extrauterine pregnancies. Not equivalent to “cornual
pregnancy” = pregnancy in a horn of a uterus bicornis or unicornis. Mortality 2.5
%, i.e., approx. 7x higher than in cases of tubal pregnancy, due to acute intra-
abdominal hemorrhage due to tubal/uterine rupture.
• The tubal/uterine rupture may also occur late (i.e., between 7 – 16 weeks
gestation).
Simultaneous occurrence ooff intrauterine and extrauterine
pregnancy == heterotopic pregnancy
• Rare in the event of spontaneous conception
• More common after pharmacological treatment of infertility or in vitro
fertilization (1,5 of 1000 pregnancies obtained via assisted reproduction).
• Patients who are undergoing ART for infertility should receive weekly
ultrasounds after a positive pregnancy test has been found
• In ultrasound-con�rmed intrauterine early-stage pregnancy a concomitant
additional ectopic pregnancy is very rare.
• 1:30'000 pregnancies

Epidemiology,, risk factors
Incidence
• 2 of 100 pregnancies (frequency increasing due to higher rates of salpingitis
and of use of arti�cial reproductive technologies).
• In individual cases, the pathogenesis for EP remains unknown. However, the
factors are always maternal; fetal-related factors (e.g. chromosomal
abnormalities) do not increase the risk of EP.
The most common cause ooff fallopian tube pregnancies iiss tubal damage
either post--iinn��ammation oorr due ttoo scarring
History of salpingitis
• After chlamydia infection
• After gonorrhea
• After other salpingitis types
History of previous extrauterine pregnancy
• After tube-preserving surgery (salpingotomy): risk of EP recurrence = 15,4 %
• After (unilateral) tube removal (salpingectomy): risk of EP recurrence = 9,8 %
• After drug therapy (methotrexate = MTX): risk of EP recurrence = 8 %
Other risk factors for an EP
• Sterility, previous infertility treatment: Abnormal tube function, hormonal
imbalance, previous treatment with clomiphene, gonadotropins, IVF
• Pregnancy with IUD: Generally, women with IUDs exhibit a lower risk of EP than
those using no contraceptive. That said, if pregnancy occurs with an IUD, the EP
rate proves greater both with levonorgestrel IUDs and copper IUDs.
• Smoking
• Age over 35 years (cumulation of risk factors)
• Tubal sterilization
• Endometriosis

Morphology
• Histologically, in up to 90 % of all tubal pregnancies, chronic salpingitis can be
detected.
• In 10 % of cases, a salpingitis isthmica nodosa (= ingrowth of the tubal mucosa
into the tubal muscles) is found.
• In half of cases, ampullary EPs exhibit an intraluminal location, while most
isthmic EPs are typically extraluminal or partly intraluminal-partly extraluminal,
which contributes to increased risk of tubal wall lesions in isthmic EPs.

Clinical picture
In most cases, an EP ends spontaneously between the 6 – 10 week gestation due to
unfavorable implantation conditions.
The clinical signs depend on the site of implantation, i.e., the EP location.
The following symptom triad must raise suspicion of tubal pregnancy in a woman of
reproductive age:
• Lower abdominal pain
• Amenorrhea and positive pregnancy test
• Vaginal bleeding or “spotting”
• Asymptomatic cases are possible.
An untreated EP often leads to a life-threatening condition for the woman:
• In case of isthmic and interstitial location, typically tubal rupture occurs
• In case of ampullar/infundibular site, on the other hand, typically tubal abortion
ensues
• For all other EP locations: acute, life-threatening bleeding complications occur
and resolves spontaneously only in rare cases (see: “expectative
management”).
Lower abdominal pain aass the leading symptom
Typically, EP-induced pain symptoms begin a few weeks after menstrual bleeding
has ceased (between 6 and 8 week gestation) with:
• Dull pain of varying intensity in the lower pelvis
• Peritoneal signs (abdominal guarding, rebound tenderness caused by blood
escaping into the free abdominal cavity)

For a patient of childbearing age, all types of abdominal pain, regardless of their
duration and intensity, should arouse suspicion of EP.
Initially the pain is unilateral but often spreads to the opposite side or generalizes.
Sometimes, cramp-like pain develops.
Radiating pain in the back, upper abdomen, and shoulder [diaphragmatic irritation] is
possible. In between, intervals with few or no symptoms may occur for hours to
days.
Regression of pain may indicate that the EP is resolving spontaneously.
Sudden pain or hyperacute pain intensi�cation (acute abdomen) is often associated
with a complication (tubal rupture, intra-abdominal bleeding with circulatory
instability: hypotension, tachycardia, cold sweat). The presence of blood in the
rectouterine pouch may trigger fecal urgency.
Vaginal bleeding
• Typically spotting.
• Also possible: recurrent extensive bleeding or continuous bleeding.
• Despite the bleeding, amenorrhea can generally be suspected based on medical
history.
The most frequent cause of spotting is that the endometrium which has
decidualized under the in�uence of progesterone is unable to be maintained due to
the empty uterine cavity and inappropriate rise in bHCG (or bHCG drop).
General symptoms
Usual symptoms are those typical of pregnancy (nausea, vomiting, and breast
tenderness) that are generally less pronounced compared to an intact intrauterine
pregnancy.

Abdominal pregnancies
Abdominal pregnancies are very rare, but are often not detected until late
gestations, even in some cases at term, due to the paucity of symptoms.
Repeated, diffuse abdominal pain during pregnancy or peritonism (after
intraperitoneal rupture of fetal membranes or bleeding) should be interpreted as
potential clinical warning signs.
Abdominal pregnancy is the only form of EP which, in exceptional cases, may lead
to the birth a living child (which must be delivered from the abdomen via
laparotomy). Depending on the location of the placenta (pelvic wall, intestine,
omentum, liver), however, the mother can develop life-threatening hemorrhages
upon spontaneous detachment of the placenta or during the attempt to remove the
placenta operatively.

Diagnostics
Clinic, bHCG serum values and transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) are crucial to
diagnosing ectopic pregnancy (see also Appendix)
Dectection of ectopic pregnancy at early gestations has become common due to
standardized (early) pregnancy monitoring.
That said, there are surprising EP diagnoses – for which doctors of nearly all �elds
must be prepared when treating women of childbearing age with corresponding
clinical symptoms.
An EP is discovered
• either via primary diagnostics, generally during regular pregnancy monitoring
• or via acute diagnostics (emergency patients; complications).
Clinical Presentation
Medical history
• The risk factors are of interest (see above).
• Thus, in women following infertility treatment, even in case of a known early
intrauterine pregnancy, it is always necessary to consider the possibility of an
“extrauterine twin”, i.e., a heterotopic EP, when lower abdomen pain persists.
Abdominal palpation
• Unilateral pain, in the lower part of the pelvis (wide differential diagnosis, e.g.,
appendicitis, ovarian cyst rupture, or ovarian torsion)
• Please note: In the event of an EP, abdominal pain on palpation may very well be
absent.
• Differential diagnosis: In normal early-stage intrauterine pregnancy, pain and
guarding in the adnexal area may be caused by an enlarged corpus luteum.
Speculum
• Typically dark-brownish discharge from the cervical canal (corresponding to the
spotting).
• In case of peritoneal irritation, the speculum examination is notably painful.
Bimanual examination
• Tenderness on palpation in adnexal area. Pain upon moving / elevating the
cervix. Potentially doughy resistance.

• Caution, as rupture of the fallopian tube may be provoked.
• The gynecological examination is sometimes di�cult to interpret.
bHCG testing and transvaginal ultrasound ((TVUS))
If the tentative diagnosis of an EP was made based on medical history and clinical
examination, the differential diagnosis must be made via the correlation of the bHCG
values with the TVUS �ndings between:
• viable early-stage intrauterine pregnancy
• non-viable intrauterine pregnancy (miscarriage)
• EP
A single bHCG analysis does not allow a distinction to be made between these three
differential diagnoses. Only serial bHCG analysis at intervals of 48–72 hours can
provide indications as to presence of a viable intrauterinepregnancy (appropriate
increase in bHCG = doubling of the previous value), a nonviable pregnancy, or an EP
(drop or inappropriate increase in bHCG = no doubling of the previous value).
The TVUS is the best imaging diagnostic modality aimed to locate an EP. That said,
for clear diagnosis of pregnancy, it is required to detect the amniotic cavity (not to be
confused with a pseudogestational sac!), yolk sac, and embryo, while viability must
be assessed by documenting cardiac activity. The TVUS can also reveal �ndings that
are suggestive of, but do not prove the presence of an EP, such as an adnexal
�ndings or free �uid in the rectouterine pouch.
Using devices available today, sonographic detection of an intrauterine pregnancy is
possible at the earliest about 10 days after a positive pregnancy test by means of
bHCG analysis of the serum [see appendix: “Diagnostic window”]. The bHCG value at
which it should be possible to diagnose an intrauterine pregnancy via TVUS is 1500
to 2000 mIU/ml (= discriminatory zone). In one study, 98 % of all intrauterine
pregnancies with bHCG values over 1500mIU/mL were detected via ultrasound. With
these bHCG values, an EP is usually not able to be located with certainty using TVUS,
but its location can only be suspected.
Algorithms for practical approach: see under “Therapy”)
Diagnostic laparoscopy
• If the serum bHCG value exceeds the discriminatory zone of 1500 to 2000 mIU/
ml and no conceptus can be found via ultrasound in the uterine cavity, this
suggests an EP, therefore a diagnostic/therapeutic laparoscopy (especially for

clinically symptomatic patients ) or drug therapy is indicated to treat the EP,
depending on the level of pain and hemodynamic state of the patient.
• At an early gestational age (4–6 weeks), in the event of absent clinical
symptoms and non-diagnostic TVUS, the laparoscopy should be postponed –
while the patient is closely monitored (clinical examinations, ultrasound, bHCG).
At an early gestational age (4–6 weeks), a tubal EP may not be recognized at all
during laparoscopy, and therefore the surgical intervention would prove useless
and may have to be repeated later.
• In general, general, however, and especially if the patient is clinically
symptomatic and potentially hemodynamic unstable, laparoscopic surgery
must be performed.
Monitoring ooff general condition
• If upon tentative diagnosis of EP, the decision is made to proceed in an
expectative manner, the signs of intra-abdominal bleeding and hypovolemic
shock (tachycardia, hypotension, and cold sweat) must be expected to occur at
any time. If the patient remains hospitalized, appropriate monitoring must be
ensured. If outpatient management is opted for, the patient and her family must
be informed of this possibility. Furthermore, it should be ensured that the
patient can be immediately hospitalized at any time.
• Fever is not a typical symptom of EP. However, a “resorption fever” [as with
intraperitoneal bleeding] or a febrile secondary infection is possible.
• Extrauterine pregnancies still remain the primary cause of pregnancy-induced
maternal mortality within the �rst trimester, despite improved diagnostic and
therapeutic methods.
4–10 % of all pregnancy-related deaths

Differential diagnosis
In the event of lower abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding, the differential diagnosis
initially proves particularly broad (until the pregnancy test is available) in the
emergency department:
• Benign and malignant tumors of the genitalia, bladder, intestines
• Infections (genital infections, urinary tract infections, or intestinal infections)
• Trauma (sexual intercourse, sexual violence, or foreign bodies)
• Nephrolithiasis, cystolithiasis
• Endometriosis
• Atrophy (atrophic colpitis)
• IUD
In the case of a positive pregnancy test, generally the relevant differential diagnoses
are narrowed to:
• Viable intrauterine pregnancy with threatened abortion
• Impaired intrauterine pregnancy (inevitable or incomplete abortion)
• Extrauterine pregnancy
• Heterotopic pregnancy
• Trophoblastic tumor

Treatment
Active treatment includes the timely termination of the extrauterine pregnancy. The
anticipated (potentially life-threatening) maternal complications do not justify any
other option. Drug therapies are now an established alternative to surgical
procedures. In this context, the respective indications and contra-indications must be
observed.
Special case without active therapy:
If the bHCG values are low [typically <200mIU/mL] and continue dropping, and there
is no pregnancy visable on ultrasound and absent or only mild clinical symptoms,
expectative management can be opted for—with the knowledge that extrauterine
pregnancies can resolve spontaneously—under close clinical, sonographic, and
biochemical monitoring. bHCG values should be monitored until they fall below the
detection limit. That said, there are no parameters to reliably predict the
spontaneous course of an EP.
Systemic therapy with methotrexate ((MTX))
As a folic acid antagonist, the cytostatic methotrexate (MTX) inhibits the DNA
synthesis of rapidly growing tissues. The use of MTX in the treatment of EP (usually
by i.m. route) is performed according to strict criteria and requires intense
monitoring (i.e., close clinical, sonographic, and laboratory checks, as well as
repeated serum bHCG analyses so as to assess response to the therapy).
Under optimal conditions, an excellent success rate (90 %) is achieved, thus
rendering MTX therapy cheaper than a surgical procedure.
The success of MTX treatment is especially dependent on the βHCG value before
treatment is initiated. The expected treatment success rate is 94 % for βHCG values
between 500 and 1000 mIU/mL, 75 % for 2500 to 3000 mIU/mL, and 66 % for 4500
mIU/mL and above. The probability of treatment success after a single
administration of MTX can be estimated via a βHCG analysis if a 15 % drop from day
4 to day 7 appears. If the βHCG value decreases by less than 15 % or even increases
beyond the initial value, then re-administration of MTX or a surgical intervention may
be required.
Indications (requirements) for MTX treatment of tubal EP

• Early cases of clinically symptom-free EP*, i.e., no or minimal pain, stable
circulation
• Serum βHCG < 5000 mIU/mL*
• No or minor adnexal �nding via ultrasound (< 4 cm in diameter)
• No fetal cardiac activity*
• Compliant patient (follow-up monitoring)
• Trophoblast persistence after salpingotomy
*for special EP locations (cervical pregnancy, interstitial tubal pregnancy), an MTX
treatment (in higher dosage, see below) is also relevant in case of higher gestational
age, positive cardiac activity, and βHCG signi�cantly above 5000 mIU/mL.
Contraindications for MTX treatment of EP
• Hemodynamic instability
• Large amount of intra-abdominal �uid
• Signi�cant pain
• Pathological laboratory parameters (hematology, liver and kidney parameters)
• Comorbidities which prohibit MTX treatment (e.g., immune de�ciency, �orid
infections, or gastric ulcer)
• Allergy/intolerance to MTX
• Heterotopic pregnancy
• Lactation
• Serum βHCG > 5000 mIU/mL
• Positive cardiac activity of EP
• Large adnexal mass (> 4 cm)
Dosing of MTX
• 50 mg/m2 of body surface or 1 mg/Kg of body weight i.m. as single dose.
• In special cases (cervical pregnancy, interstitial tubal pregnancy), several days
of MTX treatment should be opted for according to the therapeutic schedule for
trophoblastic diseases (--> G12B): MTX Day 1, 3, 5, 7 – 1mg/Kg of body weight
i.m. each as well as folic acid “rescue” Day 2, 4, 6, 8 0.1mg/kg of body weight
p.o. each
Important considerations
• An increase in bHCG values up to 4 days after MTX administration is possible
• Between Day 4 and 7 after MTX administration, however, there should be a
bHCG reduction of at least 15 %, otherwise another MTX administration is

indicated.
• Increase in abdominal pain is possible up to 4 days after MTX administration
• NSAIDs must not be used for treating pain (interaction with MTX)
• Temporary increase in adnexal �ndings detected via ultrasound proves possible
• Regular bHCG and sonographic follow-up checks are indicated until bHCG falls
below the detection limit. This may last 1-3 months after therapy initiation, but
occasionally even longer
• Bleeding complications (with the need for surgical intervention) are possible at
any time during the course of treatment
• Avoid exposure to sun (MTX-induced dermatitis)
• Administration of folic acid should be avoided in the context of MTX treatment
(this applies to single-dose treatment with MTX, but not to higher-dose
treatment with MTX, e.g., in cervical pregnancy and for trophoblastic tumors, for
which folic acid is administered as rescue treatment to minimize the adverse
effects).
• Contraceptives should be used to prevent another pregnancy before the beta
HCG levels have dropped to zero.
Surgical treatment
In general, and irrespective of the EP location, the following applies:
• Overall, in the event an EP diagnosis, surgery is performed in the majority of
cases (approx. ⅔). The procedure is generally performed via laparoscopy. In the
event of unstable circulatory conditions (hemodynamic [pre-]shock), an
emergency laparotomy is sometimes indicated.
Indications for a surgical procedure in case of EP are:
• Hemodynamic instability
• Clinical signs of intra-abdominal bleeding, suspected rupture
• Pronounced clinical symptoms (pain)
• Heterotopic pregnancy
• Desire for tubal sterilization
• Hydrosalpinx and desire for future IVF
• Absolute/relative contraindications for MTX treatment (see corresponding
section)
• Failed MTX treatment

If there is no emergency indication for surgical intervention, if possible, surgery
should be performed only if EP is expected to be identi�ed during the operation
based on vaginal ultrasound �ndings (evidence of an adnexal �nding) and the bHCG
value.
Tubal surgery –– iinn case ooff tubal pregnancy
Salpingectomy Salpingotomy
Figure 3: Tube-preserving operation.
Figure 2: Removal of the tube.
usually laparoscopic
Salpingectomy
Indications for salpingectomy include:
• Uncontrollable bleeding from the tube
• Tube irrevocably destroyed by the EP (large EP, tubal rupture)
• Simultaneous desire on the part of patient for tubal sterilization or
salpingectomy (planned IVF, or prophylaxis of ovarian cancer)
Technique:
• usually complete, in rare cases partial removal of the tube
Salpingotomy

Indication:
• Conservation of tube function in case of minimal tube damage caused by EP,
and in patients with only one fallopian tube due to prior contralateral
salpingectomy.
• Typical intervention for ampullary EP
Technique:
• Longitudinal incision of the tube on the side opposite to the mesosalpinx,
possibly after injecting vasopressin (Minirin®) and careful, atraumatic removal
of the conceptus, if possible using a laparoscopic suction-irrigator device
(hydrodissection); careful, atraumatic hemostasis via electrocoagulation; the
incision on the tube is then left open (no suture).
• As an alternative to salpingotomy, another tube-conserving surgical procedure
is possible – in rare cases – if the EP is located peripherally at the �mbrial end
of the fallopian tube (infundibular EP): This is done by squeezing the conceptus
out of the �mbrial end, a process referred to as milking out.
Trophoblast persistence occurs after salpingotomy (and after milking out) in 4 to
15% of cases. This is re�ected in a renewed increase or a lack of decrease of the
HCG values below the detection limit (<5mIU/mL) postoperatively. The
consequences of trophoblast persistence are the same as for the persistence of EP.
For these reasons, the HCG must be monitored in the short term after
salpingotomy and milking out, until it has dropped below the detection limit.
The risk of secondary bleeding is increased with salpingotomy as compared to
salpingectomy. Therefore, the bene�ts of fertility preservation versus the
disadvantages of salpingotomy must be carefully weighed out in the indication for or
against a tube-conserving surgical intervention.

Treatment ooff non--tubal EEPP
The surgical treatment of non-tubal EP (interstitial, cervical, or intramural) used to
frequently consist of emergency hysterectomy. Given that today EP diagnosis is
made earlier, drug treatments (MTX) and uterine-preserving surgical techniques are
more common.
Cervical pregnancy
First-line treatment is MTX.
In the event of bleeding in the setting of drug therapy or at curettage, bilateral
embolization or ligation of the uterine arteries (or their descending branches) should
be considered. Postoperative insertion of a balloon catheter into the uterus for
hemostasis. As a last resort when bleeding is unstoppable: hysterectomy
Pregnancy iinn the ((dehiscent)) cesarean section scar
• Increase of this EP location due to increased frequency of cesarean sections.
• The primary therapeutic focus is the removal of the ectopic pregnancy, which is
often located between the uterus and the bladder, together with the freshening
of the edges of the dehiscent scar and its closure either via laparoscopy or
laparotomy.
• Cases treated with MTX and hysteroscopic procedures are described.
Interstitial EEPP
• Differential diagnosis: Cornual pregnancy = pregnancy in the horn of a uterus
bicornis.
• The primary focus is on drug therapy with MTX or surgical procedures
(cornuostomy, cornuectomy + salpingectomy) in case of failure of MTX
treatment or bleeding complications, via laparoscopy or laparotomy.
• The ligation of the uterine vessels or their embolization must be considered in
the event of bleeding and the desire to preserve the uterus.

Trophoblastic persistence
Trophoblastic persistence refers to the persistence of vital trophoblast tissues in
situ after drug or surgical treatment, from which a sort of “local recurrence”
consisting of trophoblast tissue can form via proliferation.
Trophoblast persistence poses a relevant risk (5–20 % of cases), both after MTX
treatment and after tube-preserving surgery modes (salpingotomy/milking out of
tube).
Symptoms:
• Recurrence of pain and intra-abdominal bleeding.
• The delayed decrease in bHCG serum value during follow-up monitoring is
suggestive of trophoblastic persistence, while a plateau formation or renewed
increase proves its manifestation.
It is therefore obligatory for short-term and repeated laboratory monitoring (bHCG) to
be carried out after MTX treatment or after tube-preserving surgery in order to detect
trophoblastic persistence as early as possible.
Treatment
• (If necessary) MTX or salpingectomy.
• After initial tube-preserving treatment, the tube may subsequently need to be
removed in the event of trophoblast persistence.

Outcome
Within 24 months, ⅔ of women with a desire for a child can expect an intrauterine
pregnancy after EP surgery via salpingectomy and ¾ after salpingotomy or an EP
drug treatment (MTX) if they are under 35 years of age and have no medical history
of infertility.
The incidence of an EP recurrence is about 15 %. The recurrence rates after
salpingectomy (if one tube remains), salpingotomy, and MTX treatment are
comparable. That said, after two preceding EPs, the risk of recurrence increases up
to 30 %.

Appendix
Human chorionic gonadotropin ((bHCG)) aass aa laboratory
parameter
The glycoprotein bHCG is produced by the syncytiotrophoblast (component of the
trophoblast) at the time of implantation. Then, the bHCG secretion increases during
the �rst 10 weeks of pregnancy in correlation with the growth of the conceptus.
As a rule of thumb, the bHCG value should double in the serum every 48 to 72
hours in the event of normal intrauterine pregnancy.
An insu�cient increase (less than 53 % bHCG increase within 2 days) or even a drop
in bHCG values may suggest a nonviable intrauterine or possibly an ectopic
pregnancy.
In immunological test systems used today, antibodies against the beta subunit of the
HCG are used for pregnancy diagnostics. Several textbooks refer to HCG for reasons
of simplicity (others consistently utilize bHCG).

Vaginal ultrasound
Normalfall
Intrauterine pregnancy is visible on ultrasound after cycle Day 32 at the earliest (=
shortly after the absence of menstrual bleeding). In recent years, vaginal ultrasound
has signi�cantly re�ned the diagnosis of EP. If the uterine cavity is empty, �ndings
should be interpreted in correlation to the simultaneously measured bHCG serum
value.
The following is normal for intrauterine pregnancy:
1. It can be detected via ultrasound at the end of Week 5 or at beginning of the
Week 6 of pregnancy (chorionic cavity, yolk sac, embryo, fetal membranes,
cardiac activity)
2. It has a bHCG level of (600 -) 2000mIU/ml in Week 6
With very good ultrasound devices and under “favorable” ultrasound conditions, a
normal, intrauterine pregnancy is already detectable at a bHCG serum value of
1000mIU/ml.
In the event of a bHCG serum value of (at least) 1500mIU/ml, an intrauterine
pregnancy should be reliably diagnosable (in 98 % of cases) with the use of
conventional ultrasound equipment via transvaginal ultrasonography.
If the uterine cavity – contrary to expectations – is found via ultrasound to be
“empty”, an EP or an impaired intrauterine pregnancy, or, very rarely, a trophoblastic
tumor (e.g., placental site trophoblastic tumor --> G12B) is to be assumed.
The “discriminatory zone” for the differential diagnosis between intrauterine and
extrauterine pregnancy depends on:
• the experience of ultrasound operator
• the quality of the ultrasound equipment
• clinical conditions (e.g. presence of myomas)
• the quality of the bHCG test
