Endometrial cancer
CCoo--
Author:: DDrr.. med.. Martin Heubner
Chefarzt Gynäkologie und Direktor des Departements Frauen und Kinder, Kantonsspital Baden
Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr. med. Rainer Kimmig, PD Dr. med. Pauline Wimberger, Prof. Dr. med. Daniel Fink

Introduction
• Endometrial cancer is the most common female genital cancer in industrialized
countries.
• The most common female genital cancer worldwide is cervical cancer.
• The most common malignancy in women overall is breast cancer.
• Fourth most common tumor in women in the USA.
• Eighth most common cause of death among all cancers.
• 15 % of all women die from the disease.
Contrary to cervical cancer, endometrial cancer is:
• age-related
• hormone-dependent in most forms
• not triggered by a viral infection
• usually discovered at an opportune stage without screening, due to early
symptoms alone
As with healthy endometrium, endometrial cancer is typically (but not always)
hormone dependent.
As a reminder, healthy endometrial cells (--> endometrium = glands and stroma)
depend on estrogen to complete their proliferation. This proliferation is limited by
gestagens. As a countermeasure, gestagens carry out the secretory conversion of
the endometrium.

DDee��nitions
Terms: Endometrial cancer and uterine cancer are synonyms.

Epidemiology
Endometrial cancer is an age-associated cancer. The peak age is between 60 and 75
years (incidence increasing in recent years).
However, 5 % of patients affected are under 40 years old.
Endometrial cancer:
• Associated with colon, ovarian and breast cancer
• but not with cervical or vulvar cancer.
• Patients with hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer (HNPCC) have a 40 %
lifetime risk of developing endometrial cancer.
Estrogens stimulate the existence of the majority of endometrial cancers (namely all
highly-differentiated endometrioid types):
Highly-differentiated endometrioid endometrial cancers are generally rich in
hormone receptors. Estrogen receptors are directly relevant for carcinogenesis.
• This is because if a healthy endometrium is permanently exposed to a high
estrogen stimulus, without a counteracting gestagen effect, this �rst leads to
simple hyperplasia,
• which can then lead to atypical proliferation. With time, atypical hyperplasia of
the endometrium can occur.
• Atypical hyperplasia most often involves high concentrations of estrogen (and
progesterone) receptors; the further progression is therefore mapped out.
In 40 % of patients with atypical hyperplasia in material from curettage,
endometrial cancer is diagnosed with histological processing of the uterus
specimen after hysterectomy.
The incidence peak of atypical hyperplasia is a good decade before that of
endometrial cancer.
Risk factors for (endometrioid) endometrial cancer
• Age
• Early menarche and/or late menopause (cumulative, higher than average
endogenous estrogen stimulus).

• Obesity (subcutaneous fatty tissue produces estrogen [extraglandular
aromatization unconjugated estriol in serum] and sex hormone-binding globulin
[SHBG]). A BMI > 30 kg/m2 is associated with a 2-3x higher risk of endometrial
cancer, and concomitant type II diabetes leads to a further increase in risk.
• Hormone Replacement Therapy (HRT): presently the main factor for exogenous
estrogen overstimulation! However, the administration of progesterone
(combination therapy) can neutralize the risk resulting from estrogen
substitution, whereby progesterone is used to return the endometrium from the
stimulating (proliferative) state to the “deactivated” (secretory) state in phases,
in a pharmacologically controlled manner. This considerably lowers the mitosis
rate and number of estrogen receptors again. If the uterus is present, HRT
should therefore always be implemented as combination therapy (i.e. estrogen
plus gestagen)!
• Certain SERMs (e.g. Tamoxifen®) – the partial estrogen agonist effect
promotes the development of EMT hyperplasia and subsequently that of an
EMT cancer.
• Corpus luteum insu�ciency with polycystic ovaries (anovulation reduced
gestagen formation relative estrogen overproduction)
Note: The traditional triad often found with type I endometrial cancer is a
combination of overweight, diabetes and arterial hypertension. Diabetes mellitus and
hypertension are not proven risk factors, but are associated with endometrial cancer
(probably as these diseases are also associated with obesity).

Histopathology and classi��cation
Malignant epithelial tumors of the endometrium are fundamentally always
adenocarcinoma. A classi�cation as type 1 and type 2 has been established:
Table 1: Type 1 carcinoma
80 % Endometrioid endometrial cancer
Often designated the typical type 1.
80 % Tumor biology: mostly hormone dependent!
80 % Mostly highly differentiated:
Mostly rich in estrogen and progesterone receptors.
Occurrence correlates with a variety of risk factors.
80 % Development: Malignant transformation of a precancerous condition
(namely endometrial hyperplasia with atypia) as a result of
hyperestrogenism.
80 % Overall, favorable prognosis. Any squamous cell involvement
(adenosquamous carcinoma) does not worsen the prognosis.
Atypical hyperplasia: precancerous condition of type 1 endometrial cancer
Table 2: Type 2 carcinoma
20 % Speci�c histological forms (see below) of adenocarcinoma are classi�ed
as type 2. This also includes serous papillary and clear-cell carcinoma.
20 % Tumor biology: mostly NOT hormone dependent!
20 % This type shows scant hormone receptors.
Often occurs without risk factors, including in particular without obesity.
20 % Development: Not a result of hyperplasia - but rather the malignant
transformation of an endometrial intraepithelial neoplasm in otherwise
atrophic endometrial tissue.
20 % Prognosis less favorable than type 1 endometrial cancer.
Molecular classi��cation ooff endometrial cancer

Particularly with endometrial cancer in early stages, the traditional
histomorphological classi�cations such as TNM stages and grading have proven to
be relatively unreliable with regard to assessing the risk of recurrence. A
classi�cation according to molecular tumor properties is therefore increasingly used
for endometrial cancer, as is already the case for many other types of tumor. In
addition to risk strati�cation, this analysis also allows for an estimation of whether
the disease occurred spontaneously or as a result of a genetic predisposition: 5 to
10 % of all endometrial cancers occur in association with HNPCC (hereditary non-
polyposis colorectal cancer) syndrome. This can be indicated by an absence of DNA
mismatch repair proteins (MMR) shown on immunohistochemical analysis. This can
be caused by promoter methylation (found in approx. 50 % of tests) and not by a
germline mutation. However, if methylation is not found, genetic counseling should
take place. If suspected HNPCC syndrome is con�rmed with genetic testing, this has
a direct impact on how the patient’s treatment is managed and the advice given to
relatives. In such cases, relatives should also be encouraged to undergo genetic
counseling.
The molecular classi�cation can provide valuable information for therapy planning
and has therefore already been included in some international guidelines. While
tumors with a p53 mutation are high-risk and should be treated aggressively,
evidence of a POLE mutation is associated with a very good prognosis and most of
these patients do not require any adjuvant therapy.
Previous data on clinical relevance were assessed retrospectively and clinical
studies on the prospective bene�ts of molecular tumor classi�cation are pending.
A distinction is drawn between the following molecular subtypes:

Clinical picture
The most important symptom of endometrial cancer is postmenopausal bleeding
from the corpus uteri (corresponding with the high age-prevalence of the disease).
The most common symptom is bleeding or a bleeding disorder!
This is a clear symptom that usually occurs at an early stage. This encourages
early diagnosis and enables early detection.
The �ve-year survival rate is correspondingly high at 80 % (higher than most
other cancers!).
Any postmenopausal bleeding could be a sign of endometrial cancer and should
(therefore) be investigated.
Irregular bleeding (metrorrhagia), a dark reddish-brown discharge, and any lower
abdominal pain can also indicate endometrial cancer.
However, endometrial cancer does not always become clinically apparent due to
bleeding! Interestingly, in asymptomatic patients, early diagnosis (e.g. by
ultrasound) does not have a favorable effect on prognosis - this is probably due
to the overall fairly favorable prognosis, particularly for type 1 cancer. [B09]
Symptoms from metastases (or metastasizing progressions) generally present
rather late with endometrial cancer.
For example: Back pain (with retroperitoneal lymph node involvement), symptoms
due to lung metastases.

Diagnosis
Speculum examination
This can determine whether bleeding is coming from the uterus. The development of
a metastasis of the endometrial cancer in the vagina can also be detected.
Cytological smears are mostly negative - and therefore cannot be used for early
detection, as a screening measure, for endometrial cancer.
Bimanual gynecological examination
• This examination is usually inconspicuous(!) in the early stage.
• In advanced stages, an enlarged uterus with a soft(!) consistency or the
involvement of the parametrium can sometimes be palpated.
Patient obesity, which is often encountered, frequently means that the clinical test is
inconclusive.
Rectal examination
This can indicate whether the tumor has already spread to the rectum. In addition,
palpation of the parametrium and rectouterine pouch is possible.
Vaginal ultrasound -- ""measuring endometrial thickness""
Ultrasound imaging of the endometrium can provide important information. A thick
endometrium with or without intrauterine �uid collection is suspicious. However,
measuring the in�ltration depth (suspicious �ndings) is di�cult.
Figure 1: Ultrasound image of an endometrial cancer.
Ultrasound image of an endometrial cancer. An inhomogeneous thickened endometrium can be seen. This
case shows no indication of an extended in�ltration of the myometrium.
Histological con��rmation with hysteroscopy and fractional
curettage

Hysteroscopies can also be carried out as an out-patient procedure, in most cases
they are done under general anesthetic.
A hysteroscope is inserted and the uterine cavity is �lled with an isotonic
solution so that it expands. This gives the investigating doctor a direct view of
the cavity pathology to be investigated, which is valuable for differential
diagnosis.
• In macroscopic terms, the location and extent of the �ndings are assessed, and
a possible cervical involvement (=stage II) is investigated.
• Targeted tissue biopsies are taken from the suspect location.
After the hysteroscopy, during the same intervention, a wide area of tissue from the
cavity and cervical canal is scraped off - “blindly” using a curette - for histological
testing.
Fractioned tissue (sequential and separated according to sample site) is
collected: (i) from the cervical canal (for targeted investigation of cervical
involvement); (ii) from the whole uterine cavity
The fractional curettage is carried out under the same partial/general anesthetic as
the hysteroscopy, after additional dilation of the cervical canal and inner cervix using
dilatators (Hegar dilators).
Note: Curettage alone has a risk of missing especially smaller carcinomas. Hence,
concomitant hysteroscopy is mandatory.
Caution: Curettage of a pronounced endometrial cancer invading the myometrium
entails a risk of uterus perforation.
Possible alternatives:: Vacuum aspiration ((== outpatient
endometrial biopsy iinn clinic))
There is a method for detecting (but not ruling out!) endometrial cancer, which can
readily be used in clinics (except in cases of cervical canal stenosis, as is sometimes
found in postmenopausal patients). A thin catheter (e.g. a Cornier Pipelle) is inserted
into the cervical canal and pushed into the cavity. Endometrial tissue is then
collected under vacuum aspiration.

• This can be used for cancer con�rmation only!
• A “negative” �nding in no way rules out cancer.
If the vacuum aspiration shows no cancerous tissue, a hysteroscopy with
fractional curettage must be carried out.
Presurgical Staging Testing
If endometrial cancer is con�rmed with histological testing, the results of the
following “preliminary staging tests” are required for planning primary treatment.
• Transvaginal ultrasound is suitable for diagnosing local metastasis, in particular
evaluating the possible involvement of the myometrium and the cervix. If the
�ndings are unclear or interpretation is limited, an MRI should be provided.
• Chest x-ray - to rule out lung metastases (rare overall, but up to 4 % with high-
risk cancers)
• Imaging with MRI or CT in case of justi�ed suspicion of or high clinical risk of
distant metastases
Side note: De�nitive staging of endometrial cancer is only carried out post surgery,
according to the additional peritoneal cytology �ndings (see below) and the
resection histology!

Differential diagnosis
Endometrial cancer is the most common malignant cause of vaginal bleeding.
However, only 1/10 postmenopausal bleeds are caused by endometrial cancer.
Other causes of bleeding are:
• DD (malignant): cervical cancer, uterine cancer, vaginal/vulvar/ovarian/fallopian
tube cancer
• DD (benign): polyps (mainly uterine polyps), submucosal myoma, bleeding from
atrophic endometrium, etc.

Growth and metastatic pathway
Endometrial cancer generally originates in the uterine fundus. Its growth is either
exophytic (into the uterine lumen) or penetrating the uterine muscles.
Direct spread
• In 10 % of cases, there is cervical involvement (DD!). Over time, spread into the
parametrium is possible. Occasionally, the vagina may be involved (mainly the
anterior vaginal wall; upper third). Suburethral involvement is a very common
localization in the vagina.
• Finally, intracanalicular tumor cell spread is possible via the fallopian tubes - in
the abdomen: peritoneal metastases.
Lymphogenic spread
• Possible pelvic lymph node involvement
• Metastases in the para-aortic lymph nodes must be considered, through direct
metastasis via the lymphatic vessels in the suspensory ligament of the ovary
(infundibulopelvic ligament).
• Ovarian metastases occur in < 10 % of cases.
Presence of secondary malignancies: the coincidence of endometrial and ovarian
cancer is not rare.
Figure 2: Indocyanine dye.
Indocyanine dye also shows the pelvic lymph �ow of the corpus uteri.
Hematogenic spread
• particularly in lungs and liver (rare, late)
Tumor grading for endometrial cancer (only applies to type 1)
• G1: well-differentiated, well-formed glandular CA (adenocarcinoma); < 5 % solid
component
• G2: moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma; 6 to 50 % solid component

• G3: poorly differentiated, immature adenocarcinoma; > 50 % solid component

Stage classi��cation ((aass per FIGO//TNM))
De�nitive staging takes place post surgery, on the basis of histological testing of the
uterus, adnexa and lymph nodes.

Prognosis ((factors))
The generally early diagnosis is re�ected in the overall favorable 5-year survival
rate of 80 %. 3 out of 4 endometrial cancers are detected in stage I, where
prognosis is favorable.
Table 4: Relative frequency of tumor stages and prognosis
Stage Relative frequency ooff this 55--year survival rate for this
tumor stage for new detection tumor stage
Stage I 75 % of patients 85 %
Stage II 10 % of patients 75 %
Stage III 10 % of patients 55 %
Stage IV < 5 % of patients 20 %
Morphological risk factors
The histological subtype (type I/type II) and molecular properties of the tumor are
important factors for the prognosis.
The presence of lymph nodes metastases, tumor stage, grading and
histopathological tumor type are established morphological parameters for
prognosis.
If there is evidence of lymphovascular invasion (LVSI), the risk of lymph node
metastases is signi�cantly higher. However, without the presence of lymph node
metastasis, LVSI is a negative prognosis factor that impacts adjuvant therapy
planning.
Age and comorbidities are also prognostic factors that cannot be in�uenced and
that should be taken into account when planning therapy.
There is no consensus concerning the prognostic value of positive peritoneal
cytology (wash cytology).

Type 2 endometrial cancers have a poorer prognosis - they are biologically more
aggressive, and often undifferentiated.

Treatment
Surgical treatment is the treatment of choice for endometrial cancer.
However, the risks of anesthetic must be evaluated and discussed in good time:
Older patient population → concomitant factors such as obesity, high blood
pressure, diabetes mellitus (“risk triad”) must be taken into account.
Treating atypical endometrial hyperplasia
Conservative therapy for endometrial hyperplasia with atypia: e.g. 100 mg
medroxyprogesterone acetate per day or megestrol acetate per day for approx. 3
months.
For women who do not wish to have children, postmenopausal women and young
women who do not show remission on follow-up curettage, a hysterectomy should
be carried out even in case of atypical hyperplasia (postmenopausal: an invasive
cancer is found in 40 % of cases on examination of the uterus).
Treating endometrial cancer
Surgery
• First, peritoneal cytology/wash cytology is carried out. Positive cytology is
documented but the stage is not changed. There is no consensus on the
prognostic value of the test.
• The uterus and bilateral uterine appendages (fallopian tubes and ovaries) are
removed = bilateral adnexectomy = bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (also
possible laparoscopically at early stages and with a small uterus).
• Depending on stage: pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenectomy versus sentinel
node biopsy
• Depending on stage: vaginal cuff removal.
• Depending on stage: adjuvant therapy (radiotherapy and chemotherapy)

In case of high risk of metastasis: Pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenectomy (always
for grade G3 and from FIGO stage Ib).
The guidelines for the treatment of endometrial cancer anticipate pelvic and para-
aortic lymphadenectomy. However, sentinel node lymphadenectomy is gaining
traction in clinical practice, and is included as an acceptable staging method in some
international guidelines.

This recommendation is based on the observation that an incidence of lymph node
metastasis of 3 to 15 % for para-aortic lymph nodes and 8 to 19 % for pelvic lymph
nodes is reported even at stage I. In stage II, 16 to 30 % of patients have para-aortic
lymph node involvement and 29 to 40 % pelvic lymph node involvement. Systematic
lymphadenectomy is therefore currently only dispensed with in cases of
endometrioid histotype (grade 1 and 2), myometrial invasion < 50 % and - where
ascertainable - a primary tumor diameter of < 2 cm, or with an endometrioid
histotype without myometrial invasion (then regardless of primary tumor diameter
and grading).
The recommendation to dispense with lymphadenectomy in cases of early-stage
low-risk endometrial cancer is based in part on the work of Eltabbakh and Mariani. In
Eltabbakh et al’s study of 303 patients with grading 1-2 and endometrial invasion <
50 %, a relapse-free 5-year survival rate of 98,9 % and a relapse-free 20-year survival
rate of 96,7 % could be achieved with surgical treatment without lymphadenectomy
but with subsequent vaginal brachytherapy [B04, P05].
The recommendation for para-aortic lymphadenectomy in cases of high-risk
endometrial cancer is based in part on a study by Mariani et al, where treatment with
para-aortic lymphadenectomy was compared with that without para-aortic
lymphadenectomy in 131 patients. Here, an increase in relapse-free survival from 62
% to 77 % with para-aortic lymph node removal was shown for the whole cohort.
However, these results were not statistically signi�cant (p = 0,12). This situation was
different for the subgroup of patients with lymph node involvement. Here, in 51
patients, there was a highly signi�cant increase in relapse-free 5 year survival from
36 % to 76 % if the para-aortic lymph nodes were also removed (p = 0,02) [B04, B05].
Various studies have investigated the possibility of sentinel node biopsy for
endometrial cancer. Sentinel node biopsy has been introduced as an alternative to
complete lymph node dissection for lymph node staging. When carried out correctly,
it appears suitable for con�rming pN0. Several studies, including prospective cohort
studies, have shown the high sensitivity of the sentinel approach for lymph node
staging in patients with early stages of endometrial cancer (clinical stage FIGO I).
Extensive pathologic processing and assessment of sentinel lymph nodes (lymph
node ultrastaging) contributes to the discovery of small metastases that could be
missed with traditional approaches. Sentinel lymph node biopsy without dissection
of other pelvic lymph nodes is associated with a lower risk of postoperative
morbidity, in particular postoperative lymphedema. Several prospective cohort

While malignancy was previously largely a contraindication for the use of minimally
invasive surgery, this approach now has a well-established role in the treatment of
endometrial cancer. The low rate of complications and rapid post-operative
mobilization of - often comorbid - patients support this procedure [B07]. Robot-
assisted surgical techniques mean that it is now possible to use minimally invasive
approaches even for di�cult techniques. Long-term results on the oncological
equivalent effectiveness of minimally invasive surgery are not yet available. However,
applicability data and historical data to date are extremely promising [P10, P12, P13].
For type I => Surgical primary treatment takes place according to FIGO stage and
grading: [B07][P10][P12][P13]

For type II => In principle, surgical lymph node staging, in case of serous histology,
carcinosarcoma or undifferentiated carcinoma, peritoneal staging with omentectomy
and peritoneal biopsies.
Adjuvant therapy
Adjuvant radiotherapy
No radiotherapy:

• Stage IA G1/G2 with no other risk factors (e.g. p53mut, pronounced LVSI).
• Stages I/II with proven POLE mutation.
Indications for brachytherapy alone:
• Tumors in early stages with elevated risk of local recurrence: FIGO IA G3 /FIGO
IB without other risk factors, stage II without risk factors.
• Indications for pelvic teletherapy: Stages IB/II with risk factors, stages III/IV,
lymph node involvement, serous histopathology, p53mut.
Type II carcinoma
• Tumors with serous histopathology/p53mut should be treated with
brachytherapy in stage I and percutaneous radiotherapy of the pelvis from stage
II.
• If percutaneous radiotherapy and adjuvant chemotherapy is indicated,
combined radiochemotherapy (along the lines of the PORTEC-3 study) can be
considered.
Adjuvant (i.e. postsurgical) irradiation can reduce the local recurrence rate
(vaginal, pelvic). Survival is not impacted.
The conclusion of the ASTEC/EN.5 Study Group Study is that external radiotherapy
should no longer be used as routine treatment for moderate and high risk
endometrial cancer with the goal of increasing survival. However, in FIGO stages III
and IVa, it can be used in addition to chemotherapy to improve local follow-up [P08].
[P08]
• No adjuvant radiotherapy takes place in stages Ia (G1, G2) and Ib (G1).
• With high risk of vaginal recurrence: Vaginal brachytherapy for irradiation of the
vaginal cuff.
Risk factors in this regard are FIGO I and G3 or pT1b or clear-cell or serous or > FIGO
I.
• No more “routine” external irradiation!

Pelvic radiotherapy is also indicated for poorly differentiated carcinoma (G3), with
which no staging lymphadenectomy took place.
In the PORTEC 3 study, combined radiochemotherapy with cisplatin, combined with
adjuvant chemotherapy over four cycles (carboplatin/paclitaxel) showed no
signi�cant advantage in terms of overall survival versus radiotherapy alone. However,
the combination did show better progression-free survival in patients in FIGO stage
III. The optimal sequence and combination of radiotherapy and chemotherapy for
endometrial cancer has not yet been established [B10]. [B10]
Adjuvant cytostatic therapy
Indications for adjuvant chemotherapy:
• Stage III/IV, lymph node involvement, serous histopathology, p53mut.
The adjuvant use of cytostatic agents in situations at high risk of distant
metastasis * (e.g. several para-aortic lymph nodes involved or peritoneal
metastasis) also provides an advantage versus adjuvant radiotherapy alone in
terms of survival.
However, this increased survival means accepting signi�cant side effects (in a
mostly older patient cohort).
* overall in the same situation that initiates adjuvant radiotherapy
A Cochrane analysis in both 2011 and 2014 showed an advantage for adjuvant
platinum-based chemotherapy for both relapse-free and overall survival, regardless
of radiotherapy. In the advanced tumor stages III and IV, the increase in survival was
quanti�ed as approx. 25 % [P11, P14]. [P11][P14]
• Effective substance classes: Platinum salts, anthracyclines, taxanes.
• The combination of carboplatin and paclitaxel has been implemented as
adjuvant therapy in clinical practice.
• In addition, in cases of high-risk stage I carcinoma, adjuvant chemotherapy
should be considered (type 2 carcinoma, type 1: FIGO Ib G3).
• In advanced stages, chemotherapy appears to be more effective than
radiotherapy [P09].
[P09]

Palliative therapy
In case of recurrence, various factors must be taken into consideration for therapy
planning:
• Localization and spread
• Tumor properties (above all hormone receptor expression, MMR/MSI status)
• Previous therapies
• Age, general condition and patient’s personal preferences
Localized recurrence, for example at the vaginal cuff, can often be treated with
surgery and/or radiotherapy (this can often even be curative). Multifocal recurrences
and/or distant metastases generally require drug treatment.
Systemic therapy with gestagens,, ““gestagen therapy””
Well-differentiated endometrial cancer contains progesterone receptors and
estrogen receptors and often (in receptor-rich tumors in 70 % of cases) responds
to gestagen therapy.
Frequently used substances are medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA) (Depo-
Provera®), and megestrol acetate (DE: Megestat®). Some data are now available on
treatment with aromatase inhibitors, suggesting even better e�cacy in combination
with the CDK4/6 inhibitor palbociclib. The tumor must be hormone-receptor positive.
Indications: Metastatic endometrial cancer, inoperable tumors, tumor residue after
palliative surgery or palliative radiation.
Palliative chemotherapy
The same substances that are effective as adjuvant chemotherapy are effective
here.
Immunotherapy iinn palliative care

Immune checkpoint inhibitors are gaining importance in the treatment of advanced
endometrial cancer. Other than platinum-based chemotherapy, until recently there
were few effective substances, let alone a therapy standard. Tumors with mismatch
repair de�ciency (dMMR) or high microsatellite instability (MSI-high) often respond
well, sometimes for long periods, to immunotherapy. Treatment with the immune
checkpoint inhibitor pembrolizumab has thus now become the �rst-line treatment for
MSI-H/dMMR tumors. The substance dostarlimab has also shown a response rate
(dMMR, MSI-high) of almost 65 % in advanced stages, with at least stabilization in a
further 21 %. In 83 % of patients, there was a long-term response persisting after a
follow-up period of over two years.
As not all tumors present these properties (dMMR/MSI-high), studies have
investigated whether combination therapy can improve response with other tumor
properties. The Keynote-775 study is currently investigating a combination of these
two immunotherapeutics in this situation. Combination therapy with lenvatinib and
pembrolizumab has shown an improvement in overall survival of almost seven
months versus monochemotherapy. These results are encouraging and con�rm the
growing importance of immunotherapy for gynecological tumors.

Digression -- uterine sarcoma
In the case of a large uterus or rapid growth of suspected uterine �broids, the
rare uterine sarcoma must be considered as a DD.
Epidemiology:
• 3 % of malignant uterine tumors are sarcomas
Location:
• mostly in the corpus uteri.
Figure 4: Uterine sarcoma.
Three histopathological types
• Carcinosarcoma (mixed mesodermal müllerian tumor; most commonly uterine
sarcoma; average age 60 years)
• Leiomyosarcoma of the uterine wall
• Endometrial stromal sarcoma.
Clinical:
• Symptoms uncharacteristic in general. With rapid growth, pain can be a key
symptom due to tumor necrosis.
• Carcinosarcomas penetrate the uterine wall, and are aggressive and often
necrotic. Early metrorrhagia and pelvic/hematogenic metastasis are common.
• Leiomyosarcomas most often show a structure similar to �broids; it can be
di�cult to distinguish them from �broids during surgery and even histologically
(for leiomyosarcomas: core atypia and mitoses at high numbers).
Leiomyosarcomas present hematogenic metastasis. Clinically, bleeding occurs
in approx. 1/3 cases - however, leiomyosarcoma is more often an incidental
�nding in the course of histopathological processing after uterine surgery.
• Endometrial stromal sarcomas show early bleeding. Hematogenic and
lymphogenic metastasis.

Diagnosis:
• Sarcomas are of mesenchymal origin (myometrium, stroma) and are therefore
not always detected on curettage. De�nitive proof is therefore sometimes only
obtained from hysterectomy material. Accordingly, if there is clinical suspicion
of sarcoma, hysterectomy should be carried out under an oncological
perspective (above all avoiding tumor spreading due to morcellation).
Treatment:
• Treatment for uterine sarcoma takes the form of as complete as possible
surgical resection (hysterectomy, often combined with bilateral adnexectomy).
• Occasionally, radiotherapy is also used to reduce the risk of local recurrence.
In cases of carcinosarcoma, pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenectomy should also be
carried out [P01]. [P01]
In cases of uterine carcinosarcoma, the following surgical staging should take place:
• HE, bilateral adnexectomy, wash cytology and pelvic and para-aortic
lymphadenectomy (LNE)
For carcinosarcoma, the following applies:
• 30 % extrauterine manifestations.
• Radiation reduces the risk of local recurrence, but has no in�uence on overall
survival in case of metastasis.
• Cisplatin-ifosfamide-mesna vs. abdominal radiation (stage I-IV) shows a trend
for chemotherapy [P01, P02, P03, P04].
[P01][P02][P03][P04]
Carcinosarcoma response to monotherapy:
• Etoposide (Etopophos®, Etoposid®) → 7 %
• Doxorubicin (Adriblastin®, generic) → 10 %
• Cisplatin (Platinol®, generic) → 18 %
• Ifosfamide (Holoxan®) → 34 %
• Topotecan (Hycamtin®, generic) → 10 %
Prognosis:
• Overall, the prognosis is considerably worse than for endometrial cancer. Five-
year survival is 30 % (50 % in early stages, 20 % in advanced stages).

Gestational trophoblastic diseases
((GTD))
Author:: PPDD DDrr.. med.. Edward Wight
ehem. Chefarzt Gynäkologie und gyn. Onkologie (pensioniert seit März 2017), Universitätsfrauenklinik
Basel
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Dr méd. Manuela Undurraga, Prof. Dr. med. Patrick Petignat

Introduction and ddee��nition
Gestational trophoblastic diseases (GTD) comprises a heterogeneous group of
diseases originating from the trophoblastic epithelium of the placenta after "normal"
or "abnormal" fertilization. Therefore, this maternal disease does not develop from
maternal but from fetal tissue. The WHO distinguishes between benign (complete or
partial) hydatidiform moles which are limited to the uterine cavity and 5 malignant
forms of GTD: Malignant invasive mole, choriocarcinoma, atypical placental site
nodule (APSN), placental site trophoblastic tumor (PSTT), and epithelioid
trophoblastic tumors (ETT). The malignant forms of GTD are termed gestational
trophoblastic neoplasia (GTN). GTN’s may develop after different types of
antecedent pregnancies (normal pregnancy, miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy,
abortion), but particularly often after a hydatidiform mole. The clinical course (tumor
progression, myometrial invasion, and metastases) can vary signi�cantly; quickly
proliferating and metastasizing tumor types are highly sensitive to chemotherapy.
Consequently, even tumors in advanced and metastatic stages can often be cured
with chemotherapy.
Classi��cation ooff GTD
Benign GTD
• Complete hydatidiform mole
• Partial hydatidiform mole
• Other benign trophoblastic lesions are rare: exaggerated placental site (EPS),
placental site nodule (PSN) (hCG-testing for follow-up not needed)
Malignant GTD (GTN)
• Malignant invasive mole (chorioadenoma destruens)
• Choriocarcinoma
• Atypical placental site nodule (APSN)
• Placental site trophoblastic tumor (PSTT)
• Epithelioid trophoblastic tumor (ETT)

Hydatidiform moles
(= Molar Pregnancy)
Complete hydatidiform moles (CHM) differ signi�cantly from partial hydatidiform
moles (PHM) in terms of their cytogenetic, histological, clinical, and prognostic
characteristics.
Epidemiology and risk factors
There are major geographic differences in the incidence of hydatidiform moles:
• Europe, North America: 0,5–1/1000 pregnancies
• Japan, China, South Korea: 2/1000 pregnancies
• Latin America, Africa: 0,5–4,5/1000 pregnancies (limited data!)
• South-East Asia (Indonesia, India): up to 12/1000 pregnancies (though recent
studies indicate lower incidence, correlating with an increase of the Gross
National Product = GNP)
The causes of this geographical frequency distribution are unknown. Dietary factors,
maternal age, ethnicity (Non-white Hispanic women, American Indians, Alaskan
Natives and Asian women have a higher incidence of hydatidiform moles compared
to Caucasian and Black women), poor social-economic conditions and infections are
being evaluated as possible reasons for this disparity.
Risk factors for developing hydatidiform moles are:
• Maternal age: < 16 years on the one hand, > 40 years on the other (relative risk:
5–10x); women above 40 are more likely to develop a malignant trophoblastic
tumor after evaquation of a hydatidiform mole
• Previous GTD (risk of relapse: 1 to 2 %)
• Ethnicity
• Infertility?
• Malnutrition?
• Nicotine abuse?

From a pathogenetic perspective, oncogenes (activation of oncogenes and
inactivation of tumor suppressor factors: c-myc, c-erbB2, bcl-2, Rb, EGFR, NLRP7,
KHDC3L, PD-L1, and metalloproteinases) seem to play a role in the development of
hydatidiform moles and/or malignant gestational trophoblastic disease.
In Europe, partial hydatidiform moles (PHM) are twice as frequent as complete
hydatidiform moles (CHM).

Clinical picture
Complete hydatidiform mole is usually diagnosed in the �rst trimester. This is earlier
than described in historical cohorts thanks to the increased use of ultrasound and
improved quality of beta-hCG tests.
Most of the classic symptoms used to describe complete hydatiform moles are
more frequent starting the 2nd trimester, which means that symptoms at the time of
diagnosis are less pronounced today:
• Vaginal bleeding
• Elevated beta-hCG values (> 100'000 U/L)
• Anemia
• Uterus much larger than expected from gestational age
• Adnexal cysts with a diameter of more than 5 cm (theca-lutein cysts, hCG-
related)
• Hyperemesis gravidarum
• Preeclampsia
• Hyperthyroidism

Vaginal bleeding is the most common symptom of complete hydatidiform moles.
Today, anemia occurs in only 5 % of cases (previously: > 50 %). Preeclampsia occurs
in < 2 % of patients (previously: approx. 15 %). Serum beta-hCG values above 100,000
U/L are measured in almost half of women with complete hydatidiform mole and are
an essential requirement for the development of theca-lutein cysts of the ovaries.
Though rare, these cysts may cause acute abdominal pain through torsion or cyst
rupture.
A partial hydatidiform mole is usually an incidental �nding today during histological
examination of curettage material after supposed incomplete or missed abortion, or
if fetal trisomy is suspected during routine �rst trimester ultrasound.
A size of uterus, larger than expected for the gestational age is rarely observed, and
beta-hCG values above 100’000 U/L occur only in 7 % of cases.
Diagnostics
• Quantitative determination of beta-hCG in serum
• Ultrasound, Doppler
• Histopathology, immunohistochemistry, �ow cytometry
The classical ultrasound �ndings considered characteristic of a complete
hydatidiform mole is a complex, hyperechogenic intracavitary mass with numerous
small cysts (corresponding with the hydropic villi). This is seen less and less
because of early diagnosis of complete mole.
Sonographic �ndings of partial hydatidiform moles are less characteristic. Therefore,
the diagnosis of partial hydatidiform mole is suspected less frequently by ultrasound
and established only after histological examination of tissue from miscarriage or
curettage.
A sonogram with cystic lesions in the placenta and an empty amniotic sac or signs
of missed abortion may be suggestive for a partial hydatidiform mole. The ratio of
the transversal compared to the anterior-posterior diameter of the gestational sac is
typically greater than 1.5 if a partial hydatidiform mole is present (illustration 3).

Figure 3: Partial hydatidiform mole, 13th week of gestation, beta-hCG > 1 x 106 IU/L.
Despite all sonographic criteria, the diagnosis of a hydatidiform mole is suspected
preoperatively in less than 50 % of all cases that are later con�rmed by the
subsequent histological examination of the aborted material.
The insu�cient diagnostic speci�city of ultrasound examinations is also important
in view of the potential of subsequent progression to GTN of hydatidiform moles.
This is especially the case if no routine histological examination of the trophoblastic/
placental material is performed after termination of pregnancy or normal birth,
respectively.
Therapy
Hydatidiform moles are treated by suction, preferably monitored under ultrasound
guidance. Curettage is avoided as much as possible to avoid scarring of the uterus
(Asherman Syndrome):
• Though rare during suction, embolization of trophoblastic material into the
circulation and the lungs via uterine veins can lead to respiratory insu�ciency.
This can happen particularly when oxytocin or other uterotonic medications
(prostaglandins) are used prior to the dilatation of the cervix (No medical
induction of labour!). Therefore, oxytocin should only be used after
intraoperative dilatation of the cervix.
• During evacuation of the uterine cavity by means of suction, the myometrium
should be stimulated with intravenous oxytocin to promote uterine contraction
to reduce blood loss and to decrease the risk of uterine perforation (especially
in cases of advanced complete hydatidiform mole and marked dilatation of the
uterus).
• Oxytocin should be continued postoperatively to reduce the likelihood of
hemorrhage.

• Rhesus negative women require rhesus prophylaxis! (Anti-D immunoglobulin,
e.g. Rhophylac® 300)
Women must be monitored closely after a complete or partial hydatidiform mole
because of the risk of progression to GTN. Ideally, women should be registered in
centralized centers to aide in their follow-up, for this has shown a decrease in
mortality and morbidity in GTD patients.
The following postoperative follow-up schedule has proven to be effective.
For partial hydatidiform moles:
• Serial, quantitative, biweekly beta-hCG tests should be obtained until the beta-
hCG value is negative. The value of negative will depend on the laboratory and
is variable.
• Once beta-hCG tests become negative, two further beta-hCG tests at 2 weeks
interval should be performed. If the results stay negative, no further follow-up is
needed.
For complete hydatidiform moles:
• Serial, quantitative, biweekly beta-hCG tests should be obtained until the beta-
hCG value is negative.
• If beta-hCG values become negative within 8 weeks after curettage, further
beta-hCG tests in monthly intervals should be performed for 6 months.
• If beta-hCG values become negative later than 8 weeks after curettage, further
monthly beta-hCG tests should be done for 6 months after negativity had been
attained.
• If beta-hCG levels plateau or rise (examples: four or more beta-hCG values with
a plateau over at least three weeks (days 1, 7, 14, and 21), persistent beta-hCG
values more than six months after removing a molar pregnancy, or a renewed
increase of beta-hCG values by at least 10 % for three or more tests over at
least two weeks (days 1, 7, 14)), the patient has to be considered to have
malignant disease (GTN), metastatic lesions need to be excluded. Additional
therapy is indicated and can be systemic chemotherapy or further surgery,
depending on individual �ndings.
• During follow-up a further pregnancy must be avoided with effective
contraception (preferred oestro-progestative) to ensure reliable assessment of
beta-hCG testing.

The European Organization for the Treatment of Trophoblastic Diseases (EOTTD)
discourages prophylactic chemotherapy.
Pregnancy after hydatidiform mole
Patients after molar pregnancies can expect similar reproductive outcomes as
compared to the general population. In a Swedish study a history of a hydatidiform
mole was associated with an increased risk of preterm delivery in women exposed to
a molar pregnancy prior to the index birth. The risk of repeat mole was 0,4 %.
Coexisting normal pregnancy with mole
Molar pregnancy rarely coexists with normal pregnancy. Although there is a high risk
of spontaneous abortion, about 40 to 60 % result in live births. Most of these births
will be extreme pre-term, as one of the most frequent complications is pre-
eclampsia. The risk of progression to GTN in coexisting molar and normal pregnancy
is increased compared to singleton molar pregnancy (27 to 46 % compared to 15 to
20 %, respectively).
Risk ooff recurrence
Women who had experienced an initial molar pregnancy have an increased risk of
recurrence of about 1 %, compared to the normal population (10 times elevated).
This risk is associated with complete molar pregnancy rather than partial molar
pregnancy. The risk of a third hydatidiform mole is associated almost exclusively
with previous complete molar pregnancies and particularly with the rare condition of
“familial recurrent hydatidiform moles (FRHM).

Malignant forms ooff GTD ((GTN))
Invasive mole (Chorioadenoma destruens)
• Histologically characterized by the myometrial invasion of hydropic, proliferative
chorionic villi.
• Locally destructive proliferation. Typical consequences: uterine perforation,
bleeding, infections.
Choriocarcinoma
• Histologically purely epithelial tumor consisting of neoplastic cytotrophoblastic
and syncytiotrophoblastic components without chorionic villi.
• Highly malignant! Initial clinical manifestations are typically caused by
hematogenic metastatic disease (predominantly to the lungs in 80 % of cases;
to the vagina in 30 %, to the liver and the brain in 10 % of cases), which can
become extensive during the course of the disease. Intraabdominal and
intracerebral bleeding, originating from metastases to the liver and to the brain,
can be life threatening.
Atypical placental site nodule (APSN), placental site trophoblastic tumor (PSTT),
epithelioid trophoblastic tumor (ETT)
• Precursor lesion of PSTT or ETT (see below), hCG levels not elevated at
presentation or during follow-up; presence of APSN should indicate a thorough
clinical and radiologic investigation and follow-up
• Very rare (approx. 0,2 % of all GTNs)! Histologically characterized by a
proliferation of intermediate trophoblastic cells and a lack of chorionic villi, and
to a large extent also a lack of syncytiotrophoblastic cells, resulting in low
serum-hCG values in these tumors.
• Typically, the tumor-inducing pregnancy dates back several years before clinical
symptoms appear at the former site of placental implantation (PSTT) or in the
area of the chorion leave (ETT). Unlike to the other GTNs, PSTTs and ETTs do
not respond well to chemotherapy, so their prognosis in advanced tumor stages
is comparatively less favorable.
GTNs occur:
• After hydatidiform mole
• After normal pregnancy
• After miscarriage, abortion, or ectopic pregnancy

Clinical Picture
Each of the following clinical situations may indicate a GTN:
• Plateau formation or renewed increase of beta-hCG values (Beta-hCG > 5 IU/L)
(see above)
• Persistent elevation of beta-hCG for 6 months after removing a hydatidiform
mole (Beta-hCG > 5 IU/L) (see above)
• Vaginal bleeding combined with high beta-hCG values after miscarriage,
abortion, ectopic pregnancy, or delivery at term!
• Metastases of an "unknown" primary tumor in premenopausal women!
Diagnostics
If a GTN is suspected, diagnosis and therapy must be initiated immediately because
symptoms often develop rapidly (at times dramatically fast). To decrease mortality
rates, treatment should be coordinated with GTD reference centers at the University
Hospital of Geneva (HUG) or at the University Hospital of Zurich (USZ), Women’s
Clinic, Department of Gynecology.
• Medical history
• Clinical examination
• Laboratory testing: hematology, coagulation, blood chemistry, beta-hCG
• Imaging: transvaginal ultrasound (color Doppler), thorax radiograph, and, in
case of suspected lung metastasis: Computed tomography (Chest-CT scan,
abdomino-pelvic CT scan), MR imaging of the brain (always indicated if lung
metastases are found). PET-CT may be indicated in special situations
(evaluating disease recurrence that may not be detected at conventional
imaging).
• Histological veri�cation of GTN is not necessary, i.e. therapy should be started
even without or prior to histologic diagnosis.
Staging and Scoring
GTN is both staged anatomically and scored (risk-based). This helps to localize the
tumor, as well as to de�ne the risk of treatment resistance and metastasis.

Therapy
• Invasive mole and choriocarcinoma respond very well to chemotherapy, even in
metastatic stages.
• The cure rate for "low risk GTNs" is almost 100 %.
• Surgery may be considered in some cases of low risk GTN in selected cases of
residual disease.
• "High risk" and "ultra-high risk GTNs" can often be cured with polychemotherapy,
occasionally in combination with radiotherapy and surgical intervention (e.g. for
emergency situations caused by bleeding of intra-cerebral or intra-abdominal
metastases)
• In case of chemoresistance immunotherapy with checkpoint inhibitors offers a
new form of salvage therapy
• A curative therapeutic approach must always be intended even in the case of
metastases at multiple sites.
• The choice of chemotherapy regimen is based on tumor staging, GTN risk
pro�le (according to FIGO risk score 2000), and in consideration of any previous
therapies.

In general, primary monochemotherapy is used for low risk GTN (FIGO ≤ 6):
Methotrexate (MTX) with/without folic acid rescue (Leucovorin®) (depending on the
schema), or dactinomycin = actinomycin D (Cosmegen®). In case of tumor
persistence or relapse, salvage chemotherapies, usually polychemotherapies are
recommended.
Figure 5: High risk GTN and ultra-high risk GTN (modi�ed by [8]). [08]

High risk GTN (FIGO 7-12) and ultra-high risk GTN (FIGO > 12) are primarily treated
with polychemotherapy (e.g., EMA-CO, EMA-EP, see below). Additional
polychemotherapies including carboplatin, taxanes, and/or ifosfamide and recently
an immunotherapy with checkpoint inhibitors are indicated as salvage therapies.
PSTT (placental site trophoblastic tumor) and ETT (epithelioid trophoblastic tumor)
show only a limited response to chemotherapy. Therefore, the tumor should be
surgically resected (with hysterectomy or – in early stages and if family planning is
not completed – with uterus-preserving surgery), often combined with an adjuvant
systemic therapy (chemotherapy, Immunotherapy).

Other chemotherapy regimens

• BEP: Bleomycin, Etoposid (Vepesid®), Cisplatin (Platinol®)
• ICE: Ifosfamide (Holoxan®), Carboplatin (Paraplatin®), Etoposide (Vepesid®)
• MAC: MTX, Dactinomycin = Actinomycin D (Cosmegen®), Cyclophosphamide
(Endoxan®), folic acid rescue (Leucovorin®)
• PC: Paclitaxel (Taxol®) (175 mg/m2), Carboplatin (6 AUC) every 3 weeks
• PVB: Cisplatin (Platinol®), Vinblastine (Velbe®), Bleomycin (e.g., Bleomycin
Baxter®)
• TIP: Paclitaxel (Taxol®), Ifosfamide (Holoxan®), Cisplatin (Platinol®)
• TP/TE: Paclitaxel (Taxol®), Etoposide (Vepesid®), Cisplatin (Platinol®)
• VIP: Etoposide (Vepesid®), Ifosfamide (Holoxan®), Cisplatin (= Platinol®)
In case of very advanced GTN, the EMA-CO regimen can lead to severe bleeding and
organ failure. Therefore, careful induction therapy with low-dose etoposide
(Vepesid®) (100 mg/m2) combined with cisplatin (Platinol®) (20 mg/m2) may be
indicated on days 1 and 2, repeated weekly, 3 times. To reduce duration and severity
of chemotherapy-induced neutropenia of various chemotherapy regimens the
administration of �lgrastim or peg�lgrastim (Neulasta®) may be indicated.
Malignant gestational trophoblastic disease is rare in Europe. Therefore, the
chemotherapies described, especially the salvage therapies, should only be
performed in specialized institutions or after consulting with experienced
interdisciplinary teams.
Recent Developments iinn the Systemic Therapy ooff GTN
• Immunotherapy: The activity of the immune system is regulated by
checkpoints. An important role of immune checkpoints is the prevention of
immunological autoaggression, resulting in autoimmune diseases. The PD-1
receptor is such a checkpoint, which, binding to his ligand (PD-L1), leads to a
blockage/inactivation of cytotoxic cells. Tumors expressing PD-L1 (like all
histological subtypes of gestational trophoblastic neoplasia) can weaken or
deactivate immunological tumor defense through this mechanism. Checkpoint
inhibitors are monoclonal antibodies directed to either the PD-1-receptor or to
PD-L1 and consequently prevent binding and interaction of PD-1-receptor with
its ligand. As a consequence, immunological tumor defense mechanisms are
enhanced, which permits to anticipate therapeutic success using checkpoints
inhibitors in gestational trophoblastic disease, as these tumors represent a
semi- or fully allogeneic transplant. Case reports and small studies have
documented long lasting remissions using checkpoint inhibitors (e.g. anti-PD-1-

receptor antibody pembrolizumab = Keytruda®) as a salvage therapy in
chemotherapy resistant, recurrent and high risk GTN. Side effects of checkpoint
inhibitors include (as expected) various autoimmune diseases.
• Targeted therapy: Unlike PSTT, choriocarcinoma is characterized by a high
concentration of metalloproteinases. Studies are being conducted to test
whether metalloproteinase inhibitors could be a therapeutic option for
chemotherapy-resistant choriocarcinoma. In contrast, PSTT, which typically
responds poorly to chemotherapy, strongly expresses VEGF (vascular
endothelial growth factor) and angiopoietin 1 and 2. Therefore, therapeutic use
of VEGF inhibitors like bevacizumab (Avastin®) with or without anti-endoglin
antibodies are being investigated. Another promising therapeutic option for
chemotherapy-resistant GTN may be the monoclonal antibody pembrolizumab
(Keytruda®), which acts on programmed cell death ligand 1 (PD-L1), which is
strongly expressed in GTN. The mechanism of action probably utilizes the
activation of immunocompetent cells (T cells, NK cells).
Surgical Therapy ooff GTN
Indications for hysterectomy
• In early stages of GTN and for women who completed their family planning,
prior to starting chemotherapy in order to prevent the development of
chemotherapy-resistant (intrauterine) tumors
• Primary therapy for PSTT and ETT in stages 1 and 2
• Secondary therapy (after chemotherapy) for chemotherapy-resistant uterine
tumors
• In case of uncontrollable vaginal bleeding or intra-abdominal bleeding from the
uterus
• Patients above the age of 40 are more likely to develop GTN after removal of a
complete hydatidiform mole (40+ years: 53 %, 50+ years: 60 %). For this reason,
hysterectomy may be indicated in case of completed family planning.
During hysterectomy, the uterus should be manipulated as little as possible to
prevent embolization of trophoblastic material into the circulation and the lungs via
uterine veins that can lead to respiratory insu�ciency.
Uterus-preserving surgery
• This can be evaluated in early stage, low risk PSTT and ETT (restricted to the
uterus) in young women with strong desire to preserve fertility.

Surgery in case of complications (usually bleeding) caused by metastases
• Craniotomy for intra-cerebral metastases
• Laparotomy for liver metastases
• Thoracotomy, segmental lung resection for lung metastases
• Extirpation of vaginal metastases
Radiotherapy ooff GTN
In case of brain metastases (combined with MTX). Whole brain radiation is
controversial because of associated long-term toxicity and unclear evidence whether
it improves survival. Possible alternatives are stereotactic radiotherapy or the use of
the Gamma Knife.
Follow--uupp after GTN
Follow-up after completed chemotherapy with beta-hCG tests has to be implemented
depending on the risk situation. Note that GTN lesions may secrete fragmented hCG
molecules. Therefore, hCG test systems have to be used which can recognize hCG
fragments and metabolites (for example: Siemens IMMULITE ®).
After successful treatment of GTN, the risk of relapse is about 3 % and most occur in
the �rst year of follow-up. After completion of chemotherapy (therapeutic and
consolidation courses) beta-hCG levels should be monitored weekly for 6 weeks and
then in bi-weekly intervals for up to 6 months. Afterwards the patients require long-
term follow-up by means of monthly beta-hCG testing (after monochemotherapy:
lasting for 12 months after beta-hCG-levels returned to normal (< 5 IU/L); after
polychemotherapy: lasting for 18-24 months after beta-hCG levels returned to
normal), so that relapse can be promptly diagnosed. In pronounced ultra-high risk
situations follow-up should take place in specialized centers, can be indicated for
several years and may continue for life.
During the follow-up period a reliable form of hormonal contraception is mandatory
and excessive UV light exposure should be avoided for 1 year to prevent UV-induced
skin toxicity after chemotherapy.
Radiological follow-up of previously known metastatic sites should be performed
after normalization of beta-hCG values as baseline for future imaging
Despite the generally favorable diagnosis of GTN, patients occasionally suffer from
mental distress after therapy, which require psycho-oncological counseling and care.
Differential diagnosis ooff persistent,, slightly elevated hCG--values during
follow--uupp

• Dormant GTN: If no metastases are observed and beta-hCG is present in a non-
hyperglycosylated form, an expectative approach may be used with continued
beta-hCG testing. Additional courses of chemotherapy are only indicated if
beta-hCG values increase.
• Phantom hCG: Caused by circulating heterophilic anti-hCG antibodies. The
diagnosis can be made if hCG can be found in serum samples but not in the
urine.
• Cross-reactivity: Cross reactivity of the beta-hCG test with LH. This typically
occurs when chemotherapy affects ovarian function. Treatment with oral
contraceptives suppresses LH and avoids the false positive test results for
beta-hCG caused by LH.
• Pituitary hCG: In peri- and postmenopausal women, small amounts of pituitary
hCG may be released as part of a negative feedback mechanism due to
reduced ovarian estrogen production. Estrogen substitution or oral
contraceptives allow in these cases the differential diagnosis towards a GTN-
related hCG-increase.

Introduction
The two most common benign proliferative diseases in women of child-bearing age
are endometriosis and �broids.

DDee��nition
Physiologically, the endometrium, which comprises glands and the stroma, occurs
only in the uterine cavity.
Endometriosis is the presence of endometrial tissue (stroma and epithelial cells)
outside the uterus.
This is technically ectopic tissue (heterotopia).
• Endometriosis is most commonly found in the pelvis minor (= pelvic
endometriosis).
• However, endometriotic lesions can occur in almost any location in the body:
Illustration 1
From pathogenetic and clinical/therapeutic perspectives, a distinction is made
between three entities:
• peritoneal endometriosis
• ovarian endometriosis (endometrioma)
• deep endometriosis
The presence of endometrial glands and stroma in the myometrium combined with
hypertrophy of the surrounding myometrium is de�ned as adenomyosis (previously
called endometriosis interna). A distinction is made between three distinct forms of
adenomyosis:
• Diffuse adenomyosis: lesions are distributed across the myometrium
• Focal adenomyosis: lesions form a cluster (= adenomyoma)

• Cystic adenomyosis: the lesion is encapsulated. Juvenile cystic adenomyosis,
which can also occur in young women (< 20 years) is a speci�c form of this.

Epidemiology
• The peak age of diagnosis is before 30 years.
• Strong familial incidence (7x greater risk with �rst-degree relative).
Endometriosis concerns a speci�c age group:
• at least 10 % of women of child-bearing age are affected!
As a diagnosis can only be con�rmed using invasive means (diagnostic laparoscopy
and/or biopsy), prevalence data are generally imprecise. There is a high prevalence
of endometriosis in female patients with infertility (20 to 50 %) and female patients
with chronic lower abdominal pain (40 to 60 %).

Clinical morphology
While endometriosis is a benign condition, it often grows like an invasive tumor,
in�ltrating and destroying tissue and organs.
Endometriotic lesions respond to the cyclical in�uence of estrogen and progesterone
like eutopic endometrium, with proliferative and secretory phases. As a result of
localized stimulation, interaction with macrophages, leukocytes and other cells in the
peritoneal �uid leads to the release of in�ammatory substances. The ability of
endometrial tissue to implant and invade provides for its spread with organ-
distorting growth.
Hormonal stimuli have a direct in�uence on endometriotic tissue.
1. Cyclical proliferation and edema of endometriotic lesions -> volume increase
and in�ammatory reaction -> pain
2. Desquamation within a closed compartment (endometrioma) -> blood
accumulation -> various symptoms (which may be chronic due to mass effect/
capsule tension or acute due to rupture).
3. Desquamation in a hollow organ (rare) -> blood loss visible externally (cyclical
intestinal bleeding/bladder bleeding/hemoptysis).

The appearance of endometriotic lesions varies greatly and depends on the organ
involved.

Clinical picture
The presentation of endometriosis varies greatly and is often cyclical on a monthly
basis. Most symptomatic patients experience the cardinal symptoms of
dysmenorrhea (95 %) and dyspareunia (64 %), although many also experience
chronic pain (85 %) and fatigue (87 %). Dyschezia (64 %) and dysuria (31 %) can also
occur depending on the location of the endometriotic lesion.
A third of women with endometriosis are asymptomatic. Here, a diagnosis is often
made during a laparoscopy to investigate infertility. Endometriosis should
immediately be considered as a diagnosis for women who report symptoms such as
dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia and chronic recurrent lower abdominal pain.
Symptoms suggesting endometriosis
• Dysmenorrhea
• Chronic recurrent lower abdominal pain
• Menstruation disorders (hypermenorrhea, menorrhagia)
• Defecation disorders (dyschezia)
• Dyspareunia (painful intercourse)
• Infertility
• Back pain
• Dysuria (hematuria)
Any symptoms that return with menstruation or are exacerbated by menstruation -
regardless of their location or manifestation - may suggest endometriosis.
• The topographical situation generally characterizes the type of manifestation.
• Pain symptoms vary considerably. They are susceptible to impact quality of life
(including work and sexuality) on an individual basis [B05].
• In the interval between two menstrual periods, endometriosis is clinically less
active or even asymptomatic.
• Any form of amenorrhea, including pregnancy, often provides patients with
endometriosis with an asymptomatic period of time - although symptoms often
return as before after the patient gives birth.
• After menopause, the lesions transform into inactive scars due to the decrease
in ovarian estrogens: “spontaneous recovery tendency/burnout”.
• Autoimmune processes seem to modulate progression of the condition

(persistence and activity varies on an individual basis).
[B05]
Consider endometriosis! Even when symptoms appear internal or neurological. Most
common presentations of endometriosis: Lower abdominal pain and menstrual
abnormalities. Endometriosis can also be a factor in infertility.

Diagnosis
A diagnosis of endometriosis must be made as early as possible and can only be
con�rmed with a laparoscopy and/or biopsy. However, the treatment of
endometriosis has been revolutionized in recent years. Until a few years ago,
patients with pain and clinically suspected endometriosis were encouraged to
undergo a laparoscopy to con�rm the diagnosis. In line with most guidelines and the
current international consensus, today these patients are �rst given empirical drug
treatment. A diagnostic/therapeutic laparoscopy is then indicated in cases of lack of
response or insu�cient success. [B05][B06]
Steps towards diagnosis
Obligatory basic clinical examination
Illustration 5: Endometriosis in the posterior fornix.
• The history provides the reason to suspect endometriosis: family history,
infertility, cyclical lower abdominal pain (rarely arbitrary, unspeci�c lower
abdominal pain), dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, bleeding disorders.
• Careful inspection of the vulva, vagina, ectocervix and any surgery scars.
• Typical palpation �ndings on bimanual rectovaginal examination.
Palpation �ndings: may include displaced/�xed uterus / hardness in the rectouterine
pouch / adnexal mass / enlarged and in some cases hard uterus (typical of
advanced adenomyosis) / dilated rectovaginal septum with rectal �ndings.
Typical additional investigations

• Technically, the biopsy material histopathology must show both glands and
stroma.
Classi��cations
As endometriosis is a multifaceted condition, there is currently no satisfactory
classi�cation that takes into account all aspects of the disease. Nevertheless,
individual classi�cations have prevailed in everyday practice.
rASRM The American Society for Reproductive Medicine’s revised endometriosis
classi�cation. The presence of endometriosis is quanti�ed using a points system.
ENZIAN classi�cation. Used for deep endometriosis. Like the TNM system, the
letters used provide information on the location of the endometriosis. Deep lesions
are those which present invasion of 5 mm or more.
EFI. The Endometriosis Fertility Index, developed by Adamson and colleagues in
2010, is the only validated score that can be used to predict the probability of a
patient with endometriosis becoming pregnant. [B10]

Differential diagnosis
• Tumor, diverticulitis, adhesions, appendicitis, psychosomatic.
• Adnexitis, PID, ectopic pregnancy.
• Organ torsion (large ovarian cyst, result of malignancy, etc.)

Complications
• Common: Infertility; Recurrence after treatment
• Rare: Malignant degeneration (2 %)
• Further complications include (all rare): Hydroureter or hydronephrosis;
Pneumothorax or hemothorax; Anemia; Ileus; Neurological failures.

Treatment
Endometriosis is a chronic disease that requires lifelong care. An individual
treatment plan must be established according to the patient’s individual situation,
age, desire to have children and pain symptoms. Treatment objectives must be
decided with the patient during pre-treatment re�ection and consultations.
Underlying factors
• Psychological strain on the patient
• Patient age
• Location, level of activity (type of growth)
• Desire to have children, family planning
Speci�c circumstances
• Asymptomatic patient, emergency patient or “typical” patient?
• Infertility patient with symptoms?
• Asymptomatic infertility patient?
• Symptoms but (currently?) no desire to have children?
Quanti�able treatment objectives
• Combating symptoms
• Elimination of endometriosis/its complications
• Treatment of infertility
• Acute management in emergency cases
Unfortunately, all forms of treatment have a recurrence rate of 20 to 50 %. Pre-
treatment re�ection and consultations are therefore of the greatest importance!
‘‘Wait and see’’ approach
Indication: Incidental �nding during laparoscopy for other reasons; Low level of
symptoms; Scarce �ndings; No fertility issues.
Symptomatic drug treatment
• Analgesics: (NSAIDs such as mefenamic acid [Ponstan® etc.]): NSAIDs inhibit
cyclooxygenase and thus reduce prostaglandin-mediated dysmenorrhea and
in�ammation-related pain in general [B11].
• Ovulation inhibitors: Ovulation inhibitors reduce cyclical hormone �uctuations
and the formation of the endometrium, thus reducing pain associated with

menstruation. In addition, continuous administration of an ovulation inhibitor
can lead to amenorrhea. Many patients have no symptoms under this
treatment. Unfortunately, ovulation inhibitors do not slow the progression of
endometriosis for all patients [B12].
[B11][B12]
Hormonal drug treatment
The progression of many forms of endometriosis is slowed and mitigated for the
duration of treatment. Good pain relief is usually achieved.
However, symptoms often return after treatment is stopped.
Hormonal treatment works on the principle of estrogen deprivation (no estrogen in
the bloodstream means endometriosis cannot grow)! But:
• costs are often high
• little effect on endometrioma and deep endometriosis
The range of drugs (and pharmaceutical groups) available means it is possible to
establish customized treatment depending on side effects and subjective tolerability,
and taking into account the estrogen suppression effects actually achieved in each
speci�c case.
Table 3
GnRH analogues Antigonadotropins Progestogens
Either desensitization of Or direct inhibition of Or negative feedback on
pituitary FSH and LH pituitary gonadotropin the hypothalamic-
secretion (*) secretion pituitary-ovarian axis
Leading to drug-induced down-regulation of the ovaries (i.e. “switching off” of egg
production).
(*) GnRH analogues (11.25 mg leuprorelin [e.g. Eligar®, Leuprorelinr®] or 11.3
mg goserelin [e.g. Zoladex®]) are “superagonists” of the anterior lobe of the
pituitary gland: Due to their un-physiologically long stimulation, they eventually
cause the pituitary to reduce the secretion of FSH and LH - after a temporary
“�are”.

Interfering in the hormone/releasing factor balance in this way is not without its side
effects (hypoestrogenic, androgenic and mineralocorticoid).
When using GnRH analogues, induced “menopausal syndrome” (hot �ashes,
sweating, vaginal atrophy, etc.) in particular can be very disruptive or even
intolerable. The situation can be counteracted by an “add-back regimen” (adapted
hormone replacement therapy). Today, this often takes the form of tibolone (Livial®],
or low-dose transdermal estrogens (25 µg).
Tibolone [Livial®]: a synthetic steroid, the metabolites of which display mainly
estrogenic, but also progestogenic and androgenic effects.
GnRH antagonists can also be used in the treatment of endometriosis, although with
similar side effects.
Progestogens such as medroxyprogesterone acetate, dydrogesterone and dienogest
(the only progestogen speci�cally approved for the treatment of endometriosis) are
currently the �rst-line treatment. In prospective randomized studies, continuous oral
progestogen (2 mg dienogest per day) was equivalently effective as a GnRH
analogue at treating endometriosis-related pain, with dienogest being associated
with far fewer side effects. Long-term data show a permanent clinical effect
extending beyond administration. [B13]
New approaches to targeted treatments may be available in the future as our
understanding of the pathophysiology of endometriosis improves. Aromatase
inhibitors are also used to treat endometriosis, similarly to their use in breast cancer.
Other currently experimental approaches include estrogen receptor beta agonists,
angiogenesis inhibitors, antidiabetic drugs and immunomodulators.
Surgery
The surgical removal of lesions and affected parts of organs is required:
• in cases of highly organized forms of endometriosis
• at highly advanced stages of the disease
• for complications
• if de�nitive removal with high reliability is the objective
• where conservative treatment has been unsuccessful.

Typical indications for surgery: Large ovarian endometrioma; peritoneal
endometriosis with infertility.
Complex forms of endometriosis require a highly experienced surgeon and must
often be carried out in collaboration with other �elds in specialized centers. For
example, intestinal or bladder resection with or without ureter reimplantation may be
required. Most procedures can today be carried out laparoscopically. [B14][B15]
Illustration 7: Organ-conserving surgery = to
Illustration 8: Radical/ablative surgery
maintain fertility
(oophorectomy, hysterectomy, etc.)
Surgical procedures also allow biopsies to be taken, and treatment for any
secondary issues to be dealt with at the same time (e.g. adhesions).
Combination ooff surgical and hormonal treatment
• Preoperative hormonal treatment can reduce the vascularization and size of
endometriotic lesions ( reduced intraoperative blood loss). However, this drug-
based preparation for surgery requires an additional “time investment” (at least
3 months) and is expensive and not always effective.
• Long-term postoperative treatment and fertility measures are often indicated.
The likelihood of becoming pregnant is highest in the �rst year after surgery.
Multimodal pain therapy
Patients with endometriosis can experience reduced quality of life due to restrictions
affecting their work, social life and sexuality. It is therefore often necessary to treat
the pain itself in addition to actually treating the endometriosis (with drugs or
surgery). This is done by means of multimodal pain therapy comprising
physiotherapy, psychosomatics and pain therapy.

Les maladies infectieuses du tractus génital inférieur ont un spectre de pathogènes
typiques. Elles suivent un cours aigu, chronique ou infra-clinique. L’infection peut
passer inaperçue et peut, dans le cas de certains agents infectieux, conduire à un
état de portage asymptomatique. Certains agents infectieux peuvent quitter le
tractus génital inférieur, traverser le col et atteindre les organes génitaux internes.
Les organes suivants peuvent être touchés de façon isolée ou combinée :
• Vulvite (folliculite), bartholinite, colpite, cervicite, (urétrite)
• Endométrite, annexite (salpingo-ovarite), paramétrite

Ecosysteme dduu vagin ((��ore ddee Döderlein,,
��ore normal))
Certains germes ne déploient leur activité pathogène que lorsque l’écosystème
vaginal le permet. A l’âge adulte, sous l’in�uence des oestrogènes, les cellules
épithéliales vaginales emmagasinent beaucoup de glycogène. Les cellules
desquamées qui contiennent des dépôts de glycogène, constituent un substrat idéal
pour un processus de fermentation. Les « lactobacilles » (bacilles de Döderlein)
métabolisent ce glycogène dans le vagin, produisant du lactate.
L’acide lactique abaisse le pH vaginal jusqu’à des valeurs acides de 4.0-4.5. Seuls les
lactobacilles (bacilles de Döderlein) sont adaptées et survivent. Ils constituent la
�ore normale.
Les lactobaciles les plus importants de la �ore vaginale sont :
• L. crispatus
• L. gasseri
• L. iners
• L. jensenii
Cave : pas : L. acidophilus !
Fluor genitalis ((""Fluor""))
Le « �uor » génital, les leucorrhées ou les pertes désignent l’écoulement se formant
dans le vagin ; il résulte principalement d’une transsudation de l’épithélium vaginal. Il
est normal que ces pertes s’écoulent du vagin dans des proportions modérées (< 5
ml/24 h).
Les pertes se caractérisent par un pH 4.0–4.5 ; elles sont blanches, très �uides,
aigrelettes, non fétides, asymptomatiques.
En cas d’infections vaginales (ou des organes génitaux internes), la quantité des
écoulements peut augmenter; sa consistance et son odeur se modi�ent de façon
typique.
Le contenu acide des leucorrhées est in�uencé par les
• oestrogènes dont les taux �uctuent au cours de la vie
• perturbations iatrogènes de la �ore de Döderlein

pH vaginal physiologique: ≤4.5 (= contrecarre la croissance des germes
pathogènes).
Manque d’oestrogènes --> manque de glycogène dans l’épithélium vaginal -->
manque de substrat pour la fermentation bactérienne --> augmentation du pH -->
prolifération bactérienne.
Antibiothérapie à large spectre par voie systémique --> éradication des
lactobacilles (« effets secondaires ») --> fermentation diminuée --> augmentation
du pH --> prolifération bactérienne
Les infections du tractus vaginal surviennent souvent dans les situations suivantes
(exemples) :
• Une grossesse, une fausse couche et les règles diminuent les défenses du
tractus vaginal et favorisent l’infection.
• La baisse des oestrogènes à un âge avancé entraîne des modi�cations
trophiques et des sécrétions vaginales: avec l’âge, la muqueuse vaginale perd
sa fonction de protection mécanique (atrophie) et biochimique (diminution de
la quantité de lactate).
La prévalence des infections varie aussi chez les femmes dont l’écosystème est
intact. Certaines souches de lactobacilles peuvent produire du péroxyde d’hydrogène
(H202) impliqué dans la défense des germes pathogènes (inhibition) :
• les patientes dont la �ore vaginale contient des lactobacilles H202-négatif
montrent une tendance augmentée aux infections.

Infections dduu tractus genital inferieur
Généralité
• Les infections vulvaires et vaginales sont fréquentes dans la pratique
gynécologique quotidienne. Le plus souvent, il s’agit d’une infection banale,
rapidement guérissable.
• Les porteuses asymptomatiques doivent être reconnues le cas échéant (et
éventuellement traitées).
• D’une infection „débutante“ au niveau du tractus génital inférieur peut se
développer une infection gynécologique sévère, dépendant à la fois du germe
en cause et de la thérapie choisie (atteinte du tractus génital supérieur
respectivement des organes adjacents), et parfois même entraîner une
infection systémique.
• Plusieurs maladies infectieuses systémiques (syphilis par ex.), ainsi que
beaucoup de maladies in�ammatoires gynécologiques graves débutent dans le
tractus génital inférieur. On parle de maladies dites sexuellement
transmissibles (sexually transmitted disesases – STD). On distingue les
infections sexuellement transmissibles conformément au législateur et devant
être obligatoirement déclarées (par exemple syphilis et gonorrhée) des
infections sexuellement transmissibles ne devant pas l’être.
• D’un autre côté, on regroupe les maladies qui ne se manifestent pas
uniquement sexuellement mais qui, typiquement, sont transmises par voie
sexuelle lors d’une infection génitale (Candida, HPV; ces maladies
correspondent mieux au concept nouveau de « maladies sexuellement
associées »).
Vulvite
Une infection de la vulve et de l’entrée vaginale est appelée une vulvite. De manière
pratique on peut parler de vulvite dans le cadre
1. d’une affection primaire
2. d’une infection isolée de la vulve
3. d’une affection secondaire (c’est-à-dire résultant d’une infection des voies
supérieures, colpite ou cervicite)
4. d’une manifestation partielle lors d’une maladie dermatologique ou d’une
maladie systémique générale.

Une vulvite peut être d’origine endogène (dé�cit en oestrogène, diabète, allergie),
exogène (médicaments, agents nuisibles allergisants, germes infectieux) et aussi
résulter de facteurs „descendants“ (écoulement vaginal primaire ou écoulement
cervical, plus rarement incontinence urinaire ou �stule urinaire).
Les infections secondaires de la vulve résultent d’infections situées à un „étage“ plus
haut (par ex. vaginite) et sont transmises par les écoulements contaminés.

Vaginite//colpite
De nombreuses bactéries sont détectables dans le vagin, leur présence dépend de la
situation topographique (la peau, le périnée) mais aussi de la fonction du vagin (par
ex. la sexualité).
Les patientes avec une vaginite infectieuse souffrent dans
• 40-50% des cas de vaginose bactérienne
• 20-25% des cas de candidose vulvovaginale et
• 15-20% des cas de trichomonas.
En principe des germes pathogènes peuvent repousser peu à peu la �ore vaginale
protective normale et ainsi permettre l’établissement d’une colonie polymicrobienne.
Les causes possibles d’une colpite sont :
• Endogènes : dé�cit hormonal, diabète sucré, troubles d’origine psycho-sexuels,
grossesse
• Descendantes : cervicite avec écoulement, saignement menstruel, DIU en place
• Exogène : relations sexuelles, corps étranger, douche vaginale, origine
infectieuse ascendante, irritation mécanique
• Iatrogène : suite d’une thérapie antibiotique, contraceptif chimique
Clinique
Vu la variabilité du type de germe, les manifestations cliniques sont diverses. Les
leucorrhées sont caractéristiques, plus ou moins associées à un prurit, des brûlures
ou des douleurs. Dans certains cas, une urétrite est associée à des brûlures
mictionnelles. Des dyspareunies, une vulvite secondaire ou des saignements (plus
rare) peuvent également être observés.
Leucorrhées pathologiques ((Fluor vaginalis))
Les leucorrhées sont un symptôme fréquemment présent. Elles sont représentatives
pour des infections différentes. Déterminer leur origine peut apporter des
renseignements sur la cause de l’infection.

Cervicites
La cervicite est une in�ammation de l’épithélium simple cylindrique du col de l’utérus
et ses manifestations cliniques sont peu spéci�ques.
De nombreux facteurs peuvent in�uencer ou être à l’origine d’une cervicite :
• une infection primaire (par ex. chlamydiae, gonocoques, trichomomas, mycose,
herpes, HPV)
• une pathologie cervicale (par ex. érosion, déchirure du col, polype)
• une infection vaginale ascendante (p.ex. vaginose bactérienne)

• une origine psychogène (par ex. manque de libido, anorgasmie).
Germes
La plus-part des infections du col sont dues :
• chlamydia trachomatis
• herpès virus simplex (HSV) type II
• papilloma virus (HPV).
N. gonorrhoeae, bien que peu fréquent, provoque typiquement des cervicites.
Clinique
Les cervicites sont souvent paucisymptomatiques, elles se caractérisent par une
hyperhémie et une fragilisation de l’épithélium cervical (faibles saignements). Des
symptômes généraux sont rares.
