Infections dduu tractus genital superieur
Considérations générales
L’ascension des germes est la pathogenèse la plus importante des maladies
gynécologiques infectieuses dans le petit bassin (exception: tuberculose.) Les
germes ascendants traversent donc plusieurs barrières (par ex. le milieu acide
vaginal, le mucus du canal cervical). Ces barrières peuvent être affaiblies de manière
transitoire ou permanente sous l’in�uence de plusieurs facteurs locaux ou
systémiques.
La propagation de l’infection dans la cavité abdominale se fait via les pavillons
tubaires. Cette ascension des germes peut conduire à l’apparition d’une péritonite, à
des adhérences et même à une atteinte des organes abdominaux situés à distance.
A�n de décrire correctement une infection dans le petit bassin chez la femme, il est
nécessaire de préciser les organes concernés et si possible le stade et le germe en
cause :
• Endométrite, myométrite
• Salpingite
• Annexite (= salpingite + ovarite)
• Abcès tubo-ovarien
• Pelvi-péritonite
• Péritonite généralisée
• Syndrome de Fitz-Hugh-Curtis (péri-hépatite)
Endométrite
On désigne ainsi une infection de l’endomètre. Dans la plupart des cas, elle fait suite
à la propagation des germes suite à une cervicite (infection ascendante), plus
rarement à partir d’une salpingite/annexite (infection descendante) ou par
dissémination hématogène (par ex. dans le cas de la tuberculose).
On peut différencier plusieurs sous-groupes :
• les endométrites aigues
• les endométrites chroniques isolées
• Endométrite du postpartum
• �èvre puerpérale (état fébril du postpartum)
• les endométrites avec atteinte du myomètre

• le pyomètre (collection purulente intra-cavitaire secondaire à une sténose
cervicale).
Facteurs prédisposants :
• les avortements
• les accouchements
• les gestes intra-utérins (par ex. hystéroscopie, curettage fractionné)
• les polypes cervicaux
• Déchirure du col utérin
• Écoulement cervical
• les polypes endo-cavitaires
• les myomes sous-muqueux
• la présence d’un dispositif intra-utérin
Diagnostic
Il s’agit d’un diagnostic clinique.
Le diagnostic ne peut être con�rmé que par un examen histologique nécessitant un
curetage.
Une culture microbiologique peut parfois être d’une certaine utilité.
La température corporelle, les leucocytes, la CRP sont peut élevés.
Lors d’un pyomètre, nous observons :
• À l’inspection --> un écoulement cervical muco-purulent
• À la palpation --> un utérus tendu et élastique
• À l’échographie --> une accumulation de liquide (pus) dans l’utérus
Un sepsis puerpéral peut être diagnostiqué cliniquement mais aussi par des
examens de laboratoire.
Le diagnostic d’endométrite n’est �able que si un événement prédisposant est
décrit dans un délais proche et en rapport direct. Dans le cas échéant, et plus
particulièrement lors de saignements, un cancer du col utérin ou de l’utérus se
doit d’être exclu en procédant à un examen histologique (curetage)
(particulièrement chez les femmes de plus de 40 ans).
Thérapie

La thérapie est - la plus part du temps - conservatrice.
Thérapie avec des estrogènes --> Reconstruction de l’endomètre.
Retrais d’un stérilet
Un sepsis puerpéral nécessite un traitement médical intensif (soins intensifs).
Le traitement d’un pyomètre se fait en 2 temps : 1) Thérapie antibiotique à large
spectre 2) Dilatation du col cervical et drainage de la cavité utérine.
En raison du risque d’infection ascendante pouvant engendre une annexite, un
traitement antibiotique est indiqué.
Puisque dans 50% des cas, un pyomètre est associé à un cancer de l’utérus, un
curetage fractionné devrait être effectué a�n d’exclure un carcinome de l’endomètre.
Prophylaxie
Initier rapidement une thérapie lors de l’ascension d’un agent pathogène avec
salpingite et/ou annexite avec possible stérilité consécutive.
Veiller à l’éducation des enfants et des jeunes sur la sexualité, les maladies
sexuellement transmissibles et la santé sexuelle.
Veiller à une bonne récupération de l’utérus dans un temps opportun en postpartum.

Paramétrite

Il s’agit d’une infection extra-péritonéale des paramètres, en général dans le ligament
large et touchant l’ensemble des tissus conjonctifs du petit bassin, très souvent
associée à la formation d’un abcès.
Cette pathologie est rare mais sévère et est accompagnée d’un syndrome septique
(température élevée, signes infectieux importants) et de fortes douleurs pelviennes
profondes unilatérales. Le diagnostic de suspicion est obtenu par l’anamnèse, la
présence d’un facteur déclenchant (avortement, accouchement, geste intra-utérin) et
l’examen clinique.
Annexite
Il s’agit d’une infection des organes génitaux internes. En anglais, on la désigne par
„pelvic in�ammatory disease“ (PID).
Environ 10 femmes sur 1'000 sont touchées par une annexite, les nullipares deux
fois plus fréquemment que les femmes ayant eu au moins une grossesse. Elle est
causée principalement par une dissémination ascendante des germes, plus
rarement par une dissémination descendante ou en post-opératoire.
Infection ascendante
Les germes atteignent l’endomètre par le biais du col de l’utérus lors d’une cervicite
souvent asymptomatique. Par la suite, les germes peuvent gagner les trompes et les
ovaires, cependant l’infection n’est pas systématiquement bilatérale et son intensité
peut varier suivant le côté.
Infection descendante
À partir d’une appendicite, d’une péritonite ou d’une maladie intestinale.
Infection post--opératoire
Rarement suite à une intervention gynécologique ou chirurgicale.
Infection hématogène
par exemple dans le cadre d’une tuberculose.
Diagnostic dd’’une annexite

Examen clinique • défense pelvienne uni- ou bilatérale, empâtement des
annexes (en cas d’abcès: pyosalpinx, abcès tubo-ovarien,
abcès du cul de sac de Douglas)
• parfois contracture abdominale de tout le petit bassin (Cave
: pelvéo-peritonnite)
• douleurs à la mobilisation du col utérin
Examen de • NFS, VS, CRP, ß HCG, bandelette urinaire
laboratoire • Bilan de coagulation (Quick, TP, plaquettes)
Echographie • épanchement dans le cul de sac de Douglas
• épaississement tubaire (sactosalpinx)
• ovaires augmentés de taille ou mal délimitable
Prélèvement natif • Signes indicateurs : vaginose bactérienne, leucocytes,
érythrocytes, coques, peu de bactéries de Döderlein
• Frottis microbiologique cervical nécessaire dans certains
cas
Coelioscopie • différents signes possibles : hyperämie des organes
Laparoscopie génitaux internes, adhérence in�ammatoire (�brine), pyo-,
hydro- ou sactosalpinx, épanchement intra-péritonéal
• Les prélèvements tubaires per-opératoires sont conseillés
• Les prélèvements du cul de sac de douglas sont moins
probants.
L’infection se manifeste sous des formes très différentes présentant un spectre de
symptômes très large allant de signes très discrets jusqu’à des formes d’infections
mortelles.

Diagnostic
Le diagnostic est posé en fonction des antécédents médicaux, de l’examen clinique,
de l’imagerie (échographie), des données de laboratoires (marqueurs
in�ammatoires) et de la microbiologie.

Symptômes classiques
Fièvre
Tuméfaction douloureuse des annexes avec douleurs à la mobilisation (examen
bi-manuel)
Réaction in�ammatoire pathologique dans le sérum (CRP augmenté)
Thérapie
Une thérapie ambulatoire n’est possible que dans certains cas :
• La patiente refuse catégoriquement une hospitalisation (manque de
compliance)
• Une hospitalisation est impossible
• Cas d’annexite léger, pas de tumeur palpable respectivement pas de masse
visible à l’échographie, exclusion d’une appendicite
Les abcès du Douglas, des ovaires et des trompes doivent toujours être traités lors
d’une hospitalisation.
Mesures générales
• Repos complet / alitement (!)
• Poche à glace
• Antalgiques/spasmolytiques
• Thérapie avec glucocorticoïde : controversée dans le cas de tuberculose et
contre-indiquée lors de diabète.
• Lors de diabète mellitus --> surveillance, evlt adapation des glycémies
Thérapie médicamenteuse
• Antibiotique à large spectre
• En fonction des résultats à disposition, plus particulièrement de
l’antibiogramme, le traitement antibiotique devrait cibler la/les bactérie(s)
responsable(s) avec un dosage adéquat pour une durée su�sante mais limitée
• Un traitement antibiotique prolongé pour une durée de 3 semaines est indiqué
dans le cas d’une annexite chronique.
Thérapie chirurgicale

Lorsque les thérapies médicamenteuses et conservatrices ne montrent pas
d’amélioration, un traitement chirurgical est indiqué, par laparoscopie (pelviscopie)
ou même laparotomie.
Lors d’une opération, il faut prendre en compte l’âge de la patiente ainsi que son
désir d’enfant resp. sa situation familiale.
Une opération en cas d’annexite aigue est indiquée uniquement dans le cas d’une
péritonite purulente en dehors du petit bassin, plus particulièrement lors d’un
traitement conservateur insu�sant.

Infections sexuellement transmissibles
((IST))
Les infections sexuellement transmissibles (IST) sont toutes les maladies
infectieuses transmises par contact sexuel. Le terme MST (maladie sexuellement
transmissible) a été remplacé par celui d'IST (infection sexuellement transmissible),
car le terme « infection » plutôt que « maladie » prend mieux en compte le fait que
certaines infections sont asymptomatiques.
(autre terme plus ancien: maladie vénérienne)
(termes en anglais : Sexually Transmitted Diseases (STD) ou Sexually transmitted
Infections (STI)).
De nombreuses maladies et pathogènes sont concernés et peuvent être divisé en
plusieurs catégories: les infections sexuellement transmissibles classiques, les
autres infections sexuellement transmissibles, et les infections sexuellement
transmissibles avec atteinte d’autres organes.
Elles peuvent être causées par des bactéries, des virus, des champignons, des
protozoaires ou des parasites.
Classi��cation empirique des infections sexuellement transmissibles
Tableau 4 (EGONEbasic)
Classi��cation Pathologie oouu pathogène Voire llee
chapitre
IST typiques Syphilis --> G3B
IST typiques Gonorrhée --> G3B

IST typiques Chancre de Ducrey ou --> G3B

transmission sexuelle
Bien que les IST « classiques » (syphilis, gonorrhée, chancre de Ducrey ou lymphome
vénérien) soient importantes, elles jouent un rôle de plus en plus petit dans nos
régions en raison de leur faible incidence au pro�t d’autres infections gagnant en
importance, telles que l’hépatite B, l’infection du VIH et du SIDA, l’herpes génital, les
infections avec Chlamydia et HPV.
Les IST sont transmises lors de rapports non protégés, les risques existant lors des
rapports vaginaux, oraux et anaux. L’utilisation de mesure de protection
prophylactique (type préservatif) ne garantit pas totalement l’absence de risque de
contamination. Les enfants peuvent également être touchés par des IST => il faut
alors rechercher un abus sexuel.
Plusieurs facteurs de risques de transmission des IST chez la femme ont été
identi�és, quoique ces facteurs de risque re�ètent assez bien le vécu sexuel ainsi
que le degré d’éducation sexuelle :
• âge de 15 à 30 ans
• dépendance à l’alcool
• abus de drogue
• changement de relation/partenaire
• voyages
• antécédents d’IST

• départ de chez les parents
• IVG
• maladie psychiatrique
• Une anamnèse détaillée, y compris une anamnèse sexuelle, peut aider au
diagnostique.
Questions importantes ddee ll’’anamnèse aa��nn ddee diagnostiquer une
infection sexuellement transmissible..
Tableau 5 (EGONEbasic)
Quand étaient les dernières relations sexuelles ?
Quelles ont été les relations sexuelles des dernières semaines ?
Avec qui ? Homme/femmes ? Relation homosexuelle?
Qu’avez-vous exactement fait ?
Existe-t-il un partenaire stable ? (conjoint / partenaire �xe / connaissance de
passage / inconnu) ?
Etes-vous, ou votre partenaire, allé à l’étranger ?
Avec qui avez-vous eu des relations sexuelles ? (répéter cette question pour un
laps de temps de 3-24 mois)
Les premiers symptômes d’une infection sont les e�orescences au niveau génital
externe.

Examen dd’’infectiologie gynecologique
Le plus souvent une bonne anamnèse et une séquence simple de mesures
diagnostiques permettent l’identi�cation et la décision d’un traitement des agents
pathogènes.
En pratique, on procède d’un manière standardisée:

Anamnèse gynécologique (anamnèse générale, maladie d’accompagnement):
• promiscuité
• écoulement vaginal gênant (!); odeur dérangeante; Spotting, saignement de
contact
• prurit vulvaire
• dysurie
• douleurs (locales?, bas-abdomen ?)
• modi�cations cutanées, symptômes articulaires, symptômes oculaires
Examen clinique de la vulve, du vagin et du col - c’est-à-dire inspection externe et
examen au spéculum
• rougeur, chaleur
• tuméfaction (y compris tuméfaction des ganglions lymphatiques inguinaux)
• vésicule, ulcérations (DD : modi�cations tumorales)
• croûtes, dépôts
• suffusions, �ssure, lésions de grattage
• �uor: consistance, couleur, odeur, quantité
Complément d’examen
• recherche d’une infection ascendante (douleurs à la mobilisation du col,
douleurs à la palpation abdominale) ?
• état général ? atteinte cutanée du reste du corps ? arthrite ? conjonctivite ?
adénopathies ?
Laboratoire (écoulement prélevé lors de l’examen au spéculum)
• préparation native avec analyse au microscope par contraste de phases.
• préparation colorée (Gram, bleu de Méthylène, etc.)
• frottis spécial selon l’indication
• mesure du pH
• sniff-Test: du KOH 10% est ajouté au liquide vaginal
Autres examens spéci�ques selon l’indication.
Au moment de l’examen au spéculum, du liquide est prélevé dans le vagin et sur le
col. Il est ensuite étalé pour un examen microscopique, ainsi que pour une mesure
du pH.
Examen microscopique dd’’une préparation native

Le liquide vaginal est mélangé à 1 ml de NaCl 0.9 %. Deux gouttes sont étalées sur
un porte-objet qui sera examiné directement (sans colorant) au microscope à
contraste de phase. Remarque: plusieurs germes (par ex. gonocoques) ne se
laissent mettre en évidence qu’après une coloration !
Pour mémoire (préparation microscopique) : chez une femme sexuellement
mature, il est physiologique de voir des bactéries de Döderlein.
En cas de suspicion clinique, il importe de faire en même temps un prélèvement
cellulaire et de l’envoyer au laboratoire avec une question ciblée (par ex. recherche
de gonocoque en tant que diplocoques intracellulaires Gram negatifs).
Des méthodes supplémentaires simples comme le sniff-test lors de suspicion de
vaginose bactérienne ne sont pas chères, mais sont très utiles.
En peu de temps et à peu de frais, on peut diagnostiquer les infections vaginales
les plus répandues, telles que les mycoses (Candida), la trichomonase, les
infections mixtes, la vaginose bactérienne.
Pour les verrues vaginales d’origine virale (par ex. condylome acuminé lors d’HPV) et
herpès génital, le diagnostic est posé généralement à l’œil nu.
• Lors d’un doute sur l’existence éventuelle d’une affection herpétique, le virus
doit être recherché par des cultures spéci�ques.
Le recours à des moyens diagnostiques plus larges (tests immunologiques, cultures
spéci�ques, sérologie, PCR – Polymerase Chain Reaction) est traité dans un chapitre
dévolu spécialement aux maladies concernées [--> G3B].
Ces moyens diagnostiques sont souvent nécessaires pour reconnaître une infection
à :
• Chlamydia, une syphilis, une gonorrhée, un HIV, une infection à HPV
et bien sûr chaque fois que l’on se trouve devant un tableau infectieux
• peu clair
• suraigu
• récidivant ou résistant au traitement (« posant problème »)
• ascendant.

topic: Malformations of the female gential organs (Teil 1/6)
keywords: malformations of the female gential organs
content:
EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Malformations 〉 Malformations of the female genital organs
Last update: 2013/2024, Review 2024
Malformations ooff the female gential
organs
Author:: PPDD DDrr.. med.. Edward Wight
ehem. Chefarzt Gynäkologie und gyn. Onkologie (pensioniert seit März 2017), Universitätsfrauenklinik
Basel

Introduction
Disorders of female sexual development include the abnormal development of
the internal and external genitalia and disorders of gender identity.
Malformations of the female genital organs are part of impaired sexual
development. The latter can be subdivided into the following major groups:
• Gonadal dysgenesis
• Intersexuality
• Incomplete fusion of the Mullerian ducts
• Delayed or accelerated sexual development

Gonadal dysgenesis
There are various situations where the gonads are converted into connective tissue
strands without (functional) germ tissue (streak gonads). The chromosomal analysis
of affected individuals shows variable �ndings and the phenotype can be female,
hermaphrodite, or male. Major forms are:
Turner syndrome: The most common disorder of sexual development (1 out of 3000
newborn girls are affected). Turner syndrome is characterized by a 45 X karyotype,
streak gonads (--> primary ovarian insu�ciency --> delayed puberty --> primary
amenorrhea), sexual infantilism (= absence of the secondary gender characteristics,
thus no thelarche, pubarche or menarche), as well as a dysmorphic syndrome, but
normal intelligence.
More information about sex chromosomal aberrations on embryology.ch
Swyer syndrome (46XY gonadal dygenesis): Female phenotype, despite male
karyotype. Primary amenorrhea. The cryptorchid (undescended) gonads exhibit a
risk of degeneration into malignancy (gonadoblastoma) and must therefore be
removed prophylactically.

topic: Malformations of the female gential organs (Teil 2/6)
keywords: malformations of the female gential organs
content:
Intersexuality
Memory aid: In the embryonic period, the genitalia are undifferentiated. H-Y antigen
and testosterone in�uence the differentiation to a masculine phenotype. If these two
factors fail to have effect, embryonic genitals develop a female phenotype.
More information about genetic factors and hormones that determine sex on embryology.ch
In the event of intersexuality, either an initiated differentiation is aborted early, so
that hermaphroditism persists, or an earlier process of differentiation is transformed
into the other phenotype by a contrary development.
• True hermaphroditism (hermaphroditismus verus) is characterized by gonads
with both normal testicular and ovarian tissue present (ovotestes).
• In the event of pseudohermaphroditism, the phenotype of the genitals is
contrary to gonadal and chromosomal gender a�liation. A distinction is made
between male pseudohermaphroditism (XY, testicles, female phenotype) and
female pseudohermaphroditism (XX, ovary, male phenotype).
More information about Hermaphroditismus verus on embryology.ch
More information about pseudohermaphroditismus on embryology.ch
Table 1: Pseudohermaphroditism
Clinical picture Pathophysiology

pseudoherm. disorders Missing or dysfunctional androgen
Androgen insensitivity receptors

woman)

topic: Malformations of the female gential organs (Teil 3/6)
keywords: malformations of the female gential organs
content:
Mullerian anomalies
Mullerian ducts
The fallopian tubes develop from the cranial parts of the Mullerian ducts. The uterus
and upper two-thirds of the vagina develop from the middle and caudal parts after
their fusion. The distal third of the vagina is not formed from the Mullerian ducts, but
from the urogenital sinus.
More information about Development of the internal genital organs on embryology.ch
The following terms must be differentiated:
Table 2: De�nition of investment and developmental disorders
Agenesis The organ is not created (e.g., kidney agenesis).
Aplasia The organ is created (for example, Mullerian ducts exist), but
not developed; e.g., the vaginal aplasia in Mayer–Rokitansky–
Küster–Hauser syndrome = MRKH syndrome (see below)
Atresia Narrowing or occlusion of a hollow organ by cicatricial
stenosis or a membrane (e.g., hymenal atresia)
Complete Mullerian agenesis is uncommon. Malformations of the genitalia in the
areas of the uterus, tubes and/or vagina are generally the result of failure or
incomplete fusion of the Mullerian ducts.
Note
Hymenal atresia, in contrast, occurs as a result of the absence of membrane rupture
at the Mullerian eminence. This can result in a complete absence of organs, or there
are duplications and/or septations of varying dimensions:
Figure 1 : A – Uterus subseptus; B – Uterus septus; C – Uterus arcuatus; D – Uterus bicornis unicollis; E –
Uterus bicornis bicollis; F – Asymmetrical uterus bicornis; G – Uterus didelphys with vaginal septa
More information about this topic in relation to embryology on embryology.ch

topic: Malformations of the female gential organs (Teil 4/6)
keywords: malformations of the female gential organs
content:
The relative frequencies are:
• 18 % uterus arcuatus
• 35 % uterus septus
• 26 % uterus bicornis
• 10 % uterus unicornis
• 8 % uterus didelphys
• 3 % uterine agenesis
Clinical implications
• Uterine malformations may be associated with clinical symptoms, such as
dysmenorrhea (in the case of increased endometriosis), amenorrhea (e.g.,
hematocolpos), recurrent late abortions (1st and 2nd trimester), premature
birth, and fetal malpresentation. In addition, hemorrhages in the 2nd and 3rd
trimester (placental insertion disorders) and post-partum (placental retention,
placental accretion) are likewise described.
• However, uneventful pregnancies are possible and uneventful pregnancies are
also possiblee.
Diagnosis
In addition to gynecological examination (vaginal septa), further examinations
include ultrasound, hydrosonography, hysterosalpingography, or magnetic resonance
imaging. In such examinations, attention should be paid to malformations of the
kidneys or different urinary tract, which are present in 20 to 30 % of cases.
Management
In the case of symptomatic malformations of the Mullerian ducts, septa can be
resected transvaginally, hysteroscopically, or via laparotomy and uterotomy.
Historical information
Between 1950 and 1970, intrauterine diethylstilbestrol exposure (synthetic estrogen)
of female fetuses resulted in malformations of the genitals, vaginal adenosis, and
malignant alterations.
More information about this on embryology.ch

topic: Malformations of the female gential organs (Teil 5/6)
keywords: malformations of the female gential organs
content:
Mayer--Rokitansky--Küster--Hauser
syndrome ((MRKH))
The incidence of MRKH syndrome is 1:4000–5000 female live births.
Type I is composed of:
• aplasia of the vagina (total or at least the cranial 2/3)
• absent or rudimentary uterus
• ovaries of normal histology and function located high at the pelvic inlet
• a female phenotype and female genotype (XX)
In Type II MRKH syndrome, additional malformations may also affect the kidneys,
the skeleton, and—in rare cases—the sense of hearing, the heart, �ngers, and toes.
MRKH syndrome is often diagnosed when the patient presents with primary
amenorrhea. The indication for treatment depends on the patient’s desire for sexual
intercourse. The treatment objective is a functional neovagina.
Various techniques are available for this purpose:
• non-operative dilatation according to Frank (self dilatation by the patient of the
vaginal dimple using progressively larger dilators),
• surgical dilatation of the rudimentary Mullerian ducts according to Shears,
• intestinal transplantation from large intestine, more rarely from small intestine,
according to Baldwin (various modi�cations),
• cutaneous / musculocutaneous sliding �aps (Williams, Creatsas),
• insertion of split-thickness skin �aps into a prepared cavity between the bladder
and rectum according to McIndoe,
• stretching of the skin of the vaginal dimple through continuous tension in
cranial direction via a tensioning device (traction method) according to
Vecchietti (laparoscopic modi�cation of the original technique via laparotomy),
• and neovagina via peritoneum of the lesser pelvis (Verbenko, Davydov, Zhou).
Reconstruction producing a neovagina bares the risk of skin shrinking over time.
Whilst it is a useful method it should not be performed before adulthood when
intercourse is desired. Dilatation is encouraged as it is associated with much fewer
short and long term complications.

EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Miscarriage//abortion
〉 Induced abortion (intentional termination of pregnancy)
Last update: September 2025
Induced abortion ((intentional
termination ooff pregnancy))
Author:: Prof.. eemm.. DDrr.. med.. Sibil Tschudin
pensioniert seit Mai 2024. Ehem. Leitende Ärztin Abteilung Gyn. Sozialmedizin und Psychosomatik,
Frauenklinik USB
Authors of former script versions: Prof. Johannes Bitzer

Introduction
There is a conceptual difference regarding the word “abortion”. A distinction should
be made between a spontaneous abortion due to natural causes (“miscarriage”) and
an abortion brought about by medical intervention (“intentional termination of
pregnancy” or “induced abortion”; see below).
Cave
In English, the term “abortion” can refer to either a miscarriage or an intentional
termination of pregnancy. In fact, the term is not clearly de�ned. Ideally, more
speci�c terms should be used: “spontaneous abortion” for miscarriage and “induced
abortion” or “therapeutic abortion” for intentional termination of pregnancy.
To avoid confusion, it is advisable to consistently refer to intentional termination
of pregnancy as “induced abortion”.
From a practical point of view, spontaneous abortions are classi�ed as either early or
late. Early miscarriages are usually caused by defective embryonic development.
Late miscarriages, on the other hand, often require further investigation because
they are frequently caused by maternal factors that may pose a risk of recurrence.
Due to recent advances in neonatology, the lower limit of fetal viability has shifted.
Consequently, events occurring after the 22nd week of gestation (WG) are no longer
referred to as spontaneous abortions but as intrauterine fetal deaths (IUFDs). Partus
immaturus refers to the birth of an immature, nonviable child. Previously, it was
de�ned as occurring between 22 1/7 and 23 0/7 WG, but as mentioned, the upper
limit is now lower.

DDee��nition
• The correct English terms are: induced abortion, therapeutic abortion,
intentional termination of pregnancy.
• The latin term “interruptio”, which was commonly used in the past, means
interruption and is incorrect as the pregnancy is terminated and not just
interrupted.
In Switzerland, approximately 11,000 induced abortions are performed each year.
The rate per 1,000 women aged 15 to 44 was 6,9 in 2022, which is lower than in all
other countries that have statistics on this issue. The ratio of induced abortions to
live births is approximately 1:8 [B01]. In 95 % of cases, abortions are performed
within the �rst 12 WG at the request of the pregnant woman. Reasons for abortions
after the 12th WG are either a psychosocial distress or the desire to terminate the
pregnancy in the event of a serious abnormality in the fetus (severe malformation or
serious genetic abnormality). [B01]

Legal framework
Legislation on induced abortion used to be very restrictive in Switzerland and was
limited to medical indications until 2002, when a time limit regulation came into
force following a popular vote. Articles 118 to 120 of the Swiss Criminal Code set
out the framework conditions that apply today.
Swiss Criminal Code
Article 119 lists the conditions for induced abortion without penalty:
1. The termination of a pregnancy is exempt from penalty in the event that the
termination is, in the judgment of a physician, necessary in order to be able to
prevent the pregnant woman from sustaining serious physical injury or serious
psychological distress. The risk must be greater the more advanced the
pregnancy is.
2. The termination of a pregnancy is likewise exempt from penalty if, at the written
request of a pregnant woman, who claims that she is in a state of distress, it is
performed within twelve weeks of the start of the pregnant woman’s last period
by a physician who is licensed to practise their profession. The physician must
have a detailed consultation with the woman prior to the termination and
provide her with appropriate counselling.
3. If the woman is incapable of judgement, the consent of her legal representative
is required.
4. The cantons designate the medical practices and hospitals that ful�l the
requirements for the professional conduct of procedures to terminate
pregnancy and for the provision of counselling.
5. An abortion is reported for statistical purposes to the competent health
authority, whereby the anonymity of the woman concerned is guaranteed and
medical con�dentiality is preserved.
Article 120 lists the duties of physicians, which, in addition to the aforementioned
counselling session, obtaining the written request and anonymous noti�cation, also
include handing over the cantonal manual. In addition, pregnant women under the
age of 16 must be referred to a specialized counselling agency. Physicians who fail
to comply with these duties are liable to penalty.
Apart from Poland, which has very restrictive legislation, induced abortions on
request have been legalized in all European countries, though the time limits and
conditions vary slightly from one country to the next.

Counseling before aann induced abortion
Physicians are required to provide women who wish to terminate their pregnancy
with a personal, comprehensive counseling session regarding the desired procedure.
Women are not required to seek counseling regarding their decision to terminate
their pregnancy. However, unwanted pregnancies are often experienced as
con�ictual, so that attending physicians should always be available to provide
counseling in connection with decision-making. The aim is to assist the woman
concerned in making a decision that is informed, well-re�ected, and self-determined.
Adopting an empathetic, nonjudgmental attitude is essential. This requires
physicians to re�ect on their own views and not allow them to in�uence the
conversation. Further suggestions on how to proceed can be found in the “Manual
for counseling sessions prior to abortion”, which is available in German and French
on the Swiss Society of Gynecology and Obstetrics website [B02]. According to the
Federal Act on Pregnancy Counseling Centers, which has been in force since 1981,
every canton must also have a counseling center that offers free counseling in cases
of pregnancy con�ict. A list of all counseling centers in Switzerland can be found on
the website of “Sexuelle Gesundheit Schweiz/Santé Sexuelle Suisse/Salute
Sessuale”. [B02]
SEXUAL HEALTH SWITZERLAND (available in german & french)
Requirements for performing an abortion in accordance with the time limit
regulation:
• Request for abortion signed by the patient in accordance with Article 119 of the
Swiss Criminal Code
• Counseling and information session in accordance with legal requirements and
provision of the cantonal manual

Procedure for terminating aa pregnancy
Preliminary examinations:
• Gynecological examination
• Ultrasound to con�rm intrauterine pregnancy (gestational sac, possibly yolk
sac, possibly embryo, possibly cardiac activity) and to determine gestational
age
• Blood group
• Hemoglobin, if there are indications of anemia or increased bleeding tendency
• Possibly chlamydia test (especially before surgical abortion)
• Possibly cervical cytology (if last test result > 3 years old)
Methods:
• Up to 9 WG (63 days after last menstrual period): medical abortion with
mifepristone and misoprostol (or surgical)
• From 10 WG to 12 (14) WG: surgical abortion (suction method)
• In the second trimester: abortion induction with prostaglandins
Rhesus prophylaxis:
• If the woman is rhesus negative, anti-D prophylaxis must be administered for
each of the methods of termination of pregnancy mentioned (vacuum
aspiration, medical abortion, induction of abortion)!
• Dosage: Rhophylac® 300: 300 μg i.v. or i.m.
Surgical abortion
Surgical abortion involves using the suction method, also known as vacuum
aspiration. After priming (loosening the cervical tissue) with prostaglandin E1, the
cervical canal is dilated with Hegar rods. Then, the pregnancy is removed and the
uterine cavity emptied using a vacuum pump.
Priming signi�cantly reduces the risk of complications. Misoprostol (Cytotec®) is
usually administered vaginally at a dose of 200 to 400 µg 3 hours before the
procedure. In more advanced gestational ages (12 to 14 WG), it is recommended to
additionally administer 200 mg of mifepristone 24 to 48 hours before the
intervention.
Anesthesia
• In Switzerland, the procedure is usually performed under short general

anesthesia.
• Alternatively, suction curettage can also be performed under paracervical block
(10–20 ml of 1 % lidocaine injected into the cervix at 4, 8 and 12 hours)
Antibiotic prophylaxis
• Women undergoing surgical abortion may be given a single dose of
perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis. Suitable antibiotics include doxycycline,
metronidazole, or azithromycin. Fortunately, however, postoperative infections
are relatively rare overall, so that the minor bene�ts must be weighed against
the risk of developing resistance. Preoperative screening for sexually
transmitted infections, especially chlamydia, is important.
Surgical abortion in the �rst trimester can be considered very safe, with a mortality
rate of 0,6 deaths per 100,000 abortions. Overall, complications occur in less than 1
% of women, and only 0,3 % of women experience complications that require
hospitalization.
The following complications may occur:
• Increased bleeding due to di�cult or incomplete emptying of the uterine cavity
and/or uterine atony
• Cervical injuries during dilation (possible long-term consequences: cervical
insu�ciency in subsequent pregnancies with an increased risk of late
miscarriage or premature birth)
• Uterine perforation with the risk of intra-abdominal bleeding
• Postoperative ascending infection, up to pelvic peritonitis or sepsis (possible
long-term consequences: tubal pregnancy or tubal sterility)
• Damage to the stratum basale of the endometrium as a result of excessive
curettage (possible long-term consequences: Asherman’s syndrome, i.e., partial
or total adhesions (=synechiae) of the unterine cavity)
A meta-analysis from 2009 showed that women with more than one previous
surgical abortion had an increased risk of low birth weight (OR 1.72, 95% CI 1.45 to
2.04; 5 studies, n=49,347) and preterm birth (OR 1.93, 95% CI 1.38 to 2.71; 7 studies,
n=158,421) in subsequent pregnancies. The risks increase with the number of
previous abortions.

Medical abortion
General principles
Mifepristone, a synthetic steroid with antiprogestational effects, is used in
combination with misoprostol, a prostaglandin E1, to perform medical abortion. The
usual dose of 600 mg mifepristone and 400 µg misoprostol is successful in
approximately 94% of cases involving pregnancies of up to 49 days after last
menstrual period, i.e., in two-thirds of cases the pregnancy is terminated within the
�rst 4 hours after prostaglandin administration, and in the remaining cases over the
course of the following days. A Cochrane study showed that reducing the dose of
mifepristone from 600 mg to 200 mg makes no difference in terms of success rate
or time to expulsion. Cytotec® is not registered for the indication “abortion
induction,” but is used worldwide for this purpose.
Several international guidelines (American College of Obstetrics and Gynecology,
Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists, World Health Organization) as well
as the 2022 revised expert letter from the Swiss Society of Gynecology and
Obstetrics support the performance of medical abortion up to 63 days after last
menstrual period. According to the available study results, the combination of 200
mg mifepristone followed by misoprostol (800 µg) administered vaginally proved to
be the most suitable. Vaginal administration of misoprostol showed fewer side
effects and a higher complete expulsion rate.
Mifepristone ((Mifegyne®®))
Synthetic steroid with antiprogestational effect.
Properties//Effects
• Competitive antagonism of progesterone at its receptor, thereby interrupting
placental function
• Increase in the local supply of prostaglandin F2, thereby promoting uterine
contractions
• Softening and dilation of the cervix
• Antiglucocorticoid and weak antiandrogenic effect
Pharmacokinetics
• Metabolized in the liver, 90 % excreted in the stool
• Half-life of 24 to 54 hours
Interactions

Reduction in the effect of corticosteroids. For corticosteroid therapy (e.g., asthma),
the dose should be doubled 48 hours before to one week after Mifegyne®
administration. Nonsteroidal anti-in�ammatory drugs, including acetylsalicylic acid,
may reduce the effectiveness of the method due to inhibition of prostaglandin
synthesis.
Misoprostol ((Cytotec®®))
C16 methylated ester of prostaglandin E1.
Properties//Effects
• Dose-dependent triggering of uterine contractions through selective binding to
EP2/EP3 prostaglandin receptors
• Inhibition of gastric acid secretion, ulcer treatment
Pharmacokinetics
• Elimination: 73 % renal, 15 % intestinal
• Half-life of 20 to 30 min
Contraindications
• Adrenal insu�ciency
• Severe asthma
• Severe cardiovascular disease
• Smoking > 30 cigarettes/day
• Known allergy to mifepristone
• Previous complications following prostaglandin administration
• Severe anemia (< 100 g/l)
Pregnancy//Breastfeeding
Breastfeeding should be stopped for 4 days after taking mifepristone because it
passes into breast milk.
Reliable contraception should be used for one month following a medical abortion.
In some cases where the pregnancy was continued, as well as after the use of
misoprostol during early pregnancy, malformations (limb malformations such as
clubfoot, defects of the skull and scalp as well as cranial nerve palsies) have been
observed.
Procedure

Nowadays, medication abortion is usually performed on an outpatient basis, i.e., the
termination of pregnancy takes place at home. Most women cope well with this and
appreciate the fact that their privacy is preserved. Nevertheless, it seems that certain
factors in�uence how medication abortion at home is experienced and how stressful
it is for the woman concerned. A study conducted in the USA found that young
women and those who had not yet experienced childbirth or miscarriage
experienced heavier bleeding and more pain than expected. The same was true for
single women and those with lower levels of education.
Accordingly, the outpatient procedure should (preferably) be avoided in the following
situations:
• Risks of increased bleeding
• Hypermenorrhea (e.g., in case of uterine �broids)
• Increased bleeding tendency due to coagulation disorder
• Pre-existing severe anemia
• Psychiatric disorders
• Anxiety disorder
• Severe depression
• Age < 16 years
• Age < 18 years without adequate support
• Patient’s concerns about having an abortion at home
• No social environment that provides adequate support
• Serious communication and comprehension problems
All women must be informed in detail about
• the abortion procedure
• where to seek help in case of heavy bleeding or unforeseen circumstances
• the necessity of con�rming that the abortion is complete.
Medication abortion takes place in three steps:
• Dispensation of Mifegyne® in the form of a single oral dose of at least 200 mg
(1 tablet).
• Mifepristone intake is followed 36 to 48 hours later by at least 400 μg
misoprostol (1 tablet of Miso-One® or 2 tablets of Cytotec®) either sublingually,
buccally, or intravaginally, combined with analgesics (NSAIDs) and antiemetics.
In the 8th and 9th WG, the preferred method of administration is 800 μg
vaginally.

EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Miscarriage//abortion 〉 Spontaneous abortion (miscarriage)
Last update: September 2025
Spontaneous abortion ((miscarriage))
Author:: PPDD DDrr.. Christine Brambs CCoo--Author((ss)):: Dr. med. Andreas Studer, Dr. med. Laura
Küpfer
Chefärztin / Leitung Frauenklinik, Luzerner
Kantonsspital Luzern
Authors of former script versions: Prof. Johannes Bitzer

Introduction
There is a conceptual difference regarding the word “abortion”. A distinction should
be made between a spontaneous abortion due to natural causes (“miscarriage”) and
an abortion brought about by medical intervention (“intentional termination of
pregnancy” or “induced abortion”; see below).
Cave
In English, the term “abortion” can refer to either a miscarriage or an intentional
termination of pregnancy. In fact, the term is not clearly de�ned. Ideally, more
speci�c terms should be used: “spontaneous abortion” for miscarriage and “induced
abortion” or “therapeutic abortion” for intentional termination of pregnancy.
To avoid confusion, it is advisable to consistently refer to intentional termination
of pregnancy as “induced abortion”.
From a practical point of view, spontaneous abortions are classi�ed as either early or
late. Early miscarriages are usually caused by defective embryonic development.
Late miscarriages, on the other hand, often require further investigation because
they are frequently caused by maternal factors that may pose a risk of recurrence.
Due to recent advances in neonatology, the lower limit of fetal viability has shifted.
Consequently, events occurring after the 22nd week of gestation (WG) are no longer
referred to as spontaneous abortions but as intrauterine fetal deaths (IUFDs). Partus
immaturus refers to the birth of an immature, nonviable child. Previously, it was
de�ned as occurring between 22 1/7 and 23 0/7 WG, but as mentioned, the upper
limit is now lower.

DDee��nition
Spontaneous abortion = miscarriage
• Premature spontaneous termination of pregnancy with or without expulsion of
the dead embryo or fetus
• with a birth weight of < 500 g or
• if the weight is unknown, at a gestational age of < 22 0/7 WG
Classi��cation ooff spontaneous abortions according ttoo the
time ooff occurrence
• Early miscarriage: up to 12 0/7 WG
• Late miscarriage: 12 1/7 to 21 6/7 WG
Nomenclature ooff spontaneous abortions according ttoo
clinical situation
• Abortus imminens (threatened abortion)
• Abortus incipiens (incipient, inevitable abortion)
• Abortus incompletus (incomplete abortion)
• Abortus completus (complete abortion)
• Missed abortion: A pregnancy that is no longer viable and is in utero without
symptoms (such as bleeding, etc.).
• Abortus febrilis (febrile to septic abortion)
• Habitual abortion (at least 3 consecutive spontaneous abortions with the same
partner in a row up to 20 WG; frequency 1 to 3% of all couples of reproductive
age): The causes here are usually multifactorial and often cannot be
determined individually
Also to be mentioned is the wind egg = blighted ovum. This is a molar trophoblast
without embryonic development, i.e., a germ malformation in which the embryoblast
does not form, resulting in only a chorionic cavity without an embryo or yolk sac.
Caveat: Not to be confused with a hydatidiform mole (cf. also EGONE script G12B
“Gestational trophoblastic diseases”), which is classi�ed as a villous trophoblastic
tumor!

Epidemiology
Spontaneous abortion is the most common complication in early pregnancy. Up to
30 % of clinically con�rmed pregnancies before the 20th WG end in spontaneous
abortion. In 80 % of cases, this occurs during the �rst 12 WG. The overall risk of
spontaneous abortion after the 15th WG is low, around 0,6 % for chromosomally and
structurally normal fetuses, but it varies with maternal age and ethnicity.
The loss of subclinical or undetected pregnancies is signi�cantly higher. Nowadays,
it is assumed that 30 to 60 % of all pregnancies end in spontaneous abortion.
Under hCG monitoring, it has been shown that the overall rate of pregnancy loss is
31 %, of which 70 % occurred before the pregnancy was clinically detectable.
Regarding preimplantation losses, it is currently assumed that 50% of fertilized
oocytes do not result in a live birth.
Early miscarriages are more common (80 %) than late miscarriages.
Spontaneous abortions show a clear predominance for male fetuses, with a ratio
of 3:2.

Risk factors
Age
• 20 to 29 years old: 12 %
• > 45 years old: 65 %
• Gradual increase in between
Previous spontaneous abortion
The risk of a subsequent spontaneous abortion is 20 % after one previous
spontaneous abortion, 30 % after two, and approximately 40 % after three or more
(consecutively). For women who had a successful �rst pregnancy, the risk of a
spontaneous abortion is only 5 %.
Smoking
Heavy smokers (more than 10 cigarettes per day) have a higher risk of experiencing
a spontaneous abortion.
Alcohol
Studies are inconsistent in this regard. As a general rule, alcohol should be avoided
during pregnancy due to its teratogenicity.
Poorly controlled maternal disease
This speci�cally applies to diabetes and thyroid disorders (both hyperthyroidism and
hypothyroidism). There is no known increased risk of spontaneous abortion in
individuals with euglycemic and euthyroid metabolic status. In addition, untreated
celiac disease also appears to increase the risk of spontaneous abortion.
Cocaine
Cocaine is associated with an increased risk of spontaneous abortion, but is
primarily known for causing premature birth.
Caffeine
The impact of caffeine is unclear. Up to 3 cups per day are considered safe.
Maternal weight

Overweight (BMI >25 kg/m²) is associated with a markedly increased risk of
spontaneous abortion. Both underweight and overweight are also associated with
increased infertility.
Subchorionic hematoma
According to studies, a subchorionic hematoma leads to a higher spontaneous
abortion rate if it occupies more than 25 % of the gestational sac volume.

Etiology
Etiology ooff early miscarriages
• In over 50 % of cases, the cause is a chromosomal aberration.
• The rate of early miscarriages increases with maternal and paternal age.
• The harmful in�uence of noxae, especially nicotine, has been proven to increase
the spontaneous abortion rate.
• In cases of early miscarriage (< 6 0/7 WG), corpus luteum insu�ciency may be
a contributing factor.
Etiology ooff late miscarriages
• Cervical insu�ciency (risk factors: history of cervical trauma, infection, history
of conization, history of cervical insu�ciency) is often involved. The cervix can
no longer adequately ful�ll its supporting and closing functions, leading to an
(often asymptomatic) opening of the cervix that initially goes unnoticed.
• Mechanical insu�ciency is often accompanied by an ascending infection in the
form of chorioamnionitis.
• Anatomical cause (limited increase in uterine volume): uterine malformations,
myoma.
Etiology ooff habitual abortion
Only in cases of habitual abortion, de�ned as ≥ 3 consecutive spontaneous
abortions before the 20th WG with the same partner, are further etiological
investigations indicated.
The following diagnostic procedures may be used to determine the causes of
habitual abortion:
• BMI, medical history of noxious exposure
• Hormone analyses (thyroid, PCOS, prolactin, sugar)
• Exclusion of infections with blood samples and cervical smears, endometrial
biopsy if necessary to rule out chronic endometritis
• (Auto)immunoserology (antiphospholipid syndrome)
• Exclusion of uterine malformations by sonography, hysteroscopy,
hysterosalpingography (Ashermann's syndrome, polyps, myoma)
• Chromosome analysis of the couple (genetic counseling necessary)
• Karyotyping of the abortion material

• Coagulation testing (general thrombophilia screening is not recommended)
List ooff maternal causes ooff habitual abortion
Genital factors/abnormalities:
• Malformations: uterine duplication, intrauterine synechiae
• Submucosal myomas
• Cervical insu�ciency: possibly previous operations (conization), connective
tissue weakness
• Endometrial insu�ciency: endocrine disorder, atrophy after curettage
• Infection: cervicitis, endometritis
• Hypermotility (psychovegetative disorder, fever)
• Adenomyosis/endometriosis
Extragenital factors/abnormalities:
• Noxae: alcohol, nicotine
• Immunological (blood group antibodies, antiphospholipid syndrome)
• Endocrine disorders (diabetes mellitus, thyroid disorders)
• Viral and bacterial infections (fever-induced hypermobility of the uterus,
infectious or toxic damage to the fetus)
• Consumptive diseases
List ooff fetoplacental causes ooff habitual abortion
• Chromosomal aberrations
• Trophoblastic dysfunction (endocrine insu�ciency)

Clinical presentation and diagnosis
The symptoms of spontaneous abortion vary greatly. A spontaneous abortion
may go completely unnoticed or may be accompanied by bleeding.
• In rare cases, bleeding can be extensive and even life-threatening. In other
cases, only minor spotting occurs.
• Occasionally, abdominal discomfort (cramping or pain) may also be present,
often described as “heavier menstrual cramps”.
• Particular caution is advised in cases of fever and purulent discharge (--> an
indication of an infectious complication of the abortion and thus a medical
emergency)!
Early miscarriages (up to approximately 6 WG) are often subclinical and are
mistaken for a delayed menstrual period. A positive pregnancy test provides an
indication! These very early miscarriages do not require further treatment. Only 20 %
of these are perceived by the pregnant woman as a miscarriage.
The diagnosis of spontaneous abortion is made or veri�ed using ultrasound.

Con��rmation ooff the miscarriage diagnosis
In early pregnancy, especially without a prior ultrasound to determine the gestational
age, diagnosing a spontaneous abortion can sometimes be di�cult. Reliable signs
of miscarriage are:
• Crown-rump length ≥7 mm without positive cardiac activity
• Gestational sac with a diameter ≥25 mm without embryonic development
• Absent embryo with positive cardiac activity 2 weeks after an ultrasound in
which a gestational sac but no yolk sac was visible
• Absent embryo with positive cardiac activity ≥11 days after a gestational sac
with yolk sac was detected

Clinical engrams for the individual forms
ooff spontaneous abortion
Abortus imminens
Threatened abortion manifests as (light) vaginal bleeding with an otherwise intact
pregnancy.
Figure 1: Abortus imminens.
The cervix is preserved. The clinically decisive factor is:
• The cervix is still closed.

Vaginal examinations should be kept to a minimum. Ultrasound may reveal a
retroplacental hematoma, the extent of which is a major prognostic factor.
Abortus incipiens
The abortion process has begun. In most cases, the patient experiences a
menstrual-like bleeding. The pregnancy has been irreversibly disrupted and the
abortion can no longer be stopped.
Figure 2: Abortus incipiens.
The cervix is opened. The bleeding is usually accompanied by lower abdominal pain
and leads to an incomplete or complete abortion.

Incomplete and complete abortion
In incomplete abortion, part of the pregnancy material has been expelled. However,
the uterine cavity is not empty. In complete abortion, all of the pregnancy material
has been expelled.
Missed abortion
In the case of a missed abortion, there are generally no symptoms. It is mainly
characterized by the “silent” termination of the pregnancy. It is often diagnosed
incidentally during a routine pregnancy check-up. Ultrasound shows no cardiac
activity and the crown-rump length does not correspond to the gestational age.
Blighted ovum
The clinical presentation corresponds to that of a missed abortion. Ultrasound
reveals a gestational sac without a yolk sac or embryo.
Abortus febrilis
After an unprofessional attempt to induce an abortion, bacterial ascension can lead
to an infection of the decidua and myometrium (endomyometritis), which can spread
throughout the body. Possible consequences include parametritis, peritonitis, and
septicemia with endocarditis. A septic abortion can progress to life-threatening
septic shock with renal failure, respiratory failure (ARDS), and disseminated
intravascular coagulation (DIC).

Differential diagnosis
Differential diagnosis of vaginal bleeding in early pregnancy
• in causal connection with pregnancy: the various forms of spontaneous
abortion, ectopic pregnancy
• not in causal connection with pregnancy: polyp in the cervical canal (CC),
infections, ectopy (“ectopic bleeding”)
Differential diagnosis of pain in early pregnancy
• in causal connection with pregnancy: the various forms of spontaneous
abortion, ectopic pregnancy, stretching pain (uterus)
• not in causal connection with pregnancy: ovarian cysts (with/without bleeding,
rupture, torsion), entire range of internal medicine and surgery conditions
(appendicitis*, renal colic, constipation, etc.)
*Note: Appendicitis can manifest atypically throughout pregnancy and should
always be ruled out in cases of pain and positive in�ammation parameters.

Treatment ooff threatened abortion ((abortus
imminens))
Important: If the woman is rhesus negative, anti-D prophylaxis must be
administered for all forms of spontaneous abortion beyond 7 4/7 WG!
Abortus imminens
The “treatment” for threatened abortion consists of psychophysical relief. Generous
sick leave is recommended. Sexual intercourse should be avoided. A meta-analysis
from 2021 involving 5,682 patients concluded that the administration of vaginal
progesterone in cases of bleeding in early pregnancy and recurrent abortions can
have a positive effect on the survival of the child. Vaginal progesterone
administration is highly unlikely to cause adverse events.

Management ooff early miscarriages
For all forms of early miscarriage, there are three possible options for
hemodynamically stable women without heavy bleeding or fever, which should be
explained to the patient. In the case of a febrile abortion, immediate antibiotic
treatment and termination of pregnancy by curettage are indicated. From 7+4 WG
onwards, 300 mcg of anti-D immunoglobulin should be administered in the case of a
negative blood group.
1. Wait-and-see management
2. Drug-based management
3. Surgical management
Wait--and--see management
A wait-and-see approach is an alternative for women before the 13th WG who are in
stable clinical condition with no signs of infection. According to the British NICE
guideline, this is even recommended as the �rst-line treatment for the �rst two
weeks after diagnosis. Compared to the surgical option, there is an increased risk of
incomplete expulsion. There may be more frequent bleeding and a consequent need
for curettage. The patient should be well informed about this. Most expulsions occur
within the �rst 2 weeks. After 6 weeks, 81 % of patients have had a complete
abortion, which, according to randomized studies, corresponds to the success rate
of the drug-based option. There are no signi�cant differences between the three
options in terms of infection risk. Follow-up checks every 2 weeks are generally
recommended. If bleeding does not occur within 2 weeks, switching to the drug-
based or surgical option can be discussed. There is no consensus in the guidelines
regarding the de�nition of an incomplete abortion. In general, clinical �ndings (e.g.,
persistent bleeding) should be taken into account. According to an ACOG Practice
Bulletin, an endometrial thickness of > 30 mm is considered an incomplete abortion.
However, according to a Dutch review, most studies used a cut-off of > 15 mm. In
some practices/countries, clinical monitoring is not performed if there has been a
history of bleeding and a β-hCG test was negative 4 weeks after the initial
presentation.
Drug therapy with misoprostol

In drug-induced abortions, administering mifepristone (Mifegyne, a progesterone
receptor antagonist used off-label) before misoprostol (a prostaglandin E1 analog)
has proven effective. A Dutch double-blind, placebo-controlled study showed that
79,1 % of participants who received Mifegyne had a complete abortion, compared to
58,7 % in the placebo group.
Procedure
Administration of 200 mg Mifegyne p.o., followed by 600 to 800 mcg misoprostol
vaginally or p.o. 36 to 48 hours after taking Mifegyne. An ultrasound control should
be performed 7 to 14 days later. If residual material is present, a repeat dose of
misoprostol may be considered before opting for surgery. Typical side effects of the
drugs include lower abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, headache, dizziness, and
diarrhea
Contraindications for the wait--and--see and drug--based approaches
• Low hemoglobin/circulatory instability
• Coagulation disorders
• Lack of compliance
• Rejection by the patient
• Suspected trophoblastic disease
• Suspected ectopic pregnancy
• Contraindications for misoprostol: glaucoma, sickle cell anemia, epilepsy, mitral
stenosis
• Contraindications for mifepristone (Mifegyne): chronic corticosteroid use,
adrenal disorders
Abortion curettage
Whenever possible, curettage should be performed using suction rather than spoon.
The procedure is highly successful, with only 4 % of patients requiring further
surgery. However, the incidence of recurrent spontaneous abortions after curettage
is signi�cantly higher than after drug-based treatment. Furthermore, there is an
increased risk of Asherman's syndrome (especially with spoon curettage). In
addition, the general risks of surgery, such as perforation with injury to neighboring
organs, bleeding, and thrombosis, should not be neglected.

Treatment ooff septic abortion
If septic abortion is suspected (fever, elevated white blood cell count, residual
material visible on ultrasound), the following procedure is recommended:
• Stabilization of the patient
• Collection of swabs
• Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy (later, pathogen-speci�c)
• Provision of erythrocyte concentrates
• Termination of pregnancy by curettage as quickly as possible
The most common pathogens are anaerobic bacteria, but also: coliform bacteria,
group A streptococci, Haemophilus in�uenzae, Campylobacter jejuni. Treatment
should be initiated with broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., clindamycin + gentamicin
+/− ampicillin) appropriate for the resistance pro�le.
Caution: If Clostridium sepsis is suspected (crackling of tissues during gynecological
examination, or radiographic suspicion of air in uterine tissue), an emergency
hysterectomy may be indicated.

EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Ovarian tumors 〉 Epithelial ovarian cancer
Last update: April 2024
Epithelial ovarian cancer
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr.
med. Viola Heinzelmann-
Schwarz
Author:: PPDD DDrr.. Marcus Vetter
Leitender Arzt Medizinische Onkologie, Schwerpunkt Gynäkologische Tumoren
Authors of former script versions: Dr. med. Konstantin Dedes, Prof. Dr. med.
Daniel Fink

DDee��nition
In Switzerland, approximately 700 women per year are diagnosed with a malignant
ovarian neoplasm. Due to the complexity of the disease, treatment should take place
in specialized, accredited tumor centers and/or networks. According to the new
IVHSM (Inter-cantonal agreement on highly specialized medicine), in the future
ovarian cancer surgery will be carried out by highly-specialized doctors. Generally,
malignant ovarian tumors are classi�ed according to the tissue in which they
originate.
Epithelial neoplasms (90 %)
• Serous (55 %)
• Endometrioid (20 %)
• Mucinous (10 %)
• Clear cell (5 %)
• Carcinosarcoma (or malignant mixed Müllerian tumor) (< 5 %)
Germ cell neoplasms (5 %)
• Dysgerminoma
• Malignant teratoma
• Other rare (choriocarcinoma, gonadoblastoma)
Stromal neoplasms (5 %)
• Granulosa cell tumor
• Sertoli-Leydig cell tumor

Peritoneal cancer and fallopian tube cancer are di�cult to clinically distinguish from
epithelial ovarian cancer. Their histological properties are also very similar. The
diagnosis, staging and treatment of these neoplasms are based on the management
of epithelial ovarian cancer.
The molecular characterization of ovarian cancer types has led to a new
classi�cation. This categorizes them as serous low-grade or high-grade, which are
different in terms of genetics, pathogenesis and prognosis [B05].
The majority of low-grade serous carcinomas probably develop from benign
precursor lesions such as cystadenomas, endometriosis and borderline tumors and
histologically show good differentiation (G1).
Poorly-differentiated serous carcinomas (G3) mainly form the group of high-grade
carcinomas. With regard to aggressive high-grade tumors, the role of the distal
portion of the fallopian tube as a organ of origin is discussed, as patients at high risk
of ovarian cancer (carriers of BRCA1/2 mutations) are often diagnosed with cancer
precursors (carcinoma in situ) here (the aforementioned STIC lesions). [B05]

stage than serous high-grade carcinoma, thus overall better prognosis, adapted from: Vetter M., Leary A.

Frequency,, age distribution
Ovarian cancer is the third most common gynecological cancer (after breast and
endometrial cancer) in industrialized countries. With an incidence of approx.
15/100’000 women per year, the lifetime risk is approx. 1,5 %. In Switzerland, 700
women are diagnosed with ovarian cancer each year, and in Germany 8000 women
per year. This makes it the leading cause of death among gynecological cancers.
The peak age is 60 years (between 50 and 70 years). The incidence of borderline
tumors is approx. 2-5/100’000 women per year, with a peak age between 30 and 40
years, unlike malignant ovarian cancer. Thanks to improvements in treatment in the
form of surgery, systemic treatment and targeted therapies, the prognosis of ovarian
cancer has improved in recent years. When optimally treated, approximately 40 % of
stage III serous high-grade cancers can be cured.

Risk factors
One of the most important factors for the development of ovarian cancer are
pathogenic mutations of the BRCA 1 and 2 genes. This can give a lifetime risk of up
to 60 % for developing the disease. Approx. 20 % of women with high-grade serous
ovarian cancer have pathogenic mutations of both of these genes. Thanks to recent
advances in prophylaxis, genetics and drug treatments, awareness of the mutation
status of all women diagnosed with serous ovarian cancer is now obligatory.
One possible explanation for the pathogenesis of ovarian cancer is inclusion cyst
theory, which suggests that on each ovulation, epithelial tissue from the ovarian
capsule is included in the ovarian stroma, and that various factors can cause this to
transform into an epithelial carcinoma. More recent studies show that the most
common subtype of high-grade ovarian cancer originates in the fallopian tubes and/
or �mbriae (see above). It has also been shown that opportunistic salpingectomy in
healthy patients can reduce the risk of ovarian cancer [B10]. [B10]
Table 2: Key risk factors for ovarian cancer (incl. protective factors)
Risk factors Protective factors
Increasing age Long-term administration of ovulation
Nulliparity, infertility inhibitors
Early menarche, late menopause Multiparity
Breast cancer mutation carriers (BRCA1 Breastfeeding
and BRCA2) Tubal ligation
History of breast cancer
Estrogen and gestagen substitution
Infertility treatments
Lynch syndrome
Endometriosis
Increased ovulation/menstruation rate
Hereditary ovarian cancer in BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutation carriers, associated with a
lifetime risk of approx. 40 %, is only responsible for approx. 10 % of all ovarian
cancers.

NNoo established screening program
Given the overall low incidence rate, it has previously been impossible to establish a
screening program for the early detection of ovarian cancer due to insu�cient
effectiveness. Screening with annual transvaginal ultrasound in combination with the
sensitive tumor marker CA125 has not been shown to reduce mortality in high-risk
groups nor in the general population, including in studies with over 200’000 women
[B11]. In high-risk families, genetic counseling should always take place with
subsequent testing if indicated. If a BRCA1/2 mutation is detected, prophylactic
surgery should be carried out for healthy carriers as per current guidelines (e.g.
NCCN). [B11]
Prevention is only indicated for carriers of BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutations. Bilateral
adnexectomy should be recommended if the patient does not want (more) children
or at the latest at 40 years. If the patient wants to preserve fertility, taking an
ovulation inhibitor can signi�cantly reduce the risk of developing ovarian cancer.

Diagnosis
Ovarian cancer generally presents with few symptoms and is mostly only diagnosed
at an advanced stage. Epithelial serous high-grade cancer is generally at FIGO stage
III or IV at the time of diagnosis. Some patients are referred to gynecologists from
subspecialties like internal medicine and gastroenterology following unremarkable
�ndings.
The following are nonspeci�c symptoms:
• Increased abdominal circumference in case of ascites and/or tumor spread into
the abdomen
• Bloating, constipation, weight gain due to ascites
• Nausea and vomiting in case of subileus/ileus
• Breathlessness in case of pleural effusion or metastatic involvement of the
diaphragm
• Weight gain
• Thrombosis (more common with clear-cell carcinoma) or lymphedema from
vascular compression in the lower abdomen
De�nitive diagnosis requires surgical clari�cation with histological con�rmation by
means of laparoscopy or directly with laparotomy (staging laparotomy). Preoperative
histological examination by means of �ne-needle aspiration or punch biopsy of a
�nding limited to the ovary should not be carried out due to the risk of
intraabdominal tumor leakage. If laparoscopic diagnosis is successful, the trocar
wound sites should generally be excised during the de�nitive surgery.
A preoperative suspected diagnosis of ovarian cancer is established on the basis of
the following:
1. Clinical examination: ascites, palpable abdominal tumor
2. Gynecological status: palpable tumor in lesser pelvis, breast exam
3. Transvaginal ultrasound: enlarged suspicious cystic ovarian tumor with free
�uid (Figures 1 and 2)
4. Elevated tumor markers
5. Pelvic CT: lower abdominal tumor, ascites, in�ltration of the greater omentum
(omental cake), retroperitoneal lymph node involvement, liver parenchyma,
pleural carcinomatosis, effusion
�. In case of suspected bowel cancer: colonoscopy
7. In case of suspected stomach cancer (Krukenberg tumor): gastroscopy

The probability of the presence of ovarian cancer can be con�rmed with preoperative
signs and treatment with primary surgery initiated. In the case of advanced �ndings
(stage IV) and poor performance status, in exceptional cases pleural/ascites
puncture or peritoneal biopsy can be carried out to con�rm the diagnosis, and
preoperative chemotherapy initiated. The risk of malignancy index, for example, can
be used for triage and deciding on primary debulking/staging surgery. This is
calculated using menopause status, ultrasound �ndings and CA125 level.
Triage index:: risk ooff malignancy index [[BB1122]]
RMI = menopause status x ultrasound �ndings x absolute CA125 value
Points:
Premenopausal: 1
Postmenopausal: 3
Simple cyst: 1
Complex mass: 2
In the classical de�nition of the RMI, speci�c sonographic features (e.g. multilocular
cyst, solid component, bilateral lesion, ascites or metastases) are each assigned one
point. If one of these features is present, the ultrasound score is 1; if two or more
features are present, a score of 3 is assigned.
The Risk of Malignancy Index itself is a dimensionless index and has no unit of its
own, even though the CA-125 value, including its unit, is incorporated into the
formula.
RMI > 200 Primary surgery in gynecological center [B12]
Example 1:
• Menopause status postmenopausal: 3
• Ultrasound �ndings: 3
• CA125: 100 U/ml
• → RMI = 3 × 3 × 100 = 900
Exemple 2:
• Menopausal status premenopausal: 1
• Ultrasound �ndings: 2

• CA125: 200 U/ml
• → RMI = 1 × 2 × 200 = 400
The O-RADS Score can also be used. In this case, the morphological anomalies of
the ovary are also assessed, in addition to other �ndings such as ascites and
abnormal lymph nodes.
Both O-RADS and RMI have high sensitivity. Decisions on further treatment should
always be discussed at a multidisciplinary tumor board with gynecologic
oncologists, pathologists and internists.
Figure 1: Transvaginal ultrasound of a serous ovarian tumor.
Left: with cystic-solid components, thickened septa and papillary projections in the cyst epithelium.
Right: notable papillary structures to the right of the image.
Reliable preoperative imaging for staging does not exist. Unlike other cancers, the
pattern of metastasis is predominantly characterized by peritoneal spread and
lymphatic metastasis in the retroperitoneal lymph nodes. The sites of predilection
are the peritoneum, lesser pelvis, diaphragm, paracolic gutters and greater omentum
(�gure 2).

Figure 2: Pattern of metastasis.

Tumor markers
CCAA112255 ((Cancer Antigen 112255))
This glycoprotein is expressed on the surface of most ovarian cancer cells and
secreted in the blood. Elevated CA125 levels have high speci�city but low sensitivity
for ovarian cancer. Other malignant tumors such as endometrial cancer, breast
cancer and gastrointestinal cancer also cause elevated CA125 levels. But benign
processes involving the peritoneum (peritonitis, hepatitis, pancreatitis and
endometriosis) can also do so.
A major GCIG validation study with over 50 markers showed that CA125 remains the
best marker for follow-up testing. CA125 is also well-suited to check-ups during
treatment (chemotherapy) with high sensitivity. However, it should be remembered
that falsely high or low CA125 values can occur, and ascites puncture or pleural
puncture can reduce the marker.
Regular CA125 testing in follow-up can be discussed. In the course of the disease, a
biochemical recurrence (i.e. doubling of CA125 over the normal value) generally
occurs before CT changes or clinical symptoms. A publication by Rustin et al. (MRC
Ov05 Trial) [P07] showed that the early use of chemotherapy in cases of marker
recurrence did not improve overall prognosis. The extent to which the marker allows
early detection of an operable recurrence and that this improves the prognosis has
not been investigated. However, this would be bene�cial for patients, as operable
recurrences have a better overall prognosis according to the randomized Desktop-III
study [P08].
The KELIM score (Elimination of Constant K) is a new kinetic marker that is a good
predictor of response to primary chemotherapy (chemotherapy sensitivity). It
evaluates CA125 clearance. Patients with a KELIM score <1 have poor chemotherapy
sensitivity and will probably not bene�t especially well from chemotherapy. The
KELIM score is calculated using at least three CA125 values measured within 50 to
100 days of starting treatment and during chemotherapy. KELIM should ideally be
measured during each chemotherapy cycle [B13] (Almufti et al, Ann Oncol 2014).
[B13][P07][P08]
Estradiol
Certain stromal tumors (in particular granulosa cell tumors) can produce estradiol.
This can be used for diagnostic purposes in postmenopausal women.

AFP ((Alpha Feto Protein))
AFP is a very speci�c tumor marker for the detection and follow-up of germ cell
tumors and hepatocellular carcinoma. This protein cannot be interpreted during
pregnancy or in rare cases with benign liver conditions (hepatitis, cirrhosis) due to
false positives.
HCG ((Human Chorionic Gonadotropin))
HCG is formed in the syncytiotrophoblast cells during pregnancy and is often
elevated with germ cell tumors (dysgerminoma).
Inhibin AA//BB
Inhibin is formed in ovarian granulosa cells and serum levels are elevated with
granulosa cell tumors.

Staging and prognosis
Reference: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual 8th edition (2017) Springer
international publishing
