Treatment
For optimal gynecologic oncology treatment, preoperative testing should be used to
assess the probability of the presence of a malignant ovarian tumor. To do this, there
are diagnostic indices such as the RMI, which predict the presence of ovarian cancer
with high sensitivity. A de�nitive diagnosis is then established with intraoperative
frozen section analysis. If ovarian cancer is discovered incidentally, either during
surgery or during histological processing, without complete surgical staging being
planned and carried out, this should be repeated as quickly as possible (7 to 10
days) to prevent a poorer prognosis. If the primary surgery is laparoscopic, the trocar
wound sites should be excised.
Surgery
Surgery, in the sense of complete tumor resection/debulking, remains one of the
most important prognostic criteria for ovarian cancer. Even drug treatment cannot
compensate for insu�cient primary debulking. The main objective of surgical
treatment is complete tumor resection (= optimal cytoreductive surgery).
At the end of the surgery, the abdominal cavity should ideally be macroscopically
completely tumor-free (= R0 resection). Leaving behind residual tumor means a
poorer prognosis. As a result, ultraradical debulking surgeries to ultimately improve
the prognosis are not rare, although general status and fragility remain important
factors when selecting patients. Perioperative mortality can be higher for fragile
patients, so a balance must be struck between radicality and mortality. Nevertheless,
these patients should not be undertreated. Special geriatric assessments can help
with preoperative decision-making.
Surgical staging includes:
1. Palpation of the whole abdomen, including dome of the diaphragm
2. Hysterectomy with bilateral adnexectomy (�gure 3)
3. Pelvic + para-aortic lymphadenectomy in early stages for staging purposes.
According to data from the LION study, there is no bene�t to systematic
lymphadenectomy in advanced stages (from FIGO IIB).
4. Omentectomy
5. Appendectomy (obligatory in case of mucinous ovarian cancer)
�. Complete resection of macroscopic masses (including bowel resection,
splenectomy, partial hepatectomy, etc. if required)

Figure 3: Advanced epithelial ovarian cancer with involvement of both ovaries and uterus.
Neoadjuvant chemotherapy can be considered, but is not standard, in cases of very
advanced ovarian cancer, for example if primary macroscopic complete resection is
impossible due to tumor location or spread, or if the required level of radicality is not
clinically reasonable given the age, general condition or comorbidities of the patient.
Laparoscopic scores (such as the Fagotti score) can help evaluate the primary
operability in terms of feasibility of a R0 resection before primary debulking surgery.
With early chemotherapy, there is the danger of resistance developing on a biological
level. Several randomized studies have investigated neoadjuvant chemotherapy with
carboplatin and paclitaxel. It has not yet been possible to show a bene�t for this
approach. However, a study has shown lower preoperative mortality.
Another �eld for treatment optimization is intraoperative HIPEC chemotherapy or
intraperitoneal chemotherapy. The rationale behind this is that direct contact
between the chemotherapy and the tumor can increase cytotoxicity. This approach
has not yet been established in clinical routine, at least not at a global level. A
randomized study published in 2018 showed that, under study conditions, patients
who received HIPEC treatment with interval debulking (in case of R0 resection), i.e.
after three cycles of chemotherapy, had a better prognosis for progression-free
survival and overall survival versus intravenous chemotherapy alone [B17]. [B17]

In primary non-operable patients with extensive distant metastasis or comorbidities,
neoadjuvant chemotherapy prior to staging surgery can be considered.
Laparoscopy is not suitable for staging surgery in ovarian cancer.
For younger patients who still wish to have children, fertility-sparing treatment
(unilateral adnexectomy) can be discussed, only in early stages (T1a) and with good
additional criteria. Speci�cally, the cancer should be limited to one ovary (T1a),
histologically well de�ned (G1) with favorable histology (serous, endometrioid or
mucinous). Nevertheless, the increased risk of recurrence must be evoked.
Borderline tumors should be treated similarly to ovarian cancer by means of a
staging laparotomy. Larger series with a central pathology review have shown a
recurrence in approx. 3,7 to 7,6 % of cases on follow-up. In general, the procedure
should be the same as for invasive ovarian cancer, as in isolated cases there may be
upstaging with invasion. Surgical staging should include comprehensive inspection
and palpation of the whole abdomen with complete tumor resection (adnexectomy,
hysterectomy, wash cytology, omentectomy and peritoneal biopsy sampling) [B18,
B19].
Due to the unclear prognostic and therapeutic impact of lymphadenectomy with
clinically unremarkable retroperitoneal lymph nodes, this is not part of the standard
procedure for borderline tumors. It has not yet been possible to prove the bene�ts of
adjuvant therapy for borderline tumors, but this can be discussed in case of invasive
tumor implants. Fertility-sparing management is also possible with borderline
tumors in low-risk situations, and complete surgery can be planned for when the
patient no longer wishes to have children. [B18][B19]
Adjuvant chemotherapy

After optimal surgical treatment, adjuvant chemotherapy is generally carried out with
the aim of eliminating microscopic residual tumor and preventing recurrence. Today,
around 40 % of all epithelial ovarian cancers can generally be cured. The highest
response rate is achieved with platinum-based combination chemotherapy. Since
2004, the common standard is treatment with six cycles of carboplatin AUC5-6 in
combination with paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 every 3 weeks. Despite positive results in a
Japanese study, dose-dense and weekly treatment plans have not gained traction
due to subsequent neutral European and American studies. According to current
guidelines, patients with stage IA G1 cancer do not require adjuvant chemotherapy
and have an excellent prognosis with surgery alone. Patients with higher-grade
cancers should receive platinum-based chemotherapy [B20].
Maintenance therapy iinn primary treatment

Due to the poor prognosis of ovarian cancer, maintenance strategies have been
being researched for several decades, particularly for the common serous high-grade
subtype. For example, extended chemotherapy over one year with taxane and
radiotherapy approaches with whole abdomen radiotherapy have been investigated,
without success. Systemic bevacizumab was the �rst targeted therapy to be
recorded as a success. Bevacizumab is a monoclonal antibody against vascular
endothelial growth factor (VEGF). VEGF plays an important role in the formation of
new blood vessels, particularly in tumors, where it supports tumor growth and
metastasis. Bevacizumab inhibits the binding of VEGF to its receptors, thus blocking
the formation of new blood vessels in the tumor. This reduces the blood supply and
reverses tumor growth [B21].
With regard to ovarian cancer, several studies have been carried out to investigate
the e�cacy of bevacizumab in combination with chemotherapy. In the well-known
GOG-0218 study, it was demonstrated that the addition of bevacizumab to standard
chemotherapy signi�cantly improved the progression-free survival of patients with
advanced ovarian cancer. Similar results have been observed in other studies such
as ICON7 [B22, B23].
Furthermore, there are indications that bevacizumab could have a role to play in
platinum-refractory ovarian cancer. In the AURELIA study, combination therapy with
bevacizumab and chemotherapy showed signi�cantly longer progression-free
survival than chemotherapy alone.
Despite these promising results, further studies are required to better understand the
optimal dosage, treatment duration and potential adverse effects of bevacizumab in
ovarian cancer. Nevertheless, bevacizumab has the potential to expand the
treatment options for patients with ovarian cancer and improve their prognosis.
The second major breakthrough was the development of PARP inhibitors. One of the
�rst studies (Studie19) published several years ago showed a considerable bene�t
for patients with a recurrence of serous high-grade cancer. In this study, olaparib
after platinum-based chemotherapy in recurrence was tested against placebo and
showed clear superiority in PFS [B24]. In a later biomarker analysis, the effect was
demonstrated above all in patients with a BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation. Since then it
has been shown that patients with a BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation in particular clearly
bene�t from treatment with PARP inhibitors. Studies like SOLO1 have shown that
PARP inhibitors like olaparib can signi�cantly increase disease-free survival in
patients with BRCA-mutated ovarian cancer. Furthermore, other clinical studies such
as PAOLA-1 suggest that the combination of PARP inhibitors with bevacizumab can
improve progression-free survival in advanced ovarian cancer. Here too, the bene�t

was seen in particular for patients with a BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation, and in patients
with tumors with impaired DNA repair (HRD de�ciency).
After the approval of olaparib, a second PARP inhibitor, niraparib, was approved in
Switzerland. This also led to a rapid development. First, a clear bene�t versus
placebo could be shown in recurrence of HRD+/BRCA+, as well as BRCAwt serous
high-grade cancer (PFS bene�t).
In the PRIMA study, patients were included after initial treatment with surgery and
chemotherapy. Of the 733 randomized patients, 373 (50.9 %) had tumors with HRD
de�ciency. Among patients in this category, progression-free survival was
signi�cantly longer in the niraparib group than in the placebo group (21.9 months vs.
10.4 months; hazard ratio [HR] for disease progression or death: 0.43; 95 %
con�dence interval [CI]: 0.31 to 0.59; P < 0.001). In the overall population, the
corresponding progression-free survival was 13.8 months and 8.2 months (HR: 0.62;
95 % CI: 0.50 to 0.76; P < 0.001). The most common adverse events of grade 3 or
higher were anemia (in 31,0 % of patients), thrombocytopenia (in 28,7 %) and
neutropenia (in 12,8 %). No treatment-related deaths occurred.
Development with these drugs is progressing rapidly. Other 2nd generation PARP
inhibitors are currently in development. This aims above all to effectively block
PARP-1 or PARP-2 activity, and/or selectively inhibit PARP-1 to minimize adverse
effects such as anemia. [B21][B22][B23]
Carboplatin (AUC 5-6) in combination with paclitaxel (175 mg/m2) at three-weekly
intervals for six cycles remains the standard treatment. Longer treatment (e.g. 7 to 8
cycles) or the addition of a third drug does not give any further bene�t with regard to
disease control and should only be tested within studies.
The addition of a targeted anti-VEGF antibody (bevacizumab) gives a slight
improvement in disease-free and overall survival in advanced ovarian cancer (stage
IIIB to IV) and is the standard in high-risk situations in Switzerland. For BRCA+
patients and HRD+ tumors, PARP inhibitors are now standard as maintenance in
�rst-line and second-line treatment.
Recurrence

The majority (approx. 60 %) of patients with serous high-grade ovarian cancer with
complete remission after surgical treatment and adjuvant chemotherapy experience
recurrence within the �rst �ve years. Prognostic factors have been covered above.
Classically the dogma of platinum resistance applies, de�ned with the platinum-free
interval. The management of recurrence has changed fundamentally in recent years.
The AGO DESKTOP III trial prospectively evaluated secondary surgery with
chemotherapy and showed an improved prognosis for complete secondary resection
(R0) versus chemotherapy alone. All cases of recurrence after primary treatment
should therefore be discussed at a multidisciplinary tumor board. With regard to the
use of platinum-based chemotherapy, the terms “platinum-eligible” and “platinum-
ineligible” are today widely used in the literature. The longer the platinum-free
interval, the more likely a patient is to respond again to platinum-based
chemotherapy. However, other factors are relevant to decision-making: Bene�t of
maintenance therapy with a PARP inhibitor, CA125 kinetics, bene�t in treatment
response in �rst-line therapy etc. Ultimately it is always an individual decision that
can vary from case to case.
Antibody-drug conjugates (ADC) are a newer treatment option that will become
routine in the coming months and years. They combine an antibody against a
speci�c tumor antigen such as HER2, Trop2, folate receptor etc., a linker that binds
the antibody to the cytotoxic chemotherapy and a chemotherapy molecule. The Pan-
HER2-DESTINY study included patients with a gynecological tumor disease including
ovarian cancer that was HER2-expressing (immunohistochemistry [IHC] 3+/2+).
Patients with ovarian cancer had a response rate of up to 50 %.
In another study (SORAYA) investigating the ADC mirvetuximab soravtansine in
folate receptor-alpha (FRα) positive ovarian cancer. Patients with platinum-refractory
ovarian cancer who had already received treatment (51 % 3 lines or more) including
PARP inhibitors. Among the 105 patients, a response rate of 32.4 % was reported,
including 29 partial responses. The most common adverse events were blurred
vision, keratopathy and nausea [B25].
Conventional chemotherapy such as paclitaxel weekly, PLD and topotecan are
inferior to the new substance in terms of response rate and progression-free
survival. However, they continue to have their place in the chain of treatment.
Patients who develop a late recurrence have a high probability of responding to
second-line chemotherapy with complete remission and therefore also have a much
better prognosis. Unlike primary therapy, which is intended to cure, it is rare for

Tumors:: ""IIss the management different??""
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr.
med. Viola Heinzelmann-
Schwarz
Author:: PPDD DDrr.. Marcus Vetter
Leitender Arzt Medizinische Onkologie, Schwerpunkt Gynäkologische Tumoren

Summary
This EGONE Chapter G13B gives the reader an in-depth overview of non-epithelial
ovarian cancer. It focuses on ovarian germ cell tumors and sex cord tumors. Readers
will be guided systematically through diagnosis, genetics, treatment and follow-up.
The treatment of these orphan diseases/rare cancers is a challenge for clinicians.
The European Network for Gynecological Oncological Trial groups (ENGOT) is
currently running a trial group caring for patients with these rare tumors. In general,
management of these rare tumors should be carried out in larger Cancer Centers
with diagnostic/pathological and surgical expertise, as this improves prognosis.

topic: Treatment of Non Epithelial Ovarian Tumors is the management different (Teil 2/16)
keywords: treatment of non epithelial ovarian tumors is the management different
content:
Introduction and DDee��nition
According to the latest WHO classi�cation (table 1), ovarian tumors are divided into
the following categories: epithelial cancers (65 %), embryonal (germ cell) tumors (15
%), sex cord tumors (10 %), metastases (5 %) and other/mixed subtypes (5 %). This
EGONE Chapter G13B gives an overview of the epidemiology, diagnosis, treatment
and prognosis of non-epithelial ovarian tumors. It focuses on germ cell and sex cord
tumors. It also highlights recent �ndings from pathology and genetics, which should
improve the diagnosis and treatment of these overall highly heterogeneous and rare
cancers.
ENGOT has formed a Rare Tumor Group tasked with expanding research into rare
ovarian tumors [P01]. This initiative also aims to develop clinical trials in Europe to
investigate this very rare subgroup of ovarian tumors.
Current data is generally based on retrospective analysis. Due to the low incidence of
this group of cancers, national prospective studies are practically impossible and
registries are thus under development [P02]. The �rst step towards this is the
formation of a European rare cancer database including biobanking, in order to
better investigate the tumors. Several Swiss centers support this initiative through
the Swiss Group for Clinical Cancer Research (SAKK) network. [P01][P02]

topic: Treatment of Non Epithelial Ovarian Tumors is the management different (Teil 4/16)
keywords: treatment of non epithelial ovarian tumors is the management different
content:
Germ cell and sex cord tumors represent approximately 25 % of ovarian tumors. The
adjusted incidence rate is 3,7 cases/100,000/year for embryonal tumors and
2,1/100,000/year for sex cord tumors [P03]. These tumors therefore meet the
de�nition of “rare cancer”, which requires an incidence of under 6 cases per 100'000
inhabitants. There are not yet any specialized centers for the treatment of non-
epithelial ovarian tumors. However, it is generally recommended that rare cancers
are treated in highly specialized cancer centers, as this leads to a better prognosis
[P04]. Embryonal tumors typically affect young patients up to 25 years of age. The
median age is 18 years. The most common subgroup is mature teratoma with 95 %.
Sex cord tumors are generally found in all age groups. Granulosa cell tumors and
thecomas are most often found in peri- and postmenopausal patients. Conversely,
juvenile granulosa cell tumors, Sertoli cell tumors and Sertoli-Leydig cell tumors are
most often diagnosed in adolescents and young adults [P05]. For epithelial ovarian
cancers, diagnosis is often in higher age groups, with a median age of 62 years
[P06]. [P03][P04][P05][P06]

topic: Treatment of Non Epithelial Ovarian Tumors is the management different (Teil 5/16)
keywords: treatment of non epithelial ovarian tumors is the management different
content:
Diagnostic Work--uupp ooff Non--Epithelial
Ovarian Cancer
There are no characteristic symptoms in the case of germ cell/sex cord tumors.
Patients may present with subacute abdominal and pelvic pain and a feeling of
pressure in the pelvis. There may also be menstrual disorders. With pronounced
tumors, there may be symptoms relating to the compression of the tumor, e.g.
urinary retention due to ureter compression. With rare sex chord tumors and steroid
cell tumors, there may be increased production of steroid/sex hormones. Increased
androgen production, for example, can lead to virilization.
Diagnostic work-up generally includes: (ESMO guidelines):
• Transvaginal ultrasound
• Abdomino-pelvic computed tomography (CT) scan
• chest X-ray or CT scan
• In selected cases, the use of a positron emission tomography (PET) CT scan
can be useful.
• Laboratory tests with blood count and metabolic panel including tumor
markers: LDH, alpha-fetoprotein, hCG, inhibin and CA125
[P07]
The value of PET-CT has not yet been con�rmed in clinical trials, but there are some
reports that show superiority to conventional imaging. In a case report on a
granulosa cell tumor, the different and rather low FDG uptake could be shown [P08].
PET-CT may also be useful for the diagnosis of late recurrences [P09]. The value of
PET-CT is otherwise being investigated. [P08][P09]
Contrary to epithelial ovarian cancer where CA125 is the most important tumor
marker, other tumor markers are used with non-epithelial tumors (see table 2). With
classic germ cell tumors, above all with dysgerminoma, lactate dehydrogenase
(LDH) is used, as with male germ cell tumors. For non-dysgerminoma, alpha-
fetoprotein (AFP) and human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) are used. Inhibin B is
used for sex cord tumors (granulosa cell tumors). Tumor markers are important for
assessing progress after multimodal therapy and can trigger imaging and re-
resection.
Table 2: Data on fertility with germ cell tumor following multimodal therapy (adapted from [P16, P19]).
Studies investigating reproductive function after fertility-preserving surgery and platinum-based
chemotherapy. [P16][P19]

topic: Treatment of Non Epithelial Ovarian Tumors is the management different (Teil 7/16)
keywords: treatment of non epithelial ovarian tumors is the management different
content:
Genetics:: Molecular speci��cities and
special molecular markers iinn the
diagnosis ooff germ cell//sex cord tumors
The pathogenesis of germ cell/sex cord tumors is still not fully understood. In terms
of molecular genetics, this is a heterogeneous group of tumors. During development,
various processes play a role in transforming stem cells into mature cells.
Physiologically, various factors affect how the primordial germ cell develops into the
oocyte and ultimately the primordial follicle. When genetic or exogenous factors lead
to development being disrupted, gonadoblastoma or other malignant germ cell
tumors can develop in gonadal dysgenesis [P10]. This activates important molecular
genetic pathways: KIT, TGF-beta or WNT signaling [P11]. The type of signal alteration
determines the type of tumor that develops: dysgerminoma, yolk sac tumor,
immature teratoma, embryonal cancer or non-gestational choriocarcinoma. Germ
cell tumors re�ect the capacity of multiple stem cell lines at different stages of
differentiation.
Molecular markers iinn diagnosis and prognosis assessment
For the treatment of germ cell/sex cord tumors, it is essential, particularly if fertility-
preserving surgery and the use of chemotherapy is planned, that a precise
histopathological diagnosis is established. Diagnosis should be established by
experienced gyneco-oncological pathologists and in speci�c cases external review
should also be considered. Diagnosis can generally be established from
conventional HE sections using immunohistochemistry. In speci�c cases, molecular
markers such as SALL4, OCT4 and SOX2 are also a recent tool.
For adult granulosa cell tumors, a somatic missense mutation in the FOXL2 gene
C134W was discovered a few years ago. This is not found in epithelial ovarian
cancers. FoxL2 mutation is associated with a poorer prognosis for this tumor [P12].
FoxL2 is required for granulosa cell determination. It prevents the formation of
testes by suppressing expression of SOX9. Women without FOXL2 appear male.
FOXL2 knockout in mouse ovaries causes testes to develop [P13]. If a FOXL2
mutation is found, an epithelial tumor origin can be ruled out and a granulosa cell
tumor diagnosed. [P12][P13]

topic: Treatment of Non Epithelial Ovarian Tumors is the management different (Teil 8/16)
keywords: treatment of non epithelial ovarian tumors is the management different
content:
Treatment and Management ooff Germ Cell
Tumors
Surgical management ooff germ cell tumors
Patients with germ cell tumors are generally younger adults. Diagnosis of 60 % of
cases takes place at FIGO stage I. It is important to take fertility preservation into
account for the treatment of young patients. Surgical management is key for initial
diagnosis and treatment. In early stages, a laparoscopic procedure is possible.
Extensive staging, as for epithelial ovarian cancer, is controversial, as is the extent of
staging surgery. Prognosis at FIGO stage I is generally very good with a 5-year overall
survival rate of > 90 % [P14].
The role of systematic lymphadenectomy is not clear and there is no international
consensus. With dysgerminoma, lymphadenectomy can be discussed, as this more
commonly affects the lymph nodes, although these tumors are also sensitive to
chemotherapy. Based on current ESMO guidelines, only the removal of lymph nodes
found to be abnormal during surgery or on imaging is recommended [P07].
Fertility-preserving surgery is generally possible in early stages with an excellent
outcome. Now, this procedure is also recommended for advanced stages, as the
disease is usually chemotherapy-sensitive and there are rescue strategies with good
prognosis [P07]. If the patient plans to have no more children, there is a consensus
to carry out hysterectomy and bilateral adnexectomy. However, the data of Nasioudis
D. et. al [P15] showed that hysterectomy did not improve prognosis after diagnosis
of FIGO stage II-IV germ cell tumors. Of the 526 patients with malignant germ cell
tumors investigated, 20 % had undergone hysterectomy as part of tumor treatment.
The median age of patients who had undergone hysterectomy was 10 years older
than that of patients who were not treated with hysterectomy (median age 30,5 vs.
20 years, p < 0,001). [P07][P14][P15]
Systemic germ cell tumor therapy

topic: Treatment of Non Epithelial Ovarian Tumors is the management different (Teil 9/16)
keywords: treatment of non epithelial ovarian tumors is the management different
content:
The indication for adjuvant chemotherapy in case of stage I germ cell tumors is
controversial [P16]. From stage II, there is a clear recommendation for adjuvant
chemotherapy, particularly for immature teratoma and dysgerminoma [P07]. Yolk sac
tumors should be treated with chemotherapy as per the BEP regimen (bleomycin/
etoposide/cisplatin over �ve days) from FIGO stage I. Active surveillance is also
possible in stage IA/B.
For very large tumors, neo-adjuvant chemotherapy, e.g. as per the BEP regimen, may
be indicated [P17]. This then enables better resectability. For older patients (> 40
years), a bleomycin-free regimen should be used, particularly due to pulmonary
toxicity. At FIGO stage IA, surgical treatment alone is generally su�cient. Risk factors
that are immaterial for prognosis and decisive for chemotherapy are histological
subtype and extent of surgery. Prognosis is generally poorer in case of recurrence.
Salvage therapy includes treatment with vinblastine, ifosfamide, cisplatin (VeIP) or
paclitaxel, ifosfamide, cisplatin [P07]. [P07][P16][P17]
Salvage therapy//high--dose therapy
High-dose chemotherapy is a treatment option in cases of recurrence, as with male
germ cell tumors. This treatment has not been investigated in large-scale
randomized studies. Retrospective data from De Giorgi et. al. showed a good
response rate (complete remission of 33 %, marker-negative partial remission of 16
%, clinical bene�t rate of 75 %) with longer progression-free survival after high-dose
therapy with carboplatin [P18]. In this retrospective analysis with 51 patients (median
age 27 years), there was a high proportion of yolk sac tumors (33 %). Most primary
tumors were in the ovaries (67 %). Third-line treatment was administered to 48% of
patients. Toxicity was responsible for 6 % of deaths in the study. After a median
follow-up of 87 months after high-dose therapy, 25 (42 %) of patients were
progression-free and therefore probably recovered. [P18]
Reproductive function after multimodal treatment

topic: Treatment of Non Epithelial Ovarian Tumors is the management different (Teil 10/16)
keywords: treatment of non epithelial ovarian tumors is the management different
content:
After multimodal treatment with surgery and chemotherapy, reproductive function
can be preserved in many cases. An analysis of various series (see Table 3) and a
2007 study by Gershenson et. al with over 300 patients showed 68-99 % of patients
had normal menstrual cycles after multimodal therapy and over 160 pregnancies
[P19]. In this study, the median patient age was 24 years. A second analysis from
2015 by Solheim et al. showed that in 61 patients with malignant germ cell tumors,
gonadal function recovered after platinum-based adjuvant chemotherapy [P20].
There was a statistically signi�cant difference between patients who received more
or fewer than three cycles of chemotherapy. The 15-year cumulative fertility was 28
%. Anti-Müllerian hormone was measured for 22 patients, 2 of whom showed levels
under 3 pmol/l.
Patients should always be informed of the risk of infertility before treatment is
initiated, and fertility consultations/treatment should be carried out. [P19][P20]

topic: Treatment of Non Epithelial Ovarian Tumors is the management different (Teil 11/16)
keywords: treatment of non epithelial ovarian tumors is the management different
content:
Treatment and Management ooff Sex Cord
Tumors
Sex cord tumors:: General comments oonn sex cord tumors
Sex cord-stromal tumors are also very rarely encountered in gynecological-
oncological practice, representing 5-10 % of all ovarian tumors. The median age of
onset is 50 years and therefore much higher than that of germ cell tumors, but still
lower than that of epithelial ovarian cancers. With regard to diagnosis, histological
con�rmation is a challenge, although this has been improved by molecular
processes in recent years. Roughly 60-90 % of sex cord tumors are diagnosed at
FIGO stage I [P05].
These tumors vary considerably and an expert review/second-look review is
indicated in many cases. Histologically, there are microfollicular, trabecular, diffuse
and other forms. In terms of immunohistochemistry, the markers inhibin-alpha,
calretinin, WT1, CD56 and CK8/18 are often found. In many cases, up to 70 % of
these tumors present with estrogen secretion. In 25 % of cases there is also
endometrial hyperplasia, and in 5-10 % of cases endometrial cancer. This should be
taken into account for the diagnostic work-up.
The most common sex cord tumor 70 %) is granulosa cell tumor. The most common
symptom on diagnosis is abdominal pain [P21]. Age is an important factor in the
prognosis. Younger patients (< 50 years) generally have a better prognosis with a 5-
year disease-free survival of 93 %. Conversely, older patients (> 50 years) have a
poorer prognosis with a 5-year disease-free survival of 84 %. Other negative
prognostic factors include: Tumor/cyst rupture during surgery and higher FIGO stage
[P13].
If granulosa cell tumors or steroid cell tumors occur in childhood, endocrine
manifestations can result in pseudopuberty. Typical symptoms in patients of
reproductive age include menstrual irregularities, secondary amenorrhea, infertility
and virilization [P22]. In post-menopause, abnormal vaginal bleeding may occur.
Endometrial hyperplasia is determined in 25 % of cases and endometrial cancer in
5-10 % of cases [P23]. [P05][P13][P21][P22][P23]
Surgical therapy ooff sex cord tumors

topic: Treatment of Non Epithelial Ovarian Tumors is the management different (Teil 12/16)
keywords: treatment of non epithelial ovarian tumors is the management different
content:
Surgery is the most important form of treatment for sex cord tumors. Complete
staging comprises: Bilateral adnexectomy, hysterectomy, omentectomy (infracolic),
peritoneal biopsy and lymphadenectomy, although the latter is also controversial for
granulosa cell tumors. In early stages, retroperitoneal lymph node involvement is
very rare. In young patients and at FIGO stage IA, a fertility-preserving procedure and
uterine curettage (granulosa cell tumor) may be an option. Complete surgical staging
should be carried out from stage II [P07]. [P07]
Systemic therapy ooff sex cord tumors
Additive/adjuvant chemotherapy, similar to germ cell tumors, should take place with
3-4 cycles of the BEP regimen from FIGO stage II-IV. Patients with differentiated
histology in stage I should also receive three cycles of BEP chemotherapy. For older
patients, chemotherapy with carboplatin/paclitaxel may also be used as an
alternative, although the regimen has not been compared with the BEP regimen
[P24]. [P24]
Speci��cities ooff Sertoli--Leydig cell tumors
This sex cord tumor generally occurs in young women under 30 years of age (75 % in
under 30s). The tumor is generally diagnosed at FIGO stage I (> 95% of cases). It is
very rare (< 2 %) that both ovaries are affected. In over 50 % of cases, this tumor
manifests clinically with androgenization [P25]. [P25]
DICER11 syndrome
DICER1 syndrome has been described with juvenile granulosa cell tumors and
Sertoli-Lydig cell tumors. It is an inherited disease the increases the risk of various
benign and malignant tumors, e.g. sex cord tumors, cervical and embryonal
rhabdomyosarcoma [P26]. DICER1 is a protein that converts double-stranded RNA
and pre-microRNA into single-stranded short RNA fragments. Approximately 88 % of
patients affected by the syndrome have at least one DICER1 mutation of the DICER
gene [P27]. Mutations are only found with moderately to poorly-differentiated Sertoli-
Lydig cell tumors. Approximately 50 % of Sertoli-Lydig cell tumors have a DICER1
hotspot mutation. This causes a modi�cation in global gene expression. Aromatase,
FSHR, PGR and follistatin are reduced. FGH9 and FGFR2 are overexpressed [P28].
[P26][P27][P28]

topic: Treatment of Non Epithelial Ovarian Tumors is the management different (Teil 13/16)
keywords: treatment of non epithelial ovarian tumors is the management different
content:
Follow--uupp
Aftercare for germ cell tumors is based on clinical/gynecological examination,
laboratory tests including tumor markers (hCG, alpha-FP, LDH, CA125 and inhibin B)
and imaging such as ultrasound and CT, particularly in case of residual disease after
chemotherapy. Approximately 75 % of recurrences are diagnosed in the �rst year
after completion of therapy. Late toxicities of chemotherapy must also be taken into
consideration in clinical follow-up. For the BEP regimen, this refers to pulmonary
toxicity in particular (bleomycin) and hematological neoplasms, particularly acute
myeloid leukemia.
Follow-up for sex cord tumors also includes clinical examination, laboratory tests
(inhibin B, AMH) and imaging. Late recurrence (> 10 years) is also common.
In general, follow-up should be similar to that for epithelial ovarian cancer. This
means 3-monthly intervals in years 1 and 2, 6-monthly intervals in years 3-5 and
annual intervals in years 5-10. Follow-up extending beyond 10 years should take
place for sex cord tumors (Table 4).

topic: Precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina (Teil 1/11)
content:
EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Precancerous lesions ooff vulva//vagina
〉 Precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina
Last update: April 2024
Precancerous lesions ooff the vulva and
vagina
Author:: Prof.. DDrr.. med.. Mathias Fehr
Chefarzt Gynäkologie & Geburtshilfe, Ärztliche Leitung Brustzentrum Thurgau, Kantonsspital Frauenfeld,
Spital Thurgau AG

Vulvar intraepithelial neoplasia
Classi��cation
Squamous intraepithelial neoplasia are to be distinguished from non-squamous
intraepithelial neoplasia.
Squamous form ooff vulvar intraepithelial neoplasia
According to the current 2014 WHO classi�cation, the term VIN I-III (vulvar
intraepithelial neoplasia) has been replaced by the following nomenclature [B01]:
• Vulvar LSIL (low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion) replaces the term VIN I
and is the clinical and histological correlation of an HPV infection with low risk
of concomitant or future invasive neoplasia.
• Vulvar HSIL (high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion) replaces the terms VIN
II and III (usual type) and is an expression of an HPV infection with increased
risk of developing an invasive carcinoma.
• Differentiated VIN (d-VIN) refers to an HPV-negative VIN on the basis of chronic
dermatosis such as lichen sclerosus and lichen planus.
• Terms that used to be common, such as vulvar intraepithelial neoplasia (VIN),
Bowen's disease, bowenoid papulosis, erythroplasia of Queyrat, are all replaced
by the terms vulvar LSIL/HSIL or d-VIN.
[B01]
CAVE: Doctors must always be aware of whether HSIL is cytological (cell smear) or
histological. Cytological diagnostics should not play any role in vulvar conditions, so
vulvar LSIL/HSIL always refer to histological diagnoses.
Non-squamous forms of vulvar intraepithelial neoplasia
• Vulvar Paget's disease (intraepithelial adenocarcinoma)
• Melanoma in situ (preinvasive melanoma)
Occurrence and incidence
Vulvar squamous intraepithelial lesions

topic: Precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina (Teil 2/11)
keywords: precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina
content:
The incidence of vulvar HSIL cannot be reliably determined, as only very few cancer
registries have recorded these precancerous conditions to date. In the canton of
Waadt (Switzerland), the incidence is given as 2,9/100’000 women per year, which is
certainly too low. In a large prospective study of women under 25 years old, the
cumulative incidence of vulvar HSIL was 0,6 % within four years, corresponding to
150/100’000 [P06].
Vulvar HSIL is some 10 times more common than lesions of the vagina, but 10 times
rarer than high grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia. The incidence has increased
by over 400 % in the last 30 years, particularly in younger women. This increase is
probably caused by the increase in HPV infections, which are responsible for vulvar
HSIL. The average age of onset in Switzerland is 47 years [P01]. [P06][P01]
In terms of etiopathology, a distinction is made between:
• the common squamous intraepithelial lesion (SIL) associated with HPV
(histologically basaloid or condylomatous)
• the rarer (< 10 %) differentiated VIN (d-VIN), which shows no association with
HPV (generally p53 mutation). Lichen sclerosus or other chronic dermatosis of
the vulvar skin (lichen ruber) is often present.
Low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (LSIL)
• Low-grade cell changes caused by infection with HPV, high and low risk.
• Low risk of progression to invasive neoplasia.
High-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (HSIL)
• With this HPV high-risk associated intraepithelial neoplasia, HPV 16 is found in
76 % of cases. The lesion is often multifocal.
• The progression rate to invasive vulvar carcinoma is di�cult to determine.. In a
study, it was 12 % for HSIL that went untreated for one year.
• Up to 50 % of women with vulvar HSIL also have, at the same time or in their
personal history, squamous cell carcinoma or intraepithelial neoplasia of the
cervix or anus.
[P02][P03]
• Approx. 6 % of patients with a history of cervical HSIL develop vulvar or vaginal
HSIL years later. Another study with 3030 women with treated CIN 2+ or
microinvasive cervical carcinoma reports that 7,4 % of these women developed
a vaginal intraepithelial neoplasm (grade 2 or higher) within 45 months on

topic: Precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina (Teil 3/11)
keywords: precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina
content:
average. Regular colposcopic check-ups are therefore recommended for
women who have undergone hysterectomy due to cervical HSIL.
• Conversely, up to 47 % of patients who were treated for vulvar HSIL later
developed an anal intraepithelial neoplasm (AIN). The anal skin should
therefore also be checked during each colposcopy after CIN or VIN and at least
with HPV persistence an anoscopy to the dentate line is recommended.
[P04][P09][P10]
Spontaneous regression of vulvar HSIL occurs in young patients, as does the
regression of HSIL after pregnancy. However, there are no reliable predictive factors
for spontaneous regression [P05]. [P05]
Differentiated VIN ((dd--VIN))
• The invasive potential of differentiated VIN seems to be much higher than that
of vulvar HSIL. A ‘wait-and-see’ approach is therefore not recommended.
• Spontaneous regression has not been reported.
• Differentiated VIN occurs in older patients (usually > 60 years old) and against a
background of chronic vulvar skin disorders (lichen sclerosus and planus).
Extramammary Paget''ss disease ooff the vulva
• This affects women of peri and postmenopausal age, most often aged 60 to 70
years old.
• Extramammary Paget’s disease accounts for approximately 1 % of all vulvar
neoplasms.
• In 4 to 17 % of cases, invasive adenocarcinomas are found within or below the
surface lesion - the invasive Paget’s carcinoma. These often originate from skin
appendages.
Preferred locations include the vulvar and perineal skin, although Paget’s disease
can also occur on the axilla, areola (--> G10A), buttocks and eyelids.
Histology
High--grade Squamous intraepithelial lesions ((HSIL))
No further information will be given here on LSIL, as they are HPV infections of the
vulvar skin (see Condyloma chapter).
HSIL are characterized by:
• Increasing loss of normal epithelial maturation
• Increasing nuclear-cytoplasmic ratio in favor of nucleus

topic: Precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina (Teil 4/11)
keywords: precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina
content:
• Polymorphism
• Hyperchromasia
• Chromatin clumping
• Polynuclearism
• Single cell keratinization
• Mitotic nuclear activity
• p16 positive (overexpressed)
Figure 1: HPV associated vulvar HSIL.
Differentiated VIN ((dd--VIN))
• They are characterized by basal cell atypia with hyperchromasia, karyomegaly
and atypical mitosis of the basal cell layer.
• There is often normal terminal differentiation, but with dykeratosis and
hyperkeratosis.
• P16 negative (not overexpressed).
• Differentiated VIN is occasionally di�cult to distinguish from benign
hyperplasia and hypertrophic variants of lichen sclerosus.
Paget’’ss disease
• Paget’s disease of the vulva shows large adenocarcinoma cells, individually or
in clusters throughout all layers of the epidermis.
• Hair shafts and excretory ducts of sweat glands are also affected.
• Paget’s disease is also referred to as “adenocarcinoma in situ limited to the
epidermis”.

topic: Precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina (Teil 5/11)
keywords: precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina
content:
DD: Cells of a melanoma in situ can look very similar to Paget’s disease in terms of
morphology and distribution pattern. Additional immunohistochemical tests are
often indispensable (Paget’s disease = positive for CK 7 and CEA; melanoma in situ =
positive for S-100 and HMB-45).
Signs and symptoms
HSIL//Differentiated VIN ((dd--VIN))
Diagnosis often takes place during a routine check-up.
• There are often no symptoms (20 %).
• The main symptoms of vulvar HSIL and d-VIN are pruritus, burning and papules.
Paget’’ss disease
Symptoms mainly consist of pruritus and scaling redness. The latency between
symptoms and diagnosis is long, on average 2 years.
Figure 2: Paget’s disease of the vulva with mixed red and scaling white skin changes.
Diagnosis
Careful inspection of the vulvar skin with a colposcope (vulvoscopy) is required for
diagnosis. The region from the groin covering the mons pubis to the perianal skin
must be inspected.
HSIL//Differentiated VIN ((dd--VIN))
The use of 2 to 5 % acetic acid clari�es the extent of the HPV-associated neoplasia
by causing the thickened epithelium to swell up and scatter light, hence appearing
white. Differentiated VIN shows no reaction to acetic acid.

topic: Precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina (Teil 6/11)
keywords: precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina
content:
Figure 3: HSIL: Swelling of thickened epithelium scattering light after application of acetic acid 5
%(keratosis).
Although the vulva offers favorable conditions for diagnosis, vulvar carcinoma
continue to be diagnosed at later stages. This is because many patients only consult
a doctor late, and because the clinical picture of vulvar HSIL varies (and
misdiagnoses therefore occur). D-VIN is found in lesions of chronic dermatosis (e.g.
lichen sclerosus or lichen planus), which also complicates its diagnosis due to
alterated surrounding skin.
Figure 4: Pronounced d-VIN with surrounding lichen sclerosus.
In addition to visual inspection, colposcopy is key to diagnosis, as is histological
testing of all suspicious �ndings (using punch biopsy). Depending on the extent of
the lesion, multiple punch biopsies may be required, as microinvasive areas within
lesions clinically diagnosed as HSIL occur in up to 20 % of cases.
Paget’’ss disease

topic: Precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina (Teil 7/11)
keywords: precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina
content:
Invasiveness and thus transition into a Paget’s carcinoma is rare. However, it is
important to note that Paget’s disease is associated with other malignancies,
particularly urogenital and rectal carcinoma, in 10 to 15 % of cases. This must
therefore be ruled out. In addition, extensive veri�cation of other organs must be
carried out. Generally, in addition to gynecological investigations this involves lung,
abdominal and pelvis CT, mammography, cystoscopy and colonoscopy.
Treatment
HSIL//differentiated VIN ((dd--VIN))
The vaccination available against HPV 16 and 18 prevents vulvar SIL to a large
extent. Treatment of HSIL (like for early invasive vulvar carcinoma) should,
particularly in young patients, spare as many vulvar structures as possible [P06].
[P06]
There is no single superior treatment method. Even radical surgeries like vulvectomy
and skinning vulvectomy with split skin graft show similarly high recurrence rates to
local excision with a safety margin or biopsy and laser ablation (21 to 30 %).
• With less extensive cases, excision of the affected skin is ideal. The size of the
safety margin is unclear.
• For more extensive lesions, laser ablation can be used to conserve structures
(although multiple punch biopsies or excisions must be taken for histological
testing, so that any invasiveness is not overlooked).
Laser ablation is successful for the hairless skin of the vulva due to the lack of skin
appendages. The skin, together with the papillary dermis, must be ablated; i.e. until
the collagen �bers of the dermis are visible (level III as per Reid). For skin with hair,
the skin must be ablated to the hair follicle, which requires a very long recovery
period of 4 to 6 weeks and is therefore to be avoided where possible. Excision is
therefore preferred for skin with hair.

topic: Precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina (Teil 8/11)
keywords: precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina
content:
Immunostimulation with imiquimod 5 % (Aldara®, 3 times per week with application
in the evening and washing off in the morning) has shown complete response in 35
% and 81 % of patients in two small randomized trials. However, in the follow-up
period of just one year, invasive vulvar carcinoma occurred in one of the 26 patients.
This drug treatment, which works on the basis of immunological elimination of HSIL,
should therefore only be used with close follow-up [P03][P07][P11].
The issue of recurrence! More than 1/3 of patients treated using this method
experience a recurrence locally or in another part of the lower genital tract. [P03][P07]
[P11]
50 % of recurrences occur at the previous treatment location, and the other 50 % in
other, sometimes distant, locations. Distant recurrences are referred to as �eld
cancerization, i.e. the recurrence of HSIL occurs years later in the vagina or anus.
This is the expression of the multifocal malignant transformation after an oncogenic
HPV infection of the anogenital skin.
The risk of developing an invasive carcinoma of the vulva, vagina or anus after
treated vulvar HSIL is 6 %. 14 % of patients experience a recurrence after more than
�ve years from �rst diagnosis, highlighting the importance of regular, long-term
follow-up [P08]. [P08]
Paget’’ss disease
Vulvar Paget’s disease is di�cult to treat. An excision in healthy subjects is seldom
successful. If a safety margin is respected, the recurrence rate is approx. 10 %,
otherwise it is 50 %. Immunomodulatory treatment with imiquimod cream has also
been used successfully for Paget’s disease of the vulva. Due to the rarity of the
disease, however, there are no large case series. However, in large lesions Imiquimod
5 % three times a week is an option: A study with 24 patients treated over 16 weeks
showed a response rate of 80 % with a complete histological response in 50 % [B03].
Radiotherapy and photodynamic therapy may be treatment options, although no
large studies are available here either. [B03]

topic: Precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina (Teil 9/11)
keywords: precancerous lesions of the vulva and vagina
content:
Vaginal intraepithelial neoplasia
According to the current 2014 WHO classi�cation, the term VaIN I-III (vaginal
intraepithelial neoplasia) has been replaced by the following nomenclature:
• Vaginal LSIL (low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion) replaces the term VaIN
I and is the clinical and histological expression of a HPV infection with low risk
of concomitant or future invasive neoplasia.
• Vaginal HSIL (high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion) replaces the terms
VaIN II and III (usual type) and is an expression of a HPV infection with
increased risk of developing an invasive carcinoma.
[B01]
Occurrence and incidence
Vaginal HSIL is generally HPV-positive (> 75 % HPV-16) and mostly located in the
upper or lower third of the vagina. Multifocality in 60 % of cases. The incidence of
HSIL is low with 0,2-0,4 cases per 100'000 women per year. However, in women who
have undergone hysterectomy due to cervical HSIL, the risk of later developing
vaginal HSIL is 3,7 to 7,4 %. Overall, vaginal HSIL is probably often overlooked.
However, as vaginal carcinoma is a rare disease, the signi�cance remains unclear
[P10]. [P10]
Diagnosis
• Colposcopy (use of 2 to 5 % acetic acid and Lugol's iodine - Schiller's Iodine
test) and targeted biopsy.
• The frequency of occult invasion after biopsy-based diagnosis of vaginal HSIL
is up to 30 %.
Treatment
• CO2-Laser evaporation of vaginal HSIL is an established treatment modality
with few side effects and conservation of the function of the vagina.
• Intravaginal treatment with imiquimod 5 % is the second established treatment
option with a complete pathological response rate of 76% in a metaanalysis of
observational studies [B04].
• Cranial colpectomy for vaginal HSIL should be limited to severe cases and is
not standard of care anymore.
• The frequency of recurrence seems to depend on treatment (almost 0 % after
colpectomy and approx. 40 % after laser treatment).

content:
EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Psychosomatics 〉 Counseling for unwanted pregnancies

Counseling for unwanted pregnancies
Author:: Prof.. eemm.. DDrr.. med.. Sibil Tschudin
pensioniert seit Mai 2024. Ehem. Leitende Ärztin Abteilung Gyn. Sozialmedizin und Psychosomatik,
Frauenklinik USB
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr. med. Johannes Bitzer

Introduction
Changes in the Swiss legislation regarding abortion that entered into force 2002
have freed women and doctors from a criminally de�ned medical evaluation which
was often very di�cult and sometimes arbitrary.
In accordance with the time limit regulation which has been in effect since then, the
decision is exclusively in the hands of the woman until the 12th week of pregnancy
has been completed. At the same time, doctors are obliged to hold a consultation
with regard to the planned intervention (medical or surgical termination of
pregnancy). Even though many women have already made their decision before the
medical consultation, some are ambivalent or made their decision under the
in�uence of a third party. Especially in these con�ict situations it is important to
consider the following aspects and advice during the consultation.

Objectives and framework ooff the
preoperative counseling consultation
• Informed, self-determined and considered decision-making
• Prevention of abortions that affect the future health of the woman concerned
• Identi�cation of patients at risk for a self-damaging termination of pregnancy

topic: Counseling for unwanted pregnancies Counseling for unwanted pregnancies (Teil 2/13)
keywords: counseling for unwanted pregnancies counseling for unwanted pregnancies
content:
Informed,, self--determined and considered
decision--making
Informed
• Knowledge (awareness) of the di�culties and problems in carrying the
pregnancy
• Knowledge about the aids for carrying the pregnancy (resources)
• Knowledge of the possible short and long-term complications of an abortion, on
the physical and mental perspective Knowledge about the aids for carrying the
pregnancy (resources)
Self-determined
• Is the patient herself making the decision?
• Is another person making the decision?
• Does the decision correspond with the wishes and values of the patient?
Considered
• Has the decision been made in a crisis or in an emotional state of emergency?
• Have the emotional reactions associated with the unwanted pregnancy been
recognized and understood, and have the advantages and disadvantages of the
various options been weighed up?
• Has the patient had the opportunity to consider her possible reactions to each
option?

topic: Counseling for unwanted pregnancies Counseling for unwanted pregnancies (Teil 3/13)
keywords: counseling for unwanted pregnancies counseling for unwanted pregnancies
content:
Prevention ooff abortions that are harmful
ttoo health
Terminations of pregnancy in case of persistent ambivalence, in case of heteronomy,
in acute crisis situations, in case of pre-existing mental illness.
Framework of the consultation before an abortion
• The consultation should take place in a calm setting, with the doctor having
su�cient time for and undivided attention on the patient. The patient should be
treated with respect and acceptance. The patient is the expert in her problems
and values.
• The doctor should practice active listening. This means the following: open
questions, mirroring, summarizing, emotional response
• The doctor’s questions and information should be formulated in simple
language, tailored to the patient’s level of education.
Summary and algorithm for counseling consultation
The following list of questions can help the counselor orient themselves to how the
patient feels about the unwanted pregnancy on one hand and abortion on the other,
thus provide assistance in identifying problem cases and high-risk situations, where
further counseling and care is indicated.
With regard to her desire for an abortion, the patient is:
• ambivalent --> clarifying counseling consultation
• decided

Figure 2: Clari�cation (considered)
Figure 3: High-risk/problematic situations
Figure 4: Informed

topic: Counseling for unwanted pregnancies Counseling for unwanted pregnancies (Teil 4/13)
keywords: counseling for unwanted pregnancies counseling for unwanted pregnancies
content:
The phases ooff the consultation
Introduction and ��rst contact
In the �rst phase, the patient should have the opportunity to establish an open and
trusting relationship with the counsellor. She should feel understood and accepted
and be encouraged to speak openly about her problems. The affected woman should
have the opportunity to present her con�ict with the existing pregnancy.
Important
• Questions those are as open as possible so that the patient can spontaneously
present her individual pregnancy con�ict. Active listening.
Typical interventions
• “You have come here because you are pregnant, and this pregnancy is
problematic. Could you tell me more?”
Main content of the consultation in this phase:
• When and where was the pregnancy established?
• How did the pregnancy occur?
• What were the patient’s reactions?
• Have preliminary talks taken place? With whom, what were the results?
• Does the patient have speci�c questions and/or expectations for this
consultation?
What is the patient’s current emotional state and her current behavior?
• Tension, pressure, panic, fear, etc.
Important
• Recognition of patients with reactions of fear and panic.
• Recognition of patients with emotional ambivalence.
Recording and clarifying the signi��cance ooff the pregnancy
for the patient and her view ooff the problems iinn carrying iitt out
Important
• The experience of pregnancy is the result of the interaction of the individual life
situation (external reality) and personal feelings, thoughts and evaluations
(internal frame of reference). Both areas (external reality and internal frame of

topic: Counseling for unwanted pregnancies Counseling for unwanted pregnancies (Teil 5/13)
keywords: counseling for unwanted pregnancies counseling for unwanted pregnancies
content:
reference and evaluation) are not static, objective facts, but subjective,
potentially changing factors that are subject to numerous in�uences: Current
emotional state, reactions of the environment, previous experiences etc. The
pregnant woman must be able to develop a coherent and appropriate picture of
the pregnancy and the accompanying problems, on which she can base
decisions that are right for her in the medium and long term and that suit her.
• A pregnancy is always an ambivalent experience. It brings together both
negative and positive feelings and attitudes (fear of limitations, failure,
excessive demands on the one hand and hope for a future with a child, for
expansion, growth, love and care, family etc. on the other hand). Pregnant
women must be able to perceive and resolve this ambivalence.
• There are often various options for solving the issues associated with the
pregnancy. In case of economic problems, evaluation of potential assistance, in
case of relationship crises, possibility of couples counseling, etc. These
possible solutions may not be taken into account or ignored.
Role ooff the counselor aass aa dialog partner for clarifying how the
pregnancy iiss experienced
Typical interventions and questions
• “You've come here because you want to terminate this pregnancy. In your
opinion, what problems and di�culties are associated with this pregnancy?”
• “What was your reaction when you realized you were pregnant?”
Comment: Di�culties and problems, just like opportunities and positives, are the
result of objective facts and subjective evaluations and assessments.
The counselor should be aware of whether and to what extent she / he can relate to
these evaluations.
There can be a clear discrepancy between the evaluation of the patient and the
evaluation of the counselor for many reasons:
A) The woman attending counseling may, in the opinion of the counselor, have an
unrealistic, distorted view of the burdens of a pregnancy and/or her own resources/
possible support
• “A second child would torpedo all our life plans and disrupt our family.”
• “I won’t be able to �nish my education and my career will be completely
blocked.”

topic: Counseling for unwanted pregnancies Counseling for unwanted pregnancies (Teil 6/13)
keywords: counseling for unwanted pregnancies counseling for unwanted pregnancies
content:
B) The women attending counseling has a completely different internal evaluation
system to the counselor
• “I do not want to take on burdens and restrictions.”
• “We still have debts to pay off right now; maybe in a year I would see it
differently."
• “I see nothing positive at all in this pregnancy. It is nothing but horror.”
C) A special case is the patient who cannot and/or does not want to report burdens
and di�culties. The reference point or the inner evaluation system is the patient's
own unwillingness.
• “I feel that I just don't want. That’s it.”
The following guidelines apply to the counselor:
A) The patient’s internal evaluation and reference system has priority and must be
respected.
B) If the counselor has doubts as to the realistic evaluation of the burden, this should
be questioned by the counselor and discussed if necessary.
• “Let’s talk again about how professional training would look if you continued the
pregnancy.”
C) If the counselor thinks that the patient has a changing, unstable evaluation
system with the danger of a signi�cant revaluation after termination of pregnancy,
this should be addressed respectfully.
• “There are situations in our lives where we can’t see a way out at �rst, but later
things seem different to how we �rst thought.”
• “At the moment, you’re under pressure �nancially and from other burdens. It’s
understandable that you feel there is no place for this pregnancy. Do you think
that this will be the case after the abortion, or do you think it is possible that you
will see or evaluate things differently?”
D) If the counsellor has the impression that the patient is repressing her reference
and evaluation system (I don't want to because I don't want to) with the danger that
repressed elements of the decision-making process become visible after the
abortion and the original subjective evaluations are reversed, this di�cult point
should be respectfully addressed in order to enable the patient to reduce her
resistance.
• "I sense that it is very di�cult for you to talk about the stresses (the reasons for

topic: Counseling for unwanted pregnancies Counseling for unwanted pregnancies (Teil 7/13)
keywords: counseling for unwanted pregnancies counseling for unwanted pregnancies
content:
abortion). I also respect your wish not to make any further explanations to a
stranger like me, because pregnancy is an intimate area of life that one also
wants to protect. My concern, however, is that this "I don't want the pregnancy"
includes factors and motives that remain unconscious because they may be
painful, unpleasant or embarrassing at the moment. Nevertheless, a dialogue
on precisely these aspects could be particularly important in order to prevent
the decision from appearing in a completely different light later on.”
• "I experience you as deeply hurt at the moment and can understand that you
don't want to talk about anything further, which might increase the injury. But
there is always the danger that you hurt yourself even more, if you don’t have
the opportunity, in a consultation, to clarify what is going on inside. Even if it
hurts temporarily."
Role ooff the counselor aass aa dialog partner for clarifying ambivalence
Typical interventions and questions
• "Were your feelings about this pregnancy always the same, or were there
variations, a kind of no and yes or yes and no?"
• “Do you also see any opportunities or positive aspects in this pregnancy?”
• "How does the behavior of your partner or the environment affect your feelings
about this pregnancy?"
The aforementioned ambivalence is a normal phase in confronting a pregnancy. The
counselor can help the patient through active listening to allow these different,
potentially contradictory feelings, and �nd a solution.
A distinction can be made between the following situations:
A distinction can be made between the following situations:
• A) The woman attending counseling can perceive burdens and opportunities.
She makes a conscious evaluation for or against the pregnancy. (stage of
resolved ambivalence)
• “At �rst, I was pleased for a moment. Then it became clear that I can’t continue
this pregnancy because... “
B) The woman attending counseling can perceive burdens and opportunities, but
both sides are more or less equal. (stage of persistent ambivalence)
• “On the one hand, I want a child, but on the other I'm scared of the
responsibility. I don’t know what I should do.”

topic: Counseling for unwanted pregnancies Counseling for unwanted pregnancies (Teil 8/13)
keywords: counseling for unwanted pregnancies counseling for unwanted pregnancies
content:
C) The woman attending counseling can only perceive burdens, no opportunities.
• “This pregnancy is a catastrophe for me. I knew straight away that I have to
have an abortion.”
There are two possible reasons for this:
• Opportunities are not present or cannot be perceived.
• Opportunities are repressed and must not be taken for internal reasons (the
repressed positive).
The following guidelines apply to the counselor:
A) The counselor should accompany the patient in perceiving and solving the
ambivalence.
B) The following applies in cases of persistent ambivalence: give time, help verbalize
the ambivalence, increase awareness of implications, persistent ambivalence is
more of an argument against abortion.
C) If no positive aspects can be perceived, this should primarily be accepted.
D) If the counselor has the impression that the positive sides are being actively
suppressed, this should be questioned respectfully.
• “You say that you immediately experienced this pregnancy as a major
catastrophe. Was that because you had never really thought about having a
child, because starting a family had not yet been up for discussion?”
• “In very general terms, what are your plans and attitude with regard to a child?”
• “What importance does this have in your life?"
Role ooff the counselor aass aa dialog partner for clarifying external oorr self--
determination
If the counsellor gains the impression that the partner or the environment is a
decisive factor in experiencing pregnancy or the ambivalence towards it, she should
try to differentiate between the following situations.
A) The woman to be counseled can clearly recognize the importance of her partner's
behavior and classify it for herself
• "I know well that I would keep the child if he wanted to. But I do not want a child
without a partner, I do not want to raise a child alone. It hurts me, but there's no
other way."

topic: Counseling for unwanted pregnancies Counseling for unwanted pregnancies (Teil 9/13)
keywords: counseling for unwanted pregnancies counseling for unwanted pregnancies
content:
B) The woman attending counseling is under the intangible in�uence of her partner
or those around her, without this being clear to her
• “I'm very disappointed in him. First he wanted the child too. Now suddenly he
doesn’t. I don’t know, I somehow depend on him."
• “My husband is against the child. So I am too. If he doesn’t want it, I don’t
either.”
• "I would want to, but my husband won't."
The following guidelines apply to the counselor:
• The patient should, if at all possible, make her own decision, based on her
feelings. She should be encouraged towards self-determination and
responsibility for herself.
• The in�uence of the partner should be highlighted and worked through. Couples
counseling should be offered, and potential relationship issues discussed.
• If there is a clear discrepancy between the patient’s desire to continue the
pregnancy and her partner’s wish to terminate, the decision should not be taken
or should be postponed (couples counseling).
• If the partner’s in�uence is unclear or changing, the decision should not be
taken or should be postponed (couples counseling).
Role ooff the counselor aass aa dialog partner for ��nding solutions ttoo
problems associated with the pregnancy
Typical interventions
• “You’ve just talked about the �nancial di�culties having a child would place you
in. Now, there are various forms of assistance I would like to discuss with you.”
The patient may not have any information or may be under signi�cant pressure,
meaning that potential solutions to the problems associated with the pregnancy
have not been considered. Here, the counselor has two roles:
Role 1: She/he can suggest considering alternative solutions
• “Maybe it would be a good idea, in all the agitation, to think again about how
you could confront the di�culties you’ve described (�nancial, family etc.).”
Role 2: She/he can act as a source of information and mediator:
• Passing on the cantonal directory of advice centers and assistance.
• Direct mediation via telephone to, for example, specialist psychotherapeutic
assistance.

content:
Assessment ooff the signi��cance ooff abortion for the patient
and joint anticipation ooff possible consequences
Author:: Prof.. eemm.. DDrr.. med.. Sibil Tschudin
pensioniert seit Mai 2024. Ehem. Leitende Ärztin Abteilung Gyn. Sozialmedizin und Psychosomatik,
Frauenklinik USB

Important
• Abortion is a help and a way out of a very stressful situation for many women.
Like the pregnancy itself, however, the abortion is usually characterized as an
ambivalent experience. This ambivalence (relief and grief) is normal, but for
some women it is di�cult to comprehend and worrying.
• Abortion is ethically valued very differently from one individual to another and
the individual perception may change. This can possibly lead to a changing way
of dealing with guilt and feelings of guilt.
• The abortion is not banal for most women, but an important event in their
biography.
• It is often �rst seen as a way to return to the status quo of life before the
pregnancy (undoing something). However, most women subsequently �nd this
to be a misconception. Life afterwards is not the same as life before - in other
words, the abortion must be accepted or acceptable as part of the patient’s life
Role ooff the counselor aass aa dialog partner iinn clarifying the signi��cance ooff
the abortion ((bene��ttss and risks)) for the patient
Typical interventions:
• “Because of the problems described, you have decided that you do not wish to
continue with this pregnancy, and instead have an abortion. So let’s talk about
abortion.”
Dialog means that a reciprocal question and answer exchange, in both directions,
takes place. This process comprises various steps in which certain issues should be
discussed:
Step 11:: Attitudes and prior knowledge ooff abortion
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr. med. Johannes Bitzer

topic: Counseling for unwanted pregnancies Counseling for unwanted pregnancies (Teil 11/13)
keywords: counseling for unwanted pregnancies counseling for unwanted pregnancies
content:
Author:: Prof.. eemm.. DDrr.. med.. Sibil Tschudin
pensioniert seit Mai 2024. Ehem. Leitende Ärztin Abteilung Gyn. Sozialmedizin und Psychosomatik,
Frauenklinik USB
The patient should be able to present her view of abortion
• “What is your attitude on abortion?”
• “What risks are you aware of?”
A distinction can be made between three groups
• Women with clear, realistic ideas
• Women with unclear or incorrect ideas
• Women who do not yet have any ideas or thoughts.
Incorrect ideas should be corrected. For women who have not yet developed any
ideas, special attention should be paid to steps 2 and 3.
Step 22:: Providing information oonn abortion
Author:: Prof.. eemm.. DDrr.. med.. Sibil Tschudin
pensioniert seit Mai 2024. Ehem. Leitende Ärztin Abteilung Gyn. Sozialmedizin und Psychosomatik,
Frauenklinik USB
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr. med. Johannes Bitzer
The patient should receive all information that is relevant to her in an understandable
form.
As a basis, the following information should be given:
• How is a pregnancy terminated (surgically, with drugs)?
• What methods are used for pain relief and avoiding health risks?
• What are the possible early complications, how common are they and what can
be done to treat them?
• What do we know about late physical and mental complications and damages?
How can these be avoided as much as possible?
Step 33:: Processing and evaluating the information oonn abortion
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr. med. Johannes Bitzer

topic: Counseling for unwanted pregnancies Counseling for unwanted pregnancies (Teil 12/13)
keywords: counseling for unwanted pregnancies counseling for unwanted pregnancies
content:
Author:: Prof.. eemm.. DDrr.. med.. Sibil Tschudin
pensioniert seit Mai 2024. Ehem. Leitende Ärztin Abteilung Gyn. Sozialmedizin und Psychosomatik,
Frauenklinik USB
The patient should be able to discuss the personal signi�cance of the information
she has received with the counselor. It should be possible for her to ask questions.
• “Have you understood everything I’ve told you about abortion?”
• “How do you evaluate this information? What does this mean for you? Does that
make you feel safe or does it make you feel insecure? Do you have any fears or
doubts?”
• “Do you have any more questions that have not yet been discussed?”
End ooff the counseling and summary
Important
• The objective of the counseling is to accompany and support the patient in
making an informed, considered and self-determined decision as to whether to
continue or terminate the pregnancy.
• There are counseling situations where the woman feels she can’t continue the
pregnancy, but can’t go through with an abortion either (“I really don’t want a
child, but I can’t kill it”). For these women, it is particularly important that further
consultations are offered, and the possibility of adoption is discussed.
• Likewise, women with persistent ambivalence or strong heteronomy should
receive a new consultation appointment.
• Women for whom economic burdens are in the foreground should also be
offered a new opportunity for discussion after a specialist consultation with a
social worker.

EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Psychosomatics
〉 Psycho-oncology for gynecologic malignancies
Last update: 2017/2018
Psycho--oncology for gynecologic
malignancies
Author:: DDrr.. med.. Judith Alder CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr.
med. Johannes Bitzer
ehem. Leitende Psychologin Gyn. Sozialmedizin und Psychosomatik,
Universitätsspital Basel

DDee��nition
Psycho-oncology is the study of the relationships of cancer and the psyche. It is
a subarea of oncology in which experts from various areas (psychology, social
work, psychiatry, oncology, gynecology, nursing, art and music therapy, nutrition
counseling, and spiritual care) jointly address the emotional reactions of the
patient and her family. Psycho-oncology focuses on identifying psychosocial
factors in�uencing morbidity and mortality and on supporting the patients and
their families in coping.
DDee��nitions ooff psycho--oncological counseling and psycho--
oncological therapy
The distinction between psycho-oncological counseling and psycho-oncological
therapy is clinically relevant and was de�ned as follows in 2007 by the Swiss Society
of Psycho-Oncology (SGPO) in its guideline on psycho-oncology (Leitlinien
Psychoonkologie):
Psycho-oncological counseling includes counseling, psychoeducation, and ongoing
support for cancer patients and their families and can be carried out by quali�ed
nurses, social workers, psychologists, physicians, or other quali�ed personnel.
Psycho-oncological therapy additionally includes, in particular, psychological
diagnostics, crisis intervention, and psycho-oncological psychotherapy of patients
and their families with signi�cant mental impairment or comorbid mental health
issues and must be carried out by psycho-oncologists with psychotherapeutic
training.
Swiss Society of Psycho-Oncology
In the beginnings of psycho-oncology, questions were raised about a cancer
personality and the in�uence of critical life events on pathogenesis, which
CANNOT be maintained from today’s perspective. However, patients often
wonder why they have the disease and develop their own, subjective disease
theory.

Most frequent stressors iinn gynecologic
malignancies
Carcinoma is associated with general and locality-speci�c as well as treatment-
related stressors.
General Stressors
• Vital threat and confrontation with the �nite nature of life
• Limited lifetime in palliative situations
• Distress caused by medical procedures (see below)
• Situational adaptation requirements: Changes in treatment staff, Feeling of
helplessness, Appointment stress
• Decision-making con�icts
• Social changes: work�ow, changing role in partnership and family, friends
• Worries about family members
• Di�culty coping with everyday life
• Fear of follow-up visits and relapse
Treatment--Related Stressors
The patient is confronted with the following treatment-related changes, with which
she can cope with varying success, on the basis of her previous development and
her general distress level on the one hand, and her resources on the other:
Surgical Interventions
• Limited privacy during inpatient stay
• Changes in body image, organ loss → subjective; possibly experienced as a
loss of femininity and violation of gender identity.
• Pain
• Limited motility due to surgery and lymphedema
• Genital surgery: possible loss of reproductive capacity, hormonal changes
Chemotherapy
• Alopecia --> looking sick, loss of femininity
• Malaise and weakness --> greatly reduced performance
• Possible hormonal changes --> climacteric symptoms, mood swings
• Loss of reproductive capacity
• Tiredness up to chronic fatigue

• Gastrointestinal symptoms (e.g., vomiting, diarrhea, constipation)
• Cognitive impairment --> concentration di�culties, memory decline (as a result
of chemotherapy; colloquially called “chemo brain”)
• Mucous membrane changes
Radiation
• Burns
• Feelings of helplessness, especially in case of intracavitary radiation
• Tiredness up to chronic fatigue --> general exhaustion, excessive tiredness with
excessive need for sleep, listlessness, feeling to no longer satisfy usual
requirements, weakness
• Lymphedema
Anti-hormonal therapy
• Climacteric symptoms
• Loss of reproductive capacity
• Lack of lubrication and sexual problems

Psychological reactions
General Reactions
Carcinoma is considered to be a critical life event triggering a stress reaction in most
cases. This reaction is seen as a normal reaction to an exceptional and threatening
event.
The most common emotional reaction prior to diagnosis and/or prior to the
availability of all medical �ndings is anticipatory anxiety, which can manifest itself
more strongly than the reaction during the discussion of all �ndings. Consequently,
many patients experience this waiting period as one of the most di�cult phases.
The most common negative cognitive emotional reactions to diagnosis and during
the course of the disease are:
• Shock
• Disbelief up to denial
• Fear, panic, and fear of the future
• Despair, hopelessness, and depressive reaction
• Numbness, feeling of emptiness
• Anger, aggression, and sense of injustice
• Feeling of loss of control
• Grief
The process of coping with the disease begins with the diagnosis. Coping is de�ned
as:
Attempting to reduce, compensate for, and process existing or expected stress
caused by the disease through internal psychological (emotional and cognitive)
processes or by targeted behavior and actions. This is a process-oriented event
that depends on the various stages of the disease.
Coping strategies can be observed on the behavioral, cognitive, and emotional level.
They include:
• Trust in doctors
• Fighting spirit: “I won't give up, I’ll keep �ghting for my life and my health.”
• Denial: “I don’t have cancer; this is a wrong diagnosis; I know for sure that I just
bumped myself.”

• Self-encouragement: every day, I consciously tell myself in the mirror: “I will
make it.”
• Accept the disease as fate or an opportunity
• Distraction by reading, walking, telephone calls, visits, exercise, crafts,
meditation, relaxation, etc.
• Creative expression (music, painting, etc.)
• Looking for information (internet!)
• Talking about distress, looking for help
• And much more
Coping with the disease is considered adaptive if:
• Adaptive tasks are completed successfully, for example accepting reality,
orientation and focusing on the present, reduction of patterns of thinking that
focus on fear of the future
• Absence of mental disorders
• Low negative and high positive affect
• Adequate (condition-appropriate) level of functioning (e.g., coping with
everyday life)
• Satisfaction with and well-being in various areas of life
• Health-related quality of life
Mental Comorbidities
In 30 to 40 % of patients, phases with severe psychosocial distress occur during the
course of the disease, and a mental disorder develops as part of a maladaptive
coping process. Depending on the stage of the disease, 25 to 45 % of patients are
expected to want or need professional psychological support. A baseline diagnostic
assessment of psychological distress is therefore a continuous process and a
quality characteristic of a patient-centered approach in oncology. A signi�cant
number of patients develop a mental disorder beyond an emotional reaction to the
disease that can be considered normal. The cancer diagnosis may exacerbate a
mental disorder that is pre-existing or in remission.
• Depressive symptoms: 15 to 50 %, in terminal stage 9 to 77 %
• Anxiety disorder: 10 to 50 %, in terminal stage 50 to 80 %
• Adjustment disorder: 5 to 50 %
• Post-traumatic stress disorder: 5 to 10 %
• Somatic symptom disorder: 9 to 14 %
• Sexual dysfunction: 25 to 50 %
