e�ciency of the medical treatment, and her own coping resources, is the central
factor.
It is determined by disease-speci�c, interpersonal (e.g., personality traits such as
resilience (resistance to stress)), and sociocultural factors (e.g., the meaning of
cancer in the respective culture of the patient). The �gure below illustrates the
various factors that in�uence coping.

Goal ooff Psycho--Oncological Interventions
Improving quality ooff life
• Reduction of disease- or treatment-related symptoms (such as nausea,
vomiting, pain, insomnia, etc.)
• Reduction of trauma reactions, fear, depression, hopelessness, and
helplessness
• Strengthen potential for self-help (self-control, personal responsibility, coping:
Coping refers to the way the patient deals with and/or manages the disease.
Does she have a �ghting, optimistic, hopeful attitude, or feelings of
helplessness and hopelessness; can she accept things she cannot change and
focus on things she can change, etc.)
• Improve self-con�dence and attitude towards cancer
• Provide help to clarify misunderstandings and misinformation
• Provide support in dealing with social environment and improving social
support
• Improve doctor-patient relationship, support communication between patient,
partner, family, and medical personnel
• Support remaining health
Active Components ooff Psycho--Oncological Interventions
• Structure: Structured psychoeducational programs are more effective than
unstructured discussion groups.
• Format: Group interventions are more effective than individual therapy.
• Focus: Interventions promoting self-e�cacy are more effective.
• Duration: Longer interventions are more effective than shorter ones.
Psychopharmacological Interventions
Because various organ systems are involved, the liver and kidney metabolism are
affected due to undesirable effects and complications, and pharmacological
interactions are unpredictable, psychotropic drugs are often only prescribed
reluctantly, potentially causing an undersupply.
Antidepressants, especially SSRIs, and sedatives are employed to manage anxiety
and stress during the acute shock phase, prior to stressful interventions, in palliative
situations, and in the case of delirium.

The following factors increase the risk of drug interactions
• High protein binding
• Cytochrome P450-dependent metabolism
• Narrow therapeutic index

Psycho--Oncological Treatment Options
During surgical and oncological treatment and especially during the diagnostic and
treatment phase as well as follow-up, approx. 30-50% of patients have an indication
for supplementary psycho-oncological treatment by appropriately trained
psychologists and psychiatrists. Duration, intensity and type of treatment differ
depending on the problem and phase of the disease, ranging from a single crisis
intervention to multi-year support.
Interventions are varied and include, among others:
• Clarifying, supportive, and solution-oriented counseling in individual, couple,
family, and group settings
• Information on relaxation techniques (e.g., relaxation, breathing) and

mindfulness training (e.g., yoga, meditation)
• Development of concrete coping strategies (e.g., for anxiety, pain, and
depression)
• Sexual therapy interventions
• Bibliotherapy
• Painting and art therapy
• Exercise and movement therapy (e.g., kinesiotherapy or/and kinesiology)
• Body-based methods (autogenic training)
• Etc.
Co-therapy by a psycho-oncologist should be offered to every cancer patient at
the beginning and during the course of treatment (during treatment and at the
beginning of follow-up care, in case of relapse, and during the palliative phase).
Treatment Options
Treatment providers and affected patients can �nd experts, treatment options, self-
help groups, information and brochures at www.krebsliga.ch (search for option and
canton is possible). Various brochures and information for Germany are available at
www.krebshilfe.de.

〉 Communication and advice in gynecological consultations
Last update: January 2021
Communication and advice iinn
gynecological consultations
Author:: Cécile Longoni
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr. med. Johannes Bitzer, Dr. med. Judith Alder

Introduction
In obstetrical and gynecological consultations or visits, the aim is to record the
patient’s current issues and symptoms and establish the relevant diagnosis. It is
important to differentiate between two elements or levels to which parts of the
obstetrical/gynecological consultation or visit can be assigned: the biomedical level
with the underlying biomedical model, and the biopsychosocial level with the
underlying biopsychosocial model.
Biomedical model
• Diagnostic procedures and standards
• Therapeutic procedures and standards
Biopsychosocial model
• Relationship
• Communication
Biomedical model
On the biomedical level, during the consultation the doctor pays attention to the
structure and function of the genital organs with the aim of diagnosing and treating
obstetrical/gynecological diseases as described in textbooks. This part of the
consultation is based on the biomedical model:
• Symptoms are the consequence of objective structural changes.
• These structural changes can be identi�ed using standardized diagnostic
methods.
• They can be treated, cured or undone using standardized treatment processes.
Biopsychosocial model
The biomedical element of the consultation depends on the personal encounter
between the patient and doctor. The patient as an individual has speci�c thoughts,
feelings and behaviors, which they experience in a speci�c context. By
communicating with the patient, the doctor can develop a relationship with them.
This part of the consultation is based on the biopsychosocial model. [B07][B25]

Figure 1: Biopsychosocial model.
• Symptoms are the expression of individual suffering, brought on by a
combination of biological, psychological and social factors.
• The biopsychosocial diagnostic aims to record this individual suffering and
understand the determining factors.
• Treatment interventions include decision making and collaboration with the
patient. The patient is an active partner in the treatment.

Communication styles
Doctor--focused communication style
In this communication style, the doctor clearly takes the lead in the conversation and
also determines the agenda. Doctor-focused communication is characterized by its
unilateral, vertical nature. The doctor speaks almost exclusively. In this hierarchical
doctor-patient dialog, the patient takes on the role of an uninformed, passive
recipient. With this top-down communication, the �ow of communication goes down
from the doctor. The patient is quickly interrupted, the doctor mainly asks closed
questions (“yes”, “no” or short answers only). The aim is to provide information
quickly and e�ciently or rapidly reach a diagnosis. There is usually no room for
emotion.
The information acquired is well-structured and organized, and helps to quickly
de�ne the problem and �nd solutions using an evidence-based scienti�c approach.
This communication model is suitable for emergency wards, clearly physical
diseases or speci�c issues.
Patient--focused communication style
A different approach is offered by patient-focused communication, which involves
not only the disease but also the patient in his social context. In this communication
style, the doctor must take into account two further factors in addition to the factual
level:
• Factual level
• Emotional level
• Relationship level
Table 1: Elements of patient-focused communication
Empathy Empathy, not sympathy
How does the patient see themselves and the world?
What is important for them, or unimportant?
Respect and The patient lives in a different context and is different - but at the
value same time similar as a human being.
Be aware of this difference and similarity. Respect it and
recognize it as having value.

Authenticity Recognize strengths, weaknesses and limits, and be able to
communicate/express them if necessary
Be authentic, don’t pretend or play a role
Relationship level
Studies show that the relationship level is by far the most signi�cant aspect of
communication. It offers information on how factual statements should be
understood, as the other party pays attention to how something is expressed in
terms of body language, gestures and voice. [B25]
The following are potential strategies to help with relationship building:
• Joining (starting and developing the conversation): Building a relationship
starts from the very �rst greeting. Give your name and role, and make a
connection with the patient, e.g. “You must have been waiting a long time...", "I
see you’ve come a long way today."
• Clarify what the consultation is about: Provide the patient with space to
formulate their own ideas or questions, e.g. "How can I help you?", "Is there a
speci�c issue we should cover?"
• Content and purpose of the consultation: Establish why the patient is at the
consultation (doctor’s information), and the time limits.
• Ask for feedback: Determine whether the planned consultation matches the
patient’s needs "Is that okay for you?"
• Structure the conversation: Be transparent about limitations in terms of time
and content, e.g. in case a question is not/cannot be covered. De�ne the topics
of the conversation: what will/can be discussed in the consultation. Announce
transitions "I think I’ve now understood what your concern is. Now I’d like to ask
you some general questions about your history."
• Summarize what you’ve said and heard: The questions and concerns discussed
during the consultation often entail a great deal of information in a short time
for patients. Summarizing and asking for con�rmation makes it possible to
determine what the patient has already understood, and what must still be
clari�ed. "Can I summarize what I’ve understood so far? Please correct me if
anything is incorrect."
Emotional level

Emotions are the closest representation of the patient’s individual reality and their
perception. The emotional brain is the quickest and most intuitive. Emotions are
expressed not only with words, but also with body language. For the patient,
suppressing their emotions means they don’t feel understood. The NURS schema
can be helpful when dealing with emotions. Of course, this is not only about the
patient’s emotions, but also the doctor’s.
Figure 2: NURS schema.
Name
Identify individual emotional reactions: What feelings does the patient trigger in me?
What sensations am I noticing?
Understand
Perceive the patient’s emotions: How are they? What feelings are being expressed by
their behavior? It can be helpful to pause to give the patient time and space to
express themselves.
Respect
Respect the patient’s feelings: Express respect and acceptance speci�cally, for
example in the form of a question: “It looks like this information is a lot for you right
now, I’d like to give you a moment - please let me know when you’re ready to
continue.” Use the patient’s own words.
Support
Support talking about emotions: give the patient time, silence can be helpful; give the
patient space for corrections.
Content//subject level

The content level is where “facts” are transmitted. This almost always refers to the
subject matter content of a statement.
This part of the consultation should provide the patient with relevant information on
their condition. It is important to note that the doctor should remain in constant
contact with the patient. Information transmission should be an exchange, not a one-
way street. The patient’s experiences and circumstances should be taken into
account. In addition, the patient’s right not to want to know everything should be
respected.
Information transmission as a one-way street
• The doctor communicates their knowledge in their words
• They ensure completeness and scienti�c accuracy
• Lecture-style
• Top-down
Information exchange
• Meet the patient's needs and questions
• The doctor creates a shared platform with the patient
• The doctor aims for “adapted” information
• The doctor encourages the patient to ask questions and provides further
explanation
Elicit--provide principle
Figure 3: Elicit-Provide principle.

The Elicit-Provide principle is a useful schema for designing information
transmission as an exchange. The patient should be invited to take an active role in
the conversation, and express their own thoughts, questions and concerns. The
doctor can then address these needs and, if relevant, provide further information.
Elicit: What information does the other person need?
• What and how much does the patient want to know?
Provide: Based on the previous step, give the patient the selected basic information.
• Emphasize important details
• Break information down into small units
• Summarize important aspects of the conversation
• Give the patient opportunities to ask questions (or encourage them to ask
questions)
Elicit: The current level of understanding should be determined, for example by
asking whether the information has been understood or if something was too quick,
too unclear or incomprehensible. This also includes questions on the emotional
processing of information such as:
• "How do you feel about this information?"
• "Does what I’ve just said make you feel anxious or give you more security?"
• "Have any new questions come up?"
Provide: Based on the previous step, new needs, doubts or questions can be
covered.
When transmitting scienti�c information, it is helpful to make use of different
sensory channels, for example images, brochures, writing or drawing. The response
to different sensory channels increases understanding and retention of information.
Summary ooff patient--focused communication style

Patient-focused communication entails three levels: the subject level, the emotional
level and the relationship level. At the subject level, important information should be
transmitted to the patient as an exchange, and the patient’s questions and needs
acknowledged. The Elicit-Provide principle helps to avoid prefabricated questions in
favor of an actual dialog with the patient. Patients are involved as active participants
in the decision-making process. At the emotional level, the emotions of both the
patient and the doctor are expressed, acknowledged and addressed, with respect
and empathy. The NURS schema is helpful here. At the relationship level, the
patient’s desire for hope and help should be met with competence, honesty and
commitment, while respecting the patient’s autonomy. Forming a relationship begins
before the actual consultation begins.

Speci��cc clinical skills
Breaking bad news
Doctors are often confronted with situations where they must give the patient bad
news.
This may be a cancer diagnosis, intrauterine death or a fetal condition, a negative
result from infertility testing or treatment or in general, complications or unforeseen
treatment outcomes.
When preparing for such conversations, certain fundamental rules must be followed.
These help to remain empathetic and respectful of the patient in their situation.
Important guiding principles are:
• Share the news in a quiet, closed space (no people walking through)
• Schedule enough time (at least 15 minutes)
• Ensure as few disruptions as possible. Switch off beepers and telephones or
give them to someone else
• Before the conversation, the doctor should inform all those taking part in the
conversation

When starting the conversation, after establishing an emotional connection with the
patient (Joining: for example with a greeting "Hello [name], thank you for coming."),
the patient’s prior knowledge should be established: "What did you think when you
felt the lump in your breast?", "How do you feel about your situation after
chemotherapy?"
The words chosen for the conversation should explain the diagnosis, prognosis and
other diagnostic or therapeutic steps simply and truthfully in short, clear sentences.
Cancer, miscarriage, precancerous stages, death etc. should be talked about - the
diagnosis should not be hidden behind vague euphemisms.
It is important to tell the patient they are about to receive bad news and to prepare
them; e. g. "I'm afraid I don’t have good news for you." This gives the patient an
opportunity to prepare mentally.
After the diagnosis is given, the patient may need the doctor to give a prognosis. A
different time should be given for a prognosis of how much time is left. Give the
patient hope, but not false hope. "As you’ve asked me so openly about how long you
have before you die, I’d like to answer openly too. I think you have a few, or maybe
many, months left. We will try to support and help you as much as possible." The
conversation should cover treatment possibilities as well as the diagnosis and
prognosis. Of course, it is important to state that these are suggestions, and that the
patient is involved in the decision-making process.
Sharing bad news is an exceptional emotional situation for both the patient and the
doctor. The patient should be encouraged to express their thoughts and feelings.
Waiting in silence after breaking bad news offers the patient room to acknowledge
the information and react emotionally. Patients react differently to bad news: scared,
paralyzed, confused, shocked, distressed, stoic, weeping, etc. All these emotions are
normal reactions to being confronted with bad news.
As a �rst step, the patient’s emotions should be acknowledged (emotional level) with
sensitive verbal ("I can understand your reaction", "I understand you’re very angry the
lump wasn’t discovered six months ago") or non-verbal (e. g. nodding, facing the
patient and leaning towards them) reactions.
To conclude such conversations, the information discussed and the patient’s
reactions should be documented carefully, as well as the names of other informed
persons. The patient should be offered an opportunity to talk about the situation
again. Determine availability and responsibility, and highlight any other possibilities

for support (e. g. breast care nurse for breast cancer patients, midwives,
psychologists, social services, etc.). In addition, it can be helpful to discuss with the
patient which people in their entourage should be informed and involved.
SPIKES strategy
The SPIKES strategy from Buckman can be used as a reminder for how to prepare
for breaking bad news:
• S: Setting up
• P: Perception
• I: Invitation
• K: Knowledge
• E: Emotions
• S: Summary and Strategy
[B04]
• "Setting Up the Interview": Ensure a quiet, protected environment.
• "Assessing the Patient's Perception": Open questions to assess the patient’s
perception: "What do you currently know about your medical situation?"
• "Obtaining the Patient's Invitation": Assess the patient’s readiness to receive bad
news: "How should I present the test results?" Explain that it is possible to talk
at a later point.
• "Giving Knowledge and Information to the Patient": Signaling before breaking
bad news: "I'm sorry, I have bad news. Unfortunately, I have to tell you that..."
• Improve communication by: Adjusting to the patient’s vocabulary, i. e. using
"non-technical words" (e. g. "spread" instead of "metastasized"). Share
information in small units.
• "Addressing The Patient's Emotions With Empathic Responses": Identify and
name emotions (e.g. sadness, anger, shock). Provide space to express and
address feelings.
• "Strategy and Summary": Future steps are discussed, depending on the patient’s
condition
Summary:: Breaking bad news

Giving bad news is a conversation in an exceptional emotional situation, for both the
patient and the doctor. The conversation should take place in a respectful
environment. Certain basic rules governing the preparation of the conversation, how
the conversation goes, the chosen language and how to proceed after the
conversation must be followed. To prepare for the conversation, it is essential that a
quiet room with as few interruptions as possible be chosen. Simple language should
be used for the conversation, the diagnosis should be identi�ed clearly by name and
the patient should be prepared for the bad news before it is given.
Patients react to bad news in very different ways. The patient should be given space
to express their emotions. The doctor should acknowledge emotions and react
appropriately. If a prognosis is given, the patient should be given an honest answer,
as well as hope and support.
Before the conversation is concluded, ensure that the patient is acknowledged and
supported in their exceptional emotional situation. Establish whether or not relatives
should be informed, if additional support could be helpful, how available the doctor
can be if the patient has any questions, and when the next conversation is
scheduled. The SPIKES strategy is a useful tool.
Risk consulting
Doctors must frequently inform patients of risks, for example in menopause
consultations, oncology consultations, with contraception, before a surgery, etc.
The responsibilities of the doctor are to:
• Understand and evaluate the information
• Translating the information into patient-friendly language
• Give the patient a foundation for making health-related decisions
Patients often struggle to understand statistical data. Unclear statements on
statistical data can lead to decisions being made on the basis of misinterpreted
statistics. Typical errors are: Overwhelming use of relative risk, conditional
probabilities, single event probabilities and framing (see below).
The article “Comprehensible risk communication made easy - or: How to avoid
confusing probability statements” gives a good overview of the complex topic of risk
consulting. [B14]

Framing
Framing means giving information context. In risk communication this means that
the reference standard that applies to statistical data is relevant for the statement or
its understanding. For example, we can talk about a 3 % risk of death or a 97 %
chance of survival. Survival implies a positive outcome. Risk of death implies the risk
of a negative outcome. The statements are based on the same statistical fact, but
the emotional reaction to the information differs. To counter this, it is important to
talk about chances, not just risks. The patient should be shown both perspectives.
• "The risk of having a child with Down syndrome is 1 in 250 for women of your
age (0,5 %)."
• "The chances of having a child with no chromosome issues are 99 % for women
of your age, 249 of every 150 children born have no chromosomal
abnormalities."
Summary:: Risk consulting
It is the doctor’s responsibility to make complex statistical information, i.e. the
probability of a speci�c risk, understandable for the patient. It is helpful to use easily
understandable �gures to demonstrate verbal risk statements, e.g. using natural
frequency or absolute risk. The reference scales used should change from the
reference standard as little as possible and be explained in an easily understandable
everyday perspective. Positive and negative outcomes should be identi�ed. Risks
should be visualized wherever possible. With risk consulting, it is important to take
into account the patient’s value system and preferences, using shared decision
making as explained in the next section.
Shared Decision Making
With shared decision making, the doctor and patient work together to make joint
decisions on appropriate medical treatment.
This is done to ensure that the patient’s value system and preferences are taken into
account in the decision. The role of the doctor is to provide evidence-based
information on the risks and bene�ts of speci�c treatments in an understandable
way. The patient’s role is to assign this information a level of importance i.e. apply
their values and preferences.
Example ooff shared decision making:: Menopause consultation

The following shows the key steps of participative decision making using the
example of a 52-year-old female patient who has presented with disrupted sleep and
hot �ushes.
Figure 4: Example of shared decision making: Menopause consultation.
Summary:: Shared Decision Making

Shared decision making aims to reach decisions made jointly by the patient and
doctor. In this concept, communication is two-sided: Both parties communicate
openly, information is exchanged, questions are asked and answered.
Advice oonn coping strategies
Advice on coping strategies generally follows a diagnosis or bad news such as
cancer, chronic diseases such as endometriosis, surgical procedures with organ
removal, changes to body image or after complications and/or unsuccessful
pregnancy (miscarriage, premature birth etc.). Both of the following steps are
necessary:
Establishing the problem
Open questions should be used to invite the patient to talk about their experiences,
needs and challenges e.g.:
• "How are you now, two weeks after the surgery?"
• "When things aren’t going well, how do you express that?"
• "How do you know that you’re not yet an old woman?"
• "What sometimes helps you feel better?"
• "How have you reacted to the situation so far?"
The doctor can use empathetic statements to build a relationship with the patient
and meet them on an emotional level (Patient-focused communication style).
• "Hearing about everything that’s happened since the surgery, I can understand
that you sometimes feel desperate."
Parts of the conversation should always be summarized in useful sections in order
to ensure that the conversation is understandable for the patient and whether there
are any further questions the patient would like to ask.
Understanding current coping strategies
"Coping" here means how the patient deals with their change in situation. We
differentiate between emotional, cognitive and action-related coping strategies.
These coping strategies can be more or less helpful depending on the individual
situation:
Emotional coping strategies
• Struggling (self-pity, feeling the situation is unfair), resignation (fatalism, giving
up hope), blaming others and expressing anger (either current with regard to

treatment or in relation to the past), blaming oneself and feelings of guilt.
• Repressing emotions rather than admitting them.
• Emotional relief, optimism, con�dent attitude to understanding of the disease.
Cognitive coping strategies
• De�ection, aggravation, problem analysis.
• Humor, irony, sarcasm
• Repression, denial, trivialization of the current burden
• Acceptance, putting into perspective versus others, giving meaning, turning to
religion.
Action-related coping strategies
• Active avoidance, compensation, altruism, social withdrawal, tackling routine
work, relaxation techniques, meditation, yoga, searching for information,
making use of help from doctors and demanding attention.
The doctor can be supportive and therapeutically effective on each level.
On the emotional level, the patient can be supported with help to ease the burden,
listening and understanding.
On the cognitive level, the identi�cation and processing of “dysfunctional thoughts”
can help. This refers to destructive thoughts. The patient and doctor can assess the
truthfulness of thoughts together. For example, if the patient says “I'm de�nitely
going to die”, challenge this and if necessary, change it to “I'm going to die one day.
What support do I need to have the highest possible quality of life at this stage?”. A
crisis should be seen as a challenge - an opportunity to become stronger. Support
the patient in �nding meaning in overcoming the challenge and concentrating on
changing their life after the crisis.
On the action-related level, the doctor can help to identify helpful behaviors. This can
be made accessible to the patient by asking questions, e.g. “Over the last 48 hours,
when did you feel a little better?” and “What was different?”. Or: “In similar situations
in your life, was there anything that helped you?” The doctor can encourage social
support and decide with the patient which people in their entourage can help with
speci�c needs. This can help the patient take control, decide what can and cannot be
changed and make use of offers of help.
Summary:: Advice oonn coping strategies

When giving advice on coping strategies, it is important to establish the patient’s
issue and classify and understand their current coping strategies. There are three
levels of coping: emotional, cognitive and/or action-related. Coping strategies can be
analyzed with the patient and classi�ed as helpful or dysfunctional. Subsequent
conversations can then attempt to work towards more constructive coping
strategies.
Motivational Interviewing [[BB2200]]
In many clinical situations, the patient must change a speci�c health or disease-
related behavior. But often, this change is not achieved. This applies above all to
healthy habits and prevention, such as precautionary and early identi�cation
consultations, health advice, consultations with adolescents, menopause
consultations, prenatal care and sex counseling.
According the behavioral model developed by Prochaska und Di Clemente (1983),
when changing a behavior people go through different phases: [B19]
Figure 5: Behavior and behavioral change [B19]
By repeating these phases multiple times, new behavior can be practiced and
developed into a new habit.
A major element of Motivational Interviewing is identifying the phase in which the
patient is currently found, as various forms of communication are required. It is
therefore particularly important to identify readiness for change during the interview
and support the change process with the right interventions in the right phases.
Readiness for Change
There are two key questions for establishing readiness for change:
1. Identifying the importance of a change: “Why change?” This includes personal
values, information and expectations regarding the importance of the change.
2. Identifying con�dence in the patient’s ability to make the change: “How to

change?” (perceptions of self-e�cacy).
In a Motivational Interview, the doctor attempts to establish these factors with
questions using scales:
• Importance: "On a scale of 0 to 10, how important is it for you to change your
current behavior (0 = not important at all and 10 = extremely important)?"
• Con�dence: "If you made the decision to change right now, how con�dent are
you on a scale of 0 to 10 that you would reach your goal (0 = not con�dent at all
and 10 = maximum con�dence)?"
• Readiness: "How ready are you to change on a scale of 0 (not ready at all) to 10
(completely ready)?"
Carrying out aa Motivational Interview
During a motivational interview, these factors (importance, con�dence and
readiness) can be covered in detail. As part of the discussion with the doctor, pros
and cons of the change can be explored, and short and long-term consequences
considered. Doubts and concerns about the current behavior can be addressed and
the change anticipated. “Let’s imagine you've changed this behavior. How do you
feel? How does your routine look?”
The doctor can help to structure the change in behavior and focus on small,
manageable steps (from easy to hard) with a realistic timeframe. Overall targets can
be distinguished from strategies and speci�c tasks. This means that the patient’s
motivation to change their behavior can be continuously supported and reinforced.
It is important to take into account previous successes and failures when changing
behavior: “What has helped in the past? Where do you encounter di�culties?”. In
case of relapse, it is important to focus on what can be learned “What went wrong? “,
“What can we learn from this relapse? “.
A meta-analysis has been carried out to investigate the e�cacy of motivational
interviewing in various problem areas. It was shown to have a moderate effect. [B05]
Summary:: Motivational Interviewing

Somatoform disorders:: diagnosis and
treatment
Author:: DDrr.. Sibel Gezer-- CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr. med. Brigitte Leeners, Dr. med. Judith Alder, Prof.
Dickschat Dr. med. Johannes Bitzer
Fachärztin für Gynäkologie und
Geburtshilfe

DDee��nition and classi��cation
Physical symptoms that cannot (or cannot su�ciently) be attributed to a
physical disease. The severity of the symptoms is such that everyday function is
disrupted.
Monosymptomatic form
• One persistent symptom
Polysymptomatic form
• Several physical symptoms insu�ciently explained by physical disorders at the
same time and over time, sometimes years
Examples
• Chronic pelvic pain
• Vulvodynia
• Urogynecological diseases (overactive bladder, chronic recurrent cystitis)
• Mastodynia
How can doctors identify patients with somatoform
disorders??
• Incoherence between subjective suffering and objective �ndings
• Patient believes in a physical cause and persistently requests medical
investigations in spite of negative results
• Multiple somatic investigations
• Patient resistant to external referral to mental health specialists
• Di�cult doctor-patient relationship: Hope-disappointment cycle (see below)
• Frequent change of doctor (doctor shopping, doctor hopping, doctor swapping)
• Thick, often chaotic medical �le
• Feelings of powerlessness and disappointment in patient and medical
professionals
Classi��cation ooff somatoform disorders according ttoo ICD--1100
((FF4455))

• Rare extreme form, multiple symptoms of at least two years duration

F 45.1 Undifferentiated somatoform disorder

F 45.2 Hypochondriacal disorder

F 45.3 Somatoform autonomic dysfunction
• Symptoms involving organs under autonomic innervation
• F45.30 Cardiovascular system
• F45.31 Upper gastrointestinal tract
• F45.32 Lower gastrointestinal tract
• F45.33 Respiratory system
• F45.34 Genitourinary system
• F45.38 Other organs and systems
• F45.39 Unspeci�ed organs and systems
F 45.4 Persistent somatoform pain disorder
• Predominant complaint is pain
• F 45.41 Chronic pain disorder with somatic and psychological factors
F 45.8/9 Other/unspeci�ed somatoform disorders
Single--symptom classi��cation
For individual functional syndromes, a single-symptom classi�cation is possible, for
example:
• K 58: Irritable bowel syndrome
• K 30: Functional dyspepsia
• M 79.70: Fibromyalgia
• G 93.3: Postviral fatigue syndrome
• N 94: Pain and other conditions associated with female genital organs and
menstrual cycle
Classi��cation according ttoo ICD--1111:: Disorders ooff bodily
distress oorr bodily experience
Bodily Distress Disorder (no symptom-based differentiation). Additional
identi�cation according to severity:
• Mild Bodily Distress Disorder

• Moderate Bodily Distress Disorder
• Severe Bodily Distress Disorder
• Body Integrity Dysphoria
Classi��cation according ttoo Diagnostic and Statistical
Manual ooff Mental Disorders,, Fifth Edition ((DSM--VV))
((published June 22001133)):: Somatic Symptom Disorder ((SSD))
Replaces: Somatization disorder, somatoform disorders, hypochondriasis, pain
disorder
Criteria A, B (at least 1) and C must be met:
A) One or more somatic symptoms that are distressing or result in signi�cant
disruption of daily life.
B) Excessive thoughts, feelings, or behaviors related to the somatic symptoms or
associated health concerns as manifested by at least one of the following:
1. Disproportionate and persistent thoughts about the seriousness of one’s
symptoms.
2. Persistently high level of anxiety about health or symptoms.
3. Excessive time and energy devoted to these symptoms or health concerns.
C) Although any one somatic symptom may not be continuously present, the state of
being symptomatic is persistent (typically more than 6 months).
* Specify if: With predominant pain (previously: pain disorder): This speci�er is for
individuals whose somatic symptoms predominantly involve pain.
In the ICD-11 and DSM-V, the corresponding disorders are classi�ed as Bodily
Distress Disorder (ICD-11) and Somatic Symptom Disorder. They are no longer listed
under neurotic disorders and subtypes are omitted.
The key difference with somatoform disorders is that somatic symptom disorder
(DSM-V) and bodily distress disorder (ICD-11) can also be diagnosed in case of
symptoms of physical origin if psychological features dominate.
The most important differential diagnoses are:
• Other physical conditions
• Panic disorder

• Generalized anxiety disorder
• Depressive disorders
• Illness anxiety disorder
• Dissociative disorders (disorder with functional neurological symptoms)
• Delusional disorder
• Body dysmorphic disorder
• Obsessive-compulsive disorder

Epidemiology
In gynecology, 66 % of symptoms are medically unexplained (often not yet meeting
the criteria for a somatic disorder) [P01]. The majority of cases improve/regress
within two weeks.
• Prevalence of people with multiple somatic symptoms (at least 4 symptoms in
men and 6 in women): 5-11 %, 12-month prevalence of somatoform disorders in
women (Germany): 15% [P02].
• Higher proportion in outpatient care: 17–40 %
• Women are signi�cantly more affected: Somatization disorder 5–10:1, Pain
disorder 2:1, Hypochondriasis 1:1
[P01][P02]
Comorbidity with other mental disorders, particularly depression and anxiety, is high.

Etiology
Iatrogenic factors affecting the emergence and continuation
ooff somatoform disorders
• Overlooking the patient’s emotional cues
• Repeated somatic diagnosis for reassurance
• Sharing suspected physical diagnoses prematurely (e.g. before completing
investigations)
• Incorrect interpretation of trivial/incidental �ndings e.g. on MRI
• Prescription of medication despite uncertain diagnosis
• Only negative explanation of symptoms (“There’s nothing wrong”)
• Lack of central coordination of individual investigations and communication
with other HCPs
• Lack of clari�cation of biological, psychological and social factors
Speci��cc factors affecting the emergence and continuation
ooff somatoform disorders
Figure 1: Disorder model of functional physical symptoms (with kind permission of Prof. Dr. med. Peter
Henningsen).
Pathophysiology ooff somatoform disorders

Somatoform disorders have a multifactorial origin with a probable predominance of
psychosocial factors and their somatic correlates.
In a simpli�ed view:
Long-term stress → Chronic activation of stress response centers with impact on
immunological processes → Internal organ dysfunction, tissue damage due to
in�ammatory processes, for example → Chronic nociceptor in�ammation →
Reduced pain threshold, central sensitization associated with adrenal insu�ciency at
the endocrinal level → Disassociation of sensation of pain from original
in�ammatory trigger and independence of pain
Psychophysiological model ooff somatoform disorders
((learning theory))
In addition to these suggested, and in some cases con�rmed, pathophysiological
mechanisms, the psychophysiological model represents the patient’s speci�c
reaction modes in an understandable way:
Figure 2: Psychophysiological model of somatoform disorders.

Key assumptions for managing patients
• Patients with somatoform disorders are no more, but no less, physically ill than
other patients. For this reason, physical conditions must be ruled out repeatedly
over time, for example if symptoms change suddenly.
• The symptom is the disease. The symptom is the result of an impaired
relationship between biological, psychological and social factors.
• Symptoms are real (patients feel pain and are not “pretending”) and should not
be questioned or psychologized. The cause of the symptoms is physical, not
mental, but they have ‘taken on a life of their own’ and lead to psychological
stress.
• The question is less “why?” (cause) and more “how” the symptoms have
emerged (analysis of circumstances and behavior).
• Somatoform disorders present non-fatal chronic disruptions to quality of life;
the focus is on care, not cure.
• Somatization disorders have far-reaching social effects, which are often
gender-speci�c.
Treatment objectives
• Early identi�cation
• Secondary prevention (avoidance of iatrogenic �xation and chronic disorder)
• Extension of the patient’s explanatory model from a preoccupation with purely
physical causes to a wider biological, psychological and social understanding
of the condition.
• Patient feels accepted, respected and supported; reduced anxiety.
• The patient can establish their own connections between their situation
(“stress”), thoughts, feelings and physical symptoms and can develop an
understanding of how the body, mind and environment interact.
• They learn ways to in�uence their body through mental training and their mind
through physical training.
• They develop an understanding of quality of life and an idea of how to control
their quality of life through changes in their behavior.
• They learn about themselves and their life story. They gain insight and new
social skills. Their personal growth is encouraged.
• There may be increased motivation to undergo psychotherapy.

Diagnosis
The diagnosis of somatoform disorders always includes two dimensions:
Physical diagnosis ((bbyy exclusion))
Su�cient physical diagnosis of the physical symptoms presented is necessary.
However, repeated, unnecessary physical diagnostics for exclusionary purposes bear
the risk of the patient further �xating on a purely physical explanation of their
condition. A physical diagnosis by exclusion alone is not a su�cient basis for
diagnosis.
Planned, non-redundant diagnostic investigations carried out over a short period,
coordinated by a “case manager” who consults with other doctors.
Psychological diagnosis
Comorbidity with other psychological disorders, particularly depression and anxiety,
is high. The psychological diagnosis must therefore go beyond the quanti�cation of
symptoms and take into account current emotions, con�ictual and stressful
situations, personal di�culties and social and cultural factors.
A diagnostic recording of psychological factors should be carried out on the �rst
indication of a somatoform disorder, not on completion of all necessary physical
investigations (avoiding the notion of “all in their head”).
• Over 50 % of patients with unexplained physical symptoms reveal psychosocial
factors spontaneously.
If a somatoform disorder is suspected, expand the history beyond the leading
symptoms: other current symptoms, previous course of symptoms and
treatment, indications of other psychological impairments, current psychosocial
di�culties and trigger situations, factors perpetuating the disorder, personal
background.
Questionnaire for Evaluating Somatoform Disorders

The PHQ-15 is a module of the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ) and is used to
establish the severity of somatic symptoms. The total “somatic symptoms” scale
covers 15 somatic symptoms, corresponding to the 15 most common physical
symptoms in outpatients and the most important DSM-IV criteria for somatization
disorder. As it has been shown that the presence or absence of a physical cause has
no relevance for the diagnosis of somatoform disorder, the criterion of a physical
explanation is omitted from the diagnosis of somatoform disorder in DSM-V.
The element of the PHQ-15 that requires no physical explanation for the physical
symptoms can therefore be used to measure the severity of somatic symptoms and
the severity of somatization.
Patient Health Questionnaire-15 (PHQ-15)

Clinical management

Preventing the downward spiral
• Self-awareness for the doctor: What feelings does the patient trigger in me?
• Take the patient’s complaints seriously
• Do not take on the patient’s pressure: “lean back”, composed attitude, treatment
objective is care not cure
• Clear setting (time structure, regular appointments, etc.)
• Patient information: share diagnosis, plan further investigations, share models

Phase 6: Finding solutions, developing a treatment plan
Objective Patients gain new insights into possible connections and learn
about new possible solutions. They then decide on one or more

Knowledge “How can the patient in�uence their body through their thoughts
and skills and behaviors?”, knowledge of treatment options and sharing of

Psychotherapeutic treatment approaches
Criteria for referral ttoo psychotherapy
A psychotherapy referral, discussed in advance with the patient, should take place if:
• No improvement is observed after six months of appropriate psychosomatic
treatment or the severity/acuity of symptoms deems this appropriate
• The patient is unable to work for more than four weeks at a time
• There is a suspicion of additional acute mental disorders or obvious personal
factors with a strong in�uence
• The patient desires psychotherapeutic treatment
• Interactional issues with the patient complicate treatment
Despite the limited evidence, psychotherapy should be clearly recommended due to
the �ndings. Most evidence is available for the effect of cognitive behavioral therapy
(CBT).
Psychotherapeutic interventions
Learning physical controls on “neutral” territory
• Relaxation techniques
• Biofeedback
• Body scan meditation
Behavioral experiments
• Directing attention to change bodily perception
Provoking physical reactions
• Stair-climbing
• Abandoning protective behaviors
• Objective: Develop a neutral concept of health and increase tolerance of
(normal) physical reactions
Encouraging positive experiences and behaviors
• Encourage positive experiences and management
• “Enjoyment training”
• Increased activity (physical, social, cultural, work, leisure)
• “Distraction”
• Encourage social interactions/skills

Pharmacotherapy
In cases of comorbid depression or anxiety disorders, drug-based treatment and/or
psychotherapeutic treatment should be considered.
Temporary use of analgesics may relieve complaints and symptoms.

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 2/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Congenital malformations
Anatomical and physiological remarks
In humans, as in all mammals, the breast is located along the anatomically
determined mammary ridge. The mammary ridges extend bilaterally ventrally from
the axillary region across the thoracic and abdominal walls to the inguinal region.
Depending on the species, two or more mammary glands are formed along this line
(cf. dogs, cows, pigs). The two mammary glands of humans are paired and located
on the upper half of the thorax. Accessory breasts (usually underdeveloped) and
accessory glandular breast tissue (usually axillary) can develop along the mammary
ridge. Often, accessory glandular breast tissue only becomes clinically visible or
relevant during pregnancy/lactation, when sudden swelling can occur in the axillary
region due to mammary gland stimulation.
Breast development is triggered by the onset of hormonal activity in pre-puberty. The
appearance of breasts, which generally occurs one to two years before menarche, is
referred to as thelarche. There are several stages between thelarche and breast
maturity, which - similar to genital development - are classi�ed using the Tanner
scale.
Table 1: Tanner’s 5 stages (B1-B5) [03] [03]
Stage Age Breast development Physical development

years))
B1 < 8 No glandular tissue Pre-adolescent
B2 11 Breast buds form Growth in height
B3 12 Enlargement of breast and Height growth spurt
