topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 3/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Full differentiation of the breast is a process that takes several years and requires
regular exposure to hormones, particularly female hormones. The very last
differentiation stage of the mammary gland occurs during pregnancy and lactation,
when it performs its lactation function.
Adult breasts are subject to cyclical hormone �uctuations throughout a woman’s
reproductive life.
These cyclical breast changes are normal but can be associated with various clinical
�ndings and symptoms that women sometimes consider not only undesirable but
even pathological (mastalgia = breast pain/tension/sensitivity to touch [see below]).
Breast malformations
Amastia (absence of a breast)
• Very rare, usually unilateral
• Cause: Failure of mammary development, mammary ridge involution
• Speci�c forms: Poland's syndrome, amastia with the absence of pectoralis
muscles and syndactyly
[01]
Athelia (absence of nipple-areola complex or nipple)
• Very rare
Polythelia (accessory nipple-areola complex or nipple)
• Along the mammary ridge
• Usually identi�able as an underdeveloped pigmented protrusion
Tubular breasts (congenital breast anomaly)
• Manifests from puberty
• Breast asymmetry is common
• Underdevelopment of breast parenchyma
• Constricted breast base and higher than normal inframammary fold
• Skin envelope insu�ciency
• Areolar herniation
• Psychological stress
• Corrective surgery covered by statutory health insurance: release of constricted
breast base +/- augmentation (implant)

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 4/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Figure 1: Tubular breast (right > left) with underdevelopment of breast parenchyma, constriction of breast
base and higher than normal inframammary fold, herniated areola (with the kind authorization of Dr. D.
Vorburger).
Breast Hypoplasia//Breast Hyperplasia
Women differ widely in how they experience and evaluate their breast size, including
in relationship to their body habitus. There is no standard size. Social expectations
and cultural factors play a role. The “ideal” image portrayed in the media can lead to
doubts as early as adolescence. Deviations from perceived norms can therefore
impact quality of life and lead to psychological stress.
Table 2: Breast weight in Europe (not absolute values)
Breast size Breast weight
"Normal" breast 250 to 500 g
"Hypoplastic" breast < 250 g
"Large" breast > 500 g
Macromastia > 750 g
• Hypoplastic breasts do not present any medical problems. Breastfeeding is also
generally possible.
• Breast hyperplasia (or macromastia) can cause psychological stress.
Furthermore, the weight of the breasts can create orthopedic problems and

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 5/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
signi�cant neck and back pain. A corrective procedure in the form of breast
reduction surgery is often necessary.
[01]
Breast asymmetry
Breast asymmetry is a very frequent �nding upon close clinical examination.
Asymmetries are usually minimal or very discreet and do not trouble women.
However, severe asymmetries may have a psychological impact on affected women,
who often desire corrective plastic surgery in the case of severe asymmetry (photo
breast asymmetry). A growing tumor that could be responsible for the breast
asymmetry must be ruled out.
Figure 2: Clear asymmetry of at least two cup sizes, larger on right (with the kind authorization of Dr. D.
Vorburger).
Depending on the woman’s body habitus and breast size, the larger breast may
have to be reduced to the size of the smaller breast, or the smaller breast be
augmented - or a combination of both procedures may be performed. It must be
noted that breast asymmetry in puberty can be temporary. Breasts do not always
develop synchronously.
Adolescents require good counseling to prevent premature surgical correction! The
breasts may mature slowly and the less developed breast “catch up” after a regular
menstruation cycle is established. [01]

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 6/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Breast iinn��ammation ((mastitis))
There are two main types of mastitis (in�ammation of the mammary gland):
• Non-puerperal mastitis: All breast in�ammation outside the periods of
pregnancy and lactation
• Puerperal mastitis: In�ammation of the mammary gland during puerperium
(pregnancy and lactation period).
Puerperal mastitis
This involves the spread of pathogens (usually staphylococci) that encounter
favorable conditions in the lactating breast. Further information (e.g. prophylaxis,
therapy): O07A. Breast in�ammation (swelling, redness) most often occurs
peripherally on the breast (not periareolar) and is often accompanied by fever and
headache. Abscesses can form and are easily diagnosed on ultrasound. Wherever
possible, abscesses should be aspirated (usually repeatedly!) rather than treated
with surgical incision in order to prevent unattractive scarring of the breast.
Conventional 19 gage needles are easily blocked and lead to unsuccessful
aspiration. If the pus is thick, breast biopsy needle guides (coaxial biopsy needles)
(13 gage) are effective for draining and irrigation of the abscess cavity with local
anesthetic and NaCl. A combination of drainage and antibiotics is often required.
Although common skin bacteria such as staphylococci and streptococci are usually
responsible for breast infections, testing for methicillin-resistant staphylococcus
aureus (MRSA) should be carried out if there is no clinical improvement.

Figure 3: Puerperal mastitis with typical peripheral abscess and early rupture (with the kind authorization of
Dr. D. Vorburger).
Non--puerperal mastitis
Periductal mastitis

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 7/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
This is an in�ammation of the retroareolar milk ducts of unknown origin. Most
patients are smokers. A combination of nicotine-induced damage to the retroareolar
milk ducts and bacterial infection appears to be the cause of periductal
in�ammation (90 % of patients are smokers). Clinical signs include periareolar
redness, potentially associated with swelling and abscess formation. Such
abscesses often drain spontaneously at the edge of the areola via a �stula.
Treatment is primarily conservative (antibiotics, anti-in�ammatories, local cooling).
Empirical antibiotic treatment is administered according to the expected pathogen
spectrum (approx. 70 % aerobes, approx. 25 % anaerobes), primarily with amoxicillin/
clavulanic acid, 3 x 1 g for approx. 10 days. In case of penicillin allergy or MRSA,
clindamycin 3 x 600 mg [04]. Larger abscesses that do not form �stula should be
aspirated (if necessary, repeatedly) and treated without surgery where possible; in
combination with antibiotic treatment as per antibiogram. Patients should be
encouraged to stop smoking. In chronic cases, surgery may be carried out during
non-in�ammatory periods. The procedure should be carried out by trained breast
surgeons. Periareolar incision to remove the �stula and retroareolar excision of the
affected milk ducts with primary closure gives the best cosmetic results. The
procedure can cause loss of sensitivity in the nipple-areola complex in approx. 40%
of cases and can lead to issues with breastfeeding due to the reduction in the
number of milk ducts due to excision and subsequent scarring (�gure 4). [04]
Figure 4: Periductal mastitis, typical in smokers. In case of abscess perforation, the formation of a �stula at
the edge of the areola is common (with the kind authorization of Dr. D. Vorburger).
Rarer forms ooff mastitis

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 8/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Idiopathic Granulomatous Mastitis ((IGM))
This is a rare form of breast in�ammation of unknown origin that most often affects
an isolated ductal lobular unit. Anamnesis suggests that multiparous women are
mainly affected, several years after the lactation period. An ethnic predisposition is
probable, as this type of breast in�ammation is found more commonly in women
with Middle Eastern, North African, Asian, Latin American and Spanish origins.
Redness and swelling are usually peripheral. Imaging (mammography, ultrasound)
often reveals solid �ndings that cannot be distinguished from breast cancer. Biopsy
is essential both for microbiological diagnosis and for histological classi�cation.
Histologically, granuloma are detectable. At the microbiological level, except in rare
cases of superinfection, smears are sterile. An autoimmune process is assumed to
be the cause. Systemic steroids, in combination with methotrexate in repeat cases,
can be used for treatment. The prognosis is relatively good, although the disease can
last several months and the psychological burden is high. Good communication and
explanation are important. Surgery should be avoided as it is rarely expedient and
can lead to unsightly scarring.
Figure 5: Idiopathic granulomatous mastitis with frequent multiple, peripheral, sterile abscesses (with the
kind authorization of Dr. D. Vorburger).

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 9/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Figure 6: Unsightly scarring after surgical abscess excision (with the kind authorization of Dr. D. Vorburger).
Initial misdiagnosis is common with this pathology, leading to repeat courses of
antibiotics (DD mastitis). Surgery may also be carried out for suspected malignant
processes seen on imaging (DD breast cancer).
Note: Other rare breast in�ammations include breast tuberculosis, actinomycosis,
sarcoidosis with breast involvement, IgG4-related diseases, diabetic mastopathy and
granulomatosis with polyangiitis [21]. [21]
In terms of differential diagnosis, in�ammatory breast cancer must be
considered in every case of non-puerperal mastitis. If redness does not improve
within 48 hours of starting antibiotics, breast imaging is required. This can give
an indication for biopsy.

Cellulitis ooff the breast
This is in�ammation of the breast localized to the skin.
It is caused by common skin bacteria such as staphylococci. Previous breast
surgeries and/or radiotherapy (+/- breast lymphedema) are predisposing factors.
Overweight and diabetes mellitus can also increase the risk of cellulitis.
Mycosis must also be considered, e. g. nipple thrush, intertrigo of the inframammary
fold or pityriasis versicolor of the skin of the breast [04]. [04]
Nipple eczema: localized eczema of the nipple-areolar complex that does not
improve with topical steroids should be biopsied (core needle biopsy of the skin).
This may be Paget’s disease of the nipple. If so, core needle biopsy will �nd
histologically malignant intraepithelial adenocarcinoma cells. Paget’s disease of the
nipple can occur in isolation or in combination with concomitant invasive breast
cancer. Complete imaging of the breast must be carried out urgently. Paget’s disease
must be treated surgically as a precursor of breast cancer (ductal carcinoma in situ,
DCIS).
Figure 7: Paget’s disease of the nipple. Eczematous changes are a typical sign (with the kind authorization of
Dr. D. Vorburger).

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 10/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Mastalgia
Mastalgia is not a disease of the breast, but one of the most frequent symptoms
with which women present at senology consultations.
Mastalgia covers all manifestations of pain in the breast area.
Clinically, it is important to distinguish between cyclical and non-cyclical mastalgia.
Cyclical mastalgia
A painful sensation in the breast area that occurs during some parts of the
menstrual cycle, usually in the second half - partly in a context of premenstrual
syndrome. Patients are generally between 40 and 50 years old (anovulatory cycles,
hormonal imbalance, (increased estrogen stimulation in �rst half of cycle, lack of
antagonist (progesterone) in case of anovulation). See also below: Fibrocystic
mastopathy.
The cause is assumed to be hormonal in the widest sense, probably in the form of
estrogen/progesterone imbalance or a different receptor response to hormones.
Dietary factors and nicotine can also play a role.
There are usually no underlying organic changes.
Clinically, patients complain about swelling and tension of the breasts, especially in
the upper outer quadrants where mammary gland density is highest. The breast and
nipples are more sensitive to touch.
Treatment
• Therapeutically, local application of NSAID emulsions (diclofenac, Sportusal) or
hormonal application of progesterone in the second half of the cycle (gel:
Progestogel®) can be attempted (NB: Progestogel is contraindicated in
patients with a history of breast cancer).
• As cyclical premenstrual mastalgia occurs predominantly in natural cycles,
“elimination” of the cycle, e.g., with anovulants, can be attempted for younger
patients.
• Some complementary medicine approaches might be successful as well (e.g.,
herbal preparations such as chaste tree [Premens]).

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 11/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Acyclical mastalgia
Acyclical mastalgia can be caused by previous in�ammation with subsequent
scarring of the breast. Women with a history of breast surgery, e.g. in the context of
breast cancer with subsequent radiotherapy, may also experience acyclical
mastalgia. Pain may be caused by scar tissue, nerve damage or fat necrosis.
Note: Pain is not a typical sign of carcinoma, but may accompany breast cancer.
This must therefore be ruled out.
Furthermore, in terms of differential diagnostics, a musculoskeletal condition in the
widest sense that radiates into the breasts, where the patient feels primarily breast
pain rather than the musculoskeletal cause, must be considered.
Diagnosis
In addition to clinical examination of the breast and lymph drainage routes,
depending on the age of the patient, mammography (from 40 years) or breast
ultrasound (for younger women) is indicated for diagnostic purposes.

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 12/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Fibrocystic changes
DDee��nition
Fibrocystic breast changes include numerous hormone-dependent changes of the
mammary gland parenchyma. Since most tissue changes are “physiologic” reactions
that do not constitute illnesses per se, the term “mastopathy” should no longer be
used as a pathological entity. The risk of cancer is not increased.
Histological changes include a local or diffuse increase of connective tissue (=
�brosis), the formation of small and large cysts resulting from stasis of secretion,
partly associated with apocrine metaplasia of the cyst epithelium, as well as
proliferation of the glandular acini, which is referred to as adenosis. The surrounding
connective tissue can partly proliferate as well and/or undergo regressive changes,
leading to what is referred to as “sclerosing adenosis.” All these changes can be
accompanied by a more or less severe in�ammatory cell reaction.
It is important to note that many of these histological changes are associated with
calci�cations both in the lobular lumen and the connective tissue, visible on x-ray as
microcalci�cations. [03]
Clinical presentation
Patients typically complain of breast pain (mastalgia), which is usually cyclical and
most pronounced in the upper outer quadrants. Clinically, palpation reveals a nodular
mammary gland, which is di�cult to evaluate for the examiner, and occasionally
nipple discharge (galactorrhea). In a very large percentage of cases, however,
�brocystic changes of the mammary gland are asymptomatic and represent
incidental �ndings during the removal of benign and/or malignant lesions. They are
found in more than 50 % of middle-aged women.
Diagnosis
Suitable breast imaging must always be performed to exclude malignancy:
mammography, breast ultrasound or a combination thereof, depending on age.
Treatment
There is no causal treatment for mastopathy. The only option is to try to mitigate the
patient’s symptoms through hormonal or symptomatic treatment (see also
Treatment of mastalgia). For larger, painful cysts, drainage can provide relief. [03]

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 13/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Benign tumors ooff the breast
• Fibroadenomas are an example of benign tumors speci�cally affecting the
breast.
• As in all other organs comprising different cell types, benign tumors can
develop from stromal components in the breast (e. g. lipomas, hamartomas).
Fibroadenoma
DDee��nition
Fibroadenomas are the most common benign breast tumors. They are de�ned as
proliferations of epithelial ("adeno") and mesenchymal ("�bro") parts of the
mammary gland.
Clinical presentation
• Fibroadenoma is associated with a �rm, smooth, well delineated and very
mobile, generally painless palpatory �nding in the breast.
• In 20 % of cases, �broadenomas occur in multiples - either unilaterally or
bilaterally.
• Fibroadenomas are mainly found in young women between the ages of 15 and
35 years but are possible at any age.
• Since �broadenomas are somewhat hormone-dependent, they may grow during
a woman’s reproductive years. Existing �broadenomas are particularly likely to
grow during pregnancy.
• Usually, no new �broadenomas develop after menopause.
• Particularly in older women, �broadenomas can stop growing or even become
smaller.
• On ultrasound, �broadenomas have circumscribed margins and are oriented
parallel to the skin, with no disruption to Cooper’s ligaments and often bilateral
acoustic shadowing.

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 14/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Note: Any new palpatory �nding in the breast after menopause cannot be a
�broadenoma. Breast cancer must be assumed until proven otherwise!
Some aggressive breast cancers can be misinterpreted as �broadenomas, including
in younger patients. Family history is therefore important. If there is a signi�cant
family history of breast cancer, a breast biopsy should be carried out in case of
suspected �broadenoma in young patients. This is also the case with rapidly
growing �ndings.
If �broadenoma is suspected, diagnostic tests include clinical examination as well
as imaging, primarily breast ultrasound and - depending on age - additional
mammography. In the event of typical presentation in imaging, watchful waiting with
follow-up ultrasound (initially after 6 months) is possible. Otherwise, a core needle
biopsy is performed for histological con�rmation.
Treatment
Depending on the patient’s age and the size of the palpatory �nding, watchful waiting
is often an option, with clinical and sonographic follow-up, if all examinations
suggest a benign lesion. This also applies to women with multiple �broadenomas.
Surgical removal is indicated in the case of growth or structural changes con�rmed
on ultrasound, following core needle biopsy to con�rm diagnosis. Findings of up to 3
cm can be removed using ultrasound-guided vacuum-assisted excision. Larger
�broadenomas are often excised.
Lipoma
DDee��nition
A lipoma is an encapsulated, soft nodule consisting of mature adipose tissue cells.
All lipomas exhibit a connective tissue capsule.
Clinically, lipoma is associated with typical palpatory �ndings and slow growth, and
there is no elevated risk of breast cancer. [03]
Treatment
None
Hamartoma
DDee��nition

Hamartomas (�broadenolipomas) are rare, benign tumors of the breast. They
consist of glandular, adipose, and �brous tissue components.
Treatment
None. Excision may be performed in the event of growth.

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 15/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Lesions ooff uncertain malignant potential
((BB33 lesions))
The B classi�cation is used for pathological classi�cation of �ndings after minimally
invasive breast biopsies.
• B1: Normal tissue or uninterpretable sample
• B2: Benign lesion
• B3: Benign lesion of uncertain malignant potential
• B4: Suspicious of malignancy
• B5: Malignant
• B5a: Carcinoma in situ
• B5b: Invasive carcinoma
• B5c: Uncertain whether in situ or invasive
• B5d: Malignancy of other histogenesis or metastasis
B3 lesions include:
1. Atypical ductal and lobular hyperplasia (ADH, ALH), lobular carcinoma in situ
(LCIS), �at epithelial atypia (FEA), papillary lesions, radial scar and
�broepithelial lesions. Rare changes include adenomyoepithelioma,
microglandular adenosis, mucocele-like lesion, nodular fasciitis, desmoid-type
�bromatosis, and spindle cell lesion of unknown signi�cance.
2. They can occur with carcinoma in situ or invasive breast cancer and increase
the risk of later development of breast cancer.
[22]
Phyllodes tumors
DDee��nition
Phyllodes tumors are rare, representing only 0,5 % of all breast tumors. Phyllodes
tumors are classi�ed as benign, borderline or malignant based on the atypia and
mitotic rate of the stromal component.
• Benign phyllodes tumors never develop distant metastases but may recur
locally following excision (7 %).
• Borderline phyllodes tumors recur locally much more frequently (25 %) but
metastasize rarely (4 %).
• Malignant phyllodes tumors often recur locally as well (27 %) and develop
distant metastases (13 to 40 %). The tumor behaves like a sarcoma and

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 16/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
metastasizes hematogenously. Lymph node metastases are very rare.
Diagnosis
By means of mammography and breast ultrasound. In imaging, benign phyllodes
tumors are very similar to �broadenomas. The diagnosis is con�rmed with core
needle biopsy. In case of suspected �broadenoma over 3 cm or rapid growth, a
phyllodes tumor must be considered and a histological examination performed.
Treatment
If a borderline or malignant phyllodes tumor is suspected in core needle biopsy,
complete surgical removal of the �nding with a safety margin of 1 cm is indicated.
Recent meta-analyses indicate that there is no association between the safety
margin and risk of local recurrence for benign phyllodes tumors, so narrower
margins are acceptable in this case [23, 24]. Follow-up ultrasounds are initially
required every six months after tumorectomy. [23][24]
Atypical ductal hyperplasia ((ADH))
Diagnosis
Atypical ductal hyperplasia is one of the most commonly diagnosed B3 lesions and
often associated with microcalci�cations on mammography. ADH is characterized
by the formation of a clonal cell proliferation consisting of luminal epithelial cells.
This clonal proliferation represents a biological continuum to the point of intraductal
carcinoma.
If the sum of the diameters of all affected lumina in one terminal ductal lobular unit
(TDLU) does not exceed 2 mm, this is referred to as ADH. Proliferations larger than 2
mm or more than two completely �lled ducts are referred to as (low grade) DCIS.
Generally, the diagnosis of ADH presents an indicator lesion that increases the risk
of ipsi- and contralateral breast cancer: RR 3- to 5-fold after 10 years. In case of
multifocality or more than 3 foci with microcalci�cations, RR increases to 10.35 (95
% CI 6.13-16.4). [11]
Imaging
Microcalci�cations on mammography are common, as is asymmetric density, but
rarely a mass.
Treatment

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 17/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
If ADH is con�rmed with core needle or vacuum-assisted biopsy for a lesion visible
on imaging, open excision should take place (the rate of underestimation with
upgrading to DCIS is 5 to 50 %). Imaging-guided vacuum-assisted excision can be
carried out if the area to be excised is < 15 mm. If the ADH is larger than 15 mm,
open excision should be carried out [22]. Annual mammography is recommended
after a diagnosis of ADH [13, 14]. [13][14][22]
Intraductal Papilloma
DDee��nition
Intraductal papillomas are epithelial proliferations around a �brovascular core that
are located in the excretory ducts of the mammary gland [02]. They manifest as
increased production of �uid, which is evidenced by discharge from the affected milk
duct. The discharge may be watery, milky or bloody. [02]
Diagnosis
Mammography and ultrasound imaging. Larger papillomas can be displayed
sonographically, with the dilated milk duct with the intraductal proliferation being
visible, and microcalci�cations may be seen on mammography. An MRI is indicated
if mammography and ultrasound are normal. Individual milk duct papillomas are
often found close to the nipple, whereas multiple papillomas occur more peripherally
on the breast. Peripheral papillomas are more frequently associated with ADH (7 to
18%) and/or DCIS (3 to 10 %) than central papillomas. If there is pathological
(bloody) nipple discharge and/or a lesion on mammography/ultrasound, a vacuum-
assisted biopsy should be carried out. If there are no atypia, or ADH is present, VAB
is su�cient and follow-up can take the form of imaging. However, if biopsy (core
needle or VAB) reveals a papilloma with ADH, open surgical excision is
recommended. Multiple papillomas involve a three-fold increase in risk for invasive
breast cancer in the future. Follow-up imaging is required. [05][06][07][08][09][10]
Flat epithelial atypia ((FEA))
Diagnosis
Flat epithelial atypia (FEA) refers to epithelial changes limited to one or few layers of
cells, also referred to as columnar cell metaplasia or hyperplasia with atypia. FEA is
often associated with microcalci�cations. It can be considered a marker lesion since
a signi�cant correlation (p < 0,0001) has been demonstrated between the detection
of an FEA and discovery of an ADH or even low-grade DCIS. Breast cancer risk as a
result of FEA is estimated to be low (RR 1.47 95 % CI 1.0-2.2). [15][16][17]

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 18/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Treatment
If FEA without ADH is diagnosed on core needle biopsy or VAB, the patient can be
monitored with imaging. FEA with ADH diagnosed on core needle biopsy should be
treated with open excision or VAE.
If FEA with ADH is diagnosed on VAB and not all microcalci�cations are removed,
open excision or VAE should be carried out [22].
If FEA is con�rmed by core needle or vacuum-assisted biopsy, an open follow-up
resection can be avoided if the microcalci�cations were completely removed.
Biennial mammography is recommended after a diagnosis of FEA. [22]
Lobular ((intraepithelial)) neoplasia ((LLNN))
DDee��nition
The term lobular neoplasia (LN) covers any epithelial proliferations that originate
from the terminal ductal lobular unit. It replaces the formerly used terms lobular
hyperplasia, atypical lobular hyperplasia, and lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS). The
alternative term “lobular intraepithelial neoplasia” (LIN 1-3) has been promoted but
not yet validated by WHO. [06]
LN comprises:
• Atypical lobular hyperplasia (ALH)
• Classical lobular carcinoma in situ (classical LN)
• Both are B3 lesions
Non-classical LN includes pleomorphic LN and �orid LN, which are comparable with
DCIS. They are classi�ed as pre-malignant and categorized as B5a.
Both lesions are risk factors for invasive breast cancer for a relative increase in risk
(approx. 8-10-fold) versus the general female population. [25]
Diagnosis
LN is not detectable with imaging. It is usually an incidental �nding alongside other
lesions that are detected with mammography. It is an indicator or precursor lesion
and associated with an increased risk of ipsi- and contralateral breast cancer. The
annual breast cancer development risk is approx. 2 % per year after a diagnosis of
LCIS.
Treatment

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 19/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
If core needle biopsy reveals a classical LCIS that is visible on imaging, VAB/VAE is
recommended, followed by imaging-based monitoring.
• If there is a radiological/pathological discrepancy after core needle or vacuum
biopsy, open excision or VAE is recommended.
• If an LN of the pleomorphic subtype (B5a) is detected, follow-up open resection
is indicated.
[11][18][19][22]
Annual mammography is recommended after a diagnosis of LN.
Radial scar ((RRSS))//complex sclerosing lesions
DDee��nition
According to WHO, radial scars and complex sclerosing lesions are the same entity
with a star-like con�guration and a central �brovascular core, without reactive
�broelastic stroma. The periphery often contains enclosed glandular structures and
cysts aligned in a radial organization. These lesions are frequently associated with
calci�cations and sclerosing adenosis.
Diagnosis
Typically, RS is visible on mammography as architectural distortion, often associated
with microcalci�cations. On ultrasound, RS is only sometimes visible as a
hypoechoic, irregular mass with non-circumscribed margins. RS is often diagnosed
by means of stereotactic breast biopsy (VAB).
Treatment
RS without atypia (without ADH), diagnosed on core needle biopsy, can be treated
with VAE.
RS with atypia (with ADH) increases the risk of upgrading to invasive carcinoma. If
diagnosis is based on VAB, open excision is recommended but VAE may be
considered. [22]

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 20/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Galactorrhea,, spontaneous ((non--bloody))
nipple discharge
DDee��nition
The term galactorrhea refers to spontaneous milky or watery discharge of different
colors from the nipple. Galactorrhea is de�ned as nipple discharge unrelated to
pregnancy or the lactation period. Galactorrhea typically manifests as a bilateral,
milky nipple discharge from several milk ducts. The color of the discharge may be
white or transparent, or even yellowish, green, brown or gray, but never bloody. The
cause of galactorrhea is often hyperprolactinemia, which can develop as a side
effect of various medications (e. g. psychotropic drugs), but also in connection with
pituitary adenoma or endocrine disorders (e. g. hypothyroidism).
Physiological causes of galactorrhea or nipple discharge:
• Milky discharge is physiological during pregnancy, postpartum, lactation, and up
to 2 years after cessation of lactation.
• Nipple discharge can also develop as a result of prolonged stress or continued
breast stimulation.
The following diseases should be considered in case of non-physiological nipple
discharge:
• Diseases of the hypothalamic system (tumor, in�ammation, idiopathic),
pituitary tumor (prolactinoma), hypothyroidism, chronic kidney disease,
paraneoplastic event, (e. g. bronchial carcinoma).
• In addition, the patient’s exact medication history should be asked about, as
nipple discharge can be a side effect of numerous drugs e. g. psychotropic
drugs, opiates, antihypertensives, antihistamines, antiemetics, and many others.
Clinical Examination:
• Unilateral or bilateral spontaneous nipple discharge (inquire during history).
• Color of the discharge?
• From how many milk duct openings does the discharge appear? If the secretion
appears from a single milk duct, milk duct papilloma or even carcinoma is more
likely (see above for Management).
• Inspection and palpation of the breasts and axillary and infra-/supraclavicular
lymph nodes

topic: Benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential (Teil 21/23)
keywords: benign breast findings and lesions of uncertain malignant potential
content:
Diagnosis
For diagnosis, always consider
• Galactorrhea (= systemic), affecting both breasts, with bilateral discharge (often
more pronounced on one side) or
• Pathological nipple discharge (= local, isolated) from one milk duct with
exclusively unilateral discharge.
Examination steps for galactorrhea:
• Imaging using breast ultrasound and mammography
• Blood sampling including pregnancy test (unless postmenopausal) as well as
hormonal analysis (prolactin, thyroid hormones) and kidney panel
• If prolactin values are signi�cantly increased (e.g. > 200 pmol/L), cranial MRI
might be indicated to investigate possibility of pituitary adenoma
Treatment
The therapeutic approach is determined by the underlying cause:
• For galactorrhea: for example, drug therapy for hyperprolactinemia
(bromocriptine, lisuride or cabergoline)

EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Senology 〉 Breast cancer: spread & prevention
Last update: June 2024
Breast cancer –– Spread and prevention
Author:: Prof.. DDrr.. med.. Uwe Güth
Facharzt Gynäkologie und Geburtshilfe, Brust-Zentrum Zürich (Senior Consultant) Medizinische Fakultät
der Universität Basel
Authors of former script versions: Dr. med. Martin Heubner, Prof. Dr. med.
Mathias Fehr, Prof. Dr. med. Walter Jonat, Prof. Dr. med. Rolf Kreienberg, Dr méd.
Jean-François Delaloye

Spread
Breast cancer initially grows as a primary tumor in the breast (most common
location: upper outer quadrant, �gure 1). In addition to local growth in size, a
malignant tumor may also metastasize; typically, with breast cancer, this is
initially lymphogenic, and also hematogenous at a later point.
In patients with a clinical cancer diagnosis (≤ 5 cm, self-examination or during a
medical examination), central/retroareolar tumors are signi�cantly larger than those
located peripherally in the quadrants (25 vs. 20–21 mm; tumor size in the peripheral
regions only differs minimally). [P11]
The following prognosis factors are associated with an elevated risk of metastasis:
• Number of lymph nodes affected by metastasis (TNM classi�cation: pN
category)
• Tumor size (pT category)
• Level of tumor differentiation (grading)
• Presence of vascular or lymphatic invasion
• Tumor biology factor (hormone receptor status, Her2 overexpression, Ki-67)
Lymphogenic metastasis
The breast lymph �ows from the subepithelial to the subdermal lymphatic vessels
and channels into a subareolar plexus. From there, vessels lead deep into the
parenchyma and to the axillary lymph nodes, and less frequently to the intercostal
and parasternal lymph nodes (internal mammary chain). In rare cases, the "usual"
path to the axillary lymph nodes is bypassed, meaning that skip metastases can be
found in the high axillary or infra/supraclavicular lymph nodes. Cutaneous lymphatic
vessels can also lead to the skin over the contralateral breast or even the
contralateral axilla.
As lymph primarily drains into the axilla, this is where lymph node metastases are
most frequently found. Only approx. 3 % �ows medially to the internal mammary
chain, mostly from the deep and thoracic wall-adjacent areas of the breast. In
clinical/morphological terms, three levels of axillary lymph nodes are identi�ed
(�gure 1):
• Level I is lateral to the lateral edge of the pectoralis minor muscle
• Level II is located under the pectoralis minor muscle

• Level III is medial to the medial edge of the pectoralis minor muscle (to the
clavicle)
Figure 1: Lymph drainage pathways in breast cancer.
The dermal and parenchymal lymphatic vessels primarily lead to a speci�c lymph
node (or a group of 2 to 3 lymph nodes, but very rarely more). These represent the
lymph drainage of the breast and will be the �rst to be affected in the case of
metastasis. These sentinel lymph nodes can be marked, identi�ed during surgery
and then removed selectively (�gure 2). If these sentinel lymph nodes are tumor-free,
it can then be assumed that all other axillary lymph nodes are also tumor-free. Since
the method was introduced in the early 2000s, it has quickly become a standard
procedure and just a few years later, two-thirds of patients with breast cancer who
underwent surgery were treated with this tissue-preserving approach. The
"traditional" lymphadenectomy has therefore been replaced as the standard
procedure for surgical staging of the axilla. [B11][B23]
Peritumoral or subareolar radioactive colloid injection, usually technetium-99m. The
tracer is transported to the regional sentinel lymph node (usually in the axilla) via the
lymphatic vessels. The radioactively marked lymph node can then be identi�ed
during surgery and removed. In the early part of the “sentinel age”, blue dye was also
used for marking; this has been largely abandoned in recent years. [B21]

Figure 2: Principle of sentinel lymph node marking.
Hematogenous metastasis

Undesired adverse events are predominantly thromboembolic events. The relative
risk of pulmonary embolism under tamoxifen was 2.15; the risk of thromboembolic
events under raloxifene was 1.6. As tamoxifen, unlike raloxifene, acts as an estrogen
agonist on the endometrium, the incidence of endometrial was signi�cantly
increased in women aged over 50 years (RR: 3.28). However, hot �ashes and leg
cramps were more common with raloxifene. [B08]
Two studies have shown that aromatase inhibitors (anastrozole: Arimidex®,
generics; exemestane: Aromasin®, generics) have a preventive effect on both
invasive and preinvasive hormone receptor-positive breast cancer: In the
International Breast Cancer Intervention Study II (IBIS-II study) the risk under
anastrozole fell signi�cantly by 53 % versus placebo; in the Mammary Prevention
Trial 3 (MAP-III study) the risk reduction under exemestane was 65 %. The treatment-
related adverse effects included hot �ashes, musculoskeletal symptoms and
accelerated bone loss [P03, P05]. The data is less convincing for women who carry
the BRCA1/2 genes. However, smaller studies show good indications that tamoxifen
reduces the risk of breast cancer at least in women with the BRCA2 gene [P08].
Nevertheless, risk-reducing mastectomy is more effective in this population (see
below). [P03][P05][P08]
According to the ASCO guidelines, endocrine prevention with tamoxifen and/or
raloxifene for �ve years should be considered for women with an increased risk of
breast cancer and discussed with the patient. However, as an improvement in breast
cancer-speci�c or overall survival has not been proven for either substance, this
recommendation is controversial:
• Proponents of chemoprevention see every prevented case as an argument for
drug-based intervention.
• Critics justify their position with the fact that chemoprevention may not actually
improve breast cancer mortality and the (in their opinion too few) bene�ts do
not outweigh the potential side effects and complications of endocrine therapy
over several years.
The use of low-dose medication with tamoxifen 5 mg/d is an interesting new
development in this respect. Here, it has been shown that even with this low dose,
taken over just three years, a signi�cant reduction in new neoplastic diseases of the
breast could be achieved in an at-risk population (500 patients with biopsy-
con�rmed atypical ductal hyperplasia and lobular or ductal carcinoma in situ) (HR:
0,48), with only limited treatment-related adverse effects. [B05]

The most important method of secondary prophylaxis is breast cancer screening by
means of mammography (�gure 3). This is an x-ray examination of the mammary
glands using two views, which can be used for a truly early diagnosis of breast
cancer. In general, breast cancers which are detected early have a better prognosis
and their treatment is often simpler and less aggressive than diseases in an
advanced stage.
Screening vs. early detection: In order to avoid giving patients false expectations
with regard to the potential of the method, it is important to use the right
terminology. A “screening” mammography is not a “preventive” examination, but an
examination with the objective of early detection. A comparison with cervical
screening (a “smear” test) makes this clear: during cervical screening, a cytological
smear is used to identify premalignant changes to the cervix (dysplasia: CIN I-III),
which could, sometimes over many years, develop into cancer. From a certain stage
of development, a surgical procedure can then be carried out. Screening means that
the precancerous growth is identi�ed early and stopped, so that the cancer does not
actually develop. With mammography, this form of screening is almost impossible,
although in isolated cases, non-invasive (or not-yet-invasive) changes can be
identi�ed (e.g. ductal carcinoma in situ [DCIS], which presents at a subclinical stage
with typical grouped polymorphic microcalci�cations). The actual objective of
mammography is to identify small invasive tumors at a size that means that they are
not yet palpable. Generally, mammography does not show the cancer in its early non-
invasive form (“screening”), but with the lowest possible level of morphological
spread (“early detection”). The idea behind this being that we don’t want to only �nd
a cancer when it is palpable for the patient (e.g. 2.5 cm), but rather when it is still a
lesion that is not palpable but can only be seen with mammography (e.g. 7 mm).
Ideally, the difference in detected tumor size (7 mm vs. 2.5 cm) will be the difference
whether or not the patient can be cured.
Regular self-examination of the breasts is a useful personal screening method, not
least as it improves body awareness. However, studies have not been able to show
that this measure contributes to a reduction in breast cancer mortality. Conversely, it
leads to increased diagnostic activity and more frequent diagnosis of benign
diseases versus patients who do not carry out self-examination. [P07]

A “screening” mammography is a population-based routine examination for
asymptomatic women: Ideally, screening is provided in a highly formalized national
(in Switzerland cantonal) program. If it is indicated due to symptoms or changes
affecting the breast (e.g. pain, new palpation �ndings, skin retraction, etc.), this is a
diagnostic mammography. Major randomized studies have shown that screening
mammography reduces breast cancer mortality in women aged between 50 and 70
years by 25 to 31 % [B18]. However, it is conceivable that the increasingly successful
multimodal treatment of breast cancer will relativize the e�cacy of screening
mammography in the future. [B18]
Figure 3: Bilateral mammography (Images provided by Dr S. Dubach, Zurich Breast Center).
A) Mediolateral view, low gland parenchyma density, ACR b
B) Craniocaudal view
Case: 50-year-old postmenopausal patient with: invasive ductal carcinoma on left at 11-o’clock position cT1c
(18 mm) cN0 cM0, G2 ER/PR 100%, Her2 negative, Ki-67: 10%
If the mammography shows high gland tissue density (ACR c-d), it is not always
possible to identify small tumors reliably. In this case, an additional ultrasound
examination is recommended.

Figure 4: Ultrasound (Images provided by Dr S. Dubach, Zurich Breast Center).
A) Presentation of a tumor measuring 15x13 mm. Case: 46-year-old premenopausal patient with: invasive
ductal carcinoma on left cT1c (15 mm) cN0 cM0, G2 ER 100%, PR 80%, Her2 negative, Ki-67: 15%
B) Presentation of a tumor measuring 4x5 mm.
Case: 62-year-old patient with: invasive ductal carcinoma on right at 2-o’clock position cT1a (5 mm) cN0 cM0,
G1 ER/PR 100%, Her2 negative, Ki-67: 5%
Breast MRI (magnetic resonance mammography) has higher sensitivity than
conventional mammography, but lower speci�city [P138]. There is not yet any
evidence of a positive effect on overall mortality in the general population. For cost
reasons, it is therefore only recommended in women with a very high risk of breast
cancer (e.g. carriers of BRCA1/2 mutations) or in addition to mammography in
certain cases as a screening method. [P13]
Figure 5: Bilateral breast MRI (Image provided by Dr S. Dubach, Zurich Breast Center). Case: 71-year-old
patient with: low-grade DCIS on right at 9-o’clock position pTis (70 mm) cN0 cM0, R0, ER/PR 100%
Clari�cation: MRI-guided vacuum biopsy (right breast at 9-o’clock position, central and peripheral). Treatment:
Segmentectomy on right.

In Switzerland, the obligation of health insurance providers with regard to screening
mammography is linked to a cantonal program with precisely de�ned quality
requirements. While mammography screening is already in use in a large part of
Europe, this does not apply to all Swiss cantons. The “longest history” of
mammography screening is found in the French-speaking cantons: the �rst
screening program was established in the Cantons of Vaud, Geneva and Valais in
1999; the �rst German-speaking cantons (Thurgau, St. Gallen and Graubünden) only
began their program in 2010/2011.
Cantonal mammography screening program in Switzerland
The introduction of an o�cial mammography screening program clearly increases
the willingness of women to undergo a mammography. An evaluation of the 2012
Swiss Health Survey with regards to participation in mammography examinations, 78
% of women aged 50 to 69 years in French-speaking cantons said that they had
undergone a mammography in the last two years; in German-speaking cantons this
rate was only 35 %. [P04]
In the cantons without a screening program, health insurance providers only cover
the examination if there is a medical referral on the basis of a �nding. For women
aged under 50, the cost/bene�t ratio and risk/bene�t ratio of screening
mammography are lower. For this reason, Swiss health insurance providers are not
obligated to cover the cost of the examination for this age group, unlike in other
countries. Health insurance companies cover at most the cost of one mammography
per year, if there is a family history of breast cancer concerning the patient’s mother,
sister or daughter.
In recent years, there has been much critical discussion of mammography screening.
In addition to high cost, the following potential disadvantages are also highlighted:
• False-positive results: Suspect �ndings that lead to further investigation, but no
cancer is found.
• False-negative mammography: Cancer that is not identi�ed despite
mammography.
• Overdiagnosis: Identi�cation of cancer that has no clinical relevance with regard
to the patient’s lifetime.
• Lead-time-bias: Identi�cation of the cancer leads to earlier diagnosis but no
extension of overall survival.
• Radiation exposure: As mammography is an x-ray-based examination, it must

be assumed that the ionizing radiation itself could contribute to the induction of
breast cancer. Although this theoretical risk can be calculated, it has not been
directly proven to date. The risk for women with a known BRCA mutation may
be somewhat higher.
• Pain caused by mammography: For some women, the compression of the
mammary gland leads to very unpleasant pain, often with negative effects on
compliance. Many women assume that compression is damaging, although
this has never been proven. (Pressing the mammary gland between two plates
distributes the gland volumes better on a surface, meaning that the diameter is
lower. This leads to better image quality and lower radiation exposure).
• Many of these considerations are due to problems that are inherent to the
system, and in principle apply to all diagnostic aspects of modern medicine.

topic: Breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors (Teil 1/19)
keywords: breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors
content:
EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Senology 〉 Breast cancer: de�nition, epidemiology & risk factors
Last update: June 2024
Breast cancer:: ddee��nition,, epidemiology,,
risk factors
Author:: Prof.. DDrr.. med.. Mathias Fehr
Chefarzt Gynäkologie & Geburtshilfe, Ärztliche Leitung Brustzentrum Thurgau, Kantonsspital Frauenfeld,
Spital Thurgau AG
Authors of former script versions: Prof. Dr. med. Rolf A. Steiner, Prof. Dr. med.
Rolf Kreienberg

Introduction
Breast cancer has a high incidence, numerous therapeutic options exist, and patients
have high expectations of interdisciplinary medical teams.
Breast cancer is not a uniform disease!
• Certain breast cancers should be understood as a “systemic disease” at the
time of detection due to their tendency for metastatic dissemination.
• Other breast cancers – particularly hormone-sensitive carcinomas – remain a
local/regional disease for a long time. These cancers can be successfully
treated surgically, even in the presence of lymph node involvement.
Stem cell theory has received increasing attention in the last few years. The theory
proposes that - at �rst - one stem cell of the mammary gland epithelium mutates and
proliferates, however, it can differentiate in several pathways to a certain degree. One
pathway is to give rise to a hormone-sensitive, highly differentiated carcinoma. In the
course of the disease, the metastasized stem cell of this carcinoma can, however,
form a poorly differentiated carcinoma without hormone receptors. This
metastasized stem cell displays a different morphology than the original breast
carcinoma at the origin of the metastasis. [P10]

topic: Breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors (Teil 2/19)
keywords: breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors
content:
DDee��nition
Healthy breasts consist of fatty tissue, glandular tissue, the surrounding skin and the
nipple-areola complex.
WHO histological classi��cation:: aann overview
Noninvasive breast cancer
• Ductal carcinoma in situ = DCIS
• Lobular neoplasia = LN (previously: LCIS)
• Paget's disease of the nipple (= adenocarcinoma cells “in situ” in the epithelium
of the skin)
Invasive breast cancer
• Invasive ductal cancer
• Invasive lobular cancer
• In�ammatory breast cancer
• Speci�c types of cancer (tubular, papillary, cribriform, mucinous, medullary, etc.)
• Invasive Paget's disease (Paget cancer) of the nipple
The 2012 WHO classi�cation provides a histological grid consisting of 18 different
histological types, yet these have weak correlations with the biological and clinical
characteristics of tumors. [B01]
In contrast to this “traditional” (histological) classi�cation, the molecular biology
classi�cation, which is more meaningful for tumor biology, has been established.
This classi�cation was developed based on gene expression pro�les and
distinguishes luminal and basal types of breast carcinoma (table 1).
The breast cancer entities that can be distinguished with the help of gene expression
pro�les correspond relatively well to certain immunohistochemistry patterns.
Because breast cancer gene expression pro�les are not routinely determined,
everyday clinical practice uses immunohistochemical determination of the estrogen
receptor, progesterone receptor, HER2 receptor (or immuno�uorescence of the HER2
gene), and the proliferation fraction (Ki 67 or MIBB1).
Table 1 (EGONEplus) [P06]

expression type receptor receptor EGFR MIBB11

The basal type is more common in women of African origin and has the following
characteristics: it is always estrogen receptor negative, poorly differentiated, HER2-
negative.
The triple-negative phenotype is frequent in patients with a BRCA1 mutation.
For this reason, the occurrence of triple-negative breast cancer before age 60 is an
accepted indication for BRCA testing in Switzerland (mutation probability: > 10 %).
In accordance with European guidelines (ESMO), testing should be performed for all
women < 50 years with a triple-negative carcinoma.
Non--Invasive Breast Carcinomas:: Two Main Types
Ductual carcinoma iinn situ == DCIS:: ""Intraductal Carcinoma""
• Cells are degenerated inside the lining of the milk ducts.
• Usually occurs after the age of 40.
• More common than lobular neoplasia (LN). It is more frequently diagnosed due
to improved breast imaging (breast MRI, improved mammography techniques,
and mammography screening). Usually identi�able with mammography due to
microcalci�cations.
• Heterogeneous histology (subtypes). Heterogeneous differentiation (high grade
vs. non high grade).
• Local precancer of invasive ductal breast carcinoma! Without treatment, 30-40%
of DCIS cases become invasive carcinoma.
• Must be treated locally! Therapeutic strategy: Excision with surrounding healthy

topic: Breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors (Teil 4/19)
keywords: breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors
content:
tissue (= excision of the affected milk duct, or – if extensive – mastectomy);
post-operative radiation therapy for the remaining mammary gland in the
presence of increased risk of relapse.
Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) is a precancer!
Lobular neoplasia == LLNN ((previously:: Lobular carcinoma iinn situ == LCIS))
• Cells are degenerated inside the lining of the gland lobules.
• Usually occurs in premenopausal (younger) women.
• Often an incidental �nding in excised mammary gland tissue without correlating
imaging.
• Rarer than DCIS. It is being discovered more often due to more frequent breast
interventions.
• There are various histological subtypes.
• It is seldom detected by palpation nor by mammography.
• NOT a precancer by de�nition but considered a risk indicator given that in future
decades LN carriers may develop invasive ductal (rarer: invasive lobular)
carcinoma at the same location, in another quadrant, or in the contralateral
breast. This risk is estimated to be 1 % per year.
• After a diagnosis of LN, both breasts should be routinely checked with imaging
at short intervals (e.g., annually).
• Chemoprevention with tamoxifen (Nolvadex®, generic drugs) should be
considered. A (bilateral) prophylactic mastectomy is not justi�ed.
Lobular neoplasia (LN) is not precancerous! Internationally, the term LN has
replaced the formerly used terms lobular carcinoma in situ, LCIS and CLIS.
On the other hand, its two variants, �orid LCIS (FLCIS) and pleomorphic LCIS (PLCIS),
present different morphological and genetic characteristics and they present a
higher probability to be possible obligatory precursors of invasive carcinoma. A
signi�cant difference with classical LCIS (CLCIS) is the distribution at the breast
level, since, if CLCIS tends to present in a multifocal manner, the two variants FLCIS
and PLCIS almost always have a unifocal pattern. From a genetic point of view, the
two variants differ from CLCIS for a greater genetic instability, as a higher rate of
alteration of genes coding for tumor suppressors and proteins involved in the
regulation of cell growth and replication is documented. Although they express ER
and PR they also are HER2 positive more often than CLCIS.

topic: Breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors (Teil 5/19)
keywords: breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors
content:
Paget''ss Disease ooff the Nipple
• Adenocarcinoma cells occur “locally” in the nipple epidermis, most often due to
a cancer in the deeper layers of breast tissue but not necessariily.
• Rare. However, the clinical aspect (--> “nipple eczema”) must be known!
Invasive Breast Cancer –– many types
Invasive ductal cancer not otherwise speci��eedd ((NOS))
• 65 to 80 % of all invasive breast carcinoma
• Tends to be unifocal; additionally, often also peritumoral DCIS
• Easily detectable on mammography
• Originates in the lactiferous ducts: “milk duct cancer”
Invasive Lobular Cancer
• Only 5 to 15 % of all invasive breast carcinomas
• Frequently multifocal, multicentric, bilateral
• Poorly visible on mammogram and ultrasound
• Originates in the glandular acini at the end branches of the milk duct system in
the lobules
IInn��ammatory Breast Carcinoma
• An own entity according to the WHO. This diagnosis is made clinically.
• Poor prognosis if chemotherapy achieves no complete remission.
Invasive Paget''ss Disease ((Paget Carcinoma)) ooff the Nipple
• Rare; however, the clinical aspect (“nipple eczema”) must be known!
• Adenocarcinoma cells in the nipple epidermis, with a subjacent breast cancer or
a ductal carcinoma in situ
Particular histologies
• Tubular carcinoma: ER/PR-positive and well differentiated. Best prognosis of all
breast carcinomas
• Invasive cribriform carcinoma: Usually ER/PR-positive, favorable prognosis
• Mucinous carcinoma: Usually receptor-positive and well differentiated
• Medullary carcinoma: Axillary metastases are rare despite lack of hormone
receptors (triple negative); frequent in women with BRCA1 gene mutations,
good prognosis because of numerous tumor-in�ltrating lymphocytes
• Papillary, adenoid cystic, secretory, and apocrine carcinomas
Therapy based classi��cation

topic: Breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors (Teil 6/19)
keywords: breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors
content:
In practice, invasive ductal and lobular breast cancers are classi�ed based on their
therapeutic response (estrogen receptor ER, progesterone receptor PR, and HER2
receptor) into the following categories (�gure 1):
• Hormone-sensitive breast cancer (ER positive, incidence: 65 to 75 %)
• HER2-positive breast cancer (can be either ER positive or negative, incidence:
15 to 20 %)
• Triple-negative breast cancer (ER neg, PR neg, HER2 neg, incidence: 15 %)
Hormone sensitive breast cancer:
• Recurrence is rarer than for triple-negative or HER2-positive breast carcinomas.
• However, recurrences occur with the same frequency even 5 to 10 years after
diagnosis.
HER2- positive breast cancer:
• Overexpression of the HER2 gene causing overexpression of the HER2 receptor
on the cell surface.
• These tumors have a higher metastatic potential than HER2-negative tumors,
and they often metastasize to the brain.
• Nevertheless, monoclonal antibodies against HER2 (trastuzumab, Herceptin®
and pertuzumab, Perjeta®) offer an immunological therapeutic approach that
signi�cantly improves the prognosis, whereby the prognosis equals that of
HER2-negative tumors.
Triple negative breast cancer:
• Their gene expression pro�le often corresponds to the basal cell type, which is
not differentiated and has a high risk of metastasis.
• They metastasize to the brain and lungs more frequently than hormone-
sensitive carcinomas, but less often to the bones and liver.
• Recurrences usually occur within the �rst 3 to 5 years after diagnosis, very
rarely after 5 years.
• Caution: Medullary or apocrine breast carcinomas are usually triple negative,
but their prognosis is signi�cantly better than that of triple-negative invasive
ductal or lobular carcinomas.

topic: Breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors (Teil 7/19)
keywords: breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors
content:
Figure 1: Classi�cation of breast cancer as hormone-sensitive (estrogen receptor positive: ER+), HER2
positive (can be either ER positive or negative), and basaloid carcinomas.
Figure 2: Quadrant localization and lymphatic drainage channels of breast cancer.
Breast cancer quadrant localization (4+1; Figure 2):
• Most breast cancers (50 %) are located in the upper outer quadrant of the
breast, since this quadrant contains the largest proportion of gland
parenchyma.
• Central carcinomas (e.g., breast cancers located behind the nipple) are the
second most common location: 20 %
• Upper inner quadrant: 15 %
• Lower outer quadrant: 10 %
• Lower inner quadrant: 5 %
Lymphogenic metastasis

topic: Breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors (Teil 8/19)
keywords: breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors
content:
The axillary lymph node cluster is affected �rst, and most frequently. There are three
anatomic groups or levels of axillary lymph nodes (according to Berg): Level I to
Level III (�gure 2).
Topographical orientation: Level II is located posterior to the pectoralis minor
muscle; Level I is lateral, and Level III is medial, up to the clavicula.
Large lymph channels lead from the breast to the Level I lymph nodes, others lead to
the retrosternal lymph node chain (“internal mammary chain”), and rarely directly to
the Level II (or III) axillary lymph nodes or infraclavicularly.
Moreover, cutaneous lymph channels lead to the skin areas of the contralateral
breast. The lymphogenic direction of metastasis tends to depend on the quadrant
within which the respective breast cancer is located, although it is not necessarily
correlated with it!
Breast carcinoma with multiple tumor sites
Multifocal breast cancer
• Not rare
• More than one focus of cancer is present in the breast and all foci are in
immediate vicinity and in the same quadrant
• Distance between the foci must be < 4 cm
Multicentric breast cancer
• Rare
• More than one focus of cancer is present in the breast, and they are in different
quadrants or at least 4 cm apart
Primary bilateral breast cancer (= synchronous bilateral breast carcinomas) only
occurs in approximately 1 % of all cases.
If a cancer is diagnosed in the contralateral breast after diagnosing a breast cancer,
it is referred to as a metachronous bilateral breast carcinoma. It is also called a
contralateral recurrence, even though it is not the same histological tumor variant,
and it is typically a secondary carcinoma.
Epidemiology
Breast cancer:

topic: Breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors (Teil 9/19)
keywords: breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors
content:
• The incidence is 151/100,000 women per year in Switzerland (National cancer
incidence statistics 2023), the age standardized rate is 111,5 per 100’000
person-years.
• Incidence in Germany 122/100’000 --> most frequent type of cancer in women,
corresponding to 1/3 of all malignancies in women (incidence in men: see
below).
• Approx. 2/3 of patients are aged 60 years or older at diagnosis. The average
age of onset is 64 years.
• The risk of disease by age decade is (rounded): 30-39 years: 1:270, 40-49 years:
1:70, 50-59 years: 1:40, 60-69 years: almost 1:25 (�gure 3).
• 6485 new cases in Switzerland per year and approximately 1’377 deaths per
year.
• The lifetime risk of breast cancer is 12,7% (13 in 100, i.e. one woman in eight);
the risk of death from breast cancer is 3,6% for women.
• 70’000 new cases and 17’748 deaths per year in Germany --> most frequent
cause of death related to cancer in women.
• In the U.S., breast carcinoma is the second most frequent cause of death
related to cancer after lung carcinoma.

Figure 3: Breast cancer by age, 2016 to 2020 (age-speci�c rate, per 100’000 population).
One in eight women will suffer breast cancer in her lifetime. One third of all breast
cancers occur before the age of 60.
The previously observed North-South disparity in Europe, with a higher frequency and
probability of disease in northern European countries, is no longer observed. For
example, the incidence in Italy is higher than in Switzerland or Norway.
The incidence rate in Switzerland was on the rise until 2002, yet it has remained
constant since (�gure 4). Currently, a slightly decreasing trend in the U.S. has been
observed for the �rst time.

Figure 4: Breast cancer development over time (rate per 100’000 inhabitants, European standard).
Mortality is declining in many Western countries, including Switzerland. This is
probably due to more e�cient prevention (improved early diagnosis) and multimodal
therapies.

topic: Breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors (Teil 10/19)
keywords: breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors
content:
Risk factors
Main risk factors
• Age: The main risk factor (see above)!
• Female sex: Less than 1 % of all breast cancers occur in men (frequently in
“breast cancer families”; 52 cases/year in Switzerland).
• Positive family history: About 5 to 10 % of all breast cancers are hereditary, i.e.,
a hereditary genetic mutation can be traced to the father or mother. If the
BRCA1 or BRCA2 genetic mutation is present, the risk of suffering breast
carcinoma by the age of 80 is 67 % and 66 %, respectively.
• History of breast cancer: 5-times higher risk of having a second de novo breast
cancer.
• History of radiation therapy to the mammary glands at an early age: Women
who received radiation therapy for lymphoma to part of the mammary glands
before the age of 40 have a 5,6-times higher risk of breast cancer. Women who
received radiation for Hodgkin's disease at the age of 25 years have a risk of
breast carcinoma of 25 to 30 % at the age of 55.
• Positive family history: In the case of PALB2 mutation, the lifetime risk of breast
cancer is 45 %, for CDH1 mutation it is 53 %, for CHEK2 mutation, 22 %, and for
Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (STK11 mutation), 50 %.
Hereditary breast cancer
The genealogy of approximately 5 to 10 % of all breast cancer patients shows a
genetic origin of the disease.
A genetic mutation can only be established in 1/3 of familial breast cancer cases
(next-generation sequencing of the 11 breast cancer genes, recommended by the
National Comprehensive Cancer Network Guideline).
2 % of all women carry a genetic mutation that promotes breast cancer.
7,8% of all patients with breast carcinoma carry a relevant germline mutation in one
of the 11 known genes that promote breast cancer. [P13]

topic: Breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors (Teil 11/19)
keywords: breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors
content:
A mutation of the BCRA-1 gene directly contributes to 3,2 % of all breast carcinomas,
and a BRCA2 mutation (a DNA repair gene) directly contributes to another 3,1 %.
Breast cancer occurs at a younger age in these women and is often triple negative.
For instance, the risk of suffering from breast cancer at the age of 35 to 50 years is
up to 3 % annually for these gene carriers–compared with 0,1 % for the normal
population! The corresponding lifetime risk of breast carcinoma for these gene
carriers is 67 %. Note: These mutation carriers also have an increased risk of ovarian
cancer (45 %).
The BRCA1 gene is located on chromosome 17; the BRCA2 gene on chromosome
13. Both are tumor suppressor genes that contribute to the stability and repair of
DNA. Mutations of these genes are inherited autosomal dominantly. If the second
allele also loses its function, the respective protein can no longer be produced
correctly, leading to DNA repair disruption and malignant degeneration.
If a woman with a BRCA mutation was treated for breast cancer, the risk of
contralateral breast carcinoma increases as well. The cumulative 10-year risk for
non-mutation carriers is 6 %, for BRCA2 mutation carriers, 11 %, and for BRCA1
mutation carriers, 20 %.
Less than 4 % of breast carcinomas are caused by rare genetic syndromes such as:
• CHEK2 mutation (1,6 %)
• PALB2 (1 %)
• ATM mutation (0,7 %, ataxia telangiectasia)
• TP53 mutation (0,3 %)
• PTEN gene mutation (0,06 %, Cowden syndrome)
• CDH1 (0,14 %, hereditary diffuse gastric cancer syndrome)
• STK11 (0,03 %, Peutz-Jeghers syndrome)
[P13]

topic: Breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors (Teil 12/19)
keywords: breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors
content:
There are various models for determining the risk of breast cancer based on
personal history without genetic testing. None of the methods can integrate all
modifying factors concurrently. The Gail model takes into account menarche,
menopause, age at �rst delivery, and number of relatives with breast carcinoma, but
not relatives with ovarian carcinoma. As a result, possible heredity tends to be
underestimated. The Claus-Model can only include two affected relatives in the risk
calculation and does not consider ovarian carcinoma either [P11, P12].
For an estimate of familial risk and indication for genetic counseling, see Guidelines
for the management of women at increased familial risk of breast cancer [B02]. [B02]
[P11][P12]
Empirical mutation detection rates aass aa function ooff family history
• ≥ 3 breast carcinomas, 2 of them premenopausal: 30,7 %
• ≥ 3 breast carcinomas: 22,4 %
• 2 premenopausal breast carcinomas: 19,3 %
• 2 breast carcinomas, 1 of them premenopausal: 9,2 %
• ≥ 1 breast carcinoma and > 1 ovarian carcinoma: 48,4 %
• ≥ 2 ovarian carcinomas: 45 %
• 1 breast carcinoma ≤ 36 years of age: 10,1 %
• 1 bilateral breast carcinoma, premenopausal: 24,8 %
• 1 breast carcinoma in a male and 1 breast or ovarian carcinoma: 42,1 %
Figure 5 shows the genetic mutations associated with an increased risk of breast
carcinoma.

(AR = absolute risk in %, RR = relative risk compared to the average population. BC = breast cancer, HBOC =
hereditary breast and ovarian carcinoma, HNPCC = hereditary non-polyposis colon carcinoma
* Results from a study published in the journal “Hereditary Cancer in Clinical Practice” showed that the risk of
non-melanoma skin cancer in women who carry BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutations is similar to that of noncarrier
women. However, the risk of melanoma appears to be slightly elevated. In the study, the cumulative risk of
melanoma was 2.5% for BRCA1 carriers and 2,3 % for BRCA2 carriers, compared with 1,5 % for women in the
general population.
If no genetic testing is available, the individual risk may be estimated by family
history (table 2).

Hence, the following reduce risk: multiparity; breastfeeding (RR 0,7).
Empirically, “mammographic density” (�gure 6) is a risk factor. The risk of breast
cancer increases with increasing mammographic density. According to the American
College of Radiology, there are 4 levels of mammographic density. However, this is
not an independent risk factor, but an expression of various factors, such as
cumulative exposure to estrogen of the breast gland.[B03][B07]

topic: Breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors (Teil 15/19)
keywords: breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors
content:
Figure 6: High mammographic density of the breast parenchyma (left) indicates 4 to 6 times higher risk of
developing breast carcinoma than low density (right).
Risk ooff breast cancer and hormone replacement therapy
Analysis of epidemiological studies requires a distinction to be made between
patients currently undergoing and patients who previously underwent hormone
replacement therapy.
• After discontinuation of hormone replacement therapy, the risk of breast cancer
decreases to the risk for non-users within 1 to 4 years.
• Estrogen monotherapy presented a decreased or minimally increased risk of
breast carcinoma (plus 1 % within 20 years).
• In contrast, combined estrogen/progestogen hormone replacement therapy
presented an increased risk of breast cancer after more than 5 years of therapy
(RR 1,24).
The evidence was con�rmed in several studies:

topic: Breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors (Teil 16/19)
keywords: breast cancer definition epidemiology risk factors
content:
• After 5 years of combined estrogen/progestogen hormone replacement
therapy, the incidence of breast carcinoma increases by approximately 8
additional cases in 10'000 women per year compared to women without
hormone replacement therapy.
• Estrogen-only therapy does not show any increased risk until 7 years of
treatment. Then, observational studies show a slightly increased risk.
• Women who developed breast cancer during hormone replacement therapy
have a lower mortality than women with breast carcinoma and without previous
hormone replacement therapy.
• Combined hormone replacement therapy leads to increased mammographic
breast density (as a surrogate for the risk of breast carcinoma) in approximately
50 % of treated women. Estrogen mono-substitution has a lower effect. This
effect on mammographic breast density is dose-dependent and is no more than
5 to 10 %.
[P02][P03][P05]
For breast cancer survivors, hot �ashes are a signi�cant problem which affects
quality of life. Since tibolone (Livial®) is supposed to stimulate glandular breast
tissue less than estrogen substitution, tibolone was tested in a randomized study in
patients with a previous breast carcinoma. Unfortunately, the women treated with
tibolone had a signi�cantly higher risk of recurrence (RR 1,45), so this synthetic
estrogen is also not recommended. [P01]
Other,, minor risk Factors
• Obesity (only for postmenopausal breast carcinoma): BMI > 30: relative risk
(RR) 2
• Consumption of alcohol (linear risk increase): > 3 units per day: RR 1,4
• Height (taller women have a higher risk): low risk: approx. RR 1,2
• Long-term night shift work: RR 1,5
• Lack of physical activity: Physical activity lowers the risk of premenopausal and
postmenopausal breast carcinoma. However, the difference for premenopausal
women in the incidence of breast cancer was only 6% when the quartile of the
highest physical activity was compared to the quartile with the lowest. For
HER2 positive breast cancer the risk reduction was higher, namely 43 %
• Smoking: Highest risk for smoking before the �rst delivery: RR 1,3
[B05][P04][P09]

EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Senology 〉 Breast cancer: symptoms, prognosis & diagnosis
Last update: august 2022
Breast cancer:: symptoms,, diagnosis &&
prognosis
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr. med. Rolf Kreienberg, Prof. Dr.
med. Rolf A. Steiner, Prof. Dr. med. Mathias Fehr, Prof.
Dr. med. Walter Jonat, Dr méd. Jean-François Delaloye
Author:: DDrr.. med.. Nadia Maggi
Oberärztin Brustchirurgie, Universitätsspital Basel

Symptoms
Cardinal symptom
The cardinal symptom is a palpable, rough, painless node. With small breasts and
large tumors, there may be a change in breast size or asymmetry with the opposite
side. Tumors close to the skin can cause skin or nipple retraction - and occasionally
bulging of the breast contour.
Locally advanced stages can present skin edema (with noticeably large pores - peau
d’orange) or ulcerations. Occasionally, there may also be nodules in the skin due to
cutaneous tumor spreading. These may spread over the whole chest wall to the
sternum, clavicle and past the axilla to the shoulder blade (Carcinoma en cuirasse).
IInn��ammatory carcinoma ((22 %%))
The diagnosis of in�ammatory carcinoma is based on the clinical picture of
pronounced skin edema with swelling, reddening and overheating of the breast skin,
as found with mastitis. In 80 % of cases, histopathology con�rms a lymphatic
invasion of the skin (lymphatic system invasion).
Axillary lymph nodes
The axillary and/or supraclavicular lymph nodes may be easily palpable, even in early
stages, and cancerous (differential diagnosis: reactive lymph node changes,
particularly after previous interventional measures (�ne needle puncture, punch
biopsy) or vaccinations). Any abnormal lymph nodes must be biopsied before
surgery (histological investigation). Axillary lymph node involvement can also
manifest as lymphedema of the arms.
Changes ttoo the nipple
Bloody secretion from the nipple
This may be from papilloma or an early intraductal cancer. The cause of the
secretion must always be investigated.
Eczema--like changes ttoo the skin ooff the nipple
Paget’s disease should be considered in cases of itching, burning or eczema-like
�aking changes to the skin. A biopsy should be carried out to investigate.
Indications ooff distant metastasis

"Banal" skeletal pain in association with breast tumors may be caused by early bone
metastasis and must be investigated as part of staging. Depending on the location
of distant metastases, various clinical symptoms may occur:
• Skeleton: Skeletal or back pain, pathological fractures of the spine, pelvis or
long bones, (rarely hypercalcemia)
• Liver: Jaundice, signs of liver failure
• Lungs: Dyspnea (due to malignant pleural effusion), cough
• CNS: neurological failures

Diagnosis
Diagnosing breast tumors requires an interdisciplinary approach.
Basic examination
The necessary basic examination includes:
• Careful clinical history-taking, which is essential for the interpretation of
physical examination �ndings. It also provides important information for the
individual risk evaluation (personal history, gynecological history, family history,
hormone/drug use, evaluation of risk factors etc.)
• Clinical breast examination (bilateral): medical inspection, palpation of breast
and pathways
• Mammography
• Additional diagnostics if applicable (ultrasound, MRI)
If the clinical breast examination reveals abnormal �ndings, diagnostic imaging
should be carried out. For women under 40, ultrasound is the �rst-line imaging
method. Depending on �ndings and breast density, additional mammography and/or
breast MRI may be required.
For women over 40, mammography and ultrasound are the �rst-line imaging
methods (MRI may be used in some rare cases, see below “Imaging”).
The effects of endogenous and exogenous hormones must be taken into account
when carrying out and analyzing any diagnostic methods (pre menopause: not
before or during menstruation (optimal timing is days 8 to 14 of cycle, particularly
relevant to MRI). Post menopause: if necessary after interruption of hormone
replacement therapy).
Imaging
Mammography

Mammography is currently the only method for identifying precancerous lesions
(e.g. microcalci�cations as in ductal carcinoma in situ). It is also recognized as an
effective method for early tumor stages. Even lesions measuring < 3 mm can be
seen.
Implementation: Craniocaudal and mediolateral views as standard, if necessary
enlarged images or 3D mammography (tomosynthesis).
Evaluation: Description of �ndings (asymmetrical, suspicious densi�cation and/or
grouped malignant microcalci�cations), homogeneity and density of breast
parenchyma (ACR category), overall assessment (BI-RADS category). High density
on mammography (ACR c and d) is, in addition to BRCA1/2 mutation, the highest risk
factor. In these cases, the limited sensitivity of mammography should be raised with
an additional ultrasound (combination of both methods).
Reference for standardized classi��cation ooff breast imaging

ACR b 25–50 % glandular tissue
ACR c 50–75 % glandular tissue
ACR d > 75 % glandular tissue
