Management ooff Vulvar Cancer
Treatment algorithm

Since vulvar cancer affects predominantly elderly women with comorbidities and
advanced age, treatment management may prove to be challenging. Current
guidelines for the management of vulvar cancer are based on retrospective or
comparative studies and, because of its rarity, on data from cervical cancer. The
management of vulvar cancer should be individualized and provided by a
multidisciplinary team in a cancer center experienced in the management of vulvar
cancer. This care should include sexual medicine and psycho-oncology to provide
advice on the potential post-treatment vulvar and vaginal dysfunction.
Early stage ((FIGO:: IIAA and TNM:: TT11aa,, NN00,, MM00))
Radical local excision is recommended with the aim of achieving tumor-free
pathological margins. A minimum pathological margin of > 2 to 3 mm appears to be
su�cient, but the optimal margin remains to be determined [19]. In addition, patients
with early stage disease (depth of invasion ≤ 1 mm) do not require
lymphadenectomy [19]. Depending on the extent of the disease, radical wide local
excision, partial or complete radical vulvectomy or even pelvic exenteration may be
required. If skin grafting is needed, it should be done by a multidisciplinary team,
potentially with a plastic/reconstructive surgeon.
Adjuvant radiotherapy should be given when 1. invasive disease extends to the
pathological margins of the primary tumor and further surgical excision is not
possible, and 2. if close but clear pathological margins are present. In this situation
radiotherapy may be considered to reduce the incidence of local recurrence. There is
currently no consensus on a pathological margin distance threshold below which
adjuvant radiotherapy should be recommended [19]. Adjuvant radiotherapy should be
started within 6 weeks of surgery, if possible.
Radiotherapy is mainly used in the adjuvant setting for early stage vulvar cancer.
However, individual patients who are unable to tolerate surgery may be treated with
primary radiotherapy with or without chemotherapy [17, 19]. In regards to
chemotherapy no data area available and the most commonly used substances are
cis- or carboplatin or Ifosphamide. [17][19]
Locally advanced stage ((FIGO:: IIBB--IVA and TNM:: TT11bb,, NN00,, MM00 -- TT33,, any NN,, MM00))

Treatment of advanced vulvar cancer often involves several treatment modalities.
Primary chemoradiotherapy should be considered in advanced stages to avoid
exenterative surgery [19]. Radiosensitising chemotherapy, preferably with weekly
cisplatin, is recommended. In addition, individual women who are un�t for surgery
may be treated with primary radiotherapy with or without chemotherapy.
Surgery remains the main treatment for locally advanced vulvar cancer. Radical local
excision with inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy is recommended [17, 18, 19, 54).
Sentinel lymph node sampling is recommended in patients with stage < pT1a and
unifocal cancers < 4 cm, without obvious lymph node spread on clinical examination
and imaging. A preoperative lymphoscintigram using a radiotracer is recommended
to allow preoperative identi�cation, location and number of sentinel lymph nodes.
The radioactive tracer is used to identify the sentinel lymph node, with optional use
of blue dye or indocyanine green during the operation the day after [19].
Intraoperative assessment should be performed to avoid a second surgical
procedure. There is a risk of missing micrometastases (≤ 2 mm), but inguinofemoral
lymphadenectomy can be safely omitted in favor of radiotherapy for micrometastatic
disease [19]. An inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy of the affected area should be
performed if metastatic disease (> 2 mm) is detected in the sentinel lymph node or if
no sentinel lymph node is found [17, 19]. Bilateral sentinel lymph node sampling is
required for tumors involving the midline (and distance to the midline <= 1 cm), if
unilateral metastasis is detected, the incidence of contralateral metastasis is low
and further treatment can be limited to the affected groin [19].
Inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy is recommended for tumors ≥ 4 cm and/or
multifocal invasive disease. If the tumor is > 1 cm from the midline, only ipsilateral
inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy is recommended. In addition, contralateral
inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy should be performed if the ipsilateral nodes show
metastatic disease. Re-excision is the treatment of choice if the cancer extends to
the pathological margins of the primary tumor [17, 18, 19].
Adjuvant radiotherapy to the vulva should be considered when 1. invasive disease
extends to the pathological excision margins of the primary tumor and further
surgical excision is not possible, and 2. to reduce the incidence of local recurrence in
cases with close but clear pathological margins. In cases with > 1 metastatic lymph
node and/or the presence of extracapsular lymph node involvement, adjuvant
radiotherapy to the inguinofemoral region should be considered [17, 19]. [17][18][19][54]

Figure 3: Operation procedures with direct closure (A) and radical excision with V-Y-Plasty operation
procedure and bilateral lymphadenectomy (B and C) (with kind permission of Prof. V. Heinzelmann).
Figure 4: A – Sentinel procedure with intraoperative injection of patent blue V around the tumor; B –
Detection of the sentinel lymph node with the lymphatic vessel; C – The femoral triangle (formed by the

important anatomical features including the femoral vein (V), artery (A) and nerve (N) (with kind permission
of Prof. V. Heinzelmann).
Metastatic ((FIGO:: IVA and TNM:: Any TT,, any NN,, MM11))

Metastatic vulvar cancer is a palliative condition. There are limited treatment options
and no standard of care exists. Best supportive care should be discussed with the
patient as an alternative to medical treatment. As more than one third of vulvar
cancer cases occur in older women, it is important to carefully assess the eligibility
of these patients for cancer-speci�c treatment, taking into account overall life
expectancy and speci�c goals related to the cancer diagnosis, before initiating a
comprehensive pre-treatment evaluation [17, 19].
First-line platinum-based chemotherapy should be considered, with cisplatin or
carboplatin as the preferred regimen [17, 19]. Based on the data from cervical
cancer, adding paclitaxel and pembrolizumab in cases with PD-L1 expression and
CPS ≥ 1 and/or bevacizumab to platinum-based chemotherapy may be considered in
selected �rst-line patients, although these drugs are not approved for vulvar cancer
[55]. There is no standard treatment after progression to platinum-based �rst-line
chemotherapy. Immune-checkpoint and epidermal growth factor receptor-targeted
inhibitors may be considered as monotherapy. However, there is no speci�c approval
for any of these drugs. Therefore, enrolment of metastatic vulvar cancer patients in
clinical trials is strongly encouraged.
Treatment should be managed by a multidisciplinary team and early referral to a
palliative care specialist is recommended. Palliative care may include medications,
radiotherapy, surgery and psycho-oncological therapies that can lead to improved
outcomes and quality of life [56]. [17][19][55][56]
Local recurrent disease
Restaging by CT (or PET-CT) of the thorax/abdomen/pelvis should be performed.
The recommended treatment is radical local excision if possible, followed by
postoperative radiotherapy in naive patients. Additionally, inguinofemoral
lymphadenectomy should be performed if the depth of invasion is > 1 mm and
previous sentinel lymph node removal only was performed [19]. The indications for
postoperative radiotherapy are comparable to those for the treatment of primary
disease. If surgical treatment is not possible, chemoradiotherapy should be used [17,
19]. [17][19]
Follow--uupp

Local recurrences are most common in the �rst 2 years after treatment, and because
therapy is highly dependent on further excision or radiotherapy, detection of
recurrence as early as possible is bene�cial. Therefore, after primary surgical
treatment, the ESGO vulvar cancer guidelines [19] recommend follow-up every 3 to 4
months for the �rst 2 years after the initial postoperative follow-up of 6 to 8 weeks,
then every 6 months for the third and fourth years, and from then on long-term
follow-up, which should include clinical examination of the vulva and groin with
biopsy if suspected, and review of symptoms [57]. Follow-up after de�nitive
(chemo)radiotherapy is the same, with the exception of a �rst follow-up 10 to 12
weeks after completion of de�nitive (chemo)radiotherapy with CT or PET-CT to
document complete remission. Close follow-up is also required to check for injury or
tightness and scarring of vulvar and vaginal tissues. Some women may be advised
to use vaginal dilators as a prophylactic measure. [19][57]
Prognosis
The 5-year survival rate for people with vulvar cancer is 67,1 to 74 % [2, 3, 5]. The
survival rate is strongly related to the stage of the disease at diagnosis and the type
of vulvar cancer. Prognosis is related to the pathologic status of the inguinal lymph
nodes and whether the disease has spread to adjacent structures and less to the
size of the primary tumor [58]. The 5-year survival rate for vulvar cancer that has not
spread beyond the vulva is almost 87 % [2]. The 5-year survival decreases to less
than 50 % if it has already spread to surrounding tissues or organs and/or to the
regional lymph nodes [2, 59]. Additionally, the presence of TP53 mutation is
associated with poor survival and short disease-free intervals [30, 43]. [02][03][05][30]
[43][58][59]
Future directions

It is important to improve the prognosis of vulvar cancer, especially for patients with
advanced stages of vulvar cancer. At the moment, there is still no evidence to
support screening in an unselected population for vulvar cancer. Furthermore,
primary and recurrent vulvar cancer does not respond well to systemic treatment
and in contrast to other gynecological cancers, targeted therapies based on genetic
and epigenetic alterations remain at an early stage in vulvar cancer. However, the
recent advances in cancer genetics and molecular oncology with high throughput
analyses, may ultimately facilitate a more precise evaluation of the genomic
landscape in vulvar cancers. Though more studies are needed to decipher the
molecular mechanisms of tumor development and identify and test potential targets
for clinical application. Future management of vulvar cancer may be a personalized
therapy management with integration of molecule/pathway-based targeted
therapies, immunotherapy, as well as traditional strategies [60, 61, 62]. [60][61][62]

Vaginal Cancer
Abstract
Vaginal cancer is a rare gynecologic cancer primarily affecting postmenopausal
women. Vaginal cancer is closely related to vulvar cancer in terms of etiology and
histology. Common clinical symptoms are abnormal vaginal bleeding (often after
sexual intercourse) and discharge, a mass or lump in the vagina that can be felt, and
pain during sexual intercourse. The most common subtype is squamous cell
carcinoma accounting for 90 % of invasive vaginal cancers. Treatment of vaginal
cancer depends on histology and staging. Vaginal cancer is staged using the FIGO
staging. The current developed treatment guidelines of vaginal cancer is based on
retrospective or comparative research, and from cervical cancer data. Radiation
therapy consisting of a combination of external beam radiation and brachytherapy is
the treatment of choice in most patients with vaginal cancer, especially in patients
with advanced-stage disease. Surgery might be considered in small stage I and II
tumors. Management should be personalized and carried out by a multidisciplinary
team in a cancer center experienced in the treatment of vaginal cancer.
Epidemiology and risk factors
Vaginal cancer is a rare cancer and accounts for about 1 to 2 % of cancers
diagnosed in the female genital system and less than 1 % of all cancer in women [1,
63, 64]. Vaginal cancer primarily affects elderly women and 75 % are caused by HPV.
The Swiss Cancer report 2021 showed 27 newly diagnosed and 14 deaths of vaginal
cancers per year with a mean age of 72,5 and 77,8 years, respectively [4].
The risk for vaginal cancer increases with age with 37 % in females aged 75 and over
and the highest rate in the 85 to 89 age group [64]. Additionally, HPV is found in most
cases of vaginal cancer and is present in more than 80 % of patients having
precancerous changes in the vagina called vaginal intraepithelial neoplasia (VAIN).
[01][04][20][21][63][64]
Other common risk factors besides HPV include [65, 66, 67]:
1. History of prior anogenital cancer, particularly cervical cancer
2. Previous radiotherapy
3. A mother taking Diethylstilbesterol to prevent miscarriage
4. HIV infection or a weakened immune system
5. Smoking
�. Chronic local irritation, for instance from long-term pessary use

[65][66][67]
Whilst – due to the low incidence – screening makes no sense for vaginal cancer,
the following actions help prevent and detect vaginal cancer early [20, 21, 68, 69]:
1. HPV vaccination
2. Avoiding sexual intercourse with multiple partners and practicing safe sex
3. Cessation of smoking
4. Having regular gynecologic examinations including PAP tests
[20][21][68][69]
Pathology and classi��cation

Vaginal cancer is closely related to vulvar cancer in terms of etiology and histology,
but it occurs inside the vagina and it is strictly de�ned as a cancer found in the
vagina without clinical or histologic evidence of cervical or vulvar cancer [70].
Therefore a careful examination with histological con�rmation is important, because
80 % of cases are metastatic or secondary tumors [71]. Most tumors occur in the
upper third of the vagina, usually involving the posterior wall but may extend to the
surrounding pelvic soft tissue structures, including paravaginal tissue, parametria,
urethra, bladder, and rectum [72]. The lymphatic of the upper vagina drains most
likely into the pelvic lymph nodes, including the obturator, internal iliac, and external
iliac nodes. The lower vagina most commonly drains to the inguinal and femoral
nodes, while the mid-vagina may follow both the pelvic and groin node routes [71].
There are different types of vaginal cancer, with SCC as the most common type,
accounting for about 80 to 90 % of all vaginal cancers, followed by adenocarcinoma
(8 to 10 %), and extremely rarely lymphomas, sarcomas, and melanomas of the
vagina [73, 74]. Vaginal melanoma begins in the melanocytes. Vaginal melanoma
tends to affect the lower or outer portion of the vagina. Vaginal sarcoma begins in
cells in muscle fat and other tissue under the skin, and usually forms deep in the wall
of the vagina, not on its surface. Vaginal adenocarcinoma develops from the
glandular cells in the vulva.
Vaginal SCC starts in the squamous cells of the epithelial lining of the vagina,
develops slowly, and is often caused by HPV associated VAIN [75, 76]. VAIN are
divided into three different grades. VAIN 1 which is also known as a low-grade
squamous intraepithelial lesion (LSIL) and VAIN 2 and VAIN 3 both represent high-
grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (HSIL). The integration of HPV DNA into the
host cell results in abnormal proliferation by promoting oncogenes and disrupting
genes [38, 39]. This results in an overexpression of E6 and E7 which interferes with
the two tumor suppressor genes TP53 and RB1 and causes abnormal cell
proliferation, and eventually vaginal cancer [40, 41]. However, only 2 to 12 % of the
precancerous lesions turn eventually into invasive cancer as the condition is usually
treated and the true malignant potential stays therefore obscure [77, 88]. [38][39][40]
[41][70][71][72][73][74][75][76][77][88]
Diagnosis

Patients with vaginal cancer may be asymptomatic, but common clinical symptoms
are abnormal vaginal bleeding (often after sexual intercourse) and discharge, a mass
or lump in the vagina that can be felt, and pain during sexual intercourse [71, 79]. If
the vaginal cancer has spread beyond the vagina to nearby structures and lymph
nodes the patients can suffer from painful urination, constipation, pain in the pelvis
or low in the belly, swelling in the legs, and present with a lump in the groin due to
lymph node metastases [80]. Any suspicious vaginal lesion should be biopsied to
establish a diagnosis and ensure there is no evidence of tumor on the cervix or the
vulva [71]. If vaginal cancer is con�rmed, the patient should be referred to a
gynecological oncology center and treated by a multidisciplinary team. [71][79][80]
Diagnostic and Staging

Vaginal cancer should be staged using the FIGO staging system and/or TNM
classi�cation (table 2), however, observer differences are common due to the
di�cult examination and possibilities to determine spread [81]. Spread can
particularly occur directly into the surrounding tissues and organs, lymphatic to the
pelvic and paraaortic but also inguinofemoral lymph nodes, depending on the
location of the lesion. Vaginal cancer is primarily clinically staged and includes
several diagnostic tests including a physical examination, colposcopy, tissue biopsy,
and imaging tests.
The physical examination consists of the groin and pelvic area with evaluation of the
vulva, vagina, and anus. This includes a cervical cytology, and colposcopy of the
cervix and vagina with a diagnostic representative biopsy of all lesions, avoiding
removal of the whole lesion. The pathology report on preoperative biopsy should at
least include histological type and depth of invasion. If a de�nitive diagnosis could
not be done, a partial vaginectomy to determine invasion may be necessary [82]. A
clear documentation of the clinical exam has to be done including the proximity of
the tumor to the midline structures (�gure 5) for a higher risk of bilateral spread.
Further tests may be helpful to determine the size, position and spread of the cancer.
These tests can include blood tests with biochemical pro�le, liver pro�le, and HIV
testing, cystoscopy, proctoscopy and imaging tests. Tomographic scans, particularly
MRI, can be used to assess sites of deep in�ltration into the bladder, urethra or
paravaginal tissues [82]. MRI is recommended for diagnosis, local staging, and
assessment of recurrence and complication [83, 84, 85, 86]. The use of PET-CT in
primary vaginal cancer has been found superior compared with other imaging
modalities for detecting nodal disease and distant metastasis [87]. PET-CT, however,
is also useful in detecting recurrences. [81][82][83][84][85][86][87]

Figure 5: Assessment of midline involvement in vulvar cancer.

metastasis; N – lymph node; N0 – no regional lymph node metastasis; N1 – regional lymph node metastasis;
T – tumor; TNM – tumor, node and metastasis)

T3, N1, and/or hydronephrosis. It has spread to nearby lymph

M1
Management ooff Vaginal Cancer
Treatment algorithm
The treatment depends primarily on histology, tumor volume, anatomical localization
of the tumor, stage of the disease, and age of the patient. The currently developed
treatment guidelines of vaginal cancer are based on retrospective or comparative
research, and from cervical cancer data. Radiation therapy consisting of a
combination of external beam radiation and brachytherapy is the treatment of choice
in most patients with vaginal cancer, especially in patients with advanced stage
disease [71]. Surgery and radiotherapy can be applied in early stage tumors.
Management should be personalized and carried out by a multidisciplinary team in a
cancer center experienced in the treatment of vaginal cancer. This care should also
include sexual medicine and psycho-oncology to advise for impairment of a
functional vagina subsequent after treatment. Intercourse or vaginal dilatation
therapy with the adding of topical estrogens, can support in this context. [71]
Surgery

Surgery has a limited role in treating vaginal cancer since the tumor is in close
proximity to the bladder, rectum, and urethra. The extent of the surgery depends on
the size, location and stage of the cancer. In general, primary treatment with surgery
is limited to early stage I or II con�ned to the vaginal mucosa (< 2 cm in diameter)
and limited to the proximal part of the vagina [88]. The type of surgery may include
local excision, partial vaginectomy, radical hysterectomy, and pelvic exenteration
combined with lymph node assessment. According to the literature 25 % of patients
with stage I-II disease treated with surgery required adjuvant radiation therapy due to
positive margin and/or positive lymph nodes [87, 89]. There may be a role in certain
additional situations beyond early disease. [87][88][89]
Radiotherapy
Radiotherapy is the treatment of choice in patients with vaginal cancer, especially in
advanced stages. Radiotherapy consists of a combination of external beam
radiotherapy and intracavitary radiotherapy or brachytherapy. The principal
advantage of radiotherapy is the organ preservation of the vagina and other organs.
External beam radiotherapy
External beam radiotherapy includes the primary disease and regional lymph nodes
to reduce the volume of the primary vaginal tumor, provide regional lymph node
control, and eradicate other microscopic disease [70]. The optimal or lower threshold
dose is 70 Gy, which has been shown to improve outcomes with reduced grade 3 and
4 toxicities [90, 91, 92, 93]. For the planning of the radiotherapy CT, MRI and PET-CT
are often used to help identify disease. The integration of advanced imaging,
including CT, MRI, PET-CT, together with intensity modulated radiation therapy allows
higher doses delivered to the cancer, with fewer adverse effects because dose to the
adjacent structures is limited [94, 95, 96, 97]. [70][90][91][92][93][94][95][96][97]
Brachytherapy
Brachytherapy is used to treat residual disease with an extra dose and sparing
normal tissue by placing a radioactive source directly or close to the tumor.
Brachytherapy alone is not recommended for most tumors, even early-stage, due to a
high recurrence rate [98, 99]. It has been shown that patients with vaginal cancer
treated with brachytherapy had a median overall survival (6,1 years) which was 2
years longer compared to patients who did not receive brachytherapy (3,6 years)
[100]. These �ndings are similar to results of brachytherapy in cervical cancer [101,
102, 103]. [98][99][100][101][102][103]

Chemoradiotherapy
The combination of chemotherapy with radiotherapy has been adopted in treating
vaginal cancer from the data extrapolated in patients with locally advanced cervical
cancer [97, 104]. At the moment no randomized prospective study has been
undertaken due to the rarity of the disease [105, 106]. However, chemoradiotherapy
may be considered in the treatment of vaginal cancer following a large US National
Cancer Database (NCDB) study [107], which showed that chemoradiotherapy was an
independent prognostic factor for better overall survival (56 months for
chemoradiotherapy vs 41 months for radiotherapy). In addition, a retrospective
review showed a potential improvement in overall and disease free survival [108].The
most common regimen used is weekly cisplatin at 40 mg/m2; however, other drugs
and combinations have also shown bene�t [109, 110, 111]. [97][104][105][106][107][108]
[109][110][111]
Follow--uupp
As with most cancers, the follow-up of vaginal cancer is undetermined and based on
custom and practice. Local recurrences are common and most often occur in the
�rst 2 years after treatment with survival correlated with stage of the disease [83,
112]. Therefore, a recognition of recurrence as early as possible is needed. The
follow-up should include clinical examination of the vulva and groins with a biopsy if
suspicious, and review of symptoms. MRI is a useful tool to use to differentiate
recurrence versus complications from radiotherapy and/or surgery [113].
Additionally, PET-CT may be useful in assessing recurrent disease, but MRI is a more
helpful tool in determining tumor in�ltration and volume [114]. The vaginal tissue can
be fragile and prone to injury after treatment, especially after radiotherapy. A close
follow-up is required with the assessment for injury or tightening and scarring. Some
women will be advised to use vaginal dilators as prophylaxis. [83][112][113][114]
Prognosis

The 5-year survival rate for people with vaginal cancer is 49 to 57 % [63, 115]. The
survival rate is strongly related to the stage of the disease at diagnosis and the type
of vaginal cancer. The 5-year survival rate for vaginal cancer that has not spread
beyond the vaginal wall is 69 % [63]. The 5-year survival decreases to 57 % if it has
already spread to surrounding tissues or organs and/or to the regional lymph nodes
and to 26 % if it has spread to a distant part of the body [63]. High-risk HPV DNA and
low MIB-1 index have been found to have a favorable prognostic value [116]. The
MIB-1 index shows the expression of the proliferation-associated Ki-67 antigen. In
general, vaginal adenocarcinomas have a worse prognosis than vaginal SCC [99].
Furthermore, vaginal melanoma has a very poor prognosis, with a �ve-year overall
survival of 15 % [117]. [63][99][115][116][117]
Future directions
It is important to improve the prognosis of vaginal cancer, especially for patients
with advanced stages. At the moment, there is still no evidence to support screening
in an unselected population. However, a signi�cant difference can be made in
decreasing the incidence of vaginal cancer by prevention in the form of improved
dissemination of HPV vaccines. Furthermore, primary and recurrent vaginal cancer
does not respond well to systemic treatment and in contrast to other gynecological
cancers, targeted therapies based on genetic and epigenetic alterations remain at an
early stage. One of the main reasons is that vaginal cancer is so rare and most
studies are small with a short follow-up. Therefore, treatment in multicenter clinical
trials should be considered for any type or stage of vaginal cancer to get the best
treatment available. Additionally, the recent advances in cancer genetics and
molecular oncology with high throughput analyses should be used to facilitate a
more precise evaluation of the genomic landscape in vaginal cancers. For this,
studies are needed to decipher the molecular mechanisms of tumor development
and identify and test potential targets for clinical application and improve the
outcome for patients with vaginal cancer by personalized therapy management with
integration of molecule/pathway-based targeted therapies, immunotherapy, as well
as traditional strategies.

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 1/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Genital organs 〉 Abnormal positioning of the uters - typology
Last update: January 204
Abnormal positioning ooff the uterus --
typology
CCoo--Author((ss)):: PD Dr. med. Daniele
Perucchini, Prof. Dr. med. Urs Haller
Author:: DDrr.. med.. Daniel Eduard Passweg
Leitender Arzt/Stv. Chefärzt*innen, Stadtspital Zürich Triemli

Introduction
The pelvic �oor is required to provide a �rm closure of the base of the pelvis while
enabling the urethra, vagina, and rectum to pass through it and permitting normal
micturition and defecation. With the exception of the vagina, all the woman’s internal
genital organs are covered in visceral peritoneum. Maintenance of a “physiological
position” over time involves various different muscular and connective tissue
structures. During sexual intercourse and also in childbirth, the tissues have to adapt
to mechanical stresses. These stresses applied to the woman’s pelvic �oor can
cause various problems and pathological presentations. The pelvic �oor consists of
various different components, which are all located between the peritoneum and
perineum. The physiological position of the hollow organs is maintained by
connective tissues and muscles linking them to the pelvic bones (cf. pelvic fascia).

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 2/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
Pelvic ��oor
The pelvic �oor is a tough �bromuscular meshwork of tissues forming the caudal
closure of the pelvis. The woman's internal genital organs, the bladder, and other
abdominal organs are all positioned above it.
The levator ani muscle is a key component of the pelvic �oor. The levator ani muscle
is an impressive structure consisting of multiple parts, which are arranged in pairs. It
is made up of four parts on each side:
• pubococcygeus muscle
• puborectalis muscle
• iliococcygeus musle
• coccygeus muscle
The term “pelvic �oor” is generally employed as a synonym for the levator ani.
Physiologically, the female pelvic �oor includes two gaps. These areas of weakness
are necessary for the passage of hollow organs:
• Urogenital hiatus: urethra, vagina
• Rectal hiatus: rectum
At the point where the vagina and the pelvic organs pass through the urogenital
hiatus, they are linked to the levator ani muscles by connective tissue structures;
these support the organs.
The urogenital hiatus is closed by the constant muscle tone of the levator ani
muscle, which can be adjusted as needed. This “closure” is made possible by the U-
shaped structure of the levator ani muscle. When it is contracted, the urethra, vagina
and rectum are all elevated against the posterior wall of the pubic symphysis, and
the pelvic organs located above them are therefore supported. Damage to the levator
ani muscle can lead to weakness and, therefore, relaxation of the pelvic �oor, which
results in a visible sagging of the perineum (“descending perineum syndrome“) with
a gaping introitus on gynecological examination.
The large levator ani muscle is a large structure consisting of multiple parts, which
are arranged in pairs. Various groups of muscle �bers are identi�ed, based on their
origin and their relations to the organs in the midline:
• The iliococcygeus muscle consists of a thin layer of muscles, which stretches

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 3/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
from the bony side-walls of the pelvis on one side to the other side of the pelvis.
It is located relatively laterally and, when in a standing position, it forms a
horizontal sheet. The iliococcygeus muscle originates from the arcus tendineus
levator ani (ATLA), with a few �bers possibly originating from the pubic bone. Its
�bers are inserted onto the side of the coccyx. These partly cross over the
muscles on the other side, with formation of a raphe.
• The parts of the levator ani muscle that are located medially (pubococcygeus
muscle and puborectalis muscle) form a sling-shaped “muscle strap”. This
originates laterally to the symphysis on both sides and joins behind the rectum,
with some �bers also inserted into the perineal body, vagina, and anal sphincter.
Various terms are employed in the literature to clarify the relationship between
the pubococcygeus muscle and the organs in the midline. The connection to
the vagina is called the pubovaginalis muscle, the connection to the perineum is
known as the puboperinealis muscle, and the connection to the anus is referred
to as the puboanalis muscle.
The puborectalis muscle is located more laterally and it is not considered to be one
of the pubococcygeal muscles.
Figure 1: Pelvic �oor from "below".
On the right side of the patient, the urogenital diaphragm is shown with the overlying external pelvic �oor
muscles seen from below (MSAE, m. bulbospongiosus, m. transversus perinei super�cialis, m.
ischiocavernosus) and the levator muscle can only be seen in depth. On the left side of the patient, this layer
has been removed and the m. levator ani can be seen, which is divided into its groups here.

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 4/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
Figure 2: Pelvic �oor from "above".
The m. levator ani (MLA) is shown how it is attached ventrally to the posterior wall of the os pubis on the one
hand and laterally to the arcus tendineus levator ani - a longitudinal fascia compression of the fascia of the
m. obturatorius internus - on the other. The m. obturatorius internus is not shown, although it is fascinating
that the relevant pelvic �oor muscle inserts into another muscle here. The image appears clear. Ventrally, the
MLA opens to the genital hiatus, where the urethra, vagina and rectum can exit the pelvis. Towards the dorsal
side, the MLA unites with its opposite side and forms the levator plate in the middle.

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 5/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
Figure 3: MLA in an oblique view from below, as drawn and categorized by John DeLancey's team: According
to DeLancey, the m. pubovisceralis consists of the m. pubovaginalis (not labeled), the m. puboperinealis, and
the m. puboanalis (which inserts between the MSAE and MSAI). This muscle group lifts the pelvic �oor and
forms the medial part of the MLA. The m. pubococcygeus is the pot-shaped, thin dorsal part of the MLA,
which joins the contralateral side in the middle of the levator trapezius. The m. pubrectalis is also called the
puborectalis sling because it wraps around the rectum from the outside and, like the m. pubovisceralis, pulls
towards the posterior surface of the os pubis. The m. pubrectalis forms the anorectal angle, which is
important for anal continence. The image shows that the MLA can lift and close the pelvic �oor. Here, too,
you can see that the MLA inserts on the posterior surface of the pubic bone on the one hand and on the
arcus tendineus levator ani - and thus on the m. obturatorius internus - on the other. The MLA as a whole is
innervated by the levator nerves (S3/4/5). The m. sphincter ani externus, which is also shown, belongs to the
external pelvic �oor muscles; the urogenital diaphragm (perineal membrane) is not shown.
The obturator internus muscle and piriformis muscle are larger muscles of the pelvis
side-wall.
• The obturator internus is a large, fan-shaped muscle, which originates from the
bony edges of the obturator foramen and on the surface of the obturator
membrane and also on the rami of the ischium and pubic bones. This muscle
forms the lateral wall of the pelvis and can be palpated from the vagina.
• The piriformis muscle forms the posterior wall of the pelvis. Both piriformis
muscles originate anteriorly and laterally to the sacrum in its middle and
superior parts, and leave the pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen to insert

onto the greater trochanter of the femur.

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 6/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
Endopelvic fascia ((EPF))
The term “endopelvic fascia” is used as a collective term for the connective tissue
support and connective tissue bonding materials between the bladder, urethra, and
levator ani muscle. The cervix and vagina are stabilized in relation to the side-walls
of the pelvis by the endopelvic fascia.
The anterior part of the EPF is also known as Halban’s fascia. It supports the bladder
and anchors the vagina to the side walls of the pelvis. Dorsally, it is termed the
Denonvillier’s fascia and runs between the dorsal wall of the vagina and the rectum.
The cardinal and uterosacral ligaments can also be seen as thickenings of the EPF.
The EPF consists of collagen, elastin, smooth muscle, blood vessels, and nerves. Its
composition re�ects the function of the tissue: It surrounds the blood vessels and
nerves supplying the organs, thus having a supportive function.
The endopelvic connective tissues that insert onto the uterus are referred to as the
“parametrium“, while the connective tissues that extend to the vagina are known as
the “paracolpium“.
Clinically, there are two connective tissue structures in the pelvis, of particularly
special relevance. These are the bilateral arcus tendineus levator ani (ATLA) and
arcus tendineus fasciae pelvis (ATFP).

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 7/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
Figure 4: The small basin viewed from the top left. This image also originates conceptually from the
DeLancey group. The image focuses on the endopelvic fascia (pubocervical fascia), which is important for
the anatomical concept of urogynecology. The endopelvic fascia describes the lateral �xation of the vagina
to the MLA. It is not a "real" anatomical and/or histological fascia. The image shows the arcus tendineus
(a.c.) levator ani, the �xation of the MLA to the m. obturatorius internus. A fascial longitudinal thickening in
the MLA is called a.t. fascia pelvis and describes the lateral �xation of the vagina to the MLA. The endopelvic
fascia continues cranially into the parametrial "ligaments", which are referred to as the paracolpium or then
as the parametrium or paracervix, depending on their height.
The ATLA and ATFP are tough thickenings of the obturator internus fascia and
levator ani muscle fascia, mainly consisting of collagen. The ATLA and ATFP have
the function of providing lateral support to the organs of the pelvic �oor.

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 8/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
Anteriorly, the ATLA is inserted on the posterior side of the anterior ramus of the
pubic bone on both sides, while posteriorly it inserts close to the ischial spine. The
ATLA overlies the obturator internus muscle.
The ATFP is located medially to the ATLA and inserts in the lower part of the
symphysis, quite consistently 1 cm lateral to the midline. Posteriorly, the ATLA
inserts onto the ischium immediately above the ischial spine.
DeLancey (1992) divides the supportive connective tissues (paracolpium and
parametrium)of the anterior pelvic compartment into three different levels:
• Level I contains the apical part of the supportive equipment close to the end of
the vagina
• Level II contains the mid-vaginal region
• Level III contains the distal vaginal region
Given this context, it must be pointed out that the organs supporting the pelvic �oor
form a whole, so that this division into different levels is an arti�cial one, essentially
which used for teaching purposes. The system is currently employed by
urogynecologists throughout the world. It has been shown that this concept of
various levels aids a pathophysiological understanding of examination �ndings
relating to descensus, prolapse, and urinary incontinence.

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 9/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
Figure 5: The same situation as Figure 4, except that the �gure also shows the "levels of vaginal support I - III"
according to DeLancey and also cleverly inserts coronal sections of the vagina into the drawing. An
interesting detail is that the vagina has a U-shaped cross-section cranially, because it is stretched by the a.t.
fascia pelvis, while distally the vagina has a more H-shaped cross-section, which is due to the fact that
distally there is also a rectovaginal fascia, which additionally �xes the distal vagina caudally dorsally to the
MLA via an a.t. fascia rectovaginalis. Level I describes the �xation of the cervix and the upper third of the
vagina by the parametria, level II describes the lateral �xation of the vagina by the a.t. fascia pelvis and level
III the deep �xation by medial parts of the MLA, by distal parts of the a.t. fascia pelvis and by the perineal
wedge.

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 10/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
Damage to Level I of the attachments of the paracolpium results in prolapse of the
uterus or vaginal �oor (--> G02E Types of prolapse).
Damage at Level II results in prolapse of the anterior compartment, with cystocele; in
the posterior compartment, it results in rectocele.
These defects can occur in various combinations and forms, and they are
responsible for the diverse conditions of prolapse (not “abnormal positioning” =
anteversion, ante�exion etc.), which are seen clinically. The interaction between the
muscles of the pelvic �oor and the supporting connective tissues is decisive in
determining the normal supporting function of the pelvis. When the levator ani
musculature is intact and functioning properly, the hiatus in the pelvic �oor is closed
and the ligaments and connective tissue structures of the endopelvic fascia are
subjected to only minimal pressures.
The sole function of the endopelvic connective tissue is to hold the organs in
position above the levator ani muscle. If the pelvic �oor muscle is relaxed or
damaged, the urogenital hiatus is widened, with the vagina fully exposed to the
pressure gradients between increases in abdominal pressure and atmospheric
pressure, when subjected to stresses. In these situations, the connective tissue
structures and ligaments can help keep the vagina “in situ”. However, although
ligaments can probably carry out a supporting function for a short time, they are not
as effective as the muscular structures of the pelvic �oor at maintaining continuous
support, and as a result, genital prolapse is able to occur.

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 11/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
Ligaments
In the gynecological and midwifery context, there are several pairs of ligaments that
are relevant. Together these form an extensive supporting structure that works
alongside the pelvic �oor to hold the uterus and adnexae in position. Strictly
speaking, not all of these are clearly demarcated ligaments, but some are only
thickened areas of connective tissue.

* The pelvic connective tissues on both sides are referred to as a whole as the
parametrium. The parametrium comprises the paired cardinal ligaments, the
sacro-uterine ligaments and the vesico-uterine (pubovesical) ligaments, which
radiate from the side, back and front into the pericervical connective tissue ring
and �xate the cervix and the upper third of the vagina (Level I). Cranially, it
passes into the broad ligament (laterally adjacent to the uterus). The free edge of
the broad ligament �nally forms the mesosalpinx.
** Via the inguinal canal, the round ligament leaves the pelvis and extends down
into the labia majora. As a reminder: The inguinal canal is a route of least
resistance for indirect (= lateral) inguinal hernias in women.

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 13/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
Figure 6: Ligaments and connective tissue condensations, which contribute to the suspension of the internal
genitals, are brown. The parametria (cardinal, sacro-uterine and vesico-uterine ligaments) stabilise the cervix
and the upper vaginal third and correspond to level I of vaginal support, according to DeLancey. The broad
ligament describes the intraperitoneal bilateral meso of the uterus (peritoneal duplication) and contains very
loose connective tissue. The round ligaments pull ventrally to the inguinal canal. The ovarian ligaments �x
the ovaries to the uterus. The infundibulopelvic ligaments �x the ovaries to the pelvic wall and correspond to
an elevation of the peritoneum by the underlying ovarian vessels.

Formal nomenclature used iinn abnormal
positioning
Any position of the uterus can be described using a small number of terms:
• Prolapse = descent or sagging
• Retroversion/retro�exion/displacement/torsion = abnormalities of
intraperitoneal position

““Downwards"" with gravity == prolapse
The parts of the internal genitalia that are mentioned are displaced caudally.

Description iinn the sagittal plane
• In terms of the angle between the vaginal axis and the cervical axis:
retroversion
• In terms of the angle between the cervical axis and the axis of the uterine body:
retro�exion
Figure 7: Example of abnormal positioning of the uterus.
A – physiological: anteversion-ante�exion
B – retroverted (and vertical)
C – retro�exed (but normally anteverted)
Vertical uterus refers to the loss of ante�exion but without retro�exion (report: “the
uterus is vertical”).

Description iinn the frontal plane
• A lateral tilt to the uterus: retroversion
• A lateral displacement of the whole organ: displacement
Figure 8: Example of abnormal positioning of the uterus.
A – normal
B – dextroposed
C – sinistroverted

Longitudinal axis
In addition to all the abnormal positions mentioned above, the uterus can also be
twisted in its longitudinal axis, which is called torsion.

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 14/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
Detailed axial topography ooff the vagina,,
cervix,, and body
The normal spatial relations and shape of the vagina and uterus as two connected
hollow organs is as follows:
• “The normal position of the uterus is anteversion - ante�exion."
• “The uterus is anteverted - ante�exed.”
The vagina is oriented obliquely in a dorsocranial direction (towards the sacral
promontory). Tilted forward at an angle to this (anteverted) is the cervix. At the
boundary of the cervix and body, the uterus is bent forward (ante�exed).
Version and �exion are to a certain extent related to the current situation.
Abnormalities are often reversible. The transition to a (�xed) pathological position is
�uid.
Consider the general variability of the anatomy in the woman’s pelvis: The uterus
changes its “elastic topography” in accordance with the �lling of the bladder and
rectum.

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 15/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
Clinical aspects ooff abnormal
intraperitoneal positioning ooff the uterus
In general, all changes like retroversion, retro�exion, or displacement may indicate a
space-occupying lesion or an in�ammatory or post-in�ammatory condition (scar
shrinkage, tumor, myoma, or adhesions). The uterus is pushed out of its normal
position by tumors (benign or malignant), cysts and in�ammatory processes (e. g.,
abscesses).
Displacement
Displacement of the whole uterus is rare and does not often occur. It is noticeable.
Retroversion and retro��exion
Retroversion and retro�exion are routinely found and documented during
gynecological examination with bimanual examination.
• They usually do not cause any symptoms.
• They are generally non-signi�cant incidental �ndings.
Retroversion or retro�exion, when diagnosed incidentally, should only be interpreted
and investigated in the context of additional symptoms or signs.
In particular, abnormalities that are mobile (= non-�xed) often have no pathological
cause. If, on bimanual examination, the uterus is found to be immobile (�xed
retroversion or retro�exion), further investigations must be carried out - to search for
causes, such as in�ammation or endometriosis. Depending on the signs and
symptoms, vaginal ultrasound and laparoscopy may be indicated.
Practice note: retroversion or retro�exion must be identi�ed before the use of
instruments in order to investigate the uterine cavity or prevent perforation
(curettage, hysteroscopy etc.).

topic: Abnormal positioning of the uters typology (Teil 16/17)
keywords: abnormal positioning of the uters typology
content:
Special clinical aspects ooff retro��exion
Retro�exion (of the uterus) is the most commonly found and clinically relevant
abnormal position of the uterus. A distinction is made between mobile and �xed
retro�exion.
Mobile retro��exion
This may be symptomatic. Mobile uterine retro�exion may account for the following
symptoms:
• rare: uncharacteristic groin pain or dyspareunia
• rare: dysmenorrhea
• very rare: bladder irritation (in pronounced retro�exion, the vaginal portion of the
cervix may be tilted so far forward that it presses on the �oor of the bladder)
The following are not usually found:
• Sterility
• Miscarriage
Conception and pregnancy are usually normal in cases of mobile retro�exion. A
retro�exed uterus usually straightens in the course of a pregnancy.
Retro��exion ((and//oorr retroversion)) ��xed retro��exed uterus
In a �xed retro�exed uterus, the whole uterus has grown dorsally into the adjacent
structures. Signi�cant symptoms may be present (but this is not always the case):
• Groin pain, which worsens during premenstrual and perimenstrual periods
(formally: secondary dysmenorrhea)
• Rare: dyspareunia
• Rare: symptoms associated with defecation

EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Genital organs 〉 Anatomical and physiological principles
Last update: 2015/2016
Anatomical and physiological principles
CCoo--
Author:: PPDD DDrr.. med.. Daniele Perucchini
Inhaber/Leiter Blasenzentrum AG, Konsiliararzt Klinik für Gynäkologie, UniversitätsSpital Zürich
Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr. med. Urs Haller
The control of the storing and emptying function of the urinary bladder are complex.
It involves regulatory circuits, receptors, and neurotransmitters of the somatic and
autonomous nervous system. A mixture of inhibiting and stimulating impulses is
formed. The physiological, situation-appropriate balance of these impulses can be
disrupted by pathological processes. The balance can be regenerated by means of
training or medical intervention.

Continence Factors
• “Relaxed” (i.e., adaptable) bladder muscles
• Expandable bladder (no adhesions or �brosis)
• E�cient urethral closure (by autonomous closure re�exes as well as voluntary
contraction)
• Healthy pelvic �oor with intact anatomical support
Only when the bladder muscles, the sphincter apparatus and pelvic �oor react
physiologically, are humans able to:
• Store urine that is continuously produced in the kidneys and
• Precipitate situation-appropriate micturition

Reservoir Function –– Continuous
With increasing bladder �lling, the bladder expands and the bladder muscles adapt,
which keeps the pressure inside the bladder constant. This is noticed at a �lling of
approximately 150 to 200mL! At this point, it depends on the voluntary desire of the
individual whether the bladder is �lled up further or micturition occurs. Normal
bladder capacity varies between approx. 300 to approx. 600mL.

Micturition –– Periodic
Coordinated start impulses ensure that the pelvic �oor relaxes and the urethral
sphincter starts to relax. The detrusor muscle contracts increasingly to push out the
entire bladder content through pressure. Once consciously started, the bladder is
emptied largely re�exively (voluntary interruption is almost impossible). Voluntary
activation of the abdominal prelum can increase urinary �ow.

Urinary Bladder
The main tasks of the bladder are storing urine on the one hand, and voluntarily
emptying the bladder on the other hand. Learning normal bladder function is an
important part of childhood development.
The urinary bladder is a musculomembranous low pressure storage for urine. It is
located in the lesser pelvis posterior to the symphysis and anterior to the uterus and
vagina. Depending on its �lling volume, it occupies a varying portion of the pelvis.
Normal bladder capacity (so-called maximum bladder capacity) is approximately
300-600 ml. Greater “normal bladder �lling” without urinary urgency are probably
trained. The inside of the urinary bladder is lined with a hormone-sensitive
urothelium. The urothelium is not simply a passive membrane, but plays an active
role in controlling bladder function.
Therefore, the urothelium has specialized surface proteins and ion pumps with
proteoglycans and glycoproteins. All these structures combined contribute to
impermeability. The same mechanisms provide protection from bacterial infection.
This part has not been translated into English.
Figure 1
Figure 1
The bladder wall is a �exible mesh consisting of smooth muscle. These are called
“detrusor muscle”.
The inner lining of the bladder is densely supplied with nerve endings. The �rm,
connective tissue trigonum maintains the funnel shape of the bladder neck.

Urethra
The adult female urethra is approx. 3 cm in length. The non-keratinizing squamous
epithelium in the distal part is dependent on estrogens. Cyclical and/or senile trophic
changes in�uence continence. The muscular wall structures and their innervation are
crucial (see below):
• On the inside, smooth muscles run lengthwise and circularly. These are called
the autonomous urethral sphincter (innervation: sympathetic nerve; hypogastric
nerves).
• A (circular) striated layer of muscles called voluntary urethral sphincter
(innervation: pudendal nerves) is located on the outside.
Clinical note: Age-related muscle loss and births (local denervation and direct tissue
damage) reduce the performance of the sphincter structures.

Pelvic Floor ((ccff.. GG0011BB))
The pelvic �oor is a highly complex structure that consists of several components
located between the peritoneum and perineum. The M. levator ani is a crucial part of
the pelvic �oor.
As a striated muscle, it is subject to voluntary and re�exive control. It arises laterally
at the pubic bone and tendinous arch and ends medially in the external sphincter
muscle and is attached to a connective tissue ridge between the rectum and coccyx.
The tendinous arch is a �rm strand of connective tissue running from posterior to the
symphysis to the iliac spine. It serves to suspend the aforementioned M. levator ani
and the endopelvic fascia.
For this reason, in the distal part, near the symphysis, anatomists distinguish a
tendinous arch of the pelvic fascia and a tendinous arch of the levator ani muscle.
The levator ani muscle consists of four parts: M. pubococcygeus, M. puborectalis,
M. iliococcygeus, and M. coccygeus.
The pelvic �oor closes off the osseous pelvis caudally.
The opening limited by the levator ani is called the “urogenital hiatus”. The pelvic
�oor is able to compress the hollow organs that pass through it. At the beginning of
micturition, the pelvic �oor relaxes and the urogenital hiatus opens. After micturition,
the levator ani muscle and the bladder neck resume their original position and
muscle tone allows the urogenital hiatus to close again. Because of human’s upright
posture, the pelvic �oor is subject to constant pressure. This pressure on the pelvic
�oor can increase brie�y through excessive stress (e.g., coughing, lifting heavy
loads).
Only intact structures of the complex functional pelvic �oor system can guarantee
optimal function. Damage to the pelvic �oor structures and M. levator ani can lead to
weakening and consequently relaxation of the pelvic �oor, causing a visible lowering
of the perineum (“descending perineum syndrome”) with a gaping introitus seen on
gynecological exam.
The term “endopelvic fascia” is a collective term for the connective tissue supporting
apparatus and connective tissue bonding substance between the bladder, urethra,
and M. levator ani. The cervix and vagina are secured through the endopelvic fascia
to the lateral wall of the pelvis.

The endopelvic connective tissue consists of collagen, elastin, smooth muscle,
vessels, and nerves. The tissue composition of the endopelvic fascia re�ects the
function of this tissue: On the one hand, it contains vessels and nerves to supply the
organs; on the other hand, it has a support function.

Neurophysiology ooff Bladder Control
Various centers of different hierarchies in the central nervous system and various
peripheral nerves control bladder function in a complex interaction and ensure that
both smooth and striated muscles function together.
The primary re�ex level with the micturition center (S2 to S5) located in the sacral
spinal cord is controlled by a pontine center above it. All processes in this re�ex
system occur below the level of perception.
The afferent innervation of the urinary bladder consists of both the pelvic nerve
(parasympathetic system) and the hypogastric nerve (sympathetic system) and
somatic motor nerve �bers.
Because the �bers of the hypogastric nerve arise partially from up to the level of T10,
it is understandable that in paraplegia below T10, the motor control of the urinary
bladder is indeed lost, but not the sensory perception of bladder function status.

Figure 2
Bladder �lling (left): During the �lling phase, when the bladder wall slowly expands, an afferent stimulus
occurs, which controls the sympathetically innervated bladder opening.
At the same time, the external urethral sphincter and pelvic �oor muscles are stimulated via the pudendal
nerve and direct motor nerves of segments S2-S5. As a result, the tone of these structures increases,
ensuring closure of the urinary bladder even with increasing bladder volume (guarding re�ex). Sympathetic
activation of the detrusor increases simultaneously, helping communicate accommodation of the smooth
muscles.
Spontaneous micturition (right): During spontaneous micturition, maximal parasympathetic innervation of the
detrusor occurs, which can be measured as an intravesical pressure increase. Concomitantly, the
sympathetic structures on the bladder opening are inhibited, and the striated sphincter and pelvic �oor
muscles relax. The latter can be measured in a decrease of pelvic �oor EMG activity. This ensures that the
urinary bladder can empty without any signi�cant infravesical resistance.
Table 1

Diagram ooff innervation ooff continence and
micturition control::
Figure 3: Diagram of innervation of continence and micturition control.

Cortical centers involved: A – Cerebrum (cortex); B – Basal ganglia; C Pons
Peripheral nerves involved, linked to spinal cord segments T10-11 or S2-4: D –
Hypogastric nerve; E – Pelvic nerve; F – Pudendal nerve (autonomic efferents,
autonomic afferents); G – Bladder; H – Urethral sphincter; J – Smooth detrusor
muscles; K – Pelvic �oor
• Cerebrum: Perception of bladder �lling. Voluntary inhibition or activation of
micturition.
• Basal ganglia: Unconscious inhibition of the parasympathetic micturition center.
• Pons: The pontine micturition and storage center is controlled by the cerebrum,
so it can be controlled voluntarily.
• Hypogastric nerves: These nerves transmit sympathetic impulses from
segments T10 to L2 to the bladder and pelvic �oor and to the parasympathetic
ganglia of the pelvic nerves. Alpha-adrenergic impulses stimulate contraction in
the sphincter area (reservoir function). Beta-adrenergic impulses decrease
detrusor muscle tone (reservoir function).
• Pudendal nerves: These nerves transmit cerebral impulses to the external
urethral sphincter (and pelvic �oor), leading to voluntary contraction (which to
some extent can be conditioned by training).
• Pelvic nerves: The parasympathetic (cholinergic) impulses from the sacral
micturition center (segments S2-S4) activate the tone of the smooth detrusor
muscles.
• Urothelium: Recent studies show that the bladder wall itself plays a crucial role
in �lling and micturition.
[P05]
• Pons: This also explains why after a lesion in the cortical area (e.g., stroke),
patients can no longer intervene in re�ex activities and thus lose cortical control
of bladder function. For example, in response to the urge to urinate, patients are
no longer able to delay micturition until they reach the toilet. In contrast, in
lesions below the pontine centers and above the sacral micturition center, not
only is there a loss of voluntary control but �ne control of urine storage and
micturition are also lost.
• Urothel: There is now enough evidence to show that in the bladder wall between
smooth musculature, urothelium and afferent nerve endings together with
various neurotransmitter substances there is an intensive information network
for the central nervous system, which may at least better explain the

phenomenon of bladder wall compliance than the known re�ex arcs alone.
[P05]

EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Urogynecology 〉 Overactive bladder: treatment
Last update: january 2024
Overactive bladder:: treatment
Author:: Prof.. DDrr.. med.. Cornelia Betschart
Leitende Ärztin, Klinik für Gynäkologie, UniversitätsSpital Zürich, Stv. Klinikdirektorin Klinik für
Gynäkologie & Co-Leiterin Beckenboden- und Kontinenzzentrum
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr. med. Annette Kuhn, Prof. Dr. med. Gabriel Schär

Treatment
The treatment of overactive bladder is patient-oriented and takes place in phases
(�gure 1). Initially conservative treatment, followed by more in-depth diagnostics if
the response is insu�cient and more invasive treatment. Treatment success is
evaluated after 4 to 6 weeks. A bladder diary should be completed for 1 to 3 days
before the consultation.
Figure 1: Phased approach to evaluation and treatment of overactive bladder.
The basic evaluation is aimed to diagnose and treat primary diseases that affect the
bladder: colpitis, vulvitis, or urinary tract infection (cf. G02H), tumors in the lower
pelvis (e.g., bladder papilloma, bladder cancer, large anterior uterine wall �broids),
urolithiasis, exclusion of neurological diseases (e.g. genital multiple sclerosis,
dementia) or migration/exposure of foreign materials (mesh for treatment of
prolapse or incontinence sling). All these pathologies can trigger an urge to urinate.
In the case of micro-hematuria, the lower urinary tract and, in cases of no abnormal
�ndings, the upper urinary tract should be investigated according to the guidelines.
Primary treatments modalities
The patient should learn:
• to delay micturition (behavioural therapy and bladder re-training)
• to drink su�cient quantities of liquids (drinking behavior)
• to avoid bladder-irritating substances (alcohol, substances containing caffeine
and sweeteners, spicy food).

The patient should complete a bladder diary to record micturition volume, volume
intake, frequency and urge and urge incontinence.

Parasympatholytic side effects, including those arising outside the bladder, include
dry mouth, constipation, visual disorders, central nervous system disorders, etc. In
older patients in particular, it is important to watch out for a potential summation of
anticholinergic side effects: confusion, disorientation, disturbed sleep and
forgetfulness can arise! In cases of cognitive impairment or poly-pharmacy,
particularly with co-medication with partial anticholinergic effects (e.g.
antipsychotics, antidepressants, benzodiazepines, opiates, loop diuretics),
anticholinergic agents should be used with the greatest caution.
Biotransformation by means of cytochrome P450 is an important component in the
degradation of numerous substances, and the combination of several substances
that inhibit cytochrome p450 can cause pronounced changes in plasma levels and
intensify side effects.
Of all anticholinergic substances, only trospium chloride is not metabolized by
cytochrome p450. Instead, it is excreted through the kidneys as an active metabolite.
According to current data, trospium chloride and darifenacin do not lead to impaired
cognitive function, although most studies have not been carried out in older patients.
Since 2014, a new β3 adrenergic agonist has been available on the Swiss market in
the form of mirabegron (Betmiga®). The effect of β3 agonists is observed in the
bladder wall, involving β3 adrenoreceptors. These receptors play a role in the bladder
�lling and storage phases.
Randomized studies have shown a signi�cant decrease in incontinence episodes
and increase in bladder capacity [P13].
The combination of anticholinergic and β3 adrenergic drugs is possible on the basis
of current studies and leads to an additional improvement in the urge to urinate and
incontinence [P07].
Hypertension, nasopharyngitis, itching, and headaches have been reported as side
effects of β3 agonists. [P07][P13]

Mirabegron is contraindicated in cases of severe or insu�ciently controlled
hypertension. Mirabegron is a CYP2D6 inhibitor. Caution is advised with the
additional administration of drugs that are metabolized via the same enzyme (e. g.
metoprolol and desipramine). The effects of digoxin and warfarin are also enhanced
during the administration of β3 agonists.
Table 1: Active substances, trade names and dosages of drug treatment for overactive bladder (as of
January 2024)
Active Products Dosage,, administration,, treatment duration
substance
Solifenacin Vesicare® 5 mg initially once per day p.o.; can be increased
to 10 mg per day
Tolterodine Detrusitol® 2 mg or 4 mg once per day p.o.
Darifenacin Emselex® 7,5 mg initially once per day p.o.; can be
increased to 15 mg
Fesoterodine Toviaz® 4 mg once per day p.o.; can be increased to 8 mg
Oxybutynin Kentera® 3,9 mg per day transdermal plaster; 2 plasters
Vesoxx® per week
intravesical instillation of 10-30/40 mg per day
Trospium Spasmex® 2 x 20 mg per day p.o.; short half-life
chloride Spasmo-
Urgenin®
Mirabegron Betmiga® 25 mg initially once per day p.o.; can be
(β3 agonist) increased to 50 mg per day

Injecting intravesical botulinum toxin into the detrusor muscle should only be
considered after failure of conservative treatment. Bladder capacity can be
increased through the “paralysis” of the bladder muscles. Urine retention is possible.
However, the effect of intravesical botox decreases after 6 to 9 months and the
injection can then be repeated. Tachyphylaxis has not been documented. The costs
for intravesicular botulinum toxin injection for the treatment of overactive bladder
have been covered by health insurance providers since January 1, 2015.
The effect of intravesical botox treatment is higher than that of drug-based
treatment. In a study by Schurch et al., 2007, a subjective improvement was found in
88 % of patients, 86 % had no more incontinence and in 82 %, the urge to void had
disappeared [P01].
Undesired side effects of intravesical botox treatment include temporary urine
retention, increased residual urine volume (sometimes requiring intermittent self-
catheterization) and urinary tract infection.
In certain cases, psychosomatic treatment may be indicated (the bladder as a
“mirror of the soul”).
If genital atrophy is found, local estrogenization in the form of cream, pessaries or
vaginal tablets can improve symptoms. [P01]

The following products are available (as of January 2024): Estriol e.g. Ovestin®
Oestrogynaedron® and Blissel®, as ultra-low-dose estriol preparations 0,03 mg
Kadefemin® or Gyno�or®; estradiol e.g. Vagifem® and Estring®; promestriene e.g.
Colpotrophine® (available from Zurich Cantonal Pharmacy). Local hormones are
inserted vaginally twice a week in the evening. At this dosage, progesteron
supplementation to avoid endometrial hyperplasia is not required.
In patients with a history of breast cancer or other hormone-dependent tumors,
caution is recommended and muco-adhesive gels without hormone supplementation
are the �rst choice in the treatment of urogenital atrophy and bladder irritation. If
local estrogenization is unavoidable, the lowest dosed preparations should be used.
The role of estrogens in regard to overactive bladder has not been fully clari�ed yet.
Some prospective studies have shown a bene�t as a result of local estrogenization.
Systemic estrogen therapy has shown no improvement to urge incontinence [B06].
Flavoxate (Urispas®) is an older spasmolytic drug and moderate calcium channel
blocker that inhibits phosphodiesterase and has a local anesthetic effect. Its effect
on symptoms of urgency is documented inconsistently, and side effects are low,
emphasizing its role as an additive. [B06]

Antidiuretic hormones (desmopressin) stimulate water reabsorption in the renal
medulla and can be indicated for nocturnal enuresis or nocturia in the context of
overactive bladder, with no other causes (cardiac, endocrinological, or drinking
habits). In older patients, it is important to monitor water and electrolyte balance and
blood pressure. There is currently no longer a preparation authorised for this purpose
in Switzerland.
If the above listed medicamentous treatment options fail, neuro-stimulation has
proven to be an effective treatment modality. A continuous electrical impulse, which
is transferred through surface or needle electrodes on the lower leg to the tibial
nerve or from a pacemaker implanted in the buttocks and via �ne wires into the
sacral root of S2/S3, is used to achieve a persistent reduction in the imperative urge
to void, a reduction of the micturition frequency and urge incontinence in 50 to 70 %
of patients – namly in patients that showed responses to test electrodes during a 1
to 2 week test phase [P06].
Bladder augmentation or neo-bladder: As a last resort, in cases of chronic changes
to the bladder wall, for example such as radiogenic contracted bladder, the bladder
capacity can be increased using an ileum or colon loop transplant.
If the Credé maneuver does not lead to su�cient voiding of the bladder, the neo-
bladder must be voided by means of a life-long self-catheterization. [P06]

Practice and research
The etiology of overactive bladder is still not fully understood yet; there are
myogenic, neurogenic (peripheral and central) and chronic infectious theories. In the
future, the role of the microbiome may provide clari�cation with regard to the
pathogenesis. There are also hints that OAB and bladder pain syndrome may have
similarities in regard to the local immune responses. [P14]
Simpli�ed cell-to-cell communication, for example via increased connexon
formation, as with the myocardium, is presumed but has not yet been proven for
OAB. Bladder pain syndrome (BPS; previously: interstitial cystitis or painful bladder
syndrome), where the symptoms of overactive bladder are coupled with a reduced
bladder capacity and painful micturition as well as a normal urine status, is attracting
increased attention (criteria: see www.ESSIC.org). BPS is found with endometriosis
in 30 % of cases. The quality of life of the women with BPS is affected to a higher
degree than the one that suffer from overactive bladder alone.
ESSIC International Society for the Study of Bladder Pain Syndrome (BPS)
Further substances are being investigated for the treatment of overactive bladder, in
particular where painful micturition co-exists: substances affecting the central
nervous system such as gabapentin (e.g., Neurontin®), tramadol (Tramal®), and
NK1 receptor antagonists (e.g., Aprepitant®) belong to these newer substances, on
which little clinical data has been published up to date.
Pentosan polysulphate (Elmiron®) has recently been approved in Switzerland for
bladder pain syndrome as a supportive medication to build up the urothelial
protective layer.

Author:: Prof.. DDrr méd.. Chahin Achtari CCoo--Author((ss)):: Prof. Dr méd.
Chahin Achtari, Prof. Dr. méd.
Médecin-chef au Service de gynécologie, Centres interdisciplinaires Patrick Dällenbach
d'oncologie CHUV
Authors of former script versions: G. Schär

Introduction
The internal genital organs rest on connective tissue and muscular structures that
hold them in position and in place within the lesser pelvis. Downward displacement
is a change in the position of the internal genitals in the craniocaudal axis, or within
the sagittal or frontal planes (cf. G01A).
The vagina comprises three compartments:
• Anterior compartment: related to the urethra (distal third) and the bladder
(proximal third)
• Apical compartment: comprising the cervix or vaginal apex after hysterectomy
• Posterior compartment: related to the anal canal (distal third), rectum (middle
third), and rectouterine pouch (proximal part)
Prolapse always refers to the descent of a pelvic organ in relation to its normal
position. Descent of the front vaginal wall is called cystocele and/or urethrocele.
Descent of the uterus is called uterine prolapse, and descent of the vaginal apex
(after hysterectomy) is called vaginal vault prolapse. Descent of the rear vaginal wall
is called rectocele. Enterocele refers to the descent of the rectouterine pouch,
whereby the small intestine descends between the posterior vaginal wall and the
rectum.

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification (Teil 2/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification
content:
A connective tissue/muscle defect of the supporting structures is responsible for the
slackening of the vaginal wall, causing further descent.
The pelvic organs are naturally supported by the pelvic �oor muscles and the
supporting connective tissue of the endopelvic fascia (EPF), which covers the
interior side of the levator ani muscle (cf. G01B).
The pelvic �oor muscles form a dynamic “plate” on which the pelvic �oor organs
rest. The pelvic �oor muscles comprise the puboviscerralis muscle, which forms a U-
shaped sling around the rectum, bladder and urethra, as well as the pubococcygeus
and posteriorly the iliococcygeus muscles (cf. G01B).
The pelvic organs are also anchored to the lateral pelvic wall by the EPF. The EPF is a
layer of connective tissue that stretches across the lesser pelvis while incorporating
a section of the anterior vaginal wall. The EPF also contains a network of nerves and
blood vessels that supply the pelvic organs. The anterior section of the EPF is also
called the pubocervical (Halban’s) fascia. It supports the bladder and anchors the
vagina to the lateral pelvic wall. The dorsal section is known as Denonvilliers' fascia,
running from the dorsal vaginal wall to the rectum (cf. G01B).
Surgical prolapse treatment aims to strengthen these fascial structures. The cardinal
and uterosacral ligaments are a condensed part of the endopelvic fascia. They
attach the cervix and the cranial third of the vagina to the posterior and lateral pelvic
walls. The cardinal ligaments, which stabilize the cervix and cranial vagina, are also
called the parametrium and paracolpium respectively.
The levator muscles are mainly responsible for supporting the pelvic organs. They
act as a sort of ‘�oor’ on which the pelvic organs rest. Laterally, they are attached to
the arcus tendineus levator ani, which is a connective tissue thickening found along
the lateral pelvic wall. An impairment of the levator muscles, for example due to
direct muscle trauma, a tear, or an innervation disorder, increases the stress on the
EPF. This increasing pressure causes the EPF to decompensate to a progressive
extent, becoming overstretched or ruptured, causing the vaginal wall and pelvic
organs to prolapse.
A prolapse can manifest as the descent of individual organs or as a combination of
several descended compartments. This is why it is paramount that the different
compartments are being tested and evaluated individually.

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification (Teil 3/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification
content:
DDee��nition
The true extent of organ prolapse is often only seen when the patient is in upright
position or when pressure manoeuvers (Valsalva) are applied.
The su�x “-cele” is added to the name of the organ to express descensus. This
can affect both the anterior vaginal wall (cystocele = bladder descent) and the
posterior vaginal wall (rectocele = intestinal descent).
There are various classi�cation systems for genital prolapse. Baden and Walker
(1972) based their system on the hymen as the reference level, and classify the
prolapse of the various compartments from 1 to 4.
Figure 1
• Uterine prolapse = uterine descent (midline compartment)
• Total prolapse = complete prolapse of the uterus and anterior and posterior
vaginal walls
Table 1: Baden Walker classi�cation (the halfway classi�cation)
Grade 1 Halfway to the hymen
Grade 2 Level of the hymen/introitus
Grade 3 Halfway past the hymen
Grade 4 (total Whole uterus protrudes in front of the vulva (vaginal wall
prolapse) inside out!)
Clinical assessment of classi�cation: as the patient applies pressure (Valsalva)! In
some cases, it is necessary to assess the patient in a standing position.

A more recent classi�cation system was suggested by a panel of specialists and
introduced as the standard (POP-Q). This system measures speci�c points of the
compartments in relation to the hymen. This classi�cation is mainly useful for
clinical trials and enables a more objective assessment of prolapse.
Figure 2: POP-Q classi�cation.
With uterine descent, the neighboring structures always descend to some extent -
this can cause additional symptoms and should be regarded as a pathology in itself,
making treatment necessary.
Neighboring organ prolapse ((--cele))
Neighboring organ prolapse is also classi�ed (Grade I to IV):
