keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification
content:
Figure 3: Cystocele and rectocele.
Cystocele (anterior prolapse) Sections of the urinary bladder follow the downward
movement of the uterus and curve the anterior vaginal wall outwards. If the urethra
also descends, this is called cystourethrocele. Rectocele (posterior prolapse)
Sections of the rectum follow the downward movement of the uterus and curve the
posterior vaginal wall outwards. Combined �ndings are common.
Enterocele is the only form of pelvic organ prolapse that is a true hernia of the
peritoneal cavity (originating in the rectouterine pouch), with a hernial ori�ce, hernia
sac and hernia contents (intestinal loops!). It manifests as a posterior prolapse of
the upper third of the vagina. It can sometimes be di�cult to distinguish rectocele
from enterocele.

Figure 4: Enterocele.

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification (Teil 5/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification
content:
Pathophysiology
The factors and pathogenetic mechanisms that lead to downward displacement are
complex and multiple, all leading to a defect in the supporting connective tissue.
1. Weakness of the supporting connective tissue (mostly constitutional).
2. Severe trauma to the pelvic �oor during childbirth, as a result of traumatic
vaginal delivery, due to instrumental delivery, or because of high fetal birth
weight, for example.
3. In terms of prophylaxis, the pelvic �oor can be protected during pregnancy with
pelvic �oor exercises, prepartum perineal massage (improves pelvic �oor
elasticity and “compliance” during delivery) and postpartum pelvic �oor training.
Episiotomy does not exhibit any advantages for the pelvic �oor.
4. Repetitive or chronically activated abdominal pressure (heavy physical work,
COPD, etc. also contribute to a vicious circle of promoting a rectocele with stool
retention [strong pressure to defecate encourages further manifestations of
descent]).
5. Other: Pronounced obesity. Chronic glucocorticoid treatment. Pelvic �oor
innervation disorders (spinal cord disorders, spina bi�da occulta).
�. All these factors exert an even greater effect in the presence of
postmenopausal atrophy (due to estrogen de�ciency).

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification (Teil 6/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification
content:
Imaging
Certain imaging procedures provide a better understanding of the development of
prolapse. Ultrasound and MRI are the techniques most commonly employed.
Urogynecological ultrasound has been established as the standard imaging
procedure for evaluating pelvic organ prolapse.
Urogynecological ultrasound is carried out using an abdominal or vaginal probe
placed at the vaginal introitus, and provides a sagittal cross section of the pelvic
�oor in the median plane. Furthermore, 3D and 4D probes can similarly be used.
They capture a tissue block that can be analyzed later, enabling the pelvic �oor to be
shown in various planes, in particular an axial plane. This plane allows the levator
muscles (puborectalis) and its lateral outline to be examined. Overdistension of the
urogenital hiatus is thought to be one of the pathophysiological causes resulting in
prolapse issues. In addition, an extensive lateral defect of the pubovisceralis muscle
is thought to be an increased risk factor for postoperative prolapse recurrence. With
urogynecological ultrasound, it is additionally possible to view the three
compartments and a prolapse, thus obtaining further information for clinical
diagnosis.
Figure 5: Urogynecological ultrasound of a woman with stress incontinence. When relaxed (a), the urethra (U)
is positioned vertically. B = bladder, S = pubic symphysis, V = vagina, R = rectum, Ut = uterus. Under pressure
(b), the urethra rotates and descends, opening in a funnel shape (T). Upon pelvic �oor contraction (c), the
contraction of the puborectalis sling (PR) causes the rectum, vagina, and urethra to lift and move into a more
ventral position.

keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification
content:
Figure 6: Perineal ultrasound of a woman with major cystocele with midline defect: When relaxed (a), vertical
urethra and normal bladder �oor position. Under pressure (b), the bladder �oor signi�cantly descends in a
caudal direction. The external urethral ori�ce descends slightly (arrow) and the urethra shows a kinking in the
middle section.
Pelvic MRI has been established to solve speci�c issues. MR defecography has
become increasingly popular, given that it can provide information that potentially
in�uences treatment, in particular surgery. Rectocele with defecation disorder alone
can be corrected with colpoperineoplasty, while an intussusception detected on MRI
requires additional �xation of the sigma-rectum. Intussusception is the protrusion of
the intestinal wall into itself, resulting in a valve that complicates defecation.
Figure 7: MRI of an anterior rectocele measuring 39mm in depth. Measured under MRI contrast gel.

Figure 8: MRI of intussusception. X shows a folding of the intestinal wall which can cause a valve effect.

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification (Teil 8/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification
content:
Clinical presentation
Symptoms often do not correlate with anatomical severity. They depend to a large
extent on the sensitivity and basic aesthetic needs of the patient.
Anterior prolapse (cystocele)
• Urinary incontinence
• Voiding disorders (including urinary retention)
• Recurrent urinary tract infection
• Urodynamics can help distinguish the underlying issue. Major cystocele can
mask urinary incontinence due to urethral kinking. Testing should therefore
include a cough test with reduced prolapse.
Anterior prolapse (cystocele)
• Urinary incontinence
• Voiding disorders (including urinary retention)
• Recurrent urinary tract infection
• Urodynamics can help distinguish the underlying issue. Major cystocele can
mask urinary incontinence due to urethral kinking. Testing should therefore
include a cough test with reduced prolapse.
Posterior prolapse (rectocele)
• This can be accompanied by a bowel voiding disorder or fecal incontinence.
• Reducing the rectocele when defecating can help women to empty the bowels
without complication (digital evacuation).
Particularly in postmenopausal women affected by estrogen de�ciency, prolapse is
often accompanied by epithelial damage. Mechanical irritation leads to erosion or
ulceration of the external cervix and atrophied vaginal wall. Superinfections of this
epithelial damage are possible and cause symptoms that must be treated,
particularly prior to surgery, through vaginal application of estrogen. Estriol cream
may be applied vaginally once or twice per week in the evening. The creams are
supplied with an acrylic tube. This is �lled with the cream and inserted vaginally. The
stamp is then pressed to introduce the correct dose of the cream into the vagina.

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification (Teil 9/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse classification
content:
Complications
Erosion and ulceration of the vaginal wall or cervix occur due to:
• Estrogen de�ciency, resulting from a pessary, mechanical irritation from
underwear, or incontinence pads.
• Rectocele with voiding disorder: the chronic pressure can lead to the formation
of hemorrhoids.

Diagnosis
A gynecological speculum examination under strain and coughs shows the extent
and type of descent.
Cystocele is identi�ed by a bulging of the anterior vaginal wall, particularly during
speculum examination when coughing or straining or on bimanual palpation.
Rectocele is considered when the posterior vaginal wall bulges outwards (speculum
examination).
Rectal palpation (see below) is employed to distinguish between enterocele and
rectocele.
Imaging diagnostics supplement the clinical examination with additional
information, such as urethral hypermobility, presence of enterocele, and extent of
cystocele and rectocele.

Figure 9: Prolapse of the anterior and posterior vaginal wall.

Differential diagnosis
Rectal palpation for distinguishing between rectocele and enterocele:
• Rectocele (a gap in the connective tissue of the anterior rectal wall) can be
palpated during a rectal examination.
• Enterocele can be well determined on ultrasound, as it is di�cult to palpate.
• The simultaneous occurrence of recto-enterocele is possible.
Figure 10: Rectal examination to distinguish between rectocele and enterocele.

Introduction
The aim of prolapse surgery is to restore anatomy and remedy pelvic organ
descensus symptoms while maintaining the function of the vagina and neighboring
organs.
Conditions for correct treatment:
• The goals and wishes of the patient are known. What does she expect from the
treatment?
• The anatomy of the defect is known and the symptoms and functional
impairment can be put in the correct context.
• The patient’s expectations and treatment options are compatible (excessive
expectations).
• The patient has been informed of sequelae and side effects of treatment.
• Patient consent.

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment (Teil 2/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment
content:
Individual treatment options
Vaginal estrogen treatment
Localized estrogen treatment with vaginal estriol cream or vaginal pessaries can
have a positive effect on the symptoms of pelvic organ prolapse. The stretched and
displaced vaginal skin becomes less dry and thus causes less pruritis. Any dysuria is
improved or even disappears. The skin is stronger and more resistant to pressure
preventing the consequences of erosion and hemorrhage. For pessary treatment or
displacement surgery, well estrogenized vaginal skin is bene�cial.
Pessary treatment
Previously, long-term pessaries in form of a dish or ring pessary were inserted to
treat patients who could not or declined to be operated on. Today, this application is
only appropriate in very rare situations. Vaginal infections with pressure ulcers may
occur over time. Medical progress means that even elderly and multimorbid patients
can now be operated on satisfactorily.
Pessaries for self--administration
Figure 1: Cube pessary for self-administration.

If the patient prefers conservative treatment, she will be informed of pessary
treatment with a cube pessary, and the pessary will be adjusted and given to her to
take home. The �gure 1 shows a silicone cube pessary. The patient inserts the
pessary with estrogen cream daily, or for speci�c stress situations such as exercise,
and removes it by pulling on the threads. In active, healthy patients with good life
expectancy, pessary treatment is generally not a long-term treatment.

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment (Teil 3/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment
content:
Surgery
Conditions for successful pelvic organ prolapse surgery:
• Holistic preoperative planning.
• Surgical procedure based on the speci�c defect.
Recurrence and complications cannot be avoided, but can be reduced.
Indication
The patient is impaired by the pelvic organ prolapse and has a reduced quality of life
due to limitations in everyday routine or the aforementioned functional impairments.
Medical secondary problems as a result of the pelvic organ prolapse are an urgent
indication for surgical intervention. These include chronic recurrent urinary tract
infections with residual urine and above all pronounced symptoms such as renal
congestion and pyelonephritis. Pressure ulcers are practically impossible to remedy
without correcting the descensus.
There are vaginal, abdominal and laparoscopic interventions that are described
for correcting prolapse.
Vaginal surgical procedures are less invasive than abdominal procedures.
Laparoscopic operations provide lower peri- and postoperative morbidity, but entail
higher intraoperative cardiopulmonary stress. For patients in reduced general
condition, vaginal operations are therefore recommended. Abdominal procedures
such as sacrocolpopexy are associated with lower rates of recurrence than vaginal
procedures.
Surgical concept
Requirements in terms of quality of life and function, anatomical defects and life
expectancy must be included when deciding on surgery. Table 1 shows the surgical
concept for prolapse.

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment (Teil 4/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment
content:
An apical prolapse of the uterus or vaginal cuff requires an apical �xation, by means
of either vaginal sacrospinal �xation or abdominal sacrocolpopexy. Cystocele with a
midline defect can be treated with diaphragmplasty (anterior repair), while cystocele
with a paravaginal defect requires lateral anchoring (lateral repair). Rectocele can be
corrected with colpoperineoplasty. Vaginal mesh should be used with caution.
Mesh has a lower rate of recurrence but higher rate of complications, particularly
with regard to healing disorders with erosion of the mesh and corresponding
symptoms such as vaginal bleeding, infection, spontaneous pain and dyspareunia.
The use of vaginal mesh is recommended above all in recurrent cases.
Sacrocolpopexy always requires the placement of a mesh to bridge the distance
from the sacrum to the vagina.

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment (Teil 5/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment
content:
Risk constellations such as a very large prolapse, chronic bronchitis, overweight,
constipation and high physical stress through work or exercise make it necessary to
carry out an intervention with the lowest possible rate of recurrence. This applies to
sacrocolpopexy with mesh insertion. In 2011, a warning published by the FDA (US
Food and Drug Administration) concerning the use of vaginal mesh alarmed the
urogynecological world. Worries relating to the side effects and complications of
vaginal mesh implants have led to more critical scrutiny of the indication of vaginal
mesh.
For legal reasons, USA-based companies in particular have removed vaginal meshes
from the market as a result. Mesh implants require a strong indication. Not every
cystocele or rectocele requires a vaginal mesh. Table 1 above shows the evidence-
based indications for mesh, including after the FDA’s warning and results of various
recently published, well-conducted, randomized trials.
The value of hysterectomy for treating prolapse does not outweigh the practical
aspects.
If there is no uterine pathology, uterus-preserving techniques are a valuable option. If
an apical �xation of a uterine prolapse must be carried out, either a hysteropexy with
�xation of the cervix to the sacrospinal ligament or a hysterectomy with �xation of
the vaginal apex to the sacrospinal ligament can be carried out (Richter’s procedure).

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment (Teil 6/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment
content:
Uterine oorr vaginal cuff prolapse surgery
The hysterectomy is carried out to provide access to the vaginal apex and thus
improves the implementation of the suspension. With vaginal correction, a vaginal
hysterectomy, with sacrocolpopexy a subtotal hysterectomy, leaving the cervix in
place, is carried out. The cervix is left in place in order to avoid the opening of the
vagina. This is crucial as the placement of a mesh to the cervix and vagina is one of
the important �xation steps to perform a sacrocolpopexy, while the risk of wound
healing disorders and mesh exposition to the vagina is lower if the vagina is not
opened.
Sacrocolpopexy ((abdominal,, laparoscopic,, robotic))
Apical prolapse can be remedied with sacrocolpopexy in 85 to 90 % of cases. In
addition, this technique displays a lower rate of dyspareunia than vaginal
interventions. Considering that sacrocolpopexy exhibits the best success rates with
low complication rates, it was de�ned as the gold standard by Maher in a Cochrane
Review [B01].
Alternatively to sacrocolpopexy, other techniques have emerged avoiding dissection
of the sacral promontory. The lateral suspension and the pectopexy are two recent
techniques involving mesh placement to suspend the apical compartment. In the
lateral suspension operation the mesh is inserted through the abdominal wall and
attached to the uterine isthmus or vaginal cuff. In the pectopexy technique the mesh
is attached to the pectineal (Cooper’s) ligament. [B01]

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment (Teil 7/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment
content:
Figure 2: With sacrocolpopexy, the vagina is anchored to the sacrum with a y-shaped mesh, which is sutured
in place along the ventral and dorsal vagina.
Vaginal sacrospinal ��xation ((Amreich--Richter))
The most prevalent technique for vaginal �xation of the vaginal apex is sacrospinal
�xation using the Amreich-Richter technique. This can only be carried out if the
vagina is long enough to be able to �x the apex of the vagina to the sacrospinal
ligament. If this is not the case, abdominal sacrocolpopexy remains a possibility.
In case of vaginal apex or uterine prolapse, the vaginal apex is adapted to the
sacrospinal ligament. Care must be taken, however, as nerves and vessels (pudendal
blood vessels) run closely behind the ligament.

Figure 3
Figure 4: Vaginal sacrospinal �xation (Amreich-Richter).
Cystocele
Diaphragmplasty (anterior fascia repair) is indicated for a midline defect of the
endopelvic fascia.

Figure 5: The vaginal skin is opened in the midline and dissected from the endopelvic fascia located under
the bladder (I). Stitches are placed in the lateral sections of the endopelvic fascia (II) with resorbable or late-
resorbable threads and knotted in the midline (III). The bladder is elevated cranially and supported by a
reinforced layer of connective tissue. The vaginal skin in closed again (IV).
If a cystocele with a paravaginal defect (tear on the side of the endopelvic fascia) is
present, a lateral �xation of the fascia is required.
Rectocele
Rectocele is corrected with colpoperineoplasty (posterior fascia repair). To this end,
the perirectal fascia is brought together in the midline and employed for stabilizing
the preractal fascia against the vagina.

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment (Teil 8/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment
content:
Figure 7: Colpoperineoplasty.
I: The vaginal skin is incised at the midline and dissected from the endopelvic fascia located under the
rectum. II: The perirectal fasciae are brought together with resorbable sutures, with placing the stitches
laterally and knotted in the midline (III), so that the rectum is covered and stabilized by the reinforced
prerectal fascia. IV: The perineum is also brought together with a single suture in the midline in order to close
a widened genital hiatus.
While diaphragmaplasty (anterior repair) is associated with a high rate of recurrence,
attention has to be paid to the rectocele correction. Dyspareunia, in particular, must
be mentioned. The rate of dyspareunia appears to be directly connected to the
chosen method of surgery. Numerous authors accuse in particular the midline fusion
of the levator ani muscle performed during levatorplasty of being a cause of
dyspareunia. The rate of recurrence of posterior repair with aligning the fascia in the
midline is low (between 7 and 12 %).

Postoperative incontinence
About 25 % of patients likely develop stress urinary incontinence following prolapse
surgery (occult stress incontinence). A preoperative cough test with prolapse
reposition helps to evaluate the risk and advise the patient accordingly. If occult
stress urinary incontinence is diagnosed, a continence operation can be carried out
during the prolapse surgery (e.g., tension-free sling).
A wait-and-see approach may likewise be taken. To this end, the patient must be
carefully informed and prepared for the fact that stress urinary incontinence can
occur secondarily, namely in cases where no concomitant sling surgery is
performed. Though, occult urinary incontinence is not becoming overt in all cases
after POP surgery, so a two-step procedure is also advisable in order to prevent
unnecessary incontinence interventions.

topic: Pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment (Teil 9/11)
keywords: pelvic organ descensus and prolapse treatment
content:
Vaginal mesh implantation
The fact that cystocele recurrence occurs in approx. 30 to 50 % of vaginal prolapse
operations has not only led to operative modi�cation of vaginal surgeries, but also to
the search for ways to strengthen the tissue in order to lower the rate of recurrence
[P03, P04].
This has provoked a signi�cant increase in interest in mesh applications. Mesh
provides lower anatomic recurrence rates but higher complication rates with
defective healing, mesh shrinkage, infection, dyspareunia, and organ damage.
Studies have shown that subjective results of fascia operations are as good as
operations with vaginal mesh insertion. Therefore use of mesh is not only potentially
damageable for the patient but brings no subjective advantage. Use of vaginal mesh
has been progressively abandoned and even banned in many countries. [P03][P02]

topic: Stress urinary incontinence and overactive bladder  diagnosis (Teil 3/12)
keywords: stress urinary incontinence and overactive bladder  diagnosis
content:
sudden, strong urgency (not mandatory)?
• Current medication
• Previous treatments/surgery
• Personal psychological and social suffering
Bladder diary
• Information amount of drinking and micturition, and episodes of urgency and
incontinence
Figure 1: Example bladder diary.
Gynecological (pelvic �oor) examination
• Genital atrophy?
• Evidence of genital prolapse?
• Cough test with full bladder: leakage?
• Pelvic �oor muscle function: Ability to contract the pelvic �oor muscle (how
strong, how long?), any muscular defects?
Classi�cation and quanti�cation of urine leakage: Pad test (1 h pad test; 24 h pad
test)
• gives information on urine leakage in grams over 1 h or 24 h [B06]
• Predominantly used for studies

• Normal values are di�cult to de�ne - leakage should be: < 3g/24h (3 g could
possibly also be caused by physiological vaginal discharge), alternatively, < 2 g
after 1 hour of stress
[B06]
For conservative treatment, basic diagnostics (medical history, gynecological
examination and post-void residual volume using pelvic �oor sonography) are
su�cient. If conservative treatment fails or in cases of recurrent stress urinary
incontinence, further diagnostics such as urodynamic studies prior to surgical
treatment, should be performed.
The aim of urodynamic function diagnostics is to distinguish between stress
urinary incontinence which requires surgery and an overactive bladder
(previously known as urge incontinence), which can be primarily treated with
medication.

topic: Stress urinary incontinence and overactive bladder  diagnosis (Teil 4/12)
keywords: stress urinary incontinence and overactive bladder  diagnosis
content:
Pelvic ��oor sonography ((PPFF sonography))
Morphological diagnostic techniques include pelvic �oor sonography and urethro-
cystoscopy. Urogynecological ultrasound has replaced lateral cystourethrography.
The aim of morphological diagnostics is to show the anatomy of the urinary
bladder, urethra, vagina and rectum, at rest, when coughing, straining and during
pelvic �oor contraction.
This can be used to assess the position of the urethra and bladder and their changes
during strain and whether the bladder neck shows a funnel formation under
pressure.
Characteristic ultrasound �ndings with stress urinary incontinence are:
• Funneling of bladder neck under pressure
• Hypermobility of the urethral ori�ce (bladder neck)
Figure 2: PF ultrasound images of a patient with stress urinary incontinence.
Left at rest, right under strain (arrow shows funnel formation of upper urethra) At rest, the high starting
position of the bladder neck is similar to the healthy situation. However, under pressure, there is a funnel-
shaped downward movement of the bladder neck with subsequent urine loss.

Urethral pressure ((urethral pressure
measurement,, urethral pressure pro��llee))
Urodynamic methods to determine urethral closure when resting and under
stress.
Resting urethral pressure pro��llee
The normal value for urethral pressure is (100 minus age) in cm H2O. If the resting
pressure pro�le shows a hypotonic urethra (maximum ≤ 20 cm H2O), lower success
rates are to be expected for all types of incontinence surgery.
Figure 3: Example of a 40-year-old woman with stress urinary incontinence. (Maximum age-related urethral
closure pressure should be 60 cm H2O; here it is reduced to 40 cm H2O).
Urethral stress pro��llee
Pressure measurement with simultaneous repeated coughing (= stress test).
Figure 4: Continent stress pressure pro�le: With every cough, a pressure increase is observed in the urethra.
This ensures continence.

topic: Stress urinary incontinence and overactive bladder  diagnosis (Teil 5/12)
keywords: stress urinary incontinence and overactive bladder  diagnosis
content:
Figure 5: Incontinent stress pressure pro�le: With every cough, a pressure decrease is observed in the
urethra. The patient leaks urine when coughing.

Diagnosis
Medical history
• Imperative urge to urinate (overactive bladder (OAB) dry) and/or nocturia - often
associated with involuntary urine leakage (OAB wet)
• Pollakiuria (micturition > 8 times per day)
Bladder diary
• Informative for diagnosis and treatment purpose
Gynecological (pelvic �oor) examination: speculum/vaginal examination
• vaginal wall skin trophic
• vaginal discharge (amount, properties)
• Uterine descent
• Protrusion of the vaginal walls (cystocele, rectocele, enterocele)
Exclusion of vaginal and urethral infection (DD)
• Urine status and culture (culture and sensitivity): testing for typical causes of
cystitis and urethritis (including chlamydia, potentially ureaplasma and
trichomonads).
Morphological diagnostics: Reasons for urge symptoms: visible in PF
ultrasonography
• Obstructive micturition disorders following incontinence surgery, e.g. due to a
dystope TVT too close to the urethra or after colposuspension
• Urethral diverticulum
• Hematoma
• Urethra-cystoscopy (+ cough test) (see below)
Metabolic assessment
• Blood tests are used for basic and differential diagnosis (including testing for
diabetes, for example)
Urodynamics
• Cystometry: Incontinence with detrusor overactivity is found when the detrusor
muscle contractions (phases of increased intravesical pressure) occur and
urine leakage is observed at the same time.
• Other possible cystometry �ndings include: premature �rst urge to urinate/

topic: Stress urinary incontinence and overactive bladder  diagnosis (Teil 7/12)
keywords: stress urinary incontinence and overactive bladder  diagnosis
content:
Figure 12: Symptom: overactive bladder (wet).
Reason: The misplaced tape (arrow) (too high, close to the bladder neck) causes urinary urgency. The tape is
localized in the external urethral sphincter and already eroded the urethra.
Figure 13: Symptom: overactive bladder.
Reason: The urinary bladder tumor (arrow), visible with PF ultrasound, leads to urinary urgency.

Figure 14: Symptom: Irritable bladder symptoms with insu�cient Botox effect, DD triggering by recurrent
cystocele, tendency to residual urinary tendency, Bulkamid depot at the bladder entrance (arrow), TVT
localization close to the urethra (courtesy of Prof. V. Viereck).
Solution: Transurethral resection of the Bulkamid depot at the bladder neck
Urethrocystoscopy
Urethrocystoscopy is indicated to diagnose urinary incontinence, especially in case
of OAB. Bladder related causes for urge incontinence can be detected, such as
in�ammation, intravesicular foreign objects, tumors and bladder stones. At the same
time, an obstruction of the urethra can be excluded.
Figure 15: Symptom: OAB wet with typical small capacity bladder hypertrophy (severely thickened detrusor
strands) and pseudo-diverticula.

Figure 16: Symptom: OAB with urinary bladder carcinoma.
Cystometry
The pressure curve in the bladder (cystometry) is used to investigate bladder
storage function, and thus in to diagnose detrusor overactivity.
During cystometry, the bladder is back-�lled with physiological saline solution at
body temperature. Filling speed, volume and intravesical pressure are recorded over
time (top curve, red).
Figure 17: In the example shown, a pelvic �oor EMG was carried out in addition to the cystometry (lower
curve, black) - but this is not standard. The EMG shows voluntary pelvic �oor contractions after the third
pressure spike (detrusor contraction, in this case each time associated with urine loss). By this, the patient
avoids urine leakage.

topic: Stress urinary incontinence and overactive bladder  diagnosis (Teil 8/12)
keywords: stress urinary incontinence and overactive bladder  diagnosis
content:
Uro��owmetry
Measuring urine �ow (uro�owmetry) to evaluate bladder emptying disorders.
The following parameters are investigated:
• Urine �ow rate (normal value: > 15 ml per second)
• Total �ow time (normal value: < 20 seconds)
• Total urine volume (normal value: > 150 ml)
• Post voiding residual urine (< 50 ml)
Obstruction of the urethra leads to voiding disorders. The typical pattern of
uro�owmetry shows a �at curve, a low �ow rate and a long urination time. Low �ow
rates in combination with a lack of urethral relaxation and increased abdominal
pressure are an indication for postoperative micturition disorders. However, female
micturition behavior is di�cult to reproduce and depends on external factors. For
differential diagnosis, PF sonography, especially after surgery, helps to identify tape
obstruction, hematoma, or a dystope tape/mesh (�gure 12).
Figure 18: The curve shows normal bladder voiding (bell-shaped).
Figure 19: The curve shows staccato micturition (urination with interruptions, discontinuous bladder voiding).

topic: Stress urinary incontinence and overactive bladder  diagnosis (Teil 9/12)
keywords: stress urinary incontinence and overactive bladder  diagnosis
content:
Differential diagnosis
• Urine leakage associated with a strong/sudden urge to urinate is indicative for
bladder overactivity.
• Urine leakage in connection with physical exertion is indicative for stress
urinary incontinence.
• The frequent combination of stress urinary incontinence and overactive bladder
is called mixed urinary incontinence.
• An overactive bladder can turn into urge incontinence. This transition depends
on the lower anatomical position causing a decreased urethral sphincter
closure pressure and on the severity of pelvic �oor muscle defects.
Further investigations for the differential diagnosis:
• Neurological disorders with loss of control of the micturition center in the brain
stem (supraspinal re�ex incontinence; uninhibited neuropathic bladder)
• Symptomatic urinary incontinence as a result of e.g. bladder diseases (bladder
in�ammation (“simple pollakiuria with urinary tract infection”), tumors, stones)
or pronounced estrogen de�ciency (postmenopausal).
• A constant loss of urine, equally pronounced during day and night, could
indicate incontinence due to a �stula (“via falsa”).
• Predominant daytime symptoms may indicate a psychogenic component.
A dystopic tape could be the cause of the symptoms, such as persistent or recurrent
stress urinary incontinence, bladder voiding disorders, recurrent urinary tract
infections, irritable bladder symptoms, pain and dyspareunia. PF sonography
identi�es if a tape is positioned too tight to the urethra. In the 7 to 10 days after tape
insertion, obstructive symptoms can be cured by tape mobilization. The patient
typically stays continent afterwards. Ten or more days after the surgery, obstructive
voiding can only be cured by tape incision or partial tape resection. However, this
leads to a recurrence of incontinence in 52 % of all cases. Patients should be
informed about this before the operation. Recurrent incontinence can be temporarily
treated with pessary therapy and later corrected with another tape insertion. If an
obstructive tape is not split/resected within the �rst three postoperative months, the
success rates may be lower. Although the bladder obstruction has been cured, pain,
dyspareunia and irritable bladder problems may persist and become chronic.

EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Urogynecology 〉 Stress urinary incontinence: treatment
Last update: February 2024
Stress urinary incontinence:: treatment
Author:: DDrr.. med.. Hansjörg Huemer
Selbständiger Arzt, Belegarzt (Bethesda Spital Basel)
Authors of former script versions: Prof. Dr. med. Gabriel Schär, Prof. Dr. med.
Annette Kuhn, PD Dr. med. Antoine Weil

Introduction
• If stress urinary incontinence leads to psychological or social distress,
treatment is warranted.
• Conservative therapeutic modalities are usually the �rst step (�rst line therapy).
• A su�cient improvement in continence can be expected in daily routine in
approx. 50 % to 80 % of cases with conservative treatment.
• Suburethral sling surgery is the gold standard in terms of surgical treatment for
stress urinary incontinence. The success rate is approx. 85 %.
• If there is a concomitant downward displacement of the genital organs, or a
relevant psychological suffering and a patients desire for surgery, a primary
surgical treatment for anatomic correction can be appropriate.
• For a successful conservative treatment, the patient’s active participation and
thus motivation is paramount.
• The aim of urinary incontinence physiotherapy is to teach awareness (pelvic
�oor muscles, respiration, etc.), to strengthen the pelvic �oor muscles, and to
practice re�exive pelvic �oor activity (e.g., when coughing or lifting a load). The
following therapeutic modalities are currently available: Pelvic �oor muscle
training and electrical stimulation of the pelvic �oor. Both can be combined with
biofeedback. The choice between these treatment modalities is guided by
whether or not the patient can correctly activate the pelvic �oor. If testing
(ability to contract the pelvic �oor; see Diagnosis) shows that the patient can
contract the pelvic �oor autonomously, she will be instructed in pelvic �oor
muscle training. If no voluntary contraction can be felt, electrical stimulation
and biofeedback are indicated.

Pelvic ��oor training
Independent pelvic �oor muscle training: Women who can independently activate the
pelvic �oor muscles must train the pelvic �oor for a few minutes, several times a day,
by contracting the muscles, and then holding the contraction for 5 seconds before
relaxing for 10 seconds. This improves muscle strength and the neuromuscular
coordination. It is also essential that re�exive contraction on physical stress
(coughing, sneezing etc.) is recognized and employed.
Not all women are able to train their pelvic �oor muscles independently. Contrary to
other muscle groups within the body, the pelvic �oor muscles are not visible. There
are ways to make patients “more aware” of the pelvic �oor in order to facilitate
conditioning and training success. Pelvic �oor activity can be demonstrated using
additional tools like biofeedback, providing the patient with feedback on the success
of her muscle training (�gure 1).
Prior to the start of pelvic �oor training, information on the training sessions and
homework as well as the chances of success must be given to the patient. For
electrical stimulation and biofeedback it is essential that the patient understands
that a probe is inserted into the vagina, and that roughly 50 to 60 % of women are
satis�ed with their success in the �rst year after training. If training is neglected,
urinary incontinence often deteriorates again.
In most cases, conservative treatment does not enable complete continence to be
achieved. However, the effect is often su�cient for women to cope with in everyday
life and therefore be satis�ed.

Figure 1: Physiotherapy with electrical stimulation and biofeedback. The patient can insert the vaginal probe
herself. With biofeedback, the physiotherapist can instruct the patient on correct pelvic �oor training using
the EMG curve taken from the pelvic �oor. In addition, the probe can emit a current pulse to trigger a muscle
contraction.

Drugs//medical treatment
Estrogen is potentially an option, though it primarily improves the symptoms of
vaginal atrophy in postmenopausal women, rather than stress urinary incontinence.
They are therefore only an additive therapy. Local treatment with estrogen cream or
ovules is su�cient. [B06][B07]
Estriol, e.g., Ovestin® Cream and Ovules or Oestro Gynaedron® Cream are applied
vaginally once or twice per week in the evening. The creams are supplied with an
acrylic tube. The tube is �lled with the cream and must be inserted vaginally. The
stamp is then pressed in order to introduce the correct amount of cream into the
vagina.
In some EU countries, although not yet Switzerland, duloxetine (Yentreve®) has been
authorized since fall 2004. Duloxetine is a reuptake inhibitor for serotonin and
norepinephrine.
Duloxetine probably increases the neurotransmitter concentration and thus, the tone
and contractility of the internal bladder sphincter. Studies show that the frequency of
unintentional urination is reduced signi�cantly with this drug in over 50 % of patients,
and quality of life is improved. However, full recovery proves to be rare.
Yentreve® dosage = one 40mg tablet each morning and evening. A gradual increase
in dose with 2 x 20 mg per day can reduce side effects like dizziness and nausea.

Pessary treatment
Various pessaries are available on the market; they are inserted vaginally and can
lead to an improvement of the urethral closure pressure and thus, a reduction of
stress urinary incontinence. Pessary treatment can provide a valuable improvement
in stress urinary incontinence, particularly in patients with contraindications for
surgical procedures. Such an approach can be successful with little effort in women
who are only incontinent during exercise. Pessary treatment can therefore be seen
as a palliative option, which helps to avoid or to delay an operation. Urethra
pessaries (�gure 2) or so-called incontinence tampons are mainly used.
Figure 2: Urethra pessary of varying sizes.

Incontinence surgery
If the symptoms of stress urinary incontinence cannot be su�ciently treated with
conservative treatment options, incontinence surgery is indicated.
Today, suburethral sling surgery is the “gold standard”. TVT (tension-free vaginal
tape) was the �rst introduced sling and is currently the most well-known sling
procedure. TVT and subsequent sling procedures provide minimally invasive options
for vaginal continence surgery, showing very good results for continence with
minimal peri- and post-operative morbidity.
Sling surgery has largely replaced the previous colposuspension techniques. To date,
the success rate of TVT is at least equivalent to the open abdominal Burch
colposuspension; however, the cost of the surgery is lower, with post-operative
shorter hospitalization time. A polypropylene is applied to create a support under the
mid urethra (�gure 3). Following the surgery, in situations where the intrabdominal
pressure is increased (coughing, pressing, exercise), the urethra is then compressed
against the TVT tape and supports the continence mechanism.
Suburethral sling surgery may be associated with complications, such as bladder
injury, hemorrhage, infection, and wound healing disorders. Severe complications,
such as injury of the large blood vessels, bowel or nerves, prove to be extremely rare.
In addition to the proper training of surgeons, it is paramount that women are
carefully informed of possible complications so that the expected effect can be
weighed against the potential side effects.

The major success of TVT surgery has led to a variety of new sling techniques
coming onto the market. Most of these methods are closely related to TVT. The
largest difference is in the surgical technique due to a different insertion approach
(transobturator) or shorter slings (minislings). The tape material used as today's
standard is a woven mono�lament polypropylene mesh for both procedures.
The most common intraoperative complication of TVT insertion is a bladder injury,
which is why retropubic slings must always be combined with cystoscopy. When
detected during surgery by cystoscopy and handled correctly, this issue can be
corrected with no further disadvantage to the patient. Nevertheless, an approach
that avoids the retropubic area has been developed. Hereby the tape is inserted
through the obturator foramen (TOT, TVT-O) (�gure 4).
Comparative studies showed that the transobturator procedure is equivalent to the
retropubic approach in terms of success rate, but that in addition to the reduced risk
of bladder injury, other, new issues can occur, such as inguinofemoral pain for
several days post-surgery, and sometimes beyond, lateral vaginal mesh erosion with
corresponding cohabitation issues can also be seen. Here, in-depth training and
compliance with the standard operating steps are essential to avoid unnecessary
complications. The cost-bene�t-risk analysis reveals a better overall outcome for
retropubic slings.
According to current data, laser therapy has been able to achieve a slight
improvement in symptoms of mild stress incontinence [P10].
Another form of surgical therapy is the injection of bulking agents under the urethral
mucosa.
The advantage of this method is that it can be performed in LA and has very few
complications. However, the long-term results are not comparable with sling
operations [P11].
The Swiss Working Group for Urogynecology and Pelvic Floor Pathology (AUG, Swiss
Working Group for Gynecology), together with the Specialist Society for Urology, the
Swiss Medical Association and quality experts, has published an Expert Opinion with
recommendations on sling surgery. We recommend taking this Expert Opinion into
account and acting accordingly [P09]. See also expert letter Nr. 44 (about sling
operations for the treatment of female stress incontinence (stress incontinence)) of
Swiss Society for Gynecology and Obstetrics (SGGG) (available in German and

French). [P09][P10][P11]
gynécologie Suisse SGGG – expert letter
In addition, the AUG, together with the AGUB (German society) and the AUB (Austria),
was involved in the creation of a joint guideline that comprehensively covers all
topics relating to female incontinence [P10]. [P10]
In experienced hands, sling surgery is very effective (objective recovery rate approx.
85 %). Potential risks include:
• Bladder perforation
• Pain (generally short term, rarely long term)
• Dyspareunia (painful intercourse)
• Incision site healing disorders (erosion)
• Blood vessel/bowel damage
Figure 3: TVT (tension free vaginal tape) inserted behind the pubic symphysis and under the mid-section of
the urethra.
The plastic tape is used to establish a pubo-urethral anchor point for the mid-third of
the urethra:
• The TVT should neither elevate nor obstruct the urethra.
• It should act as a support to improve pressure transmission in case of physical
stress.

EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Urogynecology 〉 Urinary incontinence: classi�cation
Last update: January 2024
Urinary incontinence –– classi��cation
Author:: Prof.. DDrr.. med.. Cornelia Betschart
Leitende Ärztin, Klinik für Gynäkologie, UniversitätsSpital Zürich, Stv. Klinikdirektorin Klinik für
Gynäkologie & Co-Leiterin Beckenboden- und Kontinenzzentrum
CCoo--Author((ss)):: Dr. med. Beatrix Hämmerle-Spinnler
Authors of former script versions: PD Dr. med. Daniele Perucchini, Dr. med.
Daniel Faltin

DDee��nition
In developed countries, people aged 64 years old and older are the fastest growing
population. Urinary incontinence is a wide-spread disease and rises with aging.
Complaints range from sporadic, drop-wise urine loss to more severe urine loss with
a feeling of insecurity (smell, wetness), to social withdrawal. The personal and socio-
economic burden is highly underestimated.
Urinary incontinence is de�ned as the complaint of any involuntary leakage of urine
(International Continence Society, ICS 2002). Incontinence is not regarded as one
entity. It has to be distinguished according to symptoms and underlying diseases,
such as infections, etc., they have to be excluded (see below).
Most women do not speak to their doctor about their incontinence. This may be
in�uenced by the widespread opinion that incontinence is a normal condition after
childbirth or occurs with aging. However, a radical change is underway.
Understandably, the demands of women affected by this condition have to be
addressed. Nowadays, women want to remain their professional and private
activities and their mobility. They wish to keep their continence up to elderly age.
Even retired women do not want to give up their walks and "physically challenging"
hobbies.
DDee��nitions bbyy ICS && IUGA
The International Continence Society (ICS) and the International Urogynecological
Association (IUGA) set out the de�nitions of incontinence in joint reports. They take
into account the clinical character and symptoms, as given by the patient.
The ICS and IUGA are constantly attempting to standardize the key terms, units, and
examination tools to be used for describing continence and pelvic �oor issues, and
makes all documents relating to standardization freely available on the website
icso�ce.org.
International Continence Society (ICS)
International Urogynecological Association (IUGA)

Most common forms ooff incontinence iinn
women
Urinary incontinence is de�ned as complaints of involuntary loss of urine.
In general, there are two main forms of incontinence in women [B05]:
• Stress urinary incontinence: Involuntary loss of urine during exertion or physical
activity (e.g. during sport) or when sneezing or coughing.
• Overactive bladder (also called OAB (wet) or urge incontinence): Involuntary
loss of urine on urge. Overactive bladder can also occur without incontinence
(OAB dry). The key difference is, whether or not the toilet can be reached in time
when an urge to urinate occurs - this is also dependent on the situation (at
home, away from home, etc.).
[B05]
Mixed urinary incontinence, which is more common in older patients, refers to
the combination of both symptoms of stress incontinence and overactive
bladder. [P03]
Urine incontinence
Incontinence is a storage function pathology. The various forms of incontinence
have either a functional or an anatomical cause. The above mentioned main forms
of incontinence are by far the most frequently encountered in GP and gynecology
incontinence consultations (90 %).
Epidemiologically, stress urinary incontinence is the most common, with a
prevalence of up to approx. 80 % for geriatric people. Overactive bladder with urge
incontinence (OAB wet) makes up between 10 and 30 %, while the proportion of
mixed urinary incontinence, i.e. a combination of stress urinary incontinence and
overactive bladder, is between 20 and 60 %.

Stress urinary incontinence ((SUI))
During physical effort, such as sneezing or coughing, involuntary urine leakage
occurs without an urge to urinate. In general, any mechanical stress that leads to an
increase in abdominal pressure can trigger stress urinary incontinence. In this case,
the urethral internal sphincter opens in an uncontrolled manner, without any
simultaneous detrusor contraction.
As the pressure in the bladder only increases slightly as it �lls, due to the compliance
of the bladder walls, in phases of physical relaxation only a slightly positive pressure
gradient in the urethra is required to prevent involuntary urine leakage. This situation
changes under physical stress (e.g. sneezing and coughing), which can lead to a
rapid, signi�cant increase in intra-abdominal pressure and easily exceeds even the
best urethral closure pressure.
Two mechanisms are required to maintain continence:
• "Guarding re�ex" which actively increases the muscle tone of the urethral
sphincter muscles in these situations
• Passive transmission of the increase in intra-abdominal pressure to the urethra
through the slow twitch �bres
The more this pressure transmission declines, the greater the probability of stress
urinary incontinence arises. Conversely, excellent pressure transmission is required if
the individual urethral closure pressure is very low. Pressure transmission is
dependent on the tone of the pelvic �oor muscles and the elasticity of the connective
tissue. The urethral sphincter muscles are striated skeletal muscles and can be
trained accordingly with physiotherapy. [P05]
Main causes of stress urinary incontinence: damage to the bladder and urethra
supporting structures due to pregnancy and childbirth, or the aging process.
In the past, signi�cant damage of the supporting structures and the resulting urethral
(hyper) mobility as a cause of stress urinary incontinence was overestimated.
However, hormonal and neuronal factors also play a role in continence.
In addition to these factors, there are other risk factors such as parity, body weight
and smoking.

Figure 1: Risk factors for pelvic �oor dysfunction.

Overactive bladder ((OAB))

Synonyms: hyperactive bladder, hyperactive urinary bladder syndrome, (the term
“irritable bladder syndrome” is also sometimes used in the general press).
In 2010, the ICS/IUGA rolled out a new de�nition for the condition previously known
as urge incontinence or irritable bladder.
Since then, “Overactive Bladder (OAB)”, or overactive bladder syndrome, has been the
preferred term. This is a symptom-oriented paraphrasing of a condition. Urgency is
de�ned as a key symptom. It is understood to be an intense, sudden, urgent need to
urinate. This urge can hardly be suppressed with effort. However, “urge” can also be
understood as a “normal” urge to urinate. An urge to urinate without involuntary urine
leakage is called OAB dry. In this case, the urethral sphincter is still strong enough to
withstand the detrusor contraction with su�cient pressure. In the case of "urge
incontinence", this is no longer guaranteed, i.e. involuntary loss of urine occurs when
an imperative urge occurs (OAB wet - "wet" hyperactive bladder).
Frequency (urination > 8 times in 24 hours), reduced urination volume and nocturia
are a consequence of the imperative urge to urinate. In one third of patients with
OAB, also suffer from urge incontinence.
For the diagnosis of overactive bladder, the ICS requires to rule out metabolic and
local pathological factors (e.g. diabetes mellitus, urinary tract infection, genital
atrophy) that could explain these symptoms. However, the ruling out “of other local
pathological and endocrinal factors” is not de�ned precisely. Urine testing and
potentially blood testing (glucose) are certainly included. From an urogynecological
perspective, the assessment of the pelvic �oor including genital atrophy (pH strip
test), pelvic organ prolapse and elevated residual volume.
The cause of overactive bladder syndrome is not yet fully understood. In most
women an underlying cause cannot be found. This condition is de�ned as
"idiopathic" overactive bladder.
During urodynamics examination, two different kinds of OAB can be distinguished:
with or without uninhibited detrusor contractions. Women with bladder overactivity
without incontinence (OAB/dry) can show detrusor contractions on urodynamic
studies too. In this case, it can be assumed that the urethral sphincter is su�ciently
strong to counteract the loss of urine through the fast twitch �bres.

Mixed urinary incontinence
The de�nition of mixed urinary incontinence from the International Continence
Society (ICS) 2002 bases on history and clinical �ndings alone. The diagnosis can be
drawn if women present with symptoms of both overactive bladder and stress
urinary incontinence (mixed urinary incontinence = “the complaint of involuntary
leakage associated with urgency and also with exertion, effort, sneezing or
coughing”) [B01].
Women with mixed urinary incontinence have a higher burden of psychological
issues compared to women who suffer from OAB or SUI alone. [B01]

With urodynamic studies the pre-dominant incontinence component can be
detected. Urodynamically, women with urge-dominant mixed incontinence present
more frequently with detrusor overactivity, early �rst urge to urinate (< 150 ml
bladder volume), and lower maximum bladder capacity (< 300 ml bladder volume).
This is re�ected in the bladder diary in lower micturition volumes, lower than those of
women with pre-dominant stress urinary incontinence. Urodynamic studies are
therefore helpful to distinguish between the two conditions, especially the OAB
component, while stress urinary incontinence can usually be detected and classi�ed
from the medical history and clinical diagnostics, such as a cough test. Urodynamic
studies of most women with mixed urinary incontinence do not show stress urinary
incontinence and detrusor overactivity simultaneously. In a group of 950 incontinent
women at Duke University, 52 % had mixed symptoms, but only 13 % showed mixed
�ndings [P01].
According to Minassian, the prevalence of mixed urinary incontinence is
approximately 17 times higher than expected (stress urinary incontinence 13 %, urge
incontinence 5 %: Expected: 0,13 x 0,05 = 0,0065, but “Mixed UI found” = 11 %) [P06].
One explanation might be that the called “susceptibility model”, where the presence
of one disease supports a second disease. However, the severity of urinary
incontinence could also promote symptoms of urgency (“severity model”). A
connection between increasing severity of stress urinary incontinence and
increasing symptoms of urgency was already suggested by Bump (2003). He
hypothesized that mixed urinary incontinence is actually a “severe form” of SUI and/
or urge incontinence [P01].
Telemann (2004) was able to show that the frequency of symptoms of urgency
increases with the severity of stress incontinence [P02]. [P01][P02][P06]

Figure 2: Stress urinary incontinence. Severity and frequency of urgency.
Urodynamics should be indicated if treatment has failed after basic OAB diagnostics (basic diagnostics =
medical history, exclusion of urinary tract infection and exclusion of residual urine > 100 ml).

Rare forms ooff incontinence iinn women
Incontinence with neurogenic overactive bladder//neurogenic
urge incontinence
Women with underlying neurological conditions such as Parkinson’s disease,
multiple sclerosis and diabetes mellitus, as well as cerebral sclerosis and apoplexy,
bladder voiding disorders with urinary incontinence can be observed quite often.
Involuntary contractions occur similar to women with idiopathic overactive bladder.
This kind of urine leakage is referred to as incontinence with neurogenic detrusor
overactivity, which should replace the older term “re�ex incontinence”.
In case of urodynamically veri�ed neurogenic detrusor overactivity, there is always a
underlying neurological problem.
Chronic retention ooff urine ((previously over��ooww bladder))
Most often a consequence of long-term distension of the bladder, either due to an
intravesicular obstruction (e.g. prolapse, obstructive incontinence surgery) or as a
consequence of a peripheral bladder denervation (e.g. radical pelvic surgery). Here,
the over�lled bladder presents an intra-vesicular pressure which exceeds the urethral
closure pressure even when the body is relaxed. This results in a continuous
(“over�ow”) urine leakage.
Fistula formation
This can occur as bladder or urethral �stula and is either the result of surgical or
obstetric injury, in�ammatory or radiogenic processes or a tumor-related disease. In
rare cases, malformations in the genital tract region cause incontinence.
""Pseudoincontinence""
In terms of differential diagnosis, there are situations that resemble organic
incontinence:
• limited mobility → treatment: treat the underlying disease: e.g. arthritis,
Parkinson’s.
• with poor vision → treatment: simplify access to toilet.
Giggle incontinence

topic: Urinary tract infections in women classification (Teil 1/7)
content:
EGONEplus 〉 Gynecology EGONEplus 〉 Urogynecology 〉 Urinary tract infections in women: classi�cation
Last update: September 2024
Urinary tract infections iinn women::
classi��cation
Author:: DDrr.. med.. Gloria Ryu
Oberärztin Frauenklinik, Koordinatorin Beckenbodenzentrum, Kantonsspital Aarau
Authors of former script versions: Prof. Dr. med. G. Schär, PD Dr. med. Antoine
Weil

DDee��nitions
• Uncomplicated urinary tract infection: simple acute cystitis without fever, risk
factors, or secondary diseases. Reacts quickly to treatment.
• Complicated urinary tract infection: Infection of the urinary tract (bladder,
kidneys) in patients with risk factors or secondary diseases.
• Pyelonephritis: Urinary tract infection with kidney involvement and
corresponding symptoms including fever, kidney pain, feeling ill.
• Asymptomatic bacteriuria: Evidence of ≥ 100’000 bacteria in 1 ml urine without
typical symptoms of cystitis.

Epidemiology
Urinary tract infections are one of the most common reasons for medical
consultations. About 50 % of all women experience at least one urinary tract
infection in their lives. Annually, 20 to 40 in 1000 general practice consultations are
for urinary tract infections. 20 % of all antibiotics used in clinical practice are
prescribed for the treatment of UTIs. The �rst frequency peak occurs following �rst
sexual contact, with further peaks upon pregnancy and then within menopause.
Risk factors for urinary tract infections are as following:
• Previous urinary tract infection
• Sexual intercourse
• Use of diaphragms and spermicides
• Urinary tract infections during childhood (below 15 years)
• Mother with urinary tract infections
• Use of tampons and condoms
• Use of antibiotics in the preceding 2 to 4 weeks
• Genetic factors
• Advanced age

topic: Urinary tract infections in women classification (Teil 2/7)
keywords: urinary tract infections in women classification
content:
Diagnostics
The medical history should include the frequency of urinary tract infections and risk
factors (see above). In addition, complicated urinary tract infections must be
distinguished from uncomplicated ones. Secondary and underlying diseases should
therefore be searched for. Asking about fever, kidney pain and a general feeling of
illness likely provide information about pyelonephritis. A physical examination is
usually not necessary with uncomplicated urinary tract infections, if a reliable
medical history has been conducted.
Urine tests
Test strips are cheap and generally su�ce for diagnosing an uncomplicated urinary
tract infection. Positive nitrite test: Nitrites are released by E. Coli bacteria, and thus
have a high predictive value for E. Coli infections (approx. 90 %). However, as other
microorganisms can be present, the test’s sensitivity is only approx. 50 %. Proof of
leukocytes by means of leukocyte esterase is also very sensitive (approx. 90 %). If
both tests are negative, the probability of a urinary tract infection proves to be low.
Urine sediment testing and urine cultures are usually not necessary for
uncomplicated urinary tract infections.

Figure 1: Urine test strip. Test strips are immersed in fresh midstream urine and then analyzed against a
reference (with kind permission of Dr. G. Ryu).
Urine sediment
Urine sediment can be used to identify leukocyturia, hematuria, and bacteiuria.
Owing to the quick availability and quantitative information, such as bacteria counts,
a rapid diagnosis can be made to supplement the test strip. In addition, with
persistent symptoms, the bacteria count can help predict treatment response.
Overall, however, urine sediment testing is of little value.
Urine culture
Urine cultures are carried out in the following situations:

topic: Urinary tract infections in women classification (Teil 3/7)
keywords: urinary tract infections in women classification
content:
• Unsuccessfully treated primary uncomplicated urinary tract infection
• Recurrence of urinary tract infection
• Complicated urinary tract infection
• Abnormal test strip upon pregnancy
• Suspected pyelonephritis
Gynecological examination
A gynecological examination is rarely necessary with uncomplicated urinary tract
infections. For recurring, persistent, or complicated urinary tract infections, a
gynecological examination with vaginal examination and swabs of the vaginal
excretions and urethra is recommended. The examination should look for signs of
genital atrophy and indications of downward displacement, in particular cystocele,
prolapse of the uterus, or vaginal apex, along with any residual urine (ultrasound).
For complicated urinary tract infections, a urine culture should be obtained to
monitor the success of the treatment. In such cases, treatment duration should
exceed 3 days, with experts recommending 10 to 13 days (cf. G02H).

topic: Urinary tract infections in women classification (Teil 4/7)
keywords: urinary tract infections in women classification
content:
Pathogenesis ooff urinary tract infections
Cystitis most commonly occurs due to the ascension of perineal, perianal, and
intravaginal bacteria (�gure 2). Bacteria can travel trans-urethrally into the bladder
through mechanical manipulation, such as sexual intercourse, catheterization, or
water �ows from shower toilets. Hematogenic and lymphogenic infection pathways
are additionally possible, particularly with kidney infections, for example with
staphylococcus or tuberculosis bacteria.
Figure 2: Intestinal microorganisms colonize the perianal and vulvar skin, as well as the vagina, and ascend
trans-urethrally from there into the bladder.
Practical examples of complicated urinary tract infections. If the following
conditions are met, the urinary tract infection is classi�ed as complicated:
• Bladder catheter (transurethral and suprapubic catheter)
• Functional abnormality like pelvic organ prolapse, post-void residual volume of
(> 100 ml)
• Diabetes mellitus
• Functional or anatomical urinary tract disorders
• Immunosuppression
• Infection in children
• Infection in men
• Voidance disorders
• Kidney failure
• Pregnancy
• Urolithiasis

• Previous urinary tract surgery
• Polycystic kidney disease
• History of kidney transplantation

Predisposing factors
If factors explaining the de�nition of a complicated urinary tract infection are not
present, the following situations may lead to urinary tract infections and recurring
urinary tract infections:
Patient-related:
• Immune system defects in the bladder with lacking antibodies, for example
immunoglobulin A.
• Uro-mucosal disorders with bladder mucosa defects, which in turn facilitate
bacterial adhesion.
• Postmenopausal changes to vaginal secretions; a lack of lactobacilli leading to
an increase in pH, thus providing the perfect environment for uro-pathogenic
bacteria.
Bacteria-related:
• Virulence factors: Fimbriae are a virulence factor and potentially facilitate
bacterial adhesion (E. Coli �mbriae).

topic: Urinary tract infections in women classification (Teil 5/7)
content:
Bacterial spectrum ooff urinary tract
infections
Approximately 80 % of community-acquired urinary tract infections are caused by E.
Coli, compared with only 40 to 50 % of hospital-acquired infections. The frequency of
other pathogens such as Klebsiellae, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Enterococcus
faecalis, Streptococci, Staphylococcus epidermidis, Proteus mirabilis etc. increases
under hospital conditions. These pathogens exhibit stronger resistance to
antibiotics.
Table 1: Urinary tract infection bacterial spectrum
Community--acquired Hospital--acquired
E. coli (~ 80 %) E. coli (~ 50 %)
Staph. Saprophyticus Klebsiellae
Proteus mirabilis Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Enterococcus faecalis Enterococcus faecalis
Pseudomonas aeruginosa Streptococci
Klebsiellae Staph. epidermidis
Proteus mirabilis
A special culture medium is required to detect certain microorganisms. These
challenging microorganisms are listed in table 2.
Challenging microorganisms that require a special medium or detection method:
• Anaerobes
• Gardnerella vaginalis
• Neisseria gonorrhoeae
• Chlamydia trachomatis
• Mycoplasma hominis
• Ureaplasma urealyticum
• Herpesviruses
• Candida/Fungi
• Trichomonas vaginalis

Special culture media (cf. G03B) must therefore be requested if a urinary tract
infection is very likely, whereas urine culture has provided no indication of its cause.

Further diagnostics
Invasive and imaging diagnostics are unnecessary with simple urinary tract
infections. Structural and functional anomalies are rare. Exceptions are previous
urogenital tract interventions and persistent hematuria. In such cases, ultrasound of
the urinary tract, including the kidneys, and cystoscopy are recommended.

topic: Urinary tract infections in women treatment prophylaxis (Teil 1/13)
content:

Author:: DDrr.. med.. Gloria Ryu
Oberärztin Frauenklinik, Koordinatorin Beckenbodenzentrum, Kantonsspital Aarau
Authors of former script versions: Prof. Dr. med. Gabriel Schär, PD Dr. med.
Antoine Weil

Treatment ooff uncomplicated urinary tract
infections
Symptomatic urinary tract infections (UTI) are, in most cases, self-limiting. The
spontaneous recovery rate of a UTI is high at 50 to 70 %. Only 1 to 3 % of
uncomplicated UTIs become pyelonephritis if untreated. New treatment guidelines
therefore recommend non-antibiotic measures initially for uncomplicated urinary
tract infections in patients under 65 years old. Antibiotics are only recommended
within 48 hours if symptoms continue. In people older than 65 years, direct antibiotic
administration is still recommended for UTIs, as urosepsis and UTI-associated
comorbidities can occur more rapidly in this population. A urine culture is not
necessary for an uncomplicated UTI.
The following supportive measures are primarily recommended:
• Increasing �uid volumes (1,5 to 2 liters)
• Pain relief (NSAIDs)
• D-mannose
• Drinking cranberry juice or lingonberry juice
• Phytotherapeutics e.g. horseradish root (80 mg) and nasturtium herb (200 mg)
Angocin®, combination of centaury, lovage root and Rosemary leaves
Canephron®, bearberry extract Cystinol®, bearberry leaf tea
Today, the following antibiotics are recommended for uncomplicated urinary tract
infections.

The objective of treatment is an adequate start of treatment with rapid relief of
symptoms that is cost-effective with good compliance and as few side effects as
possible.
• Potential allergies must be taken into account before antibiotics are prescribed
(history).
• Broad-spectrum antibiotics such as penicillin (e.g. Augmentin®, generics) are
only rarely required in the treatment of UTIs. In the case of non-E. coli infections,
such as enterococci, which are resistant to �rst-line antibiotics, penicillin and its

derivatives are indicated. They are primarily used for pregnant women (see
Urinary Tract Infections in Pregnancy) due to their safety pro�le for the fetus.
Penicillins often cause side effects (GI tract, allergies, changes to bacterial
�ora, vaginal thrush).
• Quinolones are only indicated in rare cases and have been widely abandoned in
the treatment of UTIs due to tendinopathies and resistance. Quinolones are
contraindicated in pregnancy.

topic: Urinary tract infections in women treatment prophylaxis (Teil 4/13)
keywords: urinary tract infections in women treatment prophylaxis
content:
Treatment ooff complicated urinary tract
infections
Complicated UTIs are infections during pregnancy or in patients with uncontrolled
diabetes, hospital-acquired infections, infections in patients with kidney disorders,
history of kidney transplantation, urolithiasis, anatomical functional abnormalities
(pelvic organ prolapse, residual urine), immunosuppressive therapy, transurethral
catheters, and postoperative infections (predisposing factors).
Typically, complicated urinary tract infections are treated for longer, over a duration
of 7 to 14 days. The drugs used may be the same as those in the table above. With
complicated infections, antibiotics should be aligned with the results of
bacteriological testing with resistance screening. This also applies to recurrent
infections.

Asymptomatic bacteriuria
Asymptomatic bacteriuria (more than 10^5 bacteria/ml in midstream urine), as found
in 1 to 3 % of young women and 10 to 22 % of postmenopausal women, should not
be treated with antibiotics.
Asymptomatic bacteriuria should only be screened and treated before
urogynecological procedures. According to the latest data, screening for
asymptomatic bacteriuria and appropriate antibiotic therapy are not necessary in
pregnant women.

Check--ups for treated UTIs
With uncomplicated UTIs, no check-ups, e.g. with urine sediment or urine culture, are
necessary if the patient is free of symptoms. If the UTI symptoms continue after
treatment has ended, a urine culture with resistance screening should be carried out.

topic: Urinary tract infections in women treatment prophylaxis (Teil 5/13)
keywords: urinary tract infections in women treatment prophylaxis
content:
Urinary tract infections during pregnancy
By de�nition, a UTI during pregnancy is considered a complicated UTI.
In pregnant women, asymptomatic bacteriuria more frequently leads to
pyelonephritis, although this bears no increased risk of preterm delivery or other
neonatal/maternal complications. Asymptomatic bacteriuria is therefore no longer
screened or treated during pregnancy. Exceptions to this are pregnant women with
risk factors such as diabetes mellitus, immunosuppression, functional and structural
urinary tract disorders, history of pyelonephritis, history of preterm delivery or late
abortion. For these patients, systematic screening with urine dip testing is still
recommended. Screening is carried out using midstream urine. If this is abnormal, a
urine culture with microbial count and resistance screening should be carried out.

Pyelonephritis is more frequent during pregnancy. The risk increases in particular in
the second half of pregnancy as a result of the decreased urine �ow due to the
relative obstruction of the ureter as a result of the increasing uterine volume.
The treatment of UTIs without kidney involvement should be carried out over 3 to
5 days. If there are indications of pyelonephritis, treatment should last 10 to 14
days.
• For penicillin (e.g. Augmentin®, generics), cephalosporin (e.g. ceftriaxone,
Rocephin®), nitrofurantoin (e.g. Furadantin® retard, Uvamin® retard), their
safety during all stages of pregnancy has been proven.
• Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (e.g. Bactrim®, generics) should not be used in
the �rst and third trimesters. Trimethoprim is a folic acid antagonist and can
lead to structural defects such as neural tube defects and cardiovascular
defects.
• Quinolones, such as nor�oxacin (Noroxin®, generics) should not be used at any
stage of pregnancy, as they could theoretically cause malformations of the fetal
musculoskeletal system, developmental issues and arthropathy.
A follow-up urine culture should be carried out for pregnant women after the
completion of treatment.

Recurrent urinary tract infections
Recurrent urinary tract infections can occur if
• the treated microbe causes another infection (relapse)
• a new pathogen causes a new UTI (reinfection).
A quarter of all women with UTIs experience recurrent infections over time. There are
indications that the use of antibiotics disturbs the microbiome, encouraging
recurrent urinary tract infections.

topic: Urinary tract infections in women treatment prophylaxis (Teil 7/13)
keywords: urinary tract infections in women treatment prophylaxis
content:
Risks for recurrent UTIs
• Bladder voiding disorders with urine retention
• Cystocele with urine retention
• Intestinal and vaginal bacteria reservoir with uropathogenic microbes
• Immune system defects of the mucosa of the bladder
• Neurological disorders
• Permanent catheter
• Sexual intercourse and frequent changes in sexual partner or speci�c practices
• Use of spermicides with changes to the vaginal environment
• Pregnancy
• Diabetes mellitus
• Colpitis
The risks for recurrent infections correspond with the risks for complicated
urinary tract infections.
Strategies for preventing recurrent urinary tract infections
• Experienced patients begin conservative measures (increased �uid intake,
NSAIDs, phytotherapeutics) on the �rst symptoms, and only consult a doctor if
the symptoms do not subside after 2 to 3 days.
• Antibiotic recurrence prophylaxis with the lowest possible dose for 6 months.
• If there is an association between sexual contact and UTI, postcoital treatment
with a tablet (e.g. nitrofurantoin, sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim) can be taken.
Antibiotic recurrence prophylaxis
Antibiotic recurrence prophylaxis should be carried out at a low dose for 6 months.
The following drugs may be used:
Tabelle 5: Continuous and postcoital prophylaxis of recurrent UTIs
Antibiotics Dosage Duration
Nitrofurantoin 50–100 mg/d or 3 x/week 6 months, then withdrawal
attempt
Postcoital: within 30 min.
after intercourse

keywords: urinary tract infections in women treatment prophylaxis
content:
Antibiotics Dosage Duration
Co-trimoxazole 800/160 mg 1 x/d or 6 months, then withdrawal
(TMP/SFX) 800/160 mg 3 x/week attempt
Postcoital: within 30 min.
after intercourse
Fosfomycin 3 g every 10 d 6 months, then withdrawal
attempt
Antibiotic prophylaxis for 6 months is preventive and not therapeutic. This
means that once prophylaxis is discontinued, the risk of recurrent infections is
the same as before the start of prophylactic antibiotics.
The basis for any treatment of recurrent urinary tract infections is advice with
changes to behavior.
Behavioral changes
• Drinking 1,5 to 2 liters per day
• Cranberry juice, 300 ml per day or lingonberry 100 ml per day
• Bearberry extracts or bearberry leaf tea
• Avoiding excessive cleaning with frequent use of soap on the vulva and perianal
area
• Using pH-neutral soap
• Using shower toilets only for the anal area, not for the vulva and vaginal opening
• Cleaning after bowel movements always from front to back
• Perianal and vulvar skincare with oily, lipophilic cream
• Urinating within 1 hour after sexual intercourse
• In menopause, using vaginal estrogen creams or pessaries
• D-mannose (2 g per day), which binds to the bacterial pili and thus reduces the
adherence of E. coli to the urothelium
• In�uencing of intestinal bacteria with probiotics (lactic acid bacteria,
bi�dobacteria)
• Oral immunization with lyophilized Escherichia coli (Uro-Vaxom), 1 tbl./day for 3
months
• Acidifying the urine with L-methionine (Acimethin), 1 to 2 500 mg tablets 3x per
day
• Intravesical instillations of hyaluronic acid and chondroitin sulfate to build up
the glycosaminoglycan layer on the urothelium in case of recurrent UTI

• Plant-based drugs such as birch leaf preparations, combinations of xyloglucan
and gelatin, which form a bio�lm and reduce bacterial adherence in the
intestine (changing the intestinal microbiome) and propolis and hibiscus, which
acidify the urin

topic: Urinary tract infections in women treatment prophylaxis (Teil 9/13)
keywords: urinary tract infections in women treatment prophylaxis
content:
Drinking increases urinary �ow and thus the elimination of bacteria. Cranberries and
lingonberries contain tannins that combat the adherence of bacteria to the bladder
wall in the urinary tract. The same applies to many bladder teas.
Skincare prevents the colonization of the skin by uropathogenic microbes. However,
it is important not to disturb the natural acidic sebaceous �lm on the skin and
maintain the skin’s defenses with moisturization.
Shower toilets can be useful for anal hygiene. However, if used on the vaginal
opening, they can also �ush bacteria into the urethra and bladder. As bacterial
colonization generally originates in the intestine, cleaning from front to back after
bowel movements is recommended.
Localized application of estrogen creams and pessaries (e.g. Ortho-Gynest, Ovestin,
Oestro-Gynaedron to the vagina once or twice per week in the evening) facilitates
glycogen formation and thus the colonization of the vagina with lactobacilli. This
reduces the pH of vaginal secretions, which has an antibacterial effect. This
prophylactic effect has been proven in studies, while the other points given above are
reasonably recommended, although there is no clear data.
According to more recent studies, probiotics have been declared “friendly” bacteria
for intestinal health. The most commonly used probiotic organisms are
Lactoferment® and bi�dobacteria. It has been shown that probiotics can be used to
prevent urinary tract infections. It has also been proven that they can treat bacterial
vaginosis. Probiotics are mostly administered with yogurt. It has also been shown
that probiotics can help prevent vaginal thrush.
Oral immunization is carried out with lyophilized bacterial lysate of E. coli. This
stimulates IgA excretion in the bladder.
A study shows the effectiveness of D-mannose in comparison with a group with
antibiotic prophylaxis and with a group without prophylaxis. D-mannose was equally
effective as the antibiotic nitrofurantoin in that 85 % of women were recurrence-free,
whereas without prophylaxis only 40 % were recurrence-free. With an equivalent
effect, signi�cantly fewer side effects occurred with D-mannose (2 g/d) compared to
nitrofurantoin (50 mg/d) [P10].
Methenamine hippurate, a urine-sterilizing substance, was shown to be on a par with
